Was Mozart programmed or pre-programmed and was Newton or anyone else solely responsible for inventing anything?With Arthur I. Miller, Professor of History and Philosophy of Science, Department of Science & Technology, University College London; Michael Howe, Professor of Psychology, Exeter University; Dr Juliet Mitchell, psychoanalyst and lecturer at Cambridge University.
episodes.summaryAI: The nature and origins of genius, creativity, and inspiration. Examines whether exceptional intellectual ability is innate or cultivated, drawing on cases of Einstein, Mozart, and Newton. Addresses neurological perspectives including studies of Einstein's brain structure. Covers historical conceptions of genius from divine inspiration in antiquity through Romantic notions of the solitary creator. Explores environmental and social conditions enabling breakthroughs, the role of collaboration and prior knowledge, and whether great discoveries are inevitable or contingent on individuals. Spans psychology, neuroscience, philosophy, and history of ideas.

episodes.title: Intelligence
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a question that has stalked the twentieth century: Intelligence. Since the first IQ tests were invented in 1905, the question of what makes Homo Sapiens stupid and what makes him clever has involved human kind in sterilisation, racism and misery. How do we define intelligence, how do we measure it; what are its origins and how do we uncover it? But are we any closer to understanding what this elusive quality of intelligence is? The debate still rages as to whether we are born with it or whether intelligence is something we develop as we grow, and evidence for either camp seems to pile up almost daily. With Dr Ken Richardson, educational psychologist, former Senior Lecturer, Open University and author of The Making of Intelligence; Professor Michael Ruse Philosopher of Biology, University of Guelph, Ontario and author of Mystery of Mysteries: Is Evolution a Social Construction?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a question that has stalked the twentieth century: Intelligence. Since the first IQ tests were invented in 1905, the question of what makes Homo Sapiens stupid and what makes him clever has involved human kind in sterilisation, racism and misery. How do we define intelligence, how do we measure it; what are its origins and how do we uncover it? But are we any closer to understanding what this elusive quality of intelligence is? The debate still rages as to whether we are born with it or whether intelligence is something we develop as we grow, and evidence for either camp seems to pile up almost daily. With Dr Ken Richardson, educational psychologist, former Senior Lecturer, Open University and author of The Making of Intelligence; Professor Michael Ruse Philosopher of Biology, University of Guelph, Ontario and author of Mystery of Mysteries: Is Evolution a Social Construction?
episodes.summaryAI: The definition, measurement, and politics of human intelligence. Covers the development of IQ testing from Binet's 1905 scale onward, including its misuse in eugenics programs, forced sterilisation, and scientific racism. Examines the nature-versus-nurture debate over cognitive ability, including twin studies and environmental factors such as education and nutrition. Addresses multiple intelligence theories, cultural bias in testing, and the relationship between intelligence metrics and social outcomes. Explores what cognitive science reveals about problem-solving, memory, and abstract reasoning. Spans psychology, genetics, education policy, and ethical dimensions of intelligence research.

episodes.title: Iris Murdoch
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the author and philosopher Iris Murdoch (1919 - 1999). In her lifetime she was most celebrated for her novels such as The Bell and The Black Prince, but these are now sharing the spotlight with her philosophy. Responding to the horrors of the Second World War, she argued that morality was not subjective or a matter of taste, as many of her contemporaries held, but was objective, and good was a fact we could recognize. To tell good from bad, though, we would need to see the world as it really is, not as we want to see it, and her novels are full of characters who are not yet enlightened enough to do that.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the author and philosopher Iris Murdoch (1919 - 1999). In her lifetime she was most celebrated for her novels such as The Bell and The Black Prince, but these are now sharing the spotlight with her philosophy. episodes.summaryAI: Iris Murdoch (1919-1999), Anglo-Irish novelist and moral philosopher. Examines her literary achievements including The Bell, The Sea The Sea, and The Black Prince alongside her philosophical work challenging mid-20th-century British moral philosophy. Murdoch argued against existentialist subjectivism and emotivism, asserting that moral goodness is objective and perceivable through sustained, unselfish attention to reality. Her philosophy drew on Plato and Simone Weil. Addresses how her experience of World War II shaped her moral realism. Connects 20th-century British literature, ethics, philosophy of mind, and the relationship between fiction and philosophical inquiry.

episodes.title: Is Shakespeare History? The Plantagenets
episodes.synopsis: In the first of two programmes marking In Our Time's 20th anniversary on 15th October, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Shakespeare's versions of history, starting with the English Plantagenets. His eight plays from Richard II to Richard III were written out of order, in the Elizabethan era, and have had a significant impact on the way we see those histories today. In the second programme, Melvyn discusses the Roman plays.
episodes.description: In the first of two programmes marking In Our Time's 20th anniversary on 15th October, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Shakespeare's versions of history, starting with the English Plantagenets. His eight plays from Richard II to Richard III were written out of order, in the Elizabethan era, and have had a significant impact on the way we see those histories today. In the second programme, Melvyn discusses the Roman plays.
episodes.summaryAI: Shakespeare's dramatization of English Plantagenet history across eight plays from Richard II to Richard III, written during the Elizabethan era in non-chronological order. Examines how Shakespeare shaped lasting popular perceptions of 14th- and 15th-century English kings and the Wars of the Roses. Addresses the relationship between dramatic narrative and historical accuracy, Shakespeare's use of chronicle sources including Holinshed, and political motivations under Tudor patronage. Explores how theatrical representation becomes a dominant form of historical memory. Connects English medieval history, Renaissance drama, historiography, and the politics of historical narrative.

episodes.title: Is Shakespeare History? The Romans
episodes.synopsis: In the second of two programmes marking In Our Time's 20th anniversary on 15th October, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Shakespeare's versions of history, continuing with the Roman plays. Rome was the setting for Titus Andronicus, Julius Caesar, Coriolanus and parts of Antony and Cleopatra and these plays gave Shakespeare the chance to explore ideas too controversial for English histories. How was Shakespeare reimagining Roman history, and what impact has that had on how we see Rome today?
episodes.description: In the second of two programmes marking In Our Time's 20th anniversary on 15th October, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Shakespeare's versions of history, continuing with the Roman plays. Rome was the setting for Titus Andronicus, Julius Caesar, Coriolanus and parts of Antony and Cleopatra and these plays gave Shakespeare the chance to explore ideas too controversial for English histories. How was Shakespeare reimagining Roman history, and what impact has that had on how we see Rome today?
episodes.summaryAI: Shakespeare's engagement with Roman history through Titus Andronicus, Julius Caesar, Coriolanus, and Antony and Cleopatra. Examines how Rome provided Shakespeare a displaced setting to explore political themes—tyranny, republicanism, civil war, popular revolt—too dangerous to address in English historical contexts under Elizabethan censorship. Covers Shakespeare's use of Plutarch's Lives as primary source material. Addresses how these plays shaped later perceptions of Roman political culture and figures. Connects Renaissance English drama, ancient Roman history, classical reception, political philosophy, and the relationship between literary imagination and historical understanding.

episodes.title: Islamic Law and its Origins
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the origins and early development of Islamic law. The legal code of Islam is known as Sharia, an Arabic word meaning "the way". Its sources include the Islamic holy book the Qur'an, the words and actions of the Prophet Muhammad, and the opinions of legal scholars. In the 7th century, Sharia started to replace the tribal laws of pre-Islamic Arabia; over the next three hundred years it underwent considerable evolution as Islam spread. By 900 a body of religious and legal scholarship recognisable as classical Sharia had emerged
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the origins and early development of Islamic law. The legal code of Islam is known as Sharia, an Arabic word meaning "the way". Its sources include the Islamic holy book the Qur'an, the words and actions of the Prophet Muhammad, and the opinions of legal scholars. In the 7th century, Sharia started to replace the tribal laws of pre-Islamic Arabia; over the next three hundred years it underwent considerable evolution as Islam spread. By 900 a body of religious and legal scholarship recognisable as classical Sharia had emerged
episodes.summaryAI: Origins and early development of Islamic law (Sharia) from the 7th through 10th centuries. Covers the meaning of Sharia as divinely ordained path, its four principal sources: the Quran, Hadith (prophetic traditions), ijma (scholarly consensus), and qiyas (analogical reasoning). Examines how Sharia replaced pre-Islamic Arabian tribal customary law and the emergence of classical legal schools (madhabs) by approximately 900 CE. Addresses the role of legal scholars (ulama) in interpreting and systematizing law. Connects early Islamic history, jurisprudence, theology, and the relationship between religious authority and legal systems.

episodes.title: Italo Calvino
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Italian author of Invisible Cities, If On A Winter's Night A Traveller, Cosmicomics and other celebrated novels, fables and short stories of the 20th Century. Calvino (1923 -1985) had a passionate belief that writing and art could make life better for everyone. Despite his parents being scientists, who dearly wanted him to be a scientist too, and his time fighting with the Partisans in Liguria in WWII during which his parents were held hostage by the Nazis, Calvino turned away from realism in his writing. Ideally, he said, he would have liked to be alive in the Enlightenment. He moved towards the fantastical, drawing on his childhood reading while collecting a huge number of the fables of Italy and translating them from dialect into Italian to enrich the shared culture of his fellow citizens. His fresh perspective on the novel continues to inspire writers and delight readers in Italian and in translations around the world.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Italian author of Invisible Cities, If On A Winter's Night A Traveller, Cosmicomics and other celebrated novels, fables and short stories of the 20th Century. episodes.summaryAI: Italian author Italo Calvino (1923-1985), known for Invisible Cities, If On A Winter's Night A Traveller, and Cosmicomics. Examines his trajectory from neorealism rooted in wartime Partisan experience to experimental and fantastical fiction. Covers his collection and translation of Italian folktales from regional dialects, paralleling the Brothers Grimm. Addresses his engagement with structuralism, semiotics, and the Oulipo literary movement. Explores tension between his scientist parents' rationalism and his literary imagination. Connects 20th-century Italian literature, postmodern narrative experimentation, folklore studies, and the relationship between science and literary form.

episodes.title: James Joyce's Ulysses
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss James Joyce's novel Ulysses. First published ninety years ago in Paris, Joyce's masterpiece is a sprawling and startlingly original work charting a single day in the life of the Dubliner Leopold Bloom. Some early readers were outraged by its sexual content and daringly scatalogical humour, and the novel was banned in most English-speaking countries for a decade after it first appeared. But it was soon recognised as a genuinely innovative work: overturning the ban on its publication, an American judge described Ulysses as "a sincere and serious attempt to devise a new literary method for the observation and description of mankind."Today Ulysses is widely regarded as the greatest example of literary modernism, and a work that changed literature forever. It remains one of the most discussed novels ever written.Steven ConnorProfessor of Modern Literature and Theory at Birkbeck, University of LondonJeri JohnsonSenior Fellow in English at Exeter College, OxfordRichard BrownReader in Modern English Literature at the University of Leeds
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss James Joyce's novel Ulysses. First published ninety years ago in Paris, Joyce's masterpiece is a sprawling and startlingly original work charting a single day in the life of the Dubliner Leopold Bloom. Some early readers were outraged by its sexual content and daringly scatalogical humour, and the novel was banned in most English-speaking countries for a decade after it first appeared. But it was soon recognised as a genuinely innovative work: overturning the ban on its publication, an American judge described Ulysses as "a sincere and serious attempt to devise a new literary method for the observation and description of mankind."Today Ulysses is widely regarded as the greatest example of literary modernism, and a work that changed literature forever. It remains one of the most discussed novels ever written.Steven ConnorProfessor of Modern Literature and Theory at Birkbeck, University of LondonJeri JohnsonSenior Fellow in English at Exeter College, OxfordRichard BrownReader in Modern English Literature at the University of Leeds
episodes.summaryAI: James Joyce's Ulysses, first published in Paris in 1922, structured around a single day (June 16, 1904) in Dublin following Leopold Bloom. Examines its radical literary techniques including stream of consciousness, stylistic pastiche across episodes, and parallels with Homer's Odyssey. Covers its censorship and banning in English-speaking countries, the landmark 1933 US court ruling by Judge Woolsey permitting publication, and its role as the defining achievement of literary modernism. Addresses Joyce's innovations in language, narrative structure, and representation of interior consciousness. Connects 20th-century literature, censorship history, and modernist aesthetics.

episodes.title: Jane Eyre
episodes.synopsis: The story of Jane Eyre is one of the best-known in English fiction. Jane is the orphan who survives a miserable early life, first with her aunt at Gateshead Hall and then at Lowood School. She leaves the school for Thornfield Hall, to become governess to the French ward of Mr Rochester. She and Rochester fall in love but, at their wedding, it is revealed he is married already and his wife, insane, is kept in Thornfield's attic. When Jane Eyre was published in 1847, it was a great success and brought fame to Charlotte Bronte. Combined with Gothic mystery and horror, the book explores many themes, including the treatment of children, relations between men and women, religious faith and hypocrisy, individuality, morality, equality and the nature of true love.
episodes.description: The story of Jane Eyre is one of the best-known in English fiction. Jane is the orphan who survives a miserable early life, first with her aunt at Gateshead Hall and then at Lowood School. She leaves the school for Thornfield Hall, to become governess to the French ward of Mr Rochester. She and Rochester fall in love but, at their wedding, it is revealed he is married already and his wife, insane, is kept in Thornfield's attic. When Jane Eyre was published in 1847, it was a great success and brought fame to Charlotte Bronte. Combined with Gothic mystery and horror, the book explores many themes, including the treatment of children, relations between men and women, religious faith and hypocrisy, individuality, morality, equality and the nature of true love.
episodes.summaryAI: Charlotte Bronte's Jane Eyre (1847), tracing the orphaned protagonist from abusive childhood through Lowood school to governess at Thornfield Hall and her relationship with Rochester. Examines the novel's blending of Gothic elements—the madwoman in the attic, Bertha Mason—with social realism addressing class, gender inequality, treatment of children, and religious hypocrisy. Covers themes of female independence, moral integrity, and romantic love as partnership between equals. Addresses the novel's reception, its autobiographical dimensions, and its place in the development of the English novel. Connects Victorian literature, feminism, and social history.

episodes.title: Japan's Sakoku Period
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Japan's Sakoku period, two centuries when the country deliberately isolated itself from the Western world. Sakoku began with a series of edicts in the 1630s which restricted the rights of Japanese to leave their country and expelled most of the Europeans living there. For the next two hundred years, Dutch traders were the only Westerners free to live in Japan. It was not until 1858 and the gunboat diplomacy of the American Commodore Matthew Perry that Japan's international isolation finally ended. Although historians used to think of Japan as completely isolated from external influence during this period, recent scholarship suggests that Japanese society was far less isolated from European ideas during this period than previously thought.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Japan's Sakoku period, two centuries when the country deliberately isolated itself from the Western world. Sakoku began with a series of edicts in the 1630s which restricted the rights of Japanese to leave their country and expelled most of the Europeans living there. For the next two hundred years, Dutch traders were the only Westerners free to live in Japan. It was not until 1858 and the gunboat diplomacy of the American Commodore Matthew Perry that Japan's international isolation finally ended. Although historians used to think of Japan as completely isolated from external influence during this period, recent scholarship suggests that Japanese society was far less isolated from European ideas during this period than previously thought.
episodes.summaryAI: Japan's Sakoku period of deliberate national isolation lasting approximately two centuries (1630s-1858). Covers the Tokugawa shogunate's edicts restricting Japanese travel abroad and expelling most Europeans, driven partly by fear of Christian missionary influence. Examines the exception granted to Dutch traders confined to Dejima island in Nagasaki harbor. Addresses Commodore Perry's 1853-1854 gunboat diplomacy forcing Japan's reopening. Explores revisionist scholarship revealing continued intellectual exchange with Europe through Dutch learning (rangaku) and sustained trade with China and Korea, complicating the isolation narrative. Connects early modern Japanese history, trade, and cross-cultural relations.

episodes.title: Johannes Kepler
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the German astronomer Johannes Kepler (1571 - 1630). Although he is overshadowed today by Isaac Newton and Galileo, he is considered by many to be one of the greatest scientists in history. The three laws of planetary motion Kepler developed transformed people's understanding of the Solar System and laid the foundations for the revolutionary ideas Isaac Newton produced later. Kepler is also thought to have written one of the first works of science fiction. However, he faced a number of challenges. He had to defend his mother from charges of witchcraft, he had few financial resources and his career suffered as a result of his Lutheran faith. With David Wootton Professor of History at the University of York
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the German astronomer Johannes Kepler (1571 - 1630). Although he is overshadowed today by Isaac Newton and Galileo, he is considered by many to be one of the greatest scientists in history. The three laws of planetary motion Kepler developed transformed people's understanding of the Solar System and laid the foundations for the revolutionary ideas Isaac Newton produced later. Kepler is also thought to have written one of the first works of science fiction. However, he faced a number of challenges. He had to defend his mother from charges of witchcraft, he had few financial resources and his career suffered as a result of his Lutheran faith. With David Wootton Professor of History at the University of York
episodes.summaryAI: German astronomer Johannes Kepler (1571-1630) and his three laws of planetary motion describing elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, and the relationship between orbital period and distance. Examines how Kepler transformed Copernican astronomy by abandoning circular orbits, laying essential groundwork for Newton's gravitational theory. Covers his work in optics and his Somnium as early science fiction. Addresses biographical context including his defense of his mother against witchcraft accusations, financial hardship, and professional difficulties stemming from his Lutheran faith during Counter-Reformation conflicts. Connects astronomy, physics, history of science, and religious history.

episodes.title: John Bull
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origin of this personification of the English everyman and his development as both British and Britain in the following centuries. He first appeared along with Lewis Baboon (French) and Nicholas Frog (Dutch) in 1712 in a pamphlet that satirised the funding of the War of the Spanish Succession. The author was John Arbuthnot (1667-1735), a Scottish doctor and satirist who was part of the circle of Swift and Pope, and his John Bull was the English voter, overwhelmed by taxes that went not so much into the war itself but into the pockets of its financiers. For the next two centuries, Arbuthnot’s John Bull was a gift for cartoonists and satirists, especially when they wanted to ridicule British governments for taking advantage of the people’s patriotism.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origin of this personification of the English everyman and his development as both British and Britain in the following centuries. He first appeared along with Lewis Baboon (French) and Nicholas Frog (Dutch) in 1712 in a pamphlet that satirised the funding of the War of the Spanish Succession. The author was John Arbuthnot (1667-1735), a Scottish doctor and satirist who was part of the circle of Swift and Pope, and his John Bull was the English voter, overwhelmed by taxes that went not so much into the war itself but into the pockets of its financiers. For the next two centuries, Arbuthnot’s John Bull was a gift for cartoonists and satirists, especially when they wanted to ridicule British governments for taking advantage of the people’s patriotism.
episodes.summaryAI: John Bull as national personification of England, originating in John Arbuthnot's 1712 satirical pamphlet critiquing financing of the War of the Spanish Succession. Arbuthnot's literary circle included Swift and Pope. Bull represented the overtaxed English voter exploited by war financiers. For two centuries the character served cartoonists and satirists attacking British government policy. Covers early 18th-century British political satire, Augustan literary culture, visual caricature traditions, national identity construction through fictional archetypes, and the relationship between literature, political commentary, and popular imagery in British culture.

episodes.title: John Clare
episodes.synopsis: In a programme first broadcast in 2017, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Northamptonshire poet John Clare who, according to one of Melvyn's guests Jonathan Bate, was 'the greatest labouring-class poet that England has ever produced'. Clare worked in a tavern, as a gardener and as a farm labourer in the early 19th century and achieved his first literary success with Poems Descriptive of Rural Life and Scenery. He was praised for his descriptions of rural England and his childhood there, and his reaction to the changes he saw in the Agricultural Revolution with its enclosures, displacement and altered, disrupted landscape. Despite poor mental health and, from middle age onwards, many years in asylums, John Clare continued to write and he is now seen as one of the great poets of his age.
episodes.description: In a programme first broadcast in 2017, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Northamptonshire poet John Clare who, according to one of Melvyn's guests Jonathan Bate, was 'the greatest labouring-class poet that England has ever produced'. Clare worked in a tavern, as a gardener and as a farm labourer in the early 19th century and achieved his first literary success with Poems Descriptive of Rural Life and Scenery. He was praised for his descriptions of rural England and his childhood there, and his reaction to the changes he saw in the Agricultural Revolution with its enclosures, displacement and altered, disrupted landscape. Despite poor mental health and, from middle age onwards, many years in asylums, John Clare continued to write and he is now seen as one of the great poets of his age.
episodes.summaryAI: John Clare (1793-1864), major English Romantic-era poet from the rural labouring class in Northamptonshire. Achieved early success with Poems Descriptive of Rural Life and Nature. Distinguished by direct observational poetry of the English countryside and protest against enclosure during the Agricultural Revolution, documenting displacement of rural communities. Suffered severe mental illness, spent years in asylums yet continued writing prolifically. Represents intersection of class, literary production, land reform, environmental consciousness, and mental health in 19th-century England. Now recognized among the finest poets of his era.

episodes.title: John Dalton
episodes.synopsis: The scientist John Dalton was born in North England in 1766. Although he came from a relatively poor Quaker family, he managed to become one of the most celebrated scientists of his age. Through his work, he helped to establish Manchester as a place where not only products were made but ideas were born. His reputation during his lifetime was so high that unusually a statue was erected to him before he died. Among his interests were meteorology, gasses and colour blindness. However, he is most remembered today for his pioneering thinking in the field of atomic theory.
episodes.description: The scientist John Dalton was born in North England in 1766. Although he came from a relatively poor Quaker family, he managed to become one of the most celebrated scientists of his age. Through his work, he helped to establish Manchester as a place where not only products were made but ideas were born. His reputation during his lifetime was so high that unusually a statue was erected to him before he died. Among his interests were meteorology, gasses and colour blindness. However, he is most remembered today for his pioneering thinking in the field of atomic theory.
episodes.summaryAI: John Dalton (1766-1844), English scientist from a poor Quaker family in northern England who became pivotal in establishing modern atomic theory. Worked across meteorology, gas behavior, and color vision deficiency (Daltonism). His atomic theory proposed that elements consist of indivisible atoms with characteristic weights, transforming chemistry into a quantitative science. Contributed to Manchester's emergence as an intellectual center during the Industrial Revolution. Connects Quaker dissenting culture, self-education, and provincial scientific societies to breakthroughs in fundamental physics and chemistry during the late 18th and early 19th centuries.

episodes.title: John Donne
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Donne (1573-1631), known now as one of England’s finest poets of love and notable in his own time as an astonishing preacher. He was born a Catholic in a Protestant country and, when he married Anne More without her father's knowledge, Donne lost his job in the government circle and fell into a poverty that only ended once he became a priest in the Church of England. As Dean of St Paul’s Cathedral, his sermons were celebrated, perhaps none more than his final one in 1631 when he was plainly in his dying days, as if preaching at his own funeral.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Donne (1573-1631), known now as one of England’s finest poets of love and notable in his own time as an astonishing preacher. He was born a Catholic in a Protestant country and, when he married Anne More without her father's knowledge, Donne lost his job in the government circle and fell into a poverty that only ended once he became a priest in the Church of England. As Dean of St Paul’s Cathedral, his sermons were celebrated, perhaps none more than his final one in 1631 when he was plainly in his dying days, as if preaching at his own funeral.
episodes.summaryAI: John Donne (1573-1631), English metaphysical poet and preacher. Born into a Catholic family in Protestant Elizabethan England, navigating religious persecution and conversion. Secret marriage to Anne More destroyed his government career, plunging him into poverty. Transitioned from secular love poetry of extraordinary intellectual complexity to Anglican priesthood. As Dean of St Paul's Cathedral, became celebrated for powerful sermons meditating on mortality, particularly his final sermon delivered while visibly dying. Covers religious conflict in post-Reformation England, metaphysical poetry, relationship between erotic and devotional literature, and early modern preaching as public performance.

episodes.title: John Keats
episodes.synopsis: Misha Glenny and guests discuss the short life and lasting works of Keats (1795-1821), who in one year wrote some of the most loved poems in English. Among these are Ode to a Nightingale, Ode on a Grecian Urn and Ode on Melancholy. That most productive year began in autumn 1818, when Keats had been stung by some reviews labelling him an uncouth Cockney who should go back to his former work as an apothecary, work he had left for poetry only two years before with the encouragement of enthusiastic friends. Just over two years later, Keats was dead in Rome from tuberculosis, before his work found fame, though some who knew him, including Shelley, believed his true killer was the critics.

That most productive year began in autumn 1818, when Keats had been stung by some reviews labelling him an uncouth Cockney who should go back to his former work as an apothecary, work he had left for poetry only two years before with the encouragement of enthusiastic friends. Just over two years later, Keats was dead in Rome from tuberculosis, before his work found fame, though some who knew him, including Shelley, believed his true killer was the critics.
episodes.summaryAI: John Keats (1795-1821), English Romantic poet who in a single extraordinary year produced iconic odes including Ode to a Nightingale, Ode on a Grecian Urn, and Ode on Melancholy. Trained as an apothecary before abandoning medicine for poetry. Attacked by critics as an uncouth Cockney poet, reflecting class prejudice in literary culture. Died of tuberculosis in Rome at twenty-five, achieving posthumous fame. Covers English Romanticism, the ode form, aesthetics of beauty and transience, class and literary gatekeeping, and the mythology of early death in artistic reputation.

episodes.title: John Ruskin
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of John Ruskin. He was the most brilliant art critic of his age, perhaps the most brilliant that Britain has ever produced, but he was much more than that. A champion of Turner and an enemy of Whistler, he placed the study of art and architecture at the heart of a moral assault on Victorian life. In the stone work of a Gothic cathedral, Ruskin saw all that was right about medieval society and all that was wrong about his own capitalist age.But why was Ruskin so critical of his own time? What deep currents of thought infused his ideas? And how much does our thinking, about society, the environment, art and work owe to this unusual man?With Dinah Birch, Professor of English, Liverpool University; Keith Hanley, Professor of English Literature and Director of the Ruskin Programme, Lancaster University; Stefan Collini, Professor of Intellectual History and English Literature, University of Cambridge.

episodes.summaryAI: John Ruskin (1819-1900), Britain's most influential art critic. Championed Turner's painting and opposed Whistler's aestheticism. Central argument linked art and architecture to moral and social values, using Gothic cathedral stonework as evidence of superior medieval craftsmanship versus degraded industrial capitalist labor. Placed visual culture at the heart of Victorian social criticism. Covers 19th-century art criticism, the Gothic Revival, relationships between aesthetics and ethics, critiques of industrial capitalism, architectural history from medieval to Victorian periods, and the role of the critic in shaping public taste and social reform.

episodes.title: John Wesley and Methodism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss John Wesley (1703 - 1791) and the movement he was to lead and inspire. As a student, he was mocked for approaching religion too methodically and this jibe gave a name to the movement: Methodism. Wesley took his ideas out across Britain wherever there was an appetite for Christian revival, preaching in the open, especially the new industrial areas. Others spread Methodism too, such as George Whitefield, and the sheer energy of the movement led to splits within it, but it soon became a major force.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss John Wesley (1703 - 1791) and the movement he was to lead and inspire. As a student, he was mocked for approaching religion too methodically and this jibe gave a name to the movement: Methodism. episodes.summaryAI: John Wesley (1703-1791) and the rise of Methodism as a major Protestant movement in 18th-century Britain. Originally mocked at Oxford for overly systematic religious practice, giving the movement its name. Wesley and George Whitefield pioneered open-air mass preaching, targeting new industrial working-class communities underserved by the established Church of England. The movement's evangelical energy generated internal theological splits but grew into a significant denomination. Covers Protestant revivalism, religious dissent, relationships between industrialization and spiritual life, preaching as popular communication, and the social history of 18th-century British Christianity.

episodes.title: Johnson
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Samuel Johnson, a giant of 18th century literature. “There is no arguing with Johnson, for when his pistol misses fire, he knocks you down with the butt of it." The poet Oliver Goldsmith was not alone in falling victim to the bludgeoning wit of Samuel Johnson. The greatest luminaries of 18th century England, including the painter Joshua Reynolds, the philosopher Edmund Burke and the politician Charles James Fox, all deferred to him... happily or otherwise. Samuel Johnson was credited with defining English literature with his Lives of the Poets and his edition of Shakespeare, and of defining English language with his Dictionary. Yet despite those lofty acclamations he failed to get a degree, claimed he had never finished a book, was an inveterate hack who told his friend James Boswell, "No man but a blockhead ever wrote, except for money". How did an Oxford drop-out become England's most famous and well connected man of letters? How did generations of readers come to see him as the father of English Literature? And why is he so little read today? With John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Jim McLaverty, Professor of English at Keele University; Judith Hawley, Senior Lecturer in English at Royal Holloway, University of London.

episodes.summaryAI: Samuel Johnson (1709-1784), towering figure of 18th-century English literature. Produced the landmark Dictionary of the English Language, the influential critical work Lives of the Poets, and a major edition of Shakespeare. Rose from poverty and failure to complete his Oxford degree to become England's most famous man of letters, largely through prolific hack journalism. Covers lexicography and language standardization, literary criticism and canon formation, 18th-century London literary culture, the economics of professional authorship, and the relationship between biography, reputation, and literary achievement in Augustan England.

episodes.title: Jorge Luis Borges
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of the Argentinian master of the short story, Jorge Luis Borges. Borges is one of the greatest writers of the 20th Century, best known for his intriguing short stories that play with philosophical ideas, such as identity, reality and language. His work, which includes poetry, essays, and reviews of imaginary books, has had great influence on magical realism and literary theory. He viewed the realist novel as over-rated and deluded, revelling instead in fable and imaginary worlds. He declared "people think life is the thing but I prefer reading".Translation formed an important part of his work, writing a Spanish language version of an Oscar Wilde story when aged around 9. He went on to introduce other key writers such as Faulkner and Kafka to Latin America, liberally making changes to the original work which went far beyond what was, strictly speaking, translation.He lived most of his life in obscurity, finding recognition only in his sixties when he was awarded the International Publishers' Prize which he shared with Samuel Beckett. By this point he was blind but continued to write, composing poetry in his head and reciting from memory.So how has Borges' work informed ideas about our experience of the world through language? How much was his writing shaped by his travel abroad and an unrequited love? And how has his legacy inspired the next generation of great Latin American authors such as Gabriel Garcia Marquez and Mario Vargas Llosa?With Edwin Williamson, Professor of Spanish Studies at Oxford University; Efraín Kristal, Professor of Comparative Literature at University of California, Los Angeles; Evelyn Fishburn, Professor Emeritus at London Metropolitan University and Honorary Senior Research Fellow at University College London.

episodes.summaryAI: Jorge Luis Borges (1899-1986), Argentinian writer who mastered the short story form through philosophical explorations of identity, reality, language, and infinity. Produced poetry, essays, and reviews of imaginary books. Rejected conventional realist novels as overrated. Translation was integral to his literary practice. Major influence on magical realism and postmodern literary theory. Achieved international recognition only in his sixties, sharing the International Publishers' Prize with Beckett. Covers 20th-century Latin American literature, philosophical fiction, relationships between fiction and metaphysics, translation theory, and the short story as an intellectual art form.

episodes.title: Josephus
episodes.synopsis: It is said that, in Britain from the 18th Century, copies of Josephus' works were as widespread and as well read as The Bible. Christians valued "The Antiquities of the Jews" in particular, for the retelling of parts of the Old Testament and apparently corroborating the historical existence of Jesus. Born Joseph son of Matthias, in Jerusalem, in 37AD, he fought the Romans in Galilee in the First Jewish-Roman War. He was captured by Vespasian's troops and became a Roman citizen, later describing the siege and fall of Jerusalem. His actions and writings made him a controversial figure, from his lifetime to the present day.
episodes.description: It is said that, in Britain from the 18th Century, copies of Josephus' works were as widespread and as well read as The Bible. Christians valued "The Antiquities of the Jews" in particular, for the retelling of parts of the Old Testament and apparently corroborating the historical existence of Jesus. Born Joseph son of Matthias, in Jerusalem, in 37AD, he fought the Romans in Galilee in the First Jewish-Roman War. He was captured by Vespasian's troops and became a Roman citizen, later describing the siege and fall of Jerusalem. His actions and writings made him a controversial figure, from his lifetime to the present day.
episodes.summaryAI: Josephus (37-c.100 AD), Jewish historian born in Jerusalem who fought against Rome in the First Jewish-Roman War before being captured and becoming a Roman citizen under Vespasian's patronage. Wrote The Antiquities of the Jews and an account of the siege and destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD. His apparent references to Jesus made his works valuable to early Christians seeking historical corroboration. Controversial figure regarded as both essential historical source and traitor. Covers 1st-century Roman-Jewish relations, ancient historiography, the destruction of the Second Temple, and early Christian apologetics.

episodes.title: Journey to the West
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the great novels of China’s Ming era, and perhaps the most loved. Written in 1592, it draws on the celebrated travels of a real monk from China to India a thousand years before, and on a thousand years of retellings of that story, especially the addition of a monkey as companion who, in the novel, becomes supersimian. For most readers the monk, Tripitaka, is upstaged by this irrepressible Monkey with his extraordinary powers, accompanied by the fallen but recovering deities, Pigsy and Sandy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the great novels of China’s Ming era, and perhaps the most loved. Written in 1592, it draws on the celebrated travels of a real monk from China to India a thousand years before, and on a thousand years of retellings of that story, especially the addition of a monkey as companion who, in the novel, becomes supersimian. For most readers the monk, Tripitaka, is upstaged by this irrepressible Monkey with his extraordinary powers, accompanied by the fallen but recovering deities, Pigsy and Sandy.
episodes.summaryAI: Journey to the West, one of China's greatest novels, written in 1592 during the Ming dynasty. Based on the historical 7th-century journey of the monk Xuanzang from China to India to retrieve Buddhist scriptures, enriched by a thousand years of oral and literary retelling. The monk Tripitaka is overshadowed by the trickster hero Monkey (Sun Wukong) with supernatural powers, alongside Pigsy and Sandy. Covers Chinese classical literature, Ming-era fiction, Buddhist pilgrimage narratives, mythological storytelling traditions, the interplay between historical event and literary elaboration, and Chinese folk religion.

episodes.title: Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Roy Foster, Jeri Johnson and Katherine Mullin discuss A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, James Joyce's groundbreaking 1916 novel about growing up in Catholic Ireland.Many novelists choose their own young life as the subject for their first book. But very few have subjected themselves to the intense self-scrutiny of the great Irish novelist James Joyce. In A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, published in 1916, Joyce follows his alter ego, Stephen Dedalus, from babyhood to young adulthood. He takes us from Stephen wetting the bed, through a teenage visit to a prostitute, and on through religious terrors to the prospect of freedom. When it was published, the book met with shock at its graphic honesty. Joyce shows Stephen wrestling with the pressures of his family, his Church and his nation. Yet this was far from being a straightforward youthful tirade. Joyce's novel is also daringly experimental, taking us deep into Stephen's psyche. And since its publication almost a century ago, it has had a huge influence on novelists across the world
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Roy Foster, Jeri Johnson and Katherine Mullin discuss A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, James Joyce's groundbreaking 1916 novel about growing up in Catholic Ireland.Many novelists choose their own young life as the subject for their first book. episodes.summaryAI: James Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916), a groundbreaking modernist novel tracing alter ego Stephen Dedalus from infancy through young adulthood in Catholic Ireland. Shocked readers with graphic honesty about bodily and sexual experience. Dramatizes Stephen's struggles against family, Catholic Church, and Irish nationalism. Pioneered experimental narrative techniques including free indirect discourse and stream of consciousness to render subjective psychological development. Enormously influential on 20th-century fiction worldwide. Covers literary modernism, Irish cultural identity, Catholic education, artistic vocation, and the Bildungsroman tradition.

episodes.title: Judas Maccabeus
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the revolutionary Jewish leader Judas Maccabeus. Born in the second century BC, Judas led his followers, the Maccabees, in a rebellion against the Seleucid Empire, which was attempting to impose the Greek culture and religion on the Jews. After a succession of battles he succeeded and the Seleucid king granted the Jews religious freedom. But even after that freedom was granted the struggle for political independence continued, and it was not until twenty years after Judas's death that Judaea finally became an independent state. Thanks to an extensive, if often confused, historical record of these events, the story of the Maccabees is well known. Judas Maccabeus has become a celebrated folk hero, and one of his achievements, the restoration and purification of the Temple of Jerusalem after its desecration by the Seleucids, is commemorated every year at the Jewish festival of Hanukkah

After a succession of battles he succeeded and the Seleucid king granted the Jews religious freedom. But even after that freedom was granted the struggle for political independence continued, and it was not until twenty years after Judas's death that Judaea finally became an independent state. Thanks to an extensive, if often confused, historical record of these events, the story of the Maccabees is well known. episodes.summaryAI: Judas Maccabeus, 2nd-century BC Jewish leader who led the Maccabean revolt against the Seleucid Empire's campaign of Hellenization imposing Greek culture and religion on Judaea. Won religious freedom for the Jewish people through military resistance. Political independence followed twenty years after his death. His rededication of the desecrated Temple of Jerusalem is commemorated annually at Hanukkah. Covers Hellenistic-era politics, Jewish resistance to cultural assimilation, Seleucid imperialism, the origins of Hanukkah, Second Temple Judaism, and the intersection of religious identity and political sovereignty in the ancient Near East.

episodes.title: Judith beheading Holofernes
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how artists from the Middle Ages onwards have been inspired by the Bible story of the widow who killed an Assyrian general who was besieging her village, and so saved her people from his army and from his master Nebuchadnezzar. A symbol of a woman's power and the defiance of political tyranny, the image of Judith has been sculpted by Donatello, painted on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel and, in the case of Caravaggio, Liss and Artemisia Gentileschi, been shown with vivid, disturbing detail. What do these interpretations reveal of the attitudes to power and women in their time, and of the artists' own experiences?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how artists from the Middle Ages onwards have been inspired by the Bible story of the widow who killed an Assyrian general who was besieging her village, and so saved her people from his army and from his master Nebuchadnezzar. A symbol of a woman's power and the defiance of political tyranny, the image of Judith has been sculpted by Donatello, painted on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel and, in the case of Caravaggio, Liss and Artemisia Gentileschi, been shown with vivid, disturbing detail. What do these interpretations reveal of the attitudes to power and women in their time, and of the artists' own experiences?
episodes.summaryAI: The biblical story of Judith beheading the Assyrian general Holofernes and its extensive treatment in Western art from the Middle Ages onward. Judith became a symbol of female power and resistance to political tyranny. Major depictions include Donatello's sculpture, Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling panel, and paintings by Caravaggio, Johann Liss, and Artemisia Gentileschi, the last notable for visceral realism. Covers biblical narrative in visual art, iconography of feminine heroism, Renaissance and Baroque painting and sculpture, gender and artistic representation, and the relationship between religious texts and political allegory.

episodes.title: Julian of Norwich
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the anchoress and mystic who, in the late fourteenth century, wrote about her visions of Christ suffering, in a work since known as Revelations of Divine Love. She is probably the first named woman writer in English, even if questions about her name and life remain open. Her account is an exploration of the meaning of her visions and is vivid and bold, both in its imagery and theology. From her confined cell in a Norwich parish church, in a land beset with plague, she dealt with the nature of sin and with the feminine side of God, and shared the message she received that God is love and, famously, that all shall be well and all manner of things shall be well.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the anchoress and mystic who, in the late fourteenth century, wrote about her visions of Christ suffering, in a work since known as Revelations of Divine Love. She is probably the first named woman writer in English, even if questions about her name and life remain open. Her account is an exploration of the meaning of her visions and is vivid and bold, both in its imagery and theology. From her confined cell in a Norwich parish church, in a land beset with plague, she dealt with the nature of sin and with the feminine side of God, and shared the message she received that God is love and, famously, that all shall be well and all manner of things shall be well.
episodes.summaryAI: Julian of Norwich (c.1343-c.1416), English anchoress and Christian mystic who wrote Revelations of Divine Love, probably the first surviving book by a named woman writer in English. Recorded vivid visions of Christ's suffering received during severe illness. From her enclosed cell in Norwich, developed bold theology addressing the nature of sin, divine love, and the feminine aspects of God. Famous for the phrase 'all shall be well.' Covers late medieval English mysticism, women's religious writing, anchoritic life, vernacular theology, gender and divinity, and 14th-century spirituality during the age of plague and schism.

episodes.title: Julian the Apostate
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the last pagan ruler of the Roman Empire. Fifty years after Constantine the Great converted to Christianity and introduced a policy of tolerating the faith across the empire, Julian (c.331 - 363 AD) aimed to promote paganism instead, branding Constantine the worst of all his predecessors. Julian was a philosopher-emperor in the mould of Marcus Aurelius and was noted in his lifetime for his letters and his satires, and it was his surprising success as a general in his youth in Gaul that had propelled him to power barely twenty years after a rival had slaughtered his family. Julian's pagan mission and his life were brought to a sudden end while on campaign against the Sasanian Empire in the east, but he left so much written evidence of his ideas that he remains one of the most intriguing of all the Roman emperors and a hero to the humanists of the Enlightenment.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the last pagan ruler of the Roman Empire. Fifty years after Constantine the Great converted to Christianity and introduced a policy of tolerating the faith across the empire, Julian (c.331 - 363 AD) aimed to promote paganism instead, branding Constantine the worst of all his predecessors. Julian was a philosopher-emperor in the mould of Marcus Aurelius and was noted in his lifetime for his letters and his satires, and it was his surprising success as a general in his youth in Gaul that had propelled him to power barely twenty years after a rival had slaughtered his family. Julian's pagan mission and his life were brought to a sudden end while on campaign against the Sasanian Empire in the east, but he left so much written evidence of his ideas that he remains one of the most intriguing of all the Roman emperors and a hero to the humanists of the Enlightenment.
episodes.summaryAI: Julian the Apostate (c.331-363 AD), the last pagan Roman emperor. Fifty years after Constantine's conversion to Christianity, Julian attempted to restore traditional Greco-Roman paganism and reverse Christian institutional dominance. A philosopher-emperor modeled on Marcus Aurelius who also proved an unexpectedly effective military commander in Gaul. Died on campaign against the Sasanian Empire. Later celebrated by Enlightenment thinkers as a champion of rational inquiry against religious orthodoxy. Covers late Roman imperial politics, the Christian-pagan conflict, Neoplatonic philosophy, Roman military history, and the reception of ancient figures in Enlightenment thought.

episodes.title: Julius Caesar
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life, work and reputation of Julius Caesar. Famously assassinated as he entered the Roman senate on the Ides of March, 44 BC, Caesar was an inspirational general who conquered much of Europe. He was a ruthless and canny politician who became dictator of Rome, and wrote The Gallic Wars, one of the most admired and studied works of Latin literature. Shakespeare is one of many later writers to have been fascinated by the figure of Julius Caesar.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life, work and reputation of Julius Caesar. Famously assassinated as he entered the Roman senate on the Ides of March, 44 BC, Caesar was an inspirational general who conquered much of Europe. He was a ruthless and canny politician who became dictator of Rome, and wrote The Gallic Wars, one of the most admired and studied works of Latin literature. Shakespeare is one of many later writers to have been fascinated by the figure of Julius Caesar.
episodes.summaryAI: Julius Caesar (100-44 BC), Roman general, politician, and writer assassinated on the Ides of March. Conquered Gaul and much of Western Europe through military campaigns. Navigated Roman Republican politics with ruthless skill, ultimately becoming dictator and precipitating the crisis that ended the Republic. Authored The Gallic Wars, considered a masterpiece of Latin prose. His life fascinated later writers including Shakespeare. Covers late Roman Republican politics, ancient military conquest, the transition from Republic to Empire, Latin literature, and the enduring cultural legacy of Caesar in Western political and literary imagination.

episodes.title: Jung
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the extraordinary mind of the psychiatrist Carl Gustav Jung. In 1907 Sigmund Freud met a young man and fell into a conversation that is reputed to have lasted for 13 hours. That man was the Swiss psychiatrist Carl Gustav Jung. Freud is celebrated as the great pioneer of the 20th century mind, but the idea that personality types can be 'introverted' or 'extroverted', that certain archetypal images and stories repeat themselves constantly across the collective history of mankind, and that personal individuation is the goal of life, all belong to Jung: "Your vision will become clear only when you look into your heart... Who looks outside, dreams. Who looks inside, awakens", he declared. And he also said "Show me a sane man and I will cure him for you".Who was Jung? What is the essence and influence of his thought? And how did he become such a controversial and, for many, such a beguiling figure?With Brett Kahr, Senior Clinical Research Fellow in Psychotherapy and Mental Health at the Centre for Child Mental Health in London and a practising Freudian; Ronald Hayman, writer and biographer of Jung; Andrew Samuels, Professor of Analytical Psychology at the University of Essex and a Jungian analyst in clinical practice.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the extraordinary mind of the psychiatrist Carl Gustav Jung. In 1907 Sigmund Freud met a young man and fell into a conversation that is reputed to have lasted for 13 hours. That man was the Swiss psychiatrist Carl Gustav Jung. Freud is celebrated as the great pioneer of the 20th century mind, but the idea that personality types can be 'introverted' or 'extroverted', that certain archetypal images and stories repeat themselves constantly across the collective history of mankind, and that personal individuation is the goal of life, all belong to Jung: "Your vision will become clear only when you look into your heart... Who looks outside, dreams. Who looks inside, awakens", he declared. And he also said "Show me a sane man and I will cure him for you".Who was Jung? What is the essence and influence of his thought? And how did he become such a controversial and, for many, such a beguiling figure?With Brett Kahr, Senior Clinical Research Fellow in Psychotherapy and Mental Health at the Centre for Child Mental Health in London and a practising Freudian; Ronald Hayman, writer and biographer of Jung; Andrew Samuels, Professor of Analytical Psychology at the University of Essex and a Jungian analyst in clinical practice.
episodes.summaryAI: Carl Gustav Jung (1875-1961), Swiss psychiatrist whose 1907 meeting with Freud began a transformative intellectual partnership and eventual bitter rupture. Developed distinctive psychological concepts including introversion and extroversion as personality types, the collective unconscious containing archetypal images recurring across cultures and history, and individuation as the process of psychological self-realization. His work bridges psychiatry, mythology, religion, and cultural analysis. Covers 20th-century psychology, the Freud-Jung relationship, analytical psychology, archetype theory, personality typology, and the intersection of depth psychology with comparative religion and mythology.

episodes.title: Jupiter
episodes.synopsis: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system, and it’s hard to imagine a world more alien and different from Earth. It’s known as a Gas Giant, and its diameter is eleven times the size of Earth’s: our planet would fit inside it one thousand three hundred times. But its mass is only three hundred and twenty times greater, suggesting that although Jupiter is much bigger than Earth, the stuff it’s made of is much, much lighter. When you look at it through a powerful telescope you see a mass of colourful bands and stripes: these are the tops of ferocious weather systems that tear around the planet, including the great Red Spot, probably the longest-lasting storm in the solar system. Jupiter is so enormous that it’s thought to have played an essential role in the distribution of matter as the solar system formed – and it plays an important role in hoovering up astral debris that might otherwise rain down on Earth. It’s almost a mini solar system in its own right, with 95 moons orbiting around it. At least two of these are places life might possibly be found.

It’s known as a Gas Giant, and its diameter is eleven times the size of Earth’s: our planet would fit inside it one thousand three hundred times. But its mass is only three hundred and twenty times greater, suggesting that although Jupiter is much bigger than Earth, the stuff it’s made of is much, much lighter. When you look at it through a powerful telescope you see a mass of colourful bands and stripes: these are the tops of ferocious weather systems that tear around the planet, including the great Red Spot, probably the longest-lasting storm in the solar system. Jupiter is so enormous that it’s thought to have played an essential role in the distribution of matter as the solar system formed – and it plays an important role in hoovering up astral debris that might otherwise rain down on Earth. It’s almost a mini solar system in its own right, with 95 moons orbiting around it. At least two of these are places life might possibly be found.
episodes.summaryAI: Jupiter, the largest planet in the solar system with a diameter eleven times Earth's. A gas giant with ferocious weather systems including the persistent Great Red Spot storm. Played a crucial role in the early solar system's formation by influencing the distribution of matter and continues to sweep up astral debris, potentially shielding inner planets. Possesses 95 known moons constituting a mini solar system, with at least two moons (likely Europa and Ganymede or Enceladus) considered candidates for extraterrestrial life. Covers planetary science, solar system formation, astrobiology, and atmospheric dynamics.

episodes.title: Just War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea of a just war. There were theories about a justified or noble war before the birth of Christ, but it was his reported teachings and a powerful influence, particularly on the Emperor Constantine, which set the standard which had to be kept or bluntly modified. “I say unto you, love your own image,” Matthew writes, “bless them that curse you, be good to them that hate you and persecute you”. In the fifth century, the mighty St Augustus prised the Christian church away from Christ’s reported teachings and the idea of a Just War took root to be formalised and given power by St Thomas Aquinas in the thirteenth century, and by other Christian commentators even up to this day. But after a century, our century, of almost unimaginably violent conflict, does the term a Just War have any meaning at all? The historian AJP Taylor wrote that "the medieval pursuit of the just war is a pursuit as elusive as the Holy Grail. For it is almost universally true that in war each side thinks itself in the right, and there is no arbiter except victory to decide between them". So is the Christian idea of the Just War simply a way of justifying aggression or is it a moral position to take?With Professor John Keane, Professor of Politics, University of Westminster and Director of the Centre for the Study of Democracy; Dr Niall Ferguson, Fellow and Tutor in Modern History, Jesus College, Oxford and author of The Pity of War.

There were theories about a justified or noble war before the birth of Christ, but it was his reported teachings and a powerful influence, particularly on the Emperor Constantine, which set the standard which had to be kept or bluntly modified. “I say unto you, love your own image,” Matthew writes, “bless them that curse you, be good to them that hate you and persecute you”.
