episodes.summaryAI: The concept of just war in Western moral and political philosophy from pre-Christian antiquity through the modern era. Christ's teachings initially promoted pacifism, but St Augustine in the 5th century developed theological justifications for Christian warfare. St Thomas Aquinas formalized just war criteria in the 13th century, establishing conditions of legitimate authority, just cause, and right intention. Examines whether the doctrine remains meaningful after a century of industrialized and total warfare, or whether it merely provides moral cover for aggression. Covers Christian ethics, medieval philosophy, international law, military ethics, and political theology.

episodes.title: Justinian's Legal Code
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas brought together under Justinian I, Byzantine emperor in the 6th century AD, which were rediscovered in Western Europe in the Middle Ages and became very influential in the development of laws in many European nations and elsewhere.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas brought together under Justinian I, Byzantine emperor in the 6th century AD, which were rediscovered in Western Europe in the Middle Ages and became very influential in the development of laws in many European nations and elsewhere.
episodes.summaryAI: Justinian I's 6th-century legal codification in the Byzantine Empire, compiling Roman law into the Corpus Juris Civilis. Covers the systematic organization of centuries of Roman legal precedent, jurisprudence, and imperial edicts. Rediscovered in Western Europe during the Middle Ages, these texts profoundly shaped civil law traditions across continental European nations. Connects Roman imperial governance to medieval legal scholarship and modern European legal systems. Relevant to Byzantine political history, the transmission of classical knowledge, comparative legal traditions, and the enduring influence of Roman institutional frameworks on Western civilization.

episodes.title: Kafka's The Trial
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Franz Kafka's novel of power and alienation 'The Trial', in which readers follow the protagonist Joseph K into a bizarre, nightmarish world in which he stands accused of an unknown crime; courts of interrogation convene in obscure tenement buildings; and there seems to be no escape from a crushing, oppressive bureaucracy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Franz Kafka's novel of power and alienation 'The Trial', in which readers follow the protagonist Joseph K into a bizarre, nightmarish world in which he stands accused of an unknown crime; courts of interrogation convene in obscure tenement buildings; and there seems to be no escape from a crushing, oppressive bureaucracy.
episodes.summaryAI: Franz Kafka's novel The Trial (written 1914-15, published posthumously 1925), in which protagonist Joseph K faces prosecution for an unspecified crime within an opaque, labyrinthine legal system. Explores themes of alienation, bureaucratic oppression, individual powerlessness before institutional authority, and absurdist logic. Central to literary modernism and existentialist thought. Kafka's Prague Jewish background and early 20th-century Central European context inform the work. Influential on 20th-century literature, philosophy of law, and conceptions of totalitarian power. Connects to existentialism, phenomenology of guilt, and critique of modern state apparatus.

episodes.title: Kali
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Hindu goddess Kali, often depicted as dark blue, fierce, defiant, revelling in her power, and holding in her four or more arms a curved sword and a severed head with a cup underneath to catch the blood. She may have her tongue out, to catch more blood spurting from her enemies, be wearing a garland of more severed heads and a skirt of severed hands and yet she is also a nurturing mother figure, known in West Bengal as ‘Maa Kali’ and she can be fiercely protective. Sometimes she is shown as young and conventionally beautiful and at other times as old, emaciated and hungry, so defying any narrow definition.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Hindu goddess Kali, often depicted as dark blue, fierce, defiant, revelling in her power, and holding in her four or more arms a curved sword and a severed head with a cup underneath to catch the blood. She may have her tongue out, to catch more blood spurting from her enemies, be wearing a garland of more severed heads and a skirt of severed hands and yet she is also a nurturing mother figure, known in West Bengal as ‘Maa Kali’ and she can be fiercely protective. Sometimes she is shown as young and conventionally beautiful and at other times as old, emaciated and hungry, so defying any narrow definition.
episodes.summaryAI: The Hindu goddess Kali, a complex deity with traditions spanning over two millennia in South Asian religious life. Depicted as fierce destroyer wielding sword and severed heads, yet simultaneously venerated as nurturing mother figure, especially as Maa Kali in West Bengal. Iconography includes dark blue skin, garland of skulls, and skirt of severed hands. Variously portrayed as young, beautiful, old, or emaciated, resisting singular definition. Connected to Shakti theology, tantric traditions, and broader Hindu conceptions of divine femininity, destruction, time, and cosmic renewal.

episodes.title: Kant's Categorical Imperative
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, in the Enlightenment, Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) sought to define the difference between right and wrong by applying reason, looking at the intention behind actions rather than at consequences. He was inspired to find moral laws by natural philosophers such as Newton and Leibniz, who had used reason rather than emotion to analyse the world around them and had identified laws of nature. Kant argued that when someone was doing the right thing, that person was doing what was the universal law for everyone, a formulation that has been influential on moral philosophy ever since and is known as the Categorical Imperative. Arguably even more influential was one of his reformulations, echoed in The Universal Declaration of Human Rights, in which he asserted that humanity has a value of an entirely different kind from that placed on commodities. Kant argued that simply existing as a human being was valuable in itself, so that every human owed moral responsibilities to other humans and was owed responsibilities in turn.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, in the Enlightenment, Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) sought to define the difference between right and wrong by applying reason, looking at the intention behind actions rather than at consequences. He was inspired to find moral laws by natural philosophers such as Newton and Leibniz, who had used reason rather than emotion to analyse the world around them and had identified laws of nature. Kant argued that when someone was doing the right thing, that person was doing what was the universal law for everyone, a formulation that has been influential on moral philosophy ever since and is known as the Categorical Imperative. Arguably even more influential was one of his reformulations, echoed in The Universal Declaration of Human Rights, in which he asserted that humanity has a value of an entirely different kind from that placed on commodities. Kant argued that simply existing as a human being was valuable in itself, so that every human owed moral responsibilities to other humans and was owed responsibilities in turn.
episodes.summaryAI: Immanuel Kant's (1724-1804) categorical imperative as the foundation of his deontological moral philosophy. Kant grounded ethics in reason rather than consequences or divine command, arguing moral actions must conform to principles universalizable as law for all rational beings. Distinguished human dignity from commodity value, treating persons always as ends, never merely as means. Developed in the Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals (1785). Profoundly influenced Enlightenment political thought, modern human rights frameworks including the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and ongoing debates in normative ethics and political philosophy.

episodes.title: Kant's Copernican Revolution
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the insight into our relationship with the world that Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) shared in his book The Critique of Pure Reason in 1781. It was as revolutionary, in his view, as when the Polish astronomer Copernicus realised that Earth revolves around the Sun rather than the Sun around Earth. Kant's was an insight into how we understand the world around us, arguing that we can never know the world as it is, but only through the structures of our minds which shape that understanding. This idea, that the world depends on us even though we do not create it, has been one of Kant’s greatest contributions to philosophy and influences debates to this day.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the insight into our relationship with the world that Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) shared in his book The Critique of Pure Reason in 1781. It was as revolutionary, in his view, as when the Polish astronomer Copernicus realised that Earth revolves around the Sun rather than the Sun around Earth. Kant's was an insight into how we understand the world around us, arguing that we can never know the world as it is, but only through the structures of our minds which shape that understanding. This idea, that the world depends on us even though we do not create it, has been one of Kant’s greatest contributions to philosophy and influences debates to this day.
episodes.summaryAI: Kant's epistemological revolution in the Critique of Pure Reason (1781), analogized to Copernicus's reorientation of astronomy. Kant argued that human cognition does not passively mirror an external world but actively structures experience through innate mental categories such as space, time, and causality. We can know phenomena (things as they appear) but never noumena (things in themselves). This transformed metaphysics, epistemology, and philosophy of science by placing the knowing subject at the center of inquiry. Connects to rationalism, empiricism, transcendental idealism, and subsequent German idealist and phenomenological traditions.

episodes.title: Karl Barth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most influential theologians of the twentieth century. Karl Barth (1886 - 1968) rejected the liberal theology of his time which, he argued, used the Bible and religion to help humans understand themselves rather than prepare them to open themselves to divine revelation. Barth's aim was to put God and especially Christ at the centre of Christianity. He was alarmed by what he saw as the dangers in a natural theology where God might be found in a rainbow or an opera by Wagner; for if you were open to finding God in German culture, you could also be open to accepting Hitler as God’s gift as many Germans did. Barth openly refused to accept Hitler's role in the Church in the 1930s on these theological grounds as well as moral, for which he was forced to leave Germany for his native Switzerland.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most influential theologians of the twentieth century. Karl Barth (1886 - 1968) rejected the liberal theology of his time which, he argued, used the Bible and religion to help humans understand themselves rather than prepare them to open themselves to divine revelation. Barth's aim was to put God and especially Christ at the centre of Christianity. He was alarmed by what he saw as the dangers in a natural theology where God might be found in a rainbow or an opera by Wagner; for if you were open to finding God in German culture, you could also be open to accepting Hitler as God’s gift as many Germans did. Barth openly refused to accept Hitler's role in the Church in the 1930s on these theological grounds as well as moral, for which he was forced to leave Germany for his native Switzerland.
episodes.summaryAI: Karl Barth (1886-1968), Swiss Reformed theologian and one of the 20th century's most influential Christian thinkers. Rejected liberal Protestant theology's accommodation of culture and reason, reasserting the radical otherness of God and centrality of Christ through neo-orthodox or dialectical theology. Opposed natural theology, arguing God cannot be discovered through nature or culture but only through divine revelation. Actively resisted Nazi interference in the German church during the 1930s, contributing to the Barmen Declaration. Forced from his German professorship, returned to Switzerland. Shaped Protestant theology, ecumenism, and political theology.

episodes.title: Karma
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the doctrine of Karma as developed initially among Hindus, Jains and Buddhists in India from the first millennium BCE. Common to each is an idea, broadly, that you reap what you sow: how you act in this world has consequences either for your later life or your future lives, depending on your view of rebirth and transmigration. From this flow different ideas including those about free will, engagement with the world or disengagement, the nature of ethics and whether intention matters, and these ideas continue to develop today.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the doctrine of Karma as developed initially among Hindus, Jains and Buddhists in India from the first millennium BCE. Common to each is an idea, broadly, that you reap what you sow: how you act in this world has consequences either for your later life or your future lives, depending on your view of rebirth and transmigration. From this flow different ideas including those about free will, engagement with the world or disengagement, the nature of ethics and whether intention matters, and these ideas continue to develop today.
episodes.summaryAI: The doctrine of karma as developed across Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist traditions in India from the first millennium BCE onward. Karma posits that actions generate moral consequences affecting present and future lives through cycles of rebirth (samsara). Covers diverse interpretations regarding free will versus determinism, the role of intention in moral weight, whether ethical engagement or renunciation best addresses karmic accumulation, and paths to liberation (moksha, nirvana). Intersects with Indian metaphysics, soteriology, caste ideology, and comparative ethics. Connects to broader philosophical questions about moral responsibility, justice, and the nature of selfhood.

episodes.title: Kierkegaard
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rich and radical ideas of Soren Kierkegaard, often called the father of Existentialism.In 1840 a young Danish girl called Regine Olsen got engaged to her sweetheart – a modish and clever young man called Søren Kierkegaard. The two were deeply in love but soon the husband to be began to have doubts. He worried that he couldn’t make Regine happy and stay true to himself and his dreams of philosophy. It was a terrible dilemma, but Kierkegaard broke off the engagement – a decision from which neither he nor his fiancée fully recovered. This unhappy episode has become emblematic of the life and thought of Søren Kierkegaard - a philosopher who confronted the painful choices in life and who understood the darker modes of human existence. Yet Kierkegaard is much more than the gloomy Dane of reputation. A thinker of wit and elegance, his ability to live with paradox and his desire to think about individuals as free have given him great purchase in the modern world and he is known as the father of Existentialism.With Jonathan Rée, Visiting Professor at Roehampton University and the Royal College of Art; Clare Carlisle, Lecturer in Philosophy at the University of Liverpool; John Lippitt, Professor of Ethics and Philosophy of Religion at the University of Hertfordshire.

The two were deeply in love but soon the husband to be began to have doubts. He worried that he couldn’t make Regine happy and stay true to himself and his dreams of philosophy. This unhappy episode has become emblematic of the life and thought of Søren Kierkegaard - a philosopher who confronted the painful choices in life and who understood the darker modes of human existence. Yet Kierkegaard is much more than the gloomy Dane of reputation. A thinker of wit and elegance, his ability to live with paradox and his desire to think about individuals as free have given him great purchase in the modern world and he is known as the father of Existentialism.With Jonathan Rée, Visiting Professor at Roehampton University and the Royal College of Art; Clare Carlisle, Lecturer in Philosophy at the University of Liverpool; John Lippitt, Professor of Ethics and Philosophy of Religion at the University of Hertfordshire.
episodes.summaryAI: Søren Kierkegaard (1813-1855), Danish philosopher widely regarded as the father of existentialism. Emphasized individual subjective experience, freedom, anxiety, and authentic choice against Hegelian systematic philosophy. Explored despair, the leap of faith, and stages of existence (aesthetic, ethical, religious). Confronted darker dimensions of human existence including dread and absurdity. His literary style employed pseudonyms, irony, and indirect communication. Influenced 20th-century existentialism (Heidegger, Sartre, Camus), Protestant theology, psychology, and literary theory. Connects philosophy of religion, ethics of individual responsibility, and critiques of institutional Christianity in 19th-century Denmark.

episodes.title: Kinetic Theory
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how scientists sought to understand the properties of gases and the relationship between pressure and volume, and what that search unlocked. Newton theorised that there were static particles in gases that pushed against each other all the harder when volume decreased, hence the increase in pressure. Those who argued that molecules moved, and hit each other, were discredited until James Maxwell and Ludwig Boltzmann used statistics to support this kinetic theory. Ideas about atoms developed in tandem with this, and it came as a surprise to scientists in C20th that the molecules underpinning the theory actually existed and were not simply thought experiments.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how scientists sought to understand the properties of gases and the relationship between pressure and volume, and what that search unlocked. Newton theorised that there were static particles in gases that pushed against each other all the harder when volume decreased, hence the increase in pressure. Those who argued that molecules moved, and hit each other, were discredited until James Maxwell and Ludwig Boltzmann used statistics to support this kinetic theory. Ideas about atoms developed in tandem with this, and it came as a surprise to scientists in C20th that the molecules underpinning the theory actually existed and were not simply thought experiments.
episodes.summaryAI: The development of kinetic theory of gases from the 17th through 20th centuries. Newton proposed static particle models; early advocates of molecular motion were initially discredited. Maxwell and Boltzmann revolutionized understanding by applying statistical methods to molecular behavior, relating pressure, volume, and temperature to aggregate particle motion. Development proceeded alongside atomic theory and thermodynamics. Surprisingly, molecular existence remained contested into the early 20th century until Einstein's Brownian motion work and Perrin's experiments. Connects classical mechanics, statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, philosophy of scientific realism, and the history of atomic and molecular physics.

episodes.title: King Solomon
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the biblical king Solomon, celebrated for his wisdom and as the architect of the First Temple in Jerusalem. According to the Old Testament account of his life, Solomon was chosen as his father David's successor as Israelite king, and instead of praying for long life or wealth asked God for wisdom. In the words of the Authorised Version, "And there came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solomon, from all kings of the earth, which had heard of his wisdom." Solomon is an important figure in Judaism, Islam and Christianity alike, and is also credited with the authorship of several scriptural texts. His name is associated with the tradition of wisdom literature and with a large number of myths and legends. For many centuries Solomon was seen as the archetypal enlightened monarch, and his example influenced notions of kingship from the Middle Ages onwards
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the biblical king Solomon, celebrated for his wisdom and as the architect of the First Temple in Jerusalem. According to the Old Testament account of his life, Solomon was chosen as his father David's successor as Israelite king, and instead of praying for long life or wealth asked God for wisdom. In the words of the Authorised Version, "And there came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solomon, from all kings of the earth, which had heard of his wisdom." Solomon is an important figure in Judaism, Islam and Christianity alike, and is also credited with the authorship of several scriptural texts. His name is associated with the tradition of wisdom literature and with a large number of myths and legends. For many centuries Solomon was seen as the archetypal enlightened monarch, and his example influenced notions of kingship from the Middle Ages onwards
episodes.summaryAI: King Solomon, biblical ruler of ancient Israel celebrated for extraordinary wisdom, wealth, and construction of the First Temple in Jerusalem (c. 10th century BCE). Significant across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Credited with authorship of Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Songs, contributing to wisdom literature traditions. Associated with extensive mythology including stories of judgment, magical powers, and relations with the Queen of Sheba. Served as archetypal model of the wise, divinely sanctioned monarch, influencing medieval and early modern conceptions of kingship, sacred architecture, and interfaith literary traditions across the Near East and Europe.

episodes.title: Lakshmi
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origins of the Hindu goddess Lakshmi, and of the traditions that have built around her for over 3,000 years. According to the creation story of the Puranas, she came to existence in the churning of the ocean of milk. Her prominent status grew alongside other goddesses in the mainly male world of the Vedas, as female deities came to be seen as the Shakti, the energy of the gods, without which they would be powerless. Lakshmi came to represent the qualities of blessing, prosperity, fertility, beauty and good fortune and, more recently, political order, and she has a significant role in Diwali, one of the most important of the Hindu festivals.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origins of the Hindu goddess Lakshmi, and of the traditions that have built around her for over 3,000 years. According to the creation story of the Puranas, she came to existence in the churning of the ocean of milk. Her prominent status grew alongside other goddesses in the mainly male world of the Vedas, as female deities came to be seen as the Shakti, the energy of the gods, without which they would be powerless. Lakshmi came to represent the qualities of blessing, prosperity, fertility, beauty and good fortune and, more recently, political order, and she has a significant role in Diwali, one of the most important of the Hindu festivals.
episodes.summaryAI: The Hindu goddess Lakshmi, whose worship traditions developed over 3,000 years in South Asian religious life. Mythologically emerged during the churning of the ocean of milk. Embodies prosperity, fertility, beauty, good fortune, and legitimate political authority. Consort of Vishnu across his incarnations. Central to Diwali celebrations and domestic worship. Her theological significance grew as female deities were increasingly understood through Shakti theology as embodiments of divine energy. Connects to Vaishnavism, Hindu kingship ideology, devotional practices, iconographic traditions, and broader South Asian conceptions of auspiciousness, wealth, and cosmic order.

episodes.title: Lamarck and Natural Selection
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses Jean-Baptiste Lamarck, the 18th century French scientist.Charles Darwin defined Natural Selection in On the Origin of Species, Variations, however slight and from whatever cause proceeding, if they be in any degree profitable to the individuals of a species will tend to the preservation of such individuals, and will generally be inherited by the offspring. It was a simple idea that had instant recognition, How extremely stupid not to have thought of that! said T H Huxley. However, Darwin did not invent the idea of evolution and not everyone saw his ideas as original. The great geologist Charles Lyell repeatedly referred to Lamarcks theory as modified by Darwin, Darwin complained to him, I believe this way of putting the case is very injurious to its acceptance. He desperately wanted to escape the shadow of this genuine scientific precursor and what has become known as the Lamarckian Heresy has maintained a ghostly presence on the fringes of biology to this day.Who was Lamarck? How did Natural Selection escape from his shadow and gain acceptance from the scientific establishment? And has any evidence emerged that might challenge the elegant simplicity of Darwins big idea?With Sandy Knapp, Senior Botanist at the Natural History Museum, Steve Jones, Professor of Genetics in the Galton Laboratory at University College London and author of Almost Like a Whale: The Origin of Species Updated; Simon Conway Morris, Professor of Evolutionary Paleobiology at Cambridge University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg discusses Jean-Baptiste Lamarck, the 18th century French scientist.Charles Darwin defined Natural Selection in On the Origin of Species, Variations, however slight and from whatever cause proceeding, if they be in any degree profitable to the individuals of a species will tend to the preservation of such individuals, and will generally be inherited by the offspring. It was a simple idea that had instant recognition, How extremely stupid not to have thought of that! said T H Huxley. However, Darwin did not invent the idea of evolution and not everyone saw his ideas as original. The great geologist Charles Lyell repeatedly referred to Lamarcks theory as modified by Darwin, Darwin complained to him, I believe this way of putting the case is very injurious to its acceptance. He desperately wanted to escape the shadow of this genuine scientific precursor and what has become known as the Lamarckian Heresy has maintained a ghostly presence on the fringes of biology to this day.Who was Lamarck? How did Natural Selection escape from his shadow and gain acceptance from the scientific establishment? And has any evidence emerged that might challenge the elegant simplicity of Darwins big idea?With Sandy Knapp, Senior Botanist at the Natural History Museum, Steve Jones, Professor of Genetics in the Galton Laboratory at University College London and author of Almost Like a Whale: The Origin of Species Updated; Simon Conway Morris, Professor of Evolutionary Paleobiology at Cambridge University.
episodes.summaryAI: Jean-Baptiste Lamarck (1744-1829), French naturalist who proposed an early theory of biological evolution emphasizing inheritance of acquired characteristics and the progressive complexification of organisms. Preceded Darwin and influenced evolutionary thought despite later rejection of his mechanism. Darwin explicitly sought to distinguish natural selection from Lamarckian inheritance. Covers Lamarck's contributions to invertebrate taxonomy, his transformist theory, the subsequent debate over soft inheritance, and the persistent marginal presence of Lamarckian ideas in biology. Connects to history of evolutionary theory, philosophy of biology, epigenetics debates, and 18th-century French natural history.

episodes.title: Language and the Mind
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of our ideas about the formation of language. The psychologist George Miller worked out that in English there are potentially a hundred million trillion sentences of twenty words in length - that’s a hundred times the number of seconds since the birth of the universe. “Language”, as Chomsky put it, “makes infinite use of finite media”. “Language”, as Steven Pinker puts it, “comes so naturally to us that it’s easy to forget what a strange and miraculous gift it is”. “All over the world”, he writes, “members of our species spend a good part of their lives fashioning their breath into hisses and hums and squeaks and pops and are listening to others do the same”. Jean Jacques Rousseau once said that we differ from the animal kingdom in two main ways - the use of language and the prohibition of incest. Language and our ability to learn it has been held up traditionally as our species’ most remarkable achievement, marking us apart from the animals. But in the 20th century, our ideas about how language is formed are being radically challenged and altered. With Dr Jonathan Miller, medical doctor, performer, broadcaster, author and film and opera director; Steven Pinker, cognitive scientist, Professor of Psychology and Director of the Centre for Neuroscience, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, California.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of our ideas about the formation of language. The psychologist George Miller worked out that in English there are potentially a hundred million trillion sentences of twenty words in length - that’s a hundred times the number of seconds since the birth of the universe. “Language”, as Chomsky put it, “makes infinite use of finite media”. “Language”, as Steven Pinker puts it, “comes so naturally to us that it’s easy to forget what a strange and miraculous gift it is”. “All over the world”, he writes, “members of our species spend a good part of their lives fashioning their breath into hisses and hums and squeaks and pops and are listening to others do the same”. Jean Jacques Rousseau once said that we differ from the animal kingdom in two main ways - the use of language and the prohibition of incest. Language and our ability to learn it has been held up traditionally as our species’ most remarkable achievement, marking us apart from the animals. But in the 20th century, our ideas about how language is formed are being radically challenged and altered. With Dr Jonathan Miller, medical doctor, performer, broadcaster, author and film and opera director; Steven Pinker, cognitive scientist, Professor of Psychology and Director of the Centre for Neuroscience, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, California.
episodes.summaryAI: The history and philosophy of language, from Enlightenment speculation to 20th-century linguistics. Covers Rousseau's view that language and incest prohibition distinguish humans from animals, and Chomsky's transformational grammar positing innate universal structures enabling infinite expression from finite linguistic elements. Addresses debates over language origins, the relationship between language and thought, nativist versus empiricist models of acquisition, and whether language capacity is uniquely human. Connects to philosophy of mind, cognitive science, anthropology, structuralism, and evolutionary biology. Relevant to understanding human cognition, cultural development, and the nature of symbolic communication.

episodes.title: Lawrence of Arabia
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss T.E. Lawrence (1888 – 1935), better known as Lawrence of Arabia, a topic drawn from over 1200 suggestions for our Listener Week 2019. Although Lawrence started as an archaeologist in the Middle East, when World War I broke out he joined the British army and became an intelligence officer. His contact with a prominent Arab leader, Sharif Hussein, made him sympathetic to Hussein’s cause and during the Arab Revolt of 1916 he not only served the British but also the interests of Hussein. After the war he was dismayed by the peace settlement and felt that the British had broken an assurance that Sharif Hussein would lead a new Arab kingdom. Lawrence was made famous by the work of Lowell Thomas, whose film of Lawrence drew huge audiences in 1919, which led to his own book Seven Pillars of Wisdom and David Lean’s 1962 film with Peter O'Toole.

episodes.summaryAI: T.E. Lawrence (1888-1935), British archaeologist turned intelligence officer and military liaison during WWI's Arab Revolt of 1916 against Ottoman rule. His alliance with Sharif Hussein fostered sympathy for Arab self-determination. Disillusioned by the postwar peace settlement, believing Britain betrayed assurances of an independent Arab kingdom. His fame grew through Lowell Thomas's lecture films, his own memoir Seven Pillars of Wisdom, and David Lean's 1962 film. Covers Middle Eastern geopolitics, Anglo-Arab relations, Ottoman collapse, WWI desert campaigns, imperial diplomacy, and the construction of heroic myth in modern media.

episodes.title: Laws of Nature
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Laws of Nature. Since ancient times philosophers and physicists have tried to discover simple underlying principles that control the Universe: In the 6th Century BC Thales declared “Everything is water”, centuries later Aristotle claimed that all of creation was forged from four elements, Newton more successfully laid down the Law of Universal Gravitation and as we speak, contemporary scientists are struggling to complete the task of ‘String Theory’ - the quest to find a single over-arching equation that unites all of physics, and can perhaps explain the organisation of everything in existence.But are the Laws of Physics really ‘facts of life’? Is what is true in physics, true in all areas of existence? Is it even true in other areas of physics?With Mark Buchanan, physicist and author of Ubiquity; Professor Frank Close, theoretical physicist and author of Lucifer’s Legacy: The Meaning of Asymmetry; Nancy Cartwright, Professor of Philosophy, LSE.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Laws of Nature. Is it even true in other areas of physics?With Mark Buchanan, physicist and author of Ubiquity; Professor Frank Close, theoretical physicist and author of Lucifer’s Legacy: The Meaning of Asymmetry; Nancy Cartwright, Professor of Philosophy, LSE.
episodes.summaryAI: The concept of laws of nature from ancient philosophy through modern physics. Traces the progression from Thales's monism and Aristotle's four elements through Newton's universal gravitation to contemporary string theory. Examines philosophical questions about whether physical laws are discovered objective truths or human constructs, the relationship between mathematical description and physical reality, and the status of scientific laws as explanatory versus descriptive. Connects natural philosophy, metaphysics, philosophy of science, and theoretical physics. Relevant to understanding scientific methodology, realism versus instrumentalism, and the historical development of fundamental physics from antiquity to the present.

episodes.title: Le Morte d'Arthur
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Thomas Malory's "Le Morte Darthur", the epic tale of King Arthur and his knights of the Round Table. Sir Thomas Malory was a knight from Warwickshire, a respectable country gentleman and MP in the 1440s who later turned to a life of crime and spent various spells in prison. It was during Malory's final incarceration that he wrote "Le Morte Darthur", an epic work which was based primarily on French, but also some English, sources.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Thomas Malory's "Le Morte Darthur", the epic tale of King Arthur and his knights of the Round Table. Sir Thomas Malory was a knight from Warwickshire, a respectable country gentleman and MP in the 1440s who later turned to a life of crime and spent various spells in prison. It was during Malory's final incarceration that he wrote "Le Morte Darthur", an epic work which was based primarily on French, but also some English, sources.
episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Malory's Le Morte d'Arthur (printed 1485), the defining English prose compilation of Arthurian legend encompassing King Arthur, the Round Table knights, the quest for the Holy Grail, and the fall of Camelot. Malory was a Warwickshire knight and MP who turned to criminal activity and composed the work during imprisonment, drawing primarily on French Arthurian romances (Vulgate Cycle). Covers medieval chivalric ideals, courtly love, knightly honor, and the tragic dissolution of political order. Connects to medieval literary tradition, Anglo-French cultural exchange, manuscript and early print culture, and the enduring mythology of Arthurian Britain.

episodes.title: Lear
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss King Lear. Around the turn of 1606, a group of London theatre-goers braved the plague to take in a new play by the well-known impresario, Mr William Shakespeare. Packed into the Globe Theatre, they were treated to a tale of violence, hatred and betrayal so upsetting that it thereafter languished among Shakespeare’s less popular plays.The story of Lear – of a man who divides up his property and loses the love of a daughter - is an ancient and ultimately happy one. But in the hands of William Shakespeare it became a shocking and violent vision of a broken family in a godless universe. So shocking that after the playwright’s death it was shunned and rewritten with a happy ending. Only in the 19th and 20th centuries did Shakespeare’s bleak, experimental and disorientating drama attain the status it has now. But why did Shakespeare take a story from the deep history of Britain and make it so shockingly his own and when, from the Civil War to the Second World War, did this powerful and confusing tragedy emerge as Shakespeare’s greatest? With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick; Katherine Duncan-Jones, Tutorial Fellow in English at Somerville College, Oxford; Catherine Belsey, Research Professor in English at the University of Wales, Swansea

episodes.summaryAI: Shakespeare's King Lear, a tragedy transforming an earlier narrative of a king who divides his realm among his daughters, with originally happy resolution, into a devastatingly bleak drama of familial betrayal, madness, and nihilistic suffering. Shakespeare's version proved so disturbing it was replaced by Nahum Tate's happy-ending adaptation after Shakespeare's death and only regained prominence in the 19th and 20th centuries. Covers themes of authority, filial obligation, justice, nature, and human fragility. Connects to Renaissance drama, editorial and performance history, literary reception, and evolving cultural attitudes toward tragedy and monarchical power.

episodes.title: Lenin
episodes.synopsis: For some time, in some intellectual quarters in the West, Vladimir Ilich Ulyanov - also known as Lenin - was regarded as an understandable revolutionary, perhaps a necessary revolutionary given the actions of the Tsars, certainly a sympathetic revolutionary compared with his successor - Stalin. He became an icon in Russia - his body unburied, lying in Red Square in a state of permanent, imminent resurrection. The Russian Presidential Elections take place at the end of the month, and the Acting President, Vladimir Putin, promised that if he won he would finally take the body of Lenin from Red Square and bury him. But whether the country will be able to escape the extraordinary influence of the man, his ideas and his machinery of oppression is another matter. In his short period in power between 1917 and 1924 Vladimir Illyich Lenin invented the one party state, developed a model to export communism around the world and built a completely original political system that remained intact for over seventy years. What drove him and enabled him to achieve success?Robert Service, lecturer in Russian History and Fellow of St Anthony’s College, Oxford and biographer of Lenin; Vitali Vitaliev, author, columnist, broadcaster former Soviet Journalist of the Year.

episodes.summaryAI: Vladimir Ilich Lenin (1870-1924), revolutionary leader and founder of the Soviet state. During his brief period in power from 1917 to 1924, he invented the one-party state model, developed Marxist-Leninist ideology as a framework for exporting communist revolution, and built a political system that endured over seventy years. Covers his intellectual formation in Russian radical tradition, adaptation of Marxism to Russian conditions, role in the October Revolution, establishment of Bolshevik governance, civil war leadership, and the New Economic Policy. Connects to modern political theory, totalitarianism, Cold War origins, and 20th-century revolutionary movements worldwide.

episodes.title: Lévi-Strauss
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the work of the anthropologist Claude Lévi-Strauss. One of twentieth-century France's most celebrated intellectuals, Lévi-Strauss attempted to show in his work that thought processes were a feature universal to humans, whether they lived in tribal rainforest societies or in the rich intellectual life of Paris. During the 1930s he studied native Brazilian tribes in the Amazonian jungle, but for most of his long career he preferred the study to the field. He was the leading exponent of structuralism, a school of thought which was influential for decades, and was involved in a famous debate with his friend Jean-Paul Sartre, who resisted many of his ideas. His books about the nature of myth, human thought and kinship are now seen as some of the most important anthropological texts written in the twentieth century.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the work of the anthropologist Claude Lévi-Strauss. One of twentieth-century France's most celebrated intellectuals, Lévi-Strauss attempted to show in his work that thought processes were a feature universal to humans, whether they lived in tribal rainforest societies or in the rich intellectual life of Paris. During the 1930s he studied native Brazilian tribes in the Amazonian jungle, but for most of his long career he preferred the study to the field. He was the leading exponent of structuralism, a school of thought which was influential for decades, and was involved in a famous debate with his friend Jean-Paul Sartre, who resisted many of his ideas. His books about the nature of myth, human thought and kinship are now seen as some of the most important anthropological texts written in the twentieth century.
episodes.summaryAI: Claude Lévi-Strauss (1908-2009), French anthropologist and leading exponent of structuralism. Conducted fieldwork among Brazilian indigenous societies in the 1930s. Argued that underlying structures of human thought are universal across all cultures, from tribal societies to modern intellectual life. Major works on kinship systems, myth, and classification (The Elementary Structures of Kinship, Tristes Tropiques, The Savage Mind). Engaged in celebrated debate with Sartre over history and dialectical reason. Connects to linguistics (Saussure, Jakobson), philosophy, literary theory, and the broader structuralist movement in 20th-century French intellectual culture.

episodes.title: Li Shizhen
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and ideas of Li Shizhen (1518-1593) whose compendium of natural medicines is celebrated in China as the most complete survey of natural remedies of its time. He trained as a doctor and worked at the Ming court before spending almost 30 years travelling in China, inspecting local plants and animals for their properties, trying them out on himself and then describing his findings in his Compendium of Materia Medica or Bencao Gangmu, in 53 volumes. He's been called the uncrowned king of Chinese naturalists, and became a scientific hero in the 20th century after the revolution.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and ideas of Li Shizhen (1518-1593) whose compendium of natural medicines is celebrated in China as the most complete survey of natural remedies of its time. He trained as a doctor and worked at the Ming court before spending almost 30 years travelling in China, inspecting local plants and animals for their properties, trying them out on himself and then describing his findings in his Compendium of Materia Medica or Bencao Gangmu, in 53 volumes. He's been called the uncrowned king of Chinese naturalists, and became a scientific hero in the 20th century after the revolution.
episodes.summaryAI: Li Shizhen (1518-1593), Ming Dynasty Chinese physician and naturalist who compiled the Bencao Gangmu (Compendium of Materia Medica) across 53 volumes over approximately 30 years. Trained in medicine and served at the Ming court before extensive field research throughout China documenting plants, animals, and minerals with medicinal properties. Celebrated as the uncrowned king of Chinese naturalists. The compendium systematically classified nearly 1,900 substances and remains a foundational text in traditional Chinese medicine. Connects to Ming intellectual culture, natural history, pharmacology, botanical taxonomy, and comparative traditions of medical knowledge in early modern Asia.

episodes.title: Linnaeus
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life, ideas and legacy of the pioneering Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus (1707 – 1778). The philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau once wrote: "Tell him I know no greater man on earth".
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life, ideas and legacy of the pioneering Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus (1707 – 1778). The philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau once wrote: "Tell him I know no greater man on earth".
episodes.summaryAI: Carl Linnaeus (1707-1778), Swedish botanist who created the binomial nomenclature system for classifying living organisms. Established modern taxonomy, organizing plants, animals, and minerals into hierarchical categories of kingdom, class, order, genus, and species. His Systema Naturae and Species Plantarum became foundational texts for biological classification. Undertook expeditions across Lapland and Sweden cataloguing flora and fauna. Trained apostles who collected specimens worldwide. His standardized naming system transformed natural history into a rigorous discipline, bridging botany, zoology, and medicine, and remains the basis of biological classification today.

episodes.title: Lise Meitner
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the decisive role of one of the great 20th Century physicists in solving the question of nuclear fission. It is said that Meitner (1878-1968) made this breakthrough over Christmas 1938 while she was sitting on a log in Sweden during a snowy walk with her nephew Otto Frisch (1904-79). Both were Jewish-Austrian refugees who had only recently escaped from Nazi Germany. Others had already broken uranium into the smaller atom barium, but could not explain what they found; was the larger atom bursting, or the smaller atom being chipped off or was something else happening? They turned to Meitner. She, with Frisch, deduced the nucleus really was splitting like a drop of water into a dumbbell shape, with the electrical charges at each end forcing the divide, something previously thought impossible, and they named this ‘fission’. This was a crucial breakthrough for which Meitner was eventually widely recognised if not at first.

episodes.summaryAI: Lise Meitner (1878-1968), Austrian-born physicist who made the critical theoretical breakthrough explaining nuclear fission in December 1938. Long collaboration with chemist Otto Hahn in Berlin on radioactivity and transuranium elements. As a Jewish refugee from Nazi Germany, she and nephew Otto Frisch applied Bohr's liquid-drop model to deduce that uranium nuclei were splitting, coining the term fission and calculating energy release via mass-energy equivalence. Hahn received the 1944 Nobel Prize alone. Meitner's exclusion highlights gender and political biases in twentieth-century physics recognition. Connects atomic structure, nuclear physics, and wartime science.

episodes.title: Literary Modernism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss literary modernism. In James Joyce’s Ulysses he writes, “Greater love than this, he said, no man hath that a man may lay down his wife for a friend. Go thou and do likewise. Thus, or words to that effect, saith Zarathustra, sometime regius Professor of French letters to the University of Oxtail”. It is profane, it gets the Bible wrong on purpose, it nods in the direction of Nietzsche and it doesn’t quite seem to make sense - it must be modernism! The literary movement that embraced Joyce, DH Lawrence, TS Eliot, Virginia Woolf and many others in the early decades of the twentieth century.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss literary modernism. The literary movement that embraced Joyce, DH Lawrence, TS Eliot, Virginia Woolf and many others in the early decades of the twentieth century.
episodes.summaryAI: Literary modernism as a movement in the early twentieth century, encompassing James Joyce, Virginia Woolf, D.H. Lawrence, T.S. Eliot, and Ezra Pound. Characterized by stream of consciousness, fragmented narrative, formal experimentation, and rejection of Victorian literary conventions. Influenced by Nietzsche, Freud, and the cultural rupture of World War I. Key works include Ulysses, Mrs Dalloway, The Waste Land, and Sons and Lovers. Engages themes of alienation, subjectivity, urban modernity, and the collapse of shared meaning. Relationship to visual art movements like cubism and post-impressionism. Transformed the novel, poetry, and literary criticism.

episodes.title: The Written World: Literature
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg investigates how the written word, a technology originally used for accountancy, gave rise to all of human literature. He charts the emergence of poetry and history writing in the ancient world, inspects an ancient Egyptian precursor to Hamlet, and discovers how Greek literary traditions reached this country in the Middle Ages.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg investigates how the written word, a technology originally used for accountancy, gave rise to all of human literature. He charts the emergence of poetry and history writing in the ancient world, inspects an ancient Egyptian precursor to Hamlet, and discovers how Greek literary traditions reached this country in the Middle Ages.
episodes.summaryAI: Origins and development of literature from the invention of writing systems. Earliest writing served administrative and accountancy purposes in Mesopotamia and Egypt before evolving into literary expression. Covers emergence of poetry, epic narrative, and historical writing in the ancient Near East and Greece. Examines Egyptian literary texts prefiguring later dramatic themes. Traces transmission of Greek literary traditions through Roman, Arabic, and medieval European channels to Britain. Explores how oral traditions interacted with written forms. Spans cuneiform tablets through classical genres to medieval manuscript culture, connecting language systems, storytelling, and the preservation of knowledge.

episodes.title: Little Women
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Louisa May Alcott's 1868 novel, credited with starting the new genre of young adult fiction. When Alcott (1832-88) wrote Little Women, she only did so as her publisher refused to publish her father's book otherwise and as she hoped it would make money. It made Alcott's fortune. This coming of age story of Meg, Jo, Beth and Amy March, each overcoming their own moral flaws, has delighted generations of readers and was so popular from the start that Alcott wrote the second part in 1869 and further sequels and spin-offs in the coming years. Her work has inspired countless directors, composers and authors to make many reimagined versions ever since, with the sisters played by film actors such as Katherine Hepburn, Elizabeth Taylor, Winona Ryder, Claire Danes, Kirsten Dunst, Saoirse Ronan and Emma Watson.

Her work has inspired countless directors, composers and authors to make many reimagined versions ever since, with the sisters played by film actors such as Katherine Hepburn, Elizabeth Taylor, Winona Ryder, Claire Danes, Kirsten Dunst, Saoirse Ronan and Emma Watson.
episodes.summaryAI: Louisa May Alcott's Little Women (1868), a coming-of-age novel following sisters Meg, Jo, Beth, and Amy March during and after the American Civil War. Credited with originating the young adult fiction genre. Draws on Alcott's own family life and her father Bronson Alcott's transcendentalist philosophy. Themes of moral self-improvement, gender roles, female independence, poverty, and domesticity. Jo March as a proto-feminist literary figure. The novel's enormous commercial success transformed Alcott's finances. Extensive adaptation history across theatre, film, and television. Situated within nineteenth-century American literature and women's writing traditions.

episodes.title: Logic
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the history of logic. Logic, the study of reasoning and argument, first became a serious area of study in the 4th century BC through the work of Aristotle. He created a formal logical system, based on a type of argument called a syllogism, which remained in use for over two thousand years. In the nineteenth century the German philosopher and mathematician Gottlob Frege revolutionised logic, turning it into a discipline much like mathematics and capable of dealing with expressing and analysing nuanced arguments. His discoveries influenced the greatest mathematicians and philosophers of the twentieth century and considerably aided the development of the electronic computer. Today logic is a subtle system with applications in fields as diverse as mathematics, philosophy, linguistics and artificial intelligence
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the history of logic. He created a formal logical system, based on a type of argument called a syllogism, which remained in use for over two thousand years. In the nineteenth century the German philosopher and mathematician Gottlob Frege revolutionised logic, turning it into a discipline much like mathematics and capable of dealing with expressing and analysing nuanced arguments. episodes.summaryAI: History of logic from Aristotle's fourth-century BC formal system of syllogistic reasoning through medieval developments to Gottlob Frege's late nineteenth-century revolution reconceiving logic as a rigorous formal discipline akin to mathematics. Covers propositional logic, predicate calculus, and formal proof systems. Frege, Russell, Whitehead, Gödel, and Turing extended logical foundations into mathematics, philosophy of language, and computation. Gödel's incompleteness theorems revealed inherent limits of formal systems. Direct influence on development of electronic computers and artificial intelligence. Applications span mathematics, philosophy, linguistics, and computer science.

episodes.title: Logical Positivism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses Logical Positivism, the eye-wateringly radical early 20th century philosophical movement. The Logical Positivists argued that much previous philosophy was built on very shaky foundations, and they wanted to go right back to the drawing board. They insisted that philosophy - and science - had to be much more rigorous before it started making grand claims about the world. The movement began with the Vienna Circle, a group of philosophically-trained scientists and scientifically-trained philosophers, who met on Thursdays, in 'Red Vienna', in the years after the First World War. They were trying to remould philosophy in a world turned upside down not just by war, but by major advances in science. Their hero was not Descartes or Hegel but Albert Einstein. The group's new doctrine rejected great swathes of earlier philosophy, from meditations on the existence of God to declarations on the nature of History, as utterly meaningless. When the Nazis took power, they fled to England and America, where their ideas put down new roots, and went on to have a profound impact.Melvyn is joined by Barry Smith, Professor of Philosophy at the University of London; Nancy Cartwright, Professor of Philosophy at the London School of Economics; and Thomas Uebel, Professor of Philosophy at Manchester University.

episodes.summaryAI: Logical positivism and the Vienna Circle, a group of scientifically trained philosophers and philosophically trained scientists meeting in 1920s Vienna including Schlick, Carnap, Neurath, and Gödel. Developed the verification principle: statements meaningful only if empirically verifiable or logically tautological, rejecting metaphysics, theology, and ethics as cognitively meaningless. Inspired by Einstein, Russell, and Wittgenstein's Tractatus. When Nazis rose to power, members dispersed to Britain and America, profoundly influencing Anglo-American analytic philosophy, philosophy of science, and scientific methodology. Connects interwar European intellectual culture with twentieth-century epistemology.

episodes.title: London
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of London. To T.S.Eliot it was the “Unreal City”, to Wordsworth “Earth has not anything to show more fair” but to Shelley, “Hell is a city much like London”. At the start of this twenty-first century the capital city covers an area of 625 square miles, is home to 7 million souls, and has an economy which at more than £115 billion is larger than that of Saudi Arabia, Ireland or Singapore. Is this modern metropolis still the place that the poets described? Can there be such a thing as a history of a city, which in each generation sucks in its communities from around the country and around the globe? In a city whose buildings have been razed, whose people have been decimated and whose borders have been dramatically redrawn, what is there that connects it to its own past?With Peter Ackroyd, author of London: The Biography; Claire Tomalin, author and biographer of Samuel Pepys; Iain Sinclair, poet, novelist and author of Liquid City and Lights Out for the Territory.

episodes.summaryAI: History of London from Roman Londinium through medieval, Tudor, Georgian, Victorian, and modern periods. Covers the city's growth from a trading settlement on the Thames to a twenty-first-century global metropolis of seven million. Themes include successive waves of immigration, the Great Fire of 1666, industrialization, imperial capital status, the Blitz, and postwar multiculturalism. Economic power rivalling nation-states. Literary representations from Chaucer to Eliot and Wordsworth. Explores how London's identity is shaped by constant demographic renewal, commercial dynamism, and its role as a political, financial, and cultural centre within British and world history.

episodes.title: Longitude
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the search for Longitude while at sea. Following efforts by other maritime nations, the British Government passed the Longitude Act in 1714 to reward anyone who devised reliable means for ships to determine their longitude at sea. Mariners could already calculate how far they were north or south, the Latitude, using the Pole Star, but voyaging across the Atlantic to the Caribbean was much less predictable as navigators could not be sure how far east or west they were, a particular problem when heading for islands. It took fifty years of individual genius and collaboration in Britain and across Europe, among astronomers, clock makers, mathematicians and sailors, for the problem to be resolved.

episodes.summaryAI: The problem of determining longitude at sea and the British Longitude Act of 1714 offering a prize for a reliable solution. Mariners could calculate latitude from celestial observations but lacked means to fix east-west position, causing shipwrecks and navigation failures. Competing approaches included astronomical lunar distance methods championed by Nevil Maskelyne and the mechanical chronometer solution developed by John Harrison over decades. Required advances in precision clockmaking, astronomical observation, and mathematical calculation. Collaboration among astronomers, instrument makers, and sailors. Connects navigation, timekeeping technology, eighteenth-century science, and maritime exploration.

episodes.title: Lorca
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Spanish poet and playwright Federico Garcia Lorca (1898-1936), author of Blood Wedding, Yerma and The House of Bernarda Alba, who mixed the traditions of Andalusia with the avant-garde. He found his first major success with his Gypsy Ballads, although Dali, once his close friend, mocked him for these, accusing Lorca of being too conservative. He preferred performing his poems to publishing them, and his plays marked a revival in Spanish theatre. He was captured and killed by Nationalist forces at the start of the Civil War, his body never recovered, and it's been suggested this was punishment for his politics and for being openly gay. He has since been seen as the most important Spanish playwright and poet of the last century.

episodes.summaryAI: Federico Garcia Lorca (1898-1936), Spanish poet and playwright from Granada. Major works include Gypsy Ballads, Blood Wedding, Yerma, and The House of Bernarda Alba. Fused Andalusian folk traditions, flamenco, and Romani culture with surrealist and avant-garde techniques. Close associations with Dalí and Buñuel in 1920s Madrid. Preferred oral performance of poetry over publication. Themes of passion, honour, repression, rural Spain, and social constraint. Captured and executed by Nationalist forces at the outbreak of the Spanish Civil War in 1936, body never found. Regarded as Spain's most significant modern poet and dramatist.

episodes.title: Louis Pasteur
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of Louis Pasteur (1822-1895) and his extraordinary contribution to medicine and science. It is said few people have saved more lives than Pasteur. A chemist, he showed that otherwise identical molecules could exist as 'left' and 'right-handed' versions and that molecules produced by living things were always left-handed. He proposed a germ theory to replace the idea of spontaneous generation. He discovered that microorganisms cause fermentation and disease. He began the process named after him, pasteurisation, heating liquids to 50-60 C to kill microbes. He saved the beer and wine industries in France when they were struggling with microbial contamination. He saved the French silk industry when he found a way of protecting healthy silkworm eggs from disease. He developed vaccines against anthrax and rabies and helped establish immunology. Many of his ideas were developed further after his lifetime, but one of his legacies was a charitable body, the Pasteur Institute, to continue research into infectious disease.

episodes.summaryAI: Louis Pasteur (1822-1895), French chemist and microbiologist. Early work on molecular chirality demonstrated left and right-handed crystal forms. Disproved spontaneous generation. Proposed germ theory of disease, revolutionizing medicine and public health. Discovered that microorganisms cause fermentation, leading to pasteurisation to preserve wine, beer, and milk. Saved French silk, wine, and brewing industries from microbial diseases. Developed vaccines against anthrax and rabies using attenuated pathogens. Founded the Pasteur Institute in Paris. His work bridges crystallography, organic chemistry, microbiology, immunology, and industrial applications of biological science.

episodes.title: Louis XIV: The Sun King
episodes.synopsis: In 1661 the 23 year-old French king Louis the XIV had been on the throne for 18 years when his chief minister, Cardinal Mazarin, died. Louis is reported to have said to his ministers, “It is now time that I govern my affairs myself. You will assist me with your counsels when I ask for them [but] I order you to seal no orders except by my command… I order you not to sign anything, not even a passport, without my command, and to render account to me personally each day”
episodes.description: In 1661 the 23 year-old French king Louis the XIV had been on the throne for 18 years when his chief minister, Cardinal Mazarin, died. Louis is reported to have said to his ministers, “It is now time that I govern my affairs myself. You will assist me with your counsels when I ask for them [but] I order you to seal no orders except by my command… I order you not to sign anything, not even a passport, without my command, and to render account to me personally each day”
episodes.summaryAI: Louis XIV of France (1638-1715), who assumed personal rule in 1661 after Cardinal Mazarin's death, establishing the model of absolute monarchy. Built Versailles as seat of government and instrument of aristocratic control. Centralised French administration, finance, and military. Revoked the Edict of Nantes, persecuting Huguenots. Pursued expansionist wars including the War of Spanish Succession. Patronised arts, establishing academies for painting, science, music, and architecture. His reign shaped European diplomacy, state-building, court culture, and the balance of power for a century. Longest-reigning European monarch at 72 years.

episodes.title: Lyrical Ballads
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Lyrical Ballads, the collection of poems by William Wordsworth and Samuel Coleridge first published in 1798. The work was conceived as an attempt to cast off the stultifying conventions of formal 18th-century poetry. Wordsworth wrote that the poems it contains should be "considered as experiments. They were written chiefly with a view to ascertain how far the language of conversation in the middle and lower classes of society is adapted to the purpose of poetic pleasure."Lyrical Ballads contains some of the best-known work by Coleridge and Wordsworth, including The Rime of the Ancient Mariner and Tintern Abbey - and is today seen as a point of radical departure for poetry in English
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Lyrical Ballads, the collection of poems by William Wordsworth and Samuel Coleridge first published in 1798. The work was conceived as an attempt to cast off the stultifying conventions of formal 18th-century poetry. Wordsworth wrote that the poems it contains should be "considered as experiments. They were written chiefly with a view to ascertain how far the language of conversation in the middle and lower classes of society is adapted to the purpose of poetic pleasure."Lyrical Ballads contains some of the best-known work by Coleridge and Wordsworth, including The Rime of the Ancient Mariner and Tintern Abbey - and is today seen as a point of radical departure for poetry in English
episodes.summaryAI: Lyrical Ballads, poetry collection by William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge first published anonymously in 1798. Contains The Rime of the Ancient Mariner and Tintern Abbey. Wordsworth's 1800 Preface articulated a manifesto for poetry using the real language of common people and treating ordinary subjects with imaginative intensity. Rejected neoclassical conventions of eighteenth-century verse. Regarded as a founding text of English Romanticism. Explores nature, memory, rural life, supernatural experience, and the relationship between individual consciousness and the natural world. Bridges Enlightenment rationalism and Romantic sensibility in British literary history.

episodes.title: Lysenkoism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests delve into the dark world of genetics under Joseph Stalin in discussing the career of Trofim Lysenko. In 1928, as America lurched towards the Wall Street Crash, Joseph Stalin revealed his master plan - nature was to be conquered by science, Russia to be made brutally, glitteringly modern and the world transformed by communist endeavour.Into the heart of this vision stepped Trofim Lysenko, a self-taught geneticist who promised to turn Russian wasteland into a grain-laden Garden of Eden. Today, Lysenko is a byword for fraud but in Stalin’s Russia his outlandish ideas about genetic inheritance and evolution became law. They reveal a world of science distorted by ideology, where ideas were literally a matter of life and death. To disagree with Lysenko risked the gulag and yet he destroyed Soviet Agriculture and damaged, perhaps irreparably, the Soviet Union’s capacity to fight and win the Cold War. With Robert Service, Professor of Russian History at the University of Oxford; Steve Jones, Professor of Genetics at University College London; Catherine Merridale, Professor of Contemporary History at Queen Mary, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests delve into the dark world of genetics under Joseph Stalin in discussing the career of Trofim Lysenko. In 1928, as America lurched towards the Wall Street Crash, Joseph Stalin revealed his master plan - nature was to be conquered by science, Russia to be made brutally, glitteringly modern and the world transformed by communist endeavour.Into the heart of this vision stepped Trofim Lysenko, a self-taught geneticist who promised to turn Russian wasteland into a grain-laden Garden of Eden. They reveal a world of science distorted by ideology, where ideas were literally a matter of life and death. episodes.summaryAI: Trofim Lysenko and the imposition of pseudo-scientific genetics in the Soviet Union under Stalin and Khrushchev. Lysenko rejected Mendelian genetics and Darwinian natural selection in favour of Lamarckian inheritance of acquired characteristics, promising agricultural transformation. His doctrines became state-enforced orthodoxy; dissenting geneticists including Vavilov were imprisoned or executed. Devastated Soviet agriculture and biological sciences for decades. Damaged Soviet capacity in the Cold War scientific competition. A case study in ideological distortion of science, state control of research, and the consequences when political power overrides empirical evidence and scientific method.

episodes.title: Lysistrata
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Aristophanes' comedy in which the women of Athens and Sparta, led by Lysistrata, secure peace in the long-running war between them by staging a sex strike. To the men in the audience in 411BC, the idea that peace in the Peloponnesian War could be won so easily was ridiculous and the thought that their wives could have so much power over them was even more so. However Aristophanes' comedy also has the women seizing the treasure in the Acropolis that was meant to fund more fighting in an emergency, a fund the Athenians had recently had to draw on. They were in a perilous position and, much as they might laugh at Aristophanes' jokes, they knew there were real concerns about the actual cost of the war in terms of wealth and manpower.

episodes.summaryAI: Aristophanes' comedy Lysistrata, performed in Athens in 411 BC during the Peloponnesian War. Women of Athens and Sparta, led by Lysistrata, stage a sex strike and seize the treasury on the Acropolis to force men to negotiate peace. Combines bawdy humour with serious commentary on the costs of prolonged warfare in wealth and manpower. Reflects Athenian democratic culture, gender relations, and the role of comedy as political commentary in classical Greece. The play's reception history includes modern feminist and anti-war interpretations. Connects ancient theatre, Athenian politics, and Greek social structures.

episodes.title: Macbeth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of Shakespeare’s greatest tragedies. When three witches prophesy that Macbeth will be king one day, he is not prepared to wait and almost the next day he murders King Duncan as he sleeps, a guest at Macbeth’s castle. From there we explore their brutal world where few boundaries are distinct – between safe and unsafe, friend and foe, real and unreal, man and beast – until Macbeth too is slaughtered.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of Shakespeare’s greatest tragedies. When three witches prophesy that Macbeth will be king one day, he is not prepared to wait and almost the next day he murders King Duncan as he sleeps, a guest at Macbeth’s castle. From there we explore their brutal world where few boundaries are distinct – between safe and unsafe, friend and foe, real and unreal, man and beast – until Macbeth too is slaughtered.
episodes.summaryAI: Shakespeare's Macbeth, a tragedy probably written around 1606. Three witches prophesy Macbeth will become king of Scotland, prompting him and Lady Macbeth to murder King Duncan. Explores themes of ambition, tyranny, guilt, supernatural influence, and the dissolution of moral and perceptual boundaries between safety and danger, loyalty and betrayal, reality and illusion. Draws on Holinshed's Chronicles and Scottish history. Written during James I's reign, engaging contemporary interests in witchcraft, kingship, and the divine right of rulers. One of Shakespeare's shortest and most psychologically intense tragedies. Central to the English dramatic canon.

episodes.title: Machiavelli and the Italian City States
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the political philosopher Niccolo Machiavelli. In The Prince, Machiavelli's great manual of power, he wrote, "since men love as they themselves determine but fear as their ruler determines, a wise prince must rely upon what he and not others can control". He also advised, "One must be a fox in order to recognise traps, and a lion to frighten off wolves. Those who simply act like lions are stupid. So it follows that a prudent ruler cannot, and must not, honour his word when it places him at a disadvantage".What times was Machiavelli living through to take such a brutal perspective on power? How did he gain the experience to provide this advice to rulers? And was he really the amoral, or even evil figure that so many have liked to paint him?With Quentin Skinner, Regius Professor of History at the University of Cambridge; Evelyn Welch, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary, University of London; Lisa Jardine, Director of the Centre for Editing Lives and Letters at Queen Mary, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the political philosopher Niccolo Machiavelli. In The Prince, Machiavelli's great manual of power, he wrote, "since men love as they themselves determine but fear as their ruler determines, a wise prince must rely upon what he and not others can control". He also advised, "One must be a fox in order to recognise traps, and a lion to frighten off wolves. Those who simply act like lions are stupid. So it follows that a prudent ruler cannot, and must not, honour his word when it places him at a disadvantage".What times was Machiavelli living through to take such a brutal perspective on power? How did he gain the experience to provide this advice to rulers? And was he really the amoral, or even evil figure that so many have liked to paint him?With Quentin Skinner, Regius Professor of History at the University of Cambridge; Evelyn Welch, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary, University of London; Lisa Jardine, Director of the Centre for Editing Lives and Letters at Queen Mary, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: Niccolò Machiavelli (1469-1527) and the political world of the Italian city-states. The Prince, written 1513, analyses the acquisition and maintenance of political power through pragmatic, amoral statecraft. Advocates that rulers must combine the cunning of the fox with the strength of the lion. Context of Renaissance Florence, papal politics, French and Spanish invasions, and the fragmentation of Italian peninsula into competing republics and principalities. Machiavelli's experience as Florentine diplomat and secretary informed his political realism. Foundational text in political philosophy, separating politics from ethics and theology. Influenced modern theories of sovereignty and statecraft.

episodes.title: Macromolecules
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the giant molecules that form the basis of all life. Macromolecules, also known as polymers, are long chains of atoms. They form the proteins that make up our bodies, as well as many of the materials of modern life. Man's ability to mimic the structure of macromolecules has led to the invention of plastics such as nylon, paints and adhesives. Most of our clothes are made of macromolecules, and our food is macromolecular. The medical sciences are making increasingly sophisticated use of macromolecules, from growing replacement skin and bone to their increasing use in drug delivery. One of the most famous macromolecules is DNA, an infinitely more complex polymer than man has ever managed to produce. We've only known about macromolecules for just over a century, so what is the story behind them and how might they change our lives in the future
