episodes.summaryAI: Medical ethics addresses moral dilemmas arising from advancing biomedical technology. Questions include who decides allocation of scarce treatments, end-of-life care, and genetic privacy as insurance companies seek access to genomic data. Stem cell research creates tension between therapeutic potential and beliefs about embryonic life. Francis Bacon observed medicine progressing in circles rather than linearly. Landmark developments including penicillin, DNA sequencing, heart transplantation, and minimally invasive surgery each raised new ethical questions about consent, equity, human experimentation, and the boundaries between healing and enhancement.

episodes.title: Medieval Pilgrimage
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea and experience of Christian pilgrimage in Europe from the 12th to the 15th centuries, which figured so strongly in the imagination of the age. For those able and willing to travel, there were countless destinations from Jerusalem, Rome and Santiago de Compostela to the smaller local shrines associated with miracles and relics of the saints. Meanwhile, for those unable or not allowed to travel there were journeys of the mind, inspired by guidebooks that would tell the faithful how many steps they could take around their homes to replicate the walk to the main destinations in Rome and the Holy Land, passing paintings of the places on their route.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea and experience of Christian pilgrimage in Europe from the 12th to the 15th centuries, which figured so strongly in the imagination of the age. For those able and willing to travel, there were countless destinations from Jerusalem, Rome and Santiago de Compostela to the smaller local shrines associated with miracles and relics of the saints. Meanwhile, for those unable or not allowed to travel there were journeys of the mind, inspired by guidebooks that would tell the faithful how many steps they could take around their homes to replicate the walk to the main destinations in Rome and the Holy Land, passing paintings of the places on their route.
episodes.summaryAI: Christian pilgrimage in medieval Europe from the twelfth to fifteenth centuries encompassed journeys to major sacred sites including Jerusalem, Rome, and Santiago de Compostela, as well as countless local shrines housing relics. Pilgrimage served purposes of penance, healing, devotion, and spiritual merit. An elaborate infrastructure of routes, hostels, and guidebooks developed to support travelers. For those unable to undertake physical journeys, virtual pilgrimage emerged through devotional texts and mental exercises that replicated sacred walks within domestic settings, demonstrating how spiritual practice adapted to accommodate diverse circumstances and physical limitations.

episodes.title: Megaliths
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss megaliths - huge stones placed in the landscape, often visually striking and highly prominent.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss megaliths - huge stones placed in the landscape, often visually striking and highly prominent.
episodes.summaryAI: Megaliths are massive stones erected or arranged in the landscape by prehistoric peoples, often in visually striking and prominent positions. Found across Europe, Asia, and Africa, they include standing stones, stone circles, dolmens, and passage tombs. Their construction required significant communal labor and engineering knowledge, raising questions about the social organization of Neolithic and Bronze Age societies. Megaliths served diverse purposes including burial, astronomical observation, territorial marking, and ritual gathering. Their enduring presence in landscapes has inspired centuries of interpretation ranging from scientific archaeology to folklore and spiritual speculation.

episodes.title: Melisende, Queen of Jerusalem
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the most powerful woman in the Crusader states in the century after the First Crusade. Melisende (1105-61) was born and raised after the mainly Frankish crusaders had taken Jerusalem from the Fatimids, and her father was King of Jerusalem. She was married to Fulk from Anjou, on the understanding they would rule together, and for 30 years she vied with him and then their son as they struggled to consolidate their Frankish state in the Holy Land.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the most powerful woman in the Crusader states in the century after the First Crusade. Melisende (1105-61) was born and raised after the mainly Frankish crusaders had taken Jerusalem from the Fatimids, and her father was King of Jerusalem. She was married to Fulk from Anjou, on the understanding they would rule together, and for 30 years she vied with him and then their son as they struggled to consolidate their Frankish state in the Holy Land.
episodes.summaryAI: Melisende ruled as Queen of Jerusalem from 1131, becoming the most powerful woman in the Crusader states following the First Crusade. Born around 1105 after Frankish conquest of Jerusalem from the Fatimids, she married Fulk of Anjou and subsequently contested authority with both her husband and her son Baldwin III over three decades. She consolidated the Frankish state in the Holy Land through political alliances, architectural patronage, and administrative skill. Her reign demonstrates that women could exercise substantial political power within the patriarchal structures of crusader feudalism.

episodes.title: Melvyn Bragg meets Misha Glenny
episodes.synopsis: Before Misha Glenny's first edition on 15th January, BBC Radio 4's flagship news programme Today has brought Melvyn Bragg and Misha Glenny together so they can share their ideas about In Our Time's success and discuss what, if anything, will change with Misha. While Justin Webb chaired this discussion, here you will hear Melvyn introduce it and at the end he has a message for Misha and for listeners around the world.
episodes.description: Before Misha Glenny's first edition on 15th January, BBC Radio 4's flagship news programme Today has brought Melvyn Bragg and Misha Glenny together so they can share their ideas about In Our Time's success and discuss what, if anything, will change with Misha. While Justin Webb chaired this discussion, here you will hear Melvyn introduce it and at the end he has a message for Misha and for listeners around the world.
episodes.summaryAI: A brief discussion between Melvyn Bragg and journalist Misha Glenny broadcast on BBC Radio 4's Today programme before the first edition of In Our Time. They discuss expectations for the programme's format and ambitions, what subjects it might cover, and how it aims to bring academic discussion to a broad audience. The conversation reflects on intellectual broadcasting traditions and what would need to change or adapt. This short exchange predates the programme's long run and eventual milestone of over a thousand episodes.

episodes.title: Melvyn Bragg talks to Mishal Husain
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg talks to Mishal Husain about In Our Time on the occasion of the 1000th episode.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg talks to Mishal Husain about In Our Time on the occasion of the 1000th episode.
episodes.summaryAI: A conversation between Melvyn Bragg and BBC presenter Mishal Husain marking the thousandth episode of In Our Time on BBC Radio 4. They reflect on the programme's longevity, its approach to bringing academic expertise to general audiences across history, science, philosophy, religion, and culture. The discussion considers what has sustained the format over decades and how intellectual radio programming maintains relevance. This milestone interview serves as meta-commentary on the programme itself rather than engaging with a specific scholarly subject.

episodes.title: Memory
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the function and significance of memory. The great writer of remembrance, Marcel Proust, declared “We are able to find everything in our memory, which is like a dispensary or chemical laboratory in which chance steers our hand, sometimes to a soothing drug and sometimes to a dangerous poison”. The memory is vital to life and without it we could not be the people we are, but can it really contain the sum of all our experience? Is it a repository constantly mounting events waiting to be plucked to consciousness, or if not, then under what criteria are memories turfed out?With Martin Conway, Professor of Psychology at Durham University; Mike Kopelman, Professor of Neuropsychiatry at King's College London and St Thomas’ Hospital; Kim Graham, Senior Scientist at the Medical Research Council’s Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the function and significance of memory. The great writer of remembrance, Marcel Proust, declared “We are able to find everything in our memory, which is like a dispensary or chemical laboratory in which chance steers our hand, sometimes to a soothing drug and sometimes to a dangerous poison”. The memory is vital to life and without it we could not be the people we are, but can it really contain the sum of all our experience? Is it a repository constantly mounting events waiting to be plucked to consciousness, or if not, then under what criteria are memories turfed out?With Martin Conway, Professor of Psychology at Durham University; Mike Kopelman, Professor of Neuropsychiatry at King's College London and St Thomas’ Hospital; Kim Graham, Senior Scientist at the Medical Research Council’s Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit.
episodes.summaryAI: Memory encompasses the encoding, storage, and retrieval of experience, playing a central role in personal identity and cognition. Proust suggested everything resides in memory, raising questions about whether memory constitutes a complete repository of experience or selectively retains and discards according to emotional, contextual, and biological criteria. Psychology investigates short-term, long-term, episodic, semantic, and procedural memory systems. Neuropsychiatry examines brain structures including the hippocampus and amygdala that support memory formation. Disorders such as amnesia and conditions like false memory reveal how reconstructive and fallible memory processes are.

episodes.title: Memory and Culture
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss memory. At the start of the twentieth century Freud put memory at the centre of our psychology, and as the century has worn on what a nation remembers and what it should try to forget has become one of the binding political questions that modern societies face. As every second passes, humanity has a moment more to remember, and perhaps this fact alone goes a long way to explaining the ever changing role of memory, both in the mind of individuals and at the heart of the body politic. Memory, what to remember and when to forget, has personal and national implications. Whether we look to Chile, South Africa, Germany or Northern Ireland, these are all societies where the issue of memory is at the centre of the dilemmas and challenges they face. And in the mind of the individual too - as ever more forms of information crowd for space in our minds, and the image from someone else’s photograph can be more enduring than our own first hand experience of an event, can memory itself forever remain unchanged in its role within our psychology? Have our ways of remembering changed? Not in the sense neuro-biologists would explore the subject, but in its cultural and collective, as well as its individual, sense. “Memory is decidedly in fashion” writes Dr Nancy Wood, “whether attention is focused on the so-called return of repressed memories of the abused individual, or on the black holes in a nation’s recollection of its past. The topic of memory has become a compelling preoccupation”. With Professor Malcolm Bowie, Marshall Foch Professor of French Literature at Oxford University and Director of Oxford’s European Humanities Research Centre; Dr Nancy Wood, Chair of Media Studies, University of Sussex and author of Vectors of Memory.

episodes.summaryAI: Collective and individual memory as psychological, cultural, and political forces. Freud positioned memory as central to psychoanalysis and the unconscious. Nations confronting traumatic pasts—Chile under Pinochet, post-apartheid South Africa, post-war Germany, Northern Ireland's Troubles—face contested decisions about what to remember, memorialize, or suppress. Cultural practices of remembrance shift over time through oral tradition, written record, monuments, and institutional archives. Intersects political identity, trauma, reconciliation, justice, and historiography. Connects psychology of memory to sociology of collective identity and the politics of national narrative.

episodes.title: Mercantilism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, between the 16th and 18th centuries, Europe was dominated by an economic way of thinking called mercantilism. The key idea was that exports should be as high as possible and imports minimised.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, between the 16th and 18th centuries, Europe was dominated by an economic way of thinking called mercantilism. The key idea was that exports should be as high as possible and imports minimised.
episodes.summaryAI: Mercantilism dominated European economic thought from the sixteenth through eighteenth centuries, holding that national wealth derived from maximizing exports while minimizing imports to accumulate bullion and precious metals. States pursued protectionist tariffs, colonial monopolies, and regulated trade to maintain favorable balances. Mercantilist policies shaped European colonialism, chartered trading companies, and state intervention in commerce. Challenged by Adam Smith's free-trade arguments in The Wealth of Nations. Connects early modern statecraft, imperial expansion, monetary theory, and the intellectual origins of classical and modern economics.

episodes.title: Mercury
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the planet which is closest to our Sun. We see it as an evening or a morning star, close to where the Sun has just set or is about to rise, and observations of Mercury helped Copernicus understand that Earth and the other planets orbit the Sun, so displacing Earth from the centre of our system. In the 20th century, further observations of Mercury helped Einstein prove his general theory of relativity. For the last 50 years we have been sending missions there to reveal something of Mercury's secrets and how those relate to the wider universe, and he latest, BepiColombo, is out there in space now.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the planet which is closest to our Sun. We see it as an evening or a morning star, close to where the Sun has just set or is about to rise, and observations of Mercury helped Copernicus understand that Earth and the other planets orbit the Sun, so displacing Earth from the centre of our system. In the 20th century, further observations of Mercury helped Einstein prove his general theory of relativity. For the last 50 years we have been sending missions there to reveal something of Mercury's secrets and how those relate to the wider universe, and he latest, BepiColombo, is out there in space now.
episodes.summaryAI: Mercury, the smallest and innermost planet in the Solar System, orbits closest to the Sun with extreme temperature variations and virtually no atmosphere. Observations of Mercury's transit helped Copernicus develop heliocentric theory. The anomalous precession of Mercury's orbit provided key evidence confirming Einstein's general relativity. Space missions including Mariner 10, MESSENGER, and the ongoing ESA-JAXA BepiColombo mission have mapped its heavily cratered surface, thin exosphere, and unexpectedly large iron core. Connects planetary science, gravitational theory, and the history of astronomical observation.

episodes.title: Merlin
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the legendary wizard Merlin. He was sired by an incubus and born of a virgin; he was a prophet, a shape-shifter, a king-maker and a mad man of the woods. Before Gandalf there was Merlin: the power behind Arthur and a literary sensation for centuries. In a literary career spanning 1500 years, Merlin, or originally Myrddin, put the sword in the stone, built Stonehenge, knew the truth behind the Holy Grail and discovered the Elixir of Life. "Beware Merlin for he knows all things by the devil's craft" say the poisoners in Malory's Morte D'Arthur; but he is also on the side of the good and is almost Christ-like in some of the versions of his tale, and his prophesies were pored over by the medieval Church. Who was Merlinus Ambrosius, as he is sometimes known? Where does his legend spring from and how has it been appropriated and adapted over time?With Juliette Wood, Associate Lecturer in the Department of Welsh at Cardiff University, Stephen Knight, Distinguished Research Professor in English Literature at Cardiff University, Peter Forshaw, Lecturer in Renaissance Philosophies at Birkbeck, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the legendary wizard Merlin. He was sired by an incubus and born of a virgin; he was a prophet, a shape-shifter, a king-maker and a mad man of the woods. Before Gandalf there was Merlin: the power behind Arthur and a literary sensation for centuries. episodes.summaryAI: Merlin, the legendary wizard of Arthurian tradition, spans fifteen hundred years of literary history from Geoffrey of Monmouth through Malory to modern fantasy. Born of a virgin and an incubus, he serves as prophet, shape-shifter, and kingmaker who engineers Arthur's rise. Associated with placing the sword in the stone and magically constructing Stonehenge. The medieval Church appropriated his prophetic powers for Christian purposes. His character blends Celtic bardic tradition, classical seer figures, and the wild-man-of-the-woods archetype, reflecting changing cultural attitudes toward magic, prophecy, and authority.

episodes.title: Metamorphosis
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman poet Ovid and explore the theme of metamorphosis from the transformation of Narcissus to the bug of Kafka’s story, and beyond. Ovid wrote at the beginning of The Metamorphoses - in Ted Hughes’ wonderful version - “now I am ready to tell how bodies are changed/Into different bodies/I summon the supernatural beings/who first contrived/The transmogrifications/In the stuff of life./You did it for your own amusement./Descend again, be pleased to reanimate/this revival of those marvels.”And descend they did: The metamorphoses is an extraordinarily wide sweep through the teeming, changing world of Roman and Greek mythology.The tales were immensely popular in their own day, they were an inspiration to Chaucer, Ovid was Shakespeare’s favourite poet, and two thousand years after they were written the stories of shape changing still seem relevant: Ted Hughes won the Whitbread Book of the Year with his translation of Tales from Ovid in 1997, and a new collection called Ovid Metamorphosed has garnered versions of the tales from authors and poets from all over the world.With A S Byatt, novelist and one of the contributors to Ovid Metamorphosed; Dr Catherine Bates, critic and Research Fellow, University of Warwick.

episodes.summaryAI: Ovid's Metamorphoses, the sweeping Latin epic poem composed around 8 CE, recounts tales of transformation drawn from Greek and Roman mythology. Its narratives of gods, mortals, and shape-changing influenced Western literature for two millennia. Chaucer drew on Ovid extensively; Shakespeare regarded him as a favorite source. Ted Hughes's Tales from Ovid won the Whitbread Prize in 1997. The poem's central theme of metamorphosis—physical, psychological, spiritual—resonates across poetry, drama, painting, and opera, making it one of the most enduring works of classical literature.

episodes.title: Meteorology
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss meteorology. The Book of Genesis resounds with a terrible act of vengeance, carried out by an angry God seeking to punish his people. And the mechanism with which this is carried out - a catastrophic flood which wipes out evil on earth. In fact, many ancient civilisations believed extreme meteorological phenomena like thunder and lightning, hailstones and even meteors were acts of divine intervention. Running parallel with this belief, however, was also a desire to understand and explain the natural world through rational enquiry and observation. This complex relationship – between the natural world and divinity – has fascinated philosophers, artists and scientists alike from antiquity to our own time. Aristotle, for example, coined the phrase meteorology but to what extent did he link meteorological events to the cosmos and the Gods? How did the development of instrumentation during the Renaissance aid the prediction of weather events? Why did 18th century writers such as Keats feel that these scientific advances stripped the skies of its mystique and romance? And why does meteorology continue to fascinate and mystify to this day? With Vladimir Jankovic, Wellcome Research Lecturer at the Centre for the History of Science, Technology and Medicine at Manchester University;Richard Hamblyn, writer; Liba Taub, Director of the Whipple Museum of the History of Science at Cambridge University.

episodes.summaryAI: Meteorology traces from ancient attempts to understand weather through divine causation to modern scientific forecasting. Aristotle coined the term in his Meteorologica, classifying atmospheric phenomena. Biblical traditions linked storms and floods to divine will. Renaissance-era invention of the barometer, thermometer, and hygrometer enabled systematic observation and prediction. Keats lamented that science unraveled the poetry of the skies. Modern meteorology integrates thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, satellite observation, and computational modeling. Connects natural philosophy, instrumentation history, atmospheric science, and cultural responses to weather and climate.

episodes.title: Michael Faraday
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the eminent 19th-century scientist Michael Faraday. Born into a poor working-class family, he received little formal schooling but became interested in science while working as a bookbinder's apprentice. He is celebrated today for carrying out pioneering research into the relationship between electricity and magnetism. Faraday showed that if a wire was turned in the presence of a magnet or a magnet was turned in relation to a wire, an electric current was generated. This ground-breaking discovery led to the development of the electric generator and ultimately to modern power stations. During his life he became the most famous scientist in Britain and he played a key role in founding the Royal Institution's Christmas lectures which continue today.

episodes.summaryAI: Michael Faraday, born into poverty in early nineteenth-century England, became Britain's most celebrated scientist through self-education and experimental genius. His discovery that a wire moving near a magnet generates electric current established electromagnetic induction, the principle underlying electric generators, transformers, and modern power stations. He also discovered benzene, established laws of electrolysis, and introduced field theory concepts later formalized by Maxwell. Founded the Royal Institution Christmas Lectures for public science education. Connects electromagnetism, industrial revolution, social mobility, and the democratization of scientific knowledge.

episodes.title: Microbiology
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of microbiology. We have more microbes in our bodies than we have human cells. We fear them as the cause of disease, yet are reliant on them for processes as diverse as water purification, pharmaceuticals, bread-making and brewing. In the future, we may look to them to save the planet from environmental hazards as scientists exploit their ability to clean up pollution. For microbes are the great recyclers on the earth, processing everything – plants, animals and us. Without microbes life would grind to a halt. How did we first discover these invisible masters of the universe? The development of microscopes in the 17th Century played a key part, but for a while science seemed stuck in this purely observational role. It is only when Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch began to manipulate microbes in the lab two hundred years later that stunning advances were made. These breakthroughs led to an understanding of how microbes transform matter, spread disease and also prevent it with the development of antibiotics and vaccines.With John Dupré, Professor of Philosophy of Science at Exeter University; Anne Glover, Professor of Molecular and Cell Biology at Aberdeen University; and Andrew Mendelsohn, Senior Lecturer in the History of Science and Medicine at Imperial College, University of London

episodes.summaryAI: Microbiology studies organisms invisible to the naked eye—bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and archaea—that outnumber human cells in the body. Pasteur and Koch pioneered germ theory, enabling breakthroughs in antibiotics, vaccines, and sterilization. Microbes cause infectious disease yet are indispensable for water purification, pharmaceutical production, bread-making, brewing, and decomposition that recycles nutrients through ecosystems. Modern microbiology spans genetics, immunology, biotechnology, and environmental science. Connects molecular biology, public health, industrial processes, and ecological cycles fundamental to sustaining life on Earth.

episodes.title: Middlemarch
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss what Virginia Woolf called 'one of the few English novels written for grown-up people'. It was written by George Eliot, the pen name of Mary Anne Evans (1819-80), published in 8 parts in 1871-72, and was originally two separate stories which became woven together. One, 'Middlemarch', focused on a doctor, Tertius Lydgate and the other, 'Miss Brooke', on Dorothea Brooke who became the central figure in the finished work. The events are set in a small town in the Midlands, surrounded by farmland, leading up to the Reform Act 1832, and the novel explores the potential to change in matters of religion, social status, marriage and politics, and is particularly concerned with the opportunities available to women to lead fulfilling lives.

episodes.summaryAI: Middlemarch, George Eliot's 1871-72 novel, portrays a small Midlands town during the years preceding the Reform Act of 1832. Originally conceived as two separate stories, it weaves together narratives exploring religion, social hierarchy, marriage, politics, and women's constrained opportunities. Characters include the idealistic Dorothea Brooke and the ambitious physician Lydgate. Virginia Woolf called it one of the few English novels written for grown-up people. The novel's psychological realism, social breadth, and moral complexity established it as a landmark of Victorian fiction and the realist tradition.

episodes.title: Mill
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the great nineteenth century political philosopher John Stuart Mill. He believed that, 'The true philosophy is the marriage of poetry and logic'. He was one of the first thinkers to argue that a social theory must engage with ideas of culture and the internal life. He used Wordsworth to inform his social theory, he was a proto feminist and his treatise On Liberty is one of the sacred texts of liberalism. J S Mill believed that action was the natural articulation of thought. He battled throughout his life for social reform and individual freedom and was hugely influential in the extension of the vote. Few modern discussions on race, birth control, the state and human rights have not been influenced by Mill's theories. How did Mill's utilitarian background shape his political ideas? Why did he think Romantic literature was significant to the rational structure of society? On what grounds did he argue for women's equality? And how did his notions of the individual become central to modern social theory? With A C Grayling, Professor of Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London; Janet Radcliffe Richards, Reader in Bioethics at University College London; Alan Ryan, Professor of Politics at Oxford University.

episodes.summaryAI: John Stuart Mill, nineteenth-century British philosopher and political economist, authored On Liberty, a foundational text of liberal thought defending individual freedom against tyrannical majorities. A proto-feminist influenced by Harriet Taylor, he advocated women's suffrage and wrote The Subjection of Women. Mill's utilitarianism refined Bentham's framework. He drew on Wordsworth's poetry to balance rationalism with emotional depth. Campaigned for social reform, extension of the vote, birth control, and racial equality. His work on liberty, representative government, and human rights remains central to political philosophy.

episodes.title: Milton
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the literary and political career of the poet John Milton. If it wasn't for the poet Andrew Marvell we wouldn't have his later works; Paradise Lost, Paradise Regained and Samson Agonistes. Milton spent the English Civil Wars as a prominent politician and right hand man to Oliver Cromwell. When the Monarchy was restored in 1660 it was only Marvell's intervention that saved Milton from execution. By then, Marvell argued, Milton was old and blind and posed no threat to Charles II. But as a young man Milton had been an activist and pamphleteer extraordinaire. Allegedly inspired by a meeting with Galileo he wrote in passionate defence of Liberty. He detested the Church's insistence on empty ritual. And most dramatically for his time he demanded that the state serve its people rather than the people serve the state. How then should we remember Milton - as poet or politician - as an idealist or an apologist for a revolutionary yet intolerant regime? And was he a man at one with the people or an elitist who preached to the masses but lived his own life only in the most rarefied of circles? With John Carey, Emeritus Professor of English Literature at Oxford University; Lisa Jardine, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary College, University of London and Honorary Fellow of King's College Cambridge; Blair Worden, Professor of Early Modern History at the University of Sussex.

episodes.summaryAI: John Milton, seventeenth-century English poet and political thinker, served as Oliver Cromwell's Secretary for Foreign Tongues during the Commonwealth. His epic Paradise Lost reimagines the Fall of Man with theological depth and literary grandeur; Paradise Regained and Samson Agonistes followed. Milton's prose championed free speech in Areopagitica and argued that the state must serve its people. Allegedly inspired by meeting the aged Galileo in Florence. Saved from execution at the Restoration through Andrew Marvell's intervention. A towering figure connecting English literature, republican politics, and Protestant thought.

episodes.title: Miracles
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the parting of the Red Sea, the feeding of the five thousand and the general subject of miracles. Miracles have been part of human culture for thousands of years. From St Augustine in the 4th century through the medieval cult of saints to David Hume in the 18th, miracles have captured the imaginations of believers and sceptics alike. The way they have been celebrated, interpreted, dissected and refuted is a whole history of arguments between philosophy, science and religion. They have also been used by the corrupt and the powerful to gain their perverse ends. Miracles have been derided and proved to be fraudulent and yet, for many, the miraculous maintain a grip on our imagination, our language and our belief to this day. With Martin Palmer, Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture;Janet Soskice, Reader in Philosophical Theology at Cambridge University; Justin Champion, Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London.

episodes.summaryAI: Miracles as reported supernatural events have provoked philosophical, theological, and scientific debate for millennia. Biblical accounts—parting the Red Sea, feeding the five thousand—established miracles as divine signs. Augustine formulated influential theological defenses in the fourth century. Medieval saints' cults used miracles to legitimize authority and attract pilgrimage. Hume's eighteenth-century empiricist critique argued testimony could never sufficiently establish violations of natural law. Miracles have been exploited by the corrupt and powerful yet retain imaginative and spiritual power. Connects religion, epistemology, institutional authority, and popular belief.

episodes.title: Mitochondria
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the power-packs within cells in all complex life on Earth.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the power-packs within cells in all complex life on Earth.
episodes.summaryAI: Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles present in virtually all eukaryotic cells, functioning as the primary sites of aerobic respiration and ATP production that powers cellular processes. They possess their own circular DNA, supporting the endosymbiotic theory that they originated as free-living bacteria engulfed by ancestral cells over a billion years ago. Mitochondria are maternally inherited and central to studies of human evolutionary lineage. Dysfunction is linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging. They connect molecular biology, cell energetics, evolutionary theory, genetics, and biomedical research into metabolic disorders.

episodes.title: Moby Dick
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Herman Melville's (1819-1891) epic novel, published in London in 1851, the story of Captain Ahab's pursuit of a great white sperm whale that had bitten off his leg. He risks his own life and that of his crew on the Pequod, single-mindedly seeking his revenge, his story narrated by Ishmael who was taking part in a whaling expedition for the first time. This is one of the c1000 ideas which listeners sent in this autumn for our fourth Listener Week, following Kafka's The Trial in 2014, Captain Cook in 2015 and Garibaldi and the Risorgimento in 2016.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Herman Melville's (1819-1891) epic novel, published in London in 1851, the story of Captain Ahab's pursuit of a great white sperm whale that had bitten off his leg. He risks his own life and that of his crew on the Pequod, single-mindedly seeking his revenge, his story narrated by Ishmael who was taking part in a whaling expedition for the first time. episodes.summaryAI: Moby-Dick, Herman Melville's 1851 novel, follows narrator Ishmael aboard the whaling ship Pequod under Captain Ahab, who obsessively pursues the great white sperm whale that severed his leg. The novel interweaves cetology, maritime adventure, philosophical meditation, and Shakespearean rhetoric. A commercial failure in Melville's lifetime, it was rediscovered in the twentieth century as a masterpiece of American literature. Themes include obsession, nature's indifference, fate, and the limits of human knowledge. Connects American Romanticism, the whaling industry, allegorical fiction, and the development of the novel form.

episodes.title: Modern Culture
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests debate the state of Modern Culture in the 20th century. Culture used to be a word we mocked, a concept too foreign for the stout empiricists of Britain, a species of foreign flummery. Now it is all around us. We have a ministry for it and a newspaper section boldly called Culture.Is contemporary culture evidence of a moral and aesthetic decline in our civilisation this century? Or does it show a society richer and more diverse than it has ever been?Will Self is one of the best known writers of his generation - celebrated as much for his erudite vocabulary as the shocking subject matter of some of his novels and short stories. Roger Scruton is one of the most celebrated philosophers of our age - known as much for his right -wing politics as for the passion with which he defends the past as a way of looking at the future. His latest book is An Intelligent Person’s Guide to Modern Culture, and he prefaces it by saying: “You don’t have to be familiar with the entire canon of western literature and full range of artistic masterpieces but you should have some familiarity with TS Eliot, Baudelaire, Mozart, Wagner, Monet, Poussin, Tennyson, Schoenberg, George Herbert, Goethe, Nietsche, and Marx.”With Will Self, writer and novelist; Roger Scruton, novelist, philosopher and Former Professor at Birkbeck College, London.

Roger Scruton is one of the most celebrated philosophers of our age - known as much for his right -wing politics as for the passion with which he defends the past as a way of looking at the future. His latest book is An Intelligent Person’s Guide to Modern Culture, and he prefaces it by saying: “You don’t have to be familiar with the entire canon of western literature and full range of artistic masterpieces but you should have some familiarity with TS Eliot, Baudelaire, Mozart, Wagner, Monet, Poussin, Tennyson, Schoenberg, George Herbert, Goethe, Nietsche, and Marx.”With Will Self, writer and novelist; Roger Scruton, novelist, philosopher and Former Professor at Birkbeck College, London.
episodes.summaryAI: Debate over whether contemporary culture represents moral and aesthetic decline or unprecedented richness and accessibility. Roger Scruton argues genuine cultural literacy requires familiarity with canonical figures—Eliot, Baudelaire, Mozart, Wagner—while Will Self challenges such hierarchies. Tensions between high culture and popular culture, tradition and innovation, elite gatekeeping and democratic participation. Raises questions about standards of taste, the role of mass media, cultural relativism, and whether modernity erodes or enriches artistic and intellectual life. Connects aesthetics, cultural criticism, philosophy of art, and sociology of knowledge.

episodes.title: Modernist Utopias
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the mad, bad world of modern utopias. "I want to gather together about twenty souls," wrote D H Lawrence in 1915, "and sail away from this world of war and squalor and find a little colony where there shall be no money but a sort of communism as necessaries of life go, and some real decency". Utopias were in the air in the first decades of the 20th century and the literature of the period abounds with worlds of imagined escape, feminist utopias, technological nightmares and rich imaginings of the world as it could or should become. Many of the societies that writers like H G Wells created were meant seriously, as signposts to a future that would seem horrific to us now, where the weak are eradicated and the strong prosper and procreate.What was it about that era that brought forward so many imagined futures? How did utopias become the dystopias of Brave New World and 1984, and why are writers so much less likely to create a Utopia now?With John Carey, Emeritus Professor of English Literature, Oxford University and editor of The Faber Book of Utopias; Steve Connor, Professor of Modern Literature at Birkbeck, University of London; Laura Marcus, Professor of English, University of Sussex.

"I want to gather together about twenty souls," wrote D H Lawrence in 1915, "and sail away from this world of war and squalor and find a little colony where there shall be no money but a sort of communism as necessaries of life go, and some real decency". Utopias were in the air in the first decades of the 20th century and the literature of the period abounds with worlds of imagined escape, feminist utopias, technological nightmares and rich imaginings of the world as it could or should become. Many of the societies that writers like H G Wells created were meant seriously, as signposts to a future that would seem horrific to us now, where the weak are eradicated and the strong prosper and procreate.What was it about that era that brought forward so many imagined futures? How did utopias become the dystopias of Brave New World and 1984, and why are writers so much less likely to create a Utopia now?With John Carey, Emeritus Professor of English Literature, Oxford University and editor of The Faber Book of Utopias; Steve Connor, Professor of Modern Literature at Birkbeck, University of London; Laura Marcus, Professor of English, University of Sussex.
episodes.summaryAI: Utopian and dystopian literary visions in early twentieth-century modernism. D.H. Lawrence sought to establish an ideal community abroad; H.G. Wells imagined technologically transformed futures. The optimistic utopian impulse gave way to dystopian warnings in Aldous Huxley's Brave New World and George Orwell's 1984, reflecting disillusionment with totalitarianism, industrialization, and social engineering. Explores why contemporary writers are less inclined to construct utopias. Connects political philosophy, literary imagination, modernist culture, and the tension between idealism and the twentieth century's experience of authoritarian regimes.

episodes.title: Molière
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the great figures in world literature. The French playwright Molière (1622-1673) began as an actor, aiming to be a tragedian, but he was stronger in comedy, touring with a troupe for 13 years until Louis XIV summoned him to audition at the Louvre and gave him his break. It was in Paris and at Versailles that Molière wrote and performed his best known plays, among them Tartuffe, Le Misanthrope and Le Malade Imaginaire, and in time he was so celebrated that French became known as The Language of Molière.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the great figures in world literature. The French playwright Molière (1622-1673) began as an actor, aiming to be a tragedian, but he was stronger in comedy, touring with a troupe for 13 years until Louis XIV summoned him to audition at the Louvre and gave him his break. It was in Paris and at Versailles that Molière wrote and performed his best known plays, among them Tartuffe, Le Misanthrope and Le Malade Imaginaire, and in time he was so celebrated that French became known as The Language of Molière.
episodes.summaryAI: Molière, born Jean-Baptiste Poquelin in 1622, became France's greatest comic playwright. Initially pursuing tragedy as an actor, he discovered his strength in comedy. Louis XIV's patronage gave him a permanent stage. Major works include Tartuffe, satirizing religious hypocrisy; Le Misanthrope, examining social falseness; and Le Malade Imaginaire, mocking medical charlatanism—during whose performance he collapsed and died in 1673. French became known as the language of Molière. His plays blend farce, social satire, and moral insight, shaping European comic drama and theatrical tradition.

episodes.title: Monet in England
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the work of the great French Impressionist Claude Monet (1840-1926) in London, initially in 1870 and then from 1899. He spent his first visit in poverty, escaping from war in France, while by the second he had become so commercially successful that he stayed at the Savoy Hotel. There, from his balcony, he began a series of almost a hundred paintings that captured the essence of this dynamic city at that time, with fog and smoke almost obscuring the bridges, boats and Houses of Parliament. The pollution was terrible for health but the diffraction through the sooty droplets offered an ever-changing light that captivated Monet, and he was to paint the Thames more than he did his water lilies or haystacks or Rouen Cathedral. On his return to France, Monet appeared to have a new confidence to explore an art that was more abstract than impressionist.

episodes.summaryAI: Claude Monet's extensive engagement with London produced nearly one hundred paintings, rivaling his water lilies and haystacks in volume. First visiting in 1870 as a refugee from the Franco-Prussian War, he returned repeatedly from 1899, painting Thames views from the Savoy Hotel. London's fog and industrial pollution created ever-shifting atmospheric light that captivated his Impressionist eye. The series pushed his work toward greater abstraction and dissolution of form. Connects Impressionism, urban-industrial landscape, Franco-British cultural exchange, and the evolution from representational to increasingly abstract painting.

episodes.title: Money
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the power of Money. In the Bible the Old Testament and the New Testament appear to agree about the power of money: Ecclesiastes says “Money answereth all things” and Timothy says “The love of money is the root of all evil”. It is a theme that seems to echo down the centuries with seemingly everyone from Karl Marx to the cast of Cabaret crying out “Money makes the world go around”. But are economic factors really the invisible hand behind all historical events? Can everything in the end be brought down to the influence of money? With Niall Ferguson, Professor of Political and Financial History at the University of Oxford; Richard J Evans, Professor of Modern History at the University of Cambridge; Jane Humphries, reader in Economic History at Oxford University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the power of Money. episodes.summaryAI: The cultural, moral, and historical power of money examined through literary and philosophical sources. Ecclesiastes declares money answers all things; Timothy warns that love of money is the root of all evil. Marx analyzed money as embodying alienated labor and commodity fetishism. The question whether economic forces constitute the invisible hand driving all historical events connects political economy, moral philosophy, and historiography. From ancient coinage to modern capitalism, money functions as medium of exchange, store of value, symbol of power, and object of ethical scrutiny across civilizations.

episodes.title: Montaigne
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Essays of Michel de Montaigne. Born near Bordeaux in 1533, Montaigne retired from a life of public service aged 38 and began to write. He called these short works 'essais', or 'attempts'; they deal with an eclectic range of subjects, from the dauntingly weighty to the apparently trivial. Although he never considered himself a philosopher, he is often now seen as one of the most outstanding Sceptical thinkers of early modern Europe. His approachable style, intelligence and subtle thought have made him one of the most widely admired writers of the Renaissance.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Essays of Michel de Montaigne. Born near Bordeaux in 1533, Montaigne retired from a life of public service aged 38 and began to write. He called these short works 'essais', or 'attempts'; they deal with an eclectic range of subjects, from the dauntingly weighty to the apparently trivial. episodes.summaryAI: Michel de Montaigne, sixteenth-century French writer, retired from public service at thirty-eight to his tower library and invented the personal essay as a literary form. His Essais, begun around 1572, ranged across eclectic subjects—cannibals, education, friendship, death—with candid self-examination and intellectual curiosity. Recognized as early modern Europe's outstanding skeptical thinker, he questioned dogma, custom, and certainty. His approachable style, subtle reasoning, and tolerance influenced Pascal, Descartes, Shakespeare, and Enlightenment thought. Connects Renaissance humanism, skeptical philosophy, literary innovation, and the history of ideas.

episodes.title: Montesquieu
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas of Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de La Brède et de Montesquieu (1689-1755) whose works on liberty, monarchism, despotism, republicanism and the separation of powers were devoured by intellectuals across Europe and New England in the eighteenth century, transforming political philosophy and influencing the American Constitution. He argued that an individual's liberty needed protection from the arm of power, checking that by another power; where judicial, executive and legislative power were concentrated in the hands of one figure, there could be no personal liberty.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas of Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de La Brède et de Montesquieu (1689-1755) whose works on liberty, monarchism, despotism, republicanism and the separation of powers were devoured by intellectuals across Europe and New England in the eighteenth century, transforming political philosophy and influencing the American Constitution. He argued that an individual's liberty needed protection from the arm of power, checking that by another power; where judicial, executive and legislative power were concentrated in the hands of one figure, there could be no personal liberty.
episodes.summaryAI: Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu (1689-1755), French Enlightenment political philosopher. His theory of separation of powers into judicial, executive, and legislative branches as essential protection for individual liberty against concentrated authority. Analysis of government types including monarchy, despotism, and republicanism. Major influence on the American Constitution and modern constitutional design. His empirical comparative method distinguished his approach from earlier abstract political philosophy. Connects Enlightenment thought, constitutional theory, and the intellectual origins of modern democratic governance.

episodes.title: Moses Mendelssohn
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the work and influence of the eighteenth-century philosopher Moses Mendelssohn. A prominent figure at the court of Frederick the Great, Mendelssohn was one of the most significant thinkers of his age. He came from a humble, but culturally rich background and his obvious intelligence was recognised from a young age and nurtured by the local rabbi where he lived in the town of Dessau in Prussia. Moses's learning earned him the sobriquet of the 'German Socrates' and he is considered to be one of the principal architects of the Haskala, the Jewish Enlightenment, and widely regarded as having helped bring Judaism into the mainstream of European culture. Mendelssohn is perhaps best remembered today for his efforts to bring Jewish and German culture closer together and for his plea for religious toleration

episodes.summaryAI: Moses Mendelssohn (1729-1786), the 18th-century German-Jewish philosopher known as the German Socrates at the court of Frederick the Great. Principal architect of the Haskala, the Jewish Enlightenment, which brought Judaism into dialogue with mainstream European intellectual culture. His plea for religious toleration and the coexistence of reason with faith. Connects Enlightenment universalism with questions of Jewish identity, assimilation, and cultural preservation in early modern Europe. Influenced subsequent debates about Jewish emancipation and the relationship between religious tradition and modernity.

episodes.title: Mrs Dalloway
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Virginia Woolf's novel Mrs Dalloway. First published in 1925, it charts a single day in the life of Clarissa Dalloway, a prosperous member of London society, as she prepares to throw a party. Writing in her diary during the writing of the book, Woolf explained what she had set out to do: 'I want to give life and death, sanity and insanity. I want to criticize the social system, and to show it at work at its most intense.' Celebrated for its innovative narrative technique and distillation of many of the preoccupations of 1920s Britain, Mrs Dalloway is now seen as a landmark of twentieth-century fiction, and one of the finest products of literary modernism.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Virginia Woolf's novel Mrs Dalloway. First published in 1925, it charts a single day in the life of Clarissa Dalloway, a prosperous member of London society, as she prepares to throw a party. I want to criticize the social system, and to show it at work at its most intense.' Celebrated for its innovative narrative technique and distillation of many of the preoccupations of 1920s Britain, Mrs Dalloway is now seen as a landmark of twentieth-century fiction, and one of the finest products of literary modernism.
episodes.summaryAI: Virginia Woolf's 1925 novel Mrs Dalloway, a landmark of literary modernism charting a single June day in London as Clarissa Dalloway prepares to host an evening party. Woolf's innovative stream-of-consciousness narrative technique interweaves themes of life and death, sanity and insanity through the parallel stories of Clarissa and shell-shocked veteran Septimus Smith. The novel critiques the English social system, class hierarchy, and post-World War I disillusionment. Connects modernist literary experimentation, psychological realism, and the social history of 1920s Britain.

episodes.title: Multiculturalism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss multiculturalism. The divisions between people provoked and exploited because of differences in religion, culture, nationality and race seem to beset the planet the more information technology promises globalisation. A recent estimate put the figure of people living in a country other than the one of their birth at 80 million. Does this mean that, amongst these eighty million people, their country of origin, their sense of self, and their cultural history are no longer as significant as they were? And how are those eighty million people and their descendants accommodated in the country to which they have moved - do their lives exemplify the success of multicultural policies or are they subject to racism? Is it possible to define how attitudes to race and identity have changed this century, given its vast shifts of population, cultures and peoples?With Stuart Hall, former Professor of Sociology, Open University and currently on a Commission set up by the Runnymede Trust looking at the future of multi-ethnic Britain; Dr Avtar Brah, Senior Lecturer in Sociology, Birkbeck College, London University.

episodes.summaryAI: Multiculturalism as policy and lived reality, noting approximately 80 million people worldwide live outside their country of birth. Whether multicultural policies succeed in fostering integration or leave minority populations subject to racism and marginalization. Changing attitudes toward race, ethnicity, and identity across different societies. How globalisation reshapes cultural identity, challenges national cohesion, and complicates debates about immigration, belonging, and social inclusion. Connects political theory, sociology, cultural studies, and the ethics of diversity in modern democratic societies.

episodes.title: Munch and The Scream
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests David Jackson, Dorothy Rowe and Alastair Wright discuss the work of the Norwegian artist Edvard Munch, focusing on his most famous painting, The Scream.First exhibited in 1893 in Berlin, The Scream was the culmination of Munch's magnum opus, a series of paintings called The Frieze of Life. This depicted the course of human existence through burgeoning love and sexual passion to suffering, despair and death, in Munch's highly original, proto-expressionist style. His titles, from Death in the Sickroom, through Madonna to The Vampire, suggest just how directly and unironically he sought to depict the anxieties of late-19th century Europe.But against all Munch's images, it is The Scream which stands out as the work which has seared itself into the Western imagination. It remains widely celebrated for capturing the torment of existence in what appeared to many in Munch's time to be a frightening, godless world.Munch himself endured a childhood beset by illness, madness and bereavement. At 13, he was told by his father that his tuberculosis was fatal. But he survived and went on to become a major figure first in the Norwegian, then the European, avant-garde. He became involved with two of the great playwrights of the period. He collaborated with his fellow countryman Henrik Ibsen and became a close friend of the tempestuous Swede August Strindberg. He admired the work of Post-Impressionist painters such as Paul Gauguin and Vincent van Gogh and the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche, all of whom influenced his art. Munch's own influence resonated through the 20th century, from German Expressionism to Andy Warhol and beyond. His work, particularly The Scream, remains powerful today.David Jackson is Professor of Russian and Scandinavian Art Histories at the University of Leeds; Dorothy Rowe is Senior Lecturer in the History of Art at the University of Bristol; Alastair Wright is University Lecturer in the History of Art at St John's College, University of Oxford.

episodes.summaryAI: Norwegian artist Edvard Munch (1863-1944) and his iconic painting The Scream, first exhibited in Berlin in 1893 as the culmination of his Frieze of Life series. Childhood marked by illness and bereavement shaped his art. Proto-expressionist approach captured existential anguish in a godless modern world. Influence from German Expressionism through to Andy Warhol and contemporary art, establishing him as a pivotal figure between Symbolism and modern expressionism. Connects late 19th-century European art, psychological self-expression, and the development of expressionist and avant-garde movements.

episodes.title: Muslim Spain
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Muslim Spain. In 711 a small army of North African Berbers invaded Spain and established an Iberian Islamic culture that would last for over 700 years. Despite periods of infighting and persecution, Muslim Spain was a land where Muslims, Jews and Christians co-existed in relative peace and harmony. Its capital, Cordoba, although not unique amongst Spanish cities, became the centre and focus for generations of revered and respected philosophers, physicians and scholars. By the 10th century Cordoba was one of the largest cities in the world. But what some historians refer to as Cordoba’s Golden Age came to an end in the 11th century, when the society was destabilised by new threats from Africa to the South and Christendom to the North. However, it was not until 1492, when Granada fell to the Catholic monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella, that Islamic Spain was well and truly over.In that same year the Jews were expelled from its shores and Christopher Columbus set sail to lead Spanish Christian expansionism into the new world. But how did Muslims, Jews and Christians interact in practice? Was this period of apparent tolerance underpinned by a respect for each other’s sacred texts? What led to the eventual collapse of Cordoba and Islamic Spain? And are we guilty of over-romanticising this so-called golden age of co-existence? With Tim Winter, a convert to Islam and lecturer in Islamic Studies at the Faculty of Divinity at Cambridge University; Martin Palmer, Anglican lay preacher and theologian and author of The Sacred History of Britain, Mehri Niknam, Executive Director of the Maimonides Foundation, a joint Jewish-Muslim Interfaith Foundation in London.

episodes.summaryAI: Islamic civilization in the Iberian Peninsula from the Berber invasion of 711 to the fall of Granada under Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492, a span exceeding 700 years. Cordoba as a major centre for philosophy, medicine, and scholarship. Coexistence of Muslim, Jewish, and Christian communities and debates whether this convivencia represented genuine tolerance or a romanticised ideal. The 1492 expulsion of Jews and the end of Islamic Spain's multicultural legacy. Connects medieval Islamic history, interfaith relations, and the cultural and intellectual achievements of Al-Andalus.

episodes.title: Napoleon and Wellington
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the histories of Napoleon and the Duke of Wellington. On the morning of the battle of Waterloo Napoleon told his loyal lieutenants, “I tell you that Wellington is a bad general, that the English are bad troops and ce sera l’affaire d’un dejeuner”...in other words; ‘this, my friends, will be a picnic!’ But did Napoleon really have such little respect for the man who would be his nemesis, or when he dismissed the Iron Duke so lightly was he just trying to raise morale? There are some curious parallels between the two rivals, they were both born in the same year, 1769, both read the works of Caesar and chose Hannibal as their personal hero, both enjoyed the pleasures of two of the same mistresses, and they even ate the food of the same personal chef. Though Wellington bested Napoleon on the field of Waterloo in 1815, who was the greater general, who left the larger legacy and ultimately, who won? With Andrew Roberts, military historian; Mike Broers, University of Aberdeen; Belinda Beaton, from the Department of History of Art, at Oxford University.

episodes.summaryAI: Napoleon Bonaparte and the Duke of Wellington compared, culminating in their decisive encounter at Waterloo in 1815. Both were born in 1769 and both admired Hannibal as a military model. Napoleon's dismissal of Wellington before the battle and how this underestimation contributed to his defeat. Contrasting military strategies, leadership styles, and legacies, weighing which was the greater general and which left the more enduring impact on European history, military science, and post-Napoleonic political order.

episodes.title: Napoleon's Hundred Days
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Napoleon Bonaparte's temporary return to power in France in 1815, following his escape from exile on Elba . He arrived with fewer than a thousand men, yet three weeks later he had displaced Louis XVIII and taken charge of an army as large as any that the Allied Powers could muster individually. He saw that his best chance was to pick the Allies off one by one, starting with the Prussian and then the British/Allied armies in what is now Belgium. He appeared to be on the point of victory at Waterloo yet somehow it eluded him, and his plans were soon in tatters. His escape to America thwarted, he surrendered on 15th July and was exiled again but this time to Saint Helena. There he wrote his memoirs to help shape his legacy, while back in Europe there were still fears of his return.

episodes.summaryAI: Napoleon's Hundred Days in 1815, from his escape from exile on Elba to his final defeat. Arrived in France with fewer than a thousand men and within three weeks displaced the restored Bourbon monarch Louis XVIII. Near-victory at Waterloo, the factors in his ultimate defeat, and his subsequent exile to Saint Helena. Napoleon used his exile to write memoirs carefully crafted to shape his historical legacy and political mythology. Connects Napoleonic warfare, Bourbon restoration politics, and the reshaping of post-revolutionary European order.

episodes.title: Napoleon's Retreat from Moscow
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, in September 1812, Napoleon captured Moscow and waited a month for the Russians to meet him, to surrender and why, to his dismay, no-one came. Soon his triumph was revealed as a great defeat; winter was coming, supplies were low; he ordered his Grande Armée of six hundred thousand to retreat and, by the time he crossed back over the border, desertion, disease, capture, Cossacks and cold had reduced that to twenty thousand. Napoleon had shown his weakness; his Prussian allies changed sides and, within eighteen months they, the Russians and Austrians had captured Paris and the Emperor was exiled to Elba.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, in September 1812, Napoleon captured Moscow and waited a month for the Russians to meet him, to surrender and why, to his dismay, no-one came. Soon his triumph was revealed as a great defeat; winter was coming, supplies were low; he ordered his Grande Armée of six hundred thousand to retreat and, by the time he crossed back over the border, desertion, disease, capture, Cossacks and cold had reduced that to twenty thousand. Napoleon had shown his weakness; his Prussian allies changed sides and, within eighteen months they, the Russians and Austrians had captured Paris and the Emperor was exiled to Elba.
episodes.summaryAI: Napoleon's catastrophic 1812 retreat from Moscow, a turning point in European military history. His capture of Moscow in September, the futile wait for Russian surrender, and the devastating withdrawal of the Grande Armée, which shrank from 600,000 to approximately 20,000 through disease, extreme cold, starvation, and Cossack attacks. Subsequent defection of Prussian allies, the collapse of Napoleon's continental dominance, the capture of Paris within 18 months, and his first exile to Elba. Connects Napoleonic warfare, Russian history, and the reshaping of European power.

episodes.title: Nature
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the attempt to define humanity’s part in the natural world. In Childe Harold’s Pilgrimage Lord Byron wrote:“There is a pleasure in the pathless woods, There is a rapture on the lonely shore,There is society where none intrudes,By the deep Sea, and music in its roar:I love not man the less, but Nature more.” In the Bible’s book of Genesis, ‘nature’ was the paradise of Eden, but for the philosopher Thomas Hobbes it was a place of perpetual war, where the life of man was “solitary, poore, nasty, brutish and short”. The defining of Nature, whether “red in tooth and claw” or as the fount of all innocence, is an attempt to define man’s origins and purpose and humanity’s part in the natural world. With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick; Roger Scruton, Professor of Philosophy at the University of Buckingham; Karen Edwards, Lecturer in English at the University of Exeter.

In Childe Harold’s Pilgrimage Lord Byron wrote:“There is a pleasure in the pathless woods, There is a rapture on the lonely shore,There is society where none intrudes,By the deep Sea, and music in its roar:I love not man the less, but Nature more.” In the Bible’s book of Genesis, ‘nature’ was the paradise of Eden, but for the philosopher Thomas Hobbes it was a place of perpetual war, where the life of man was “solitary, poore, nasty, brutish and short”. The defining of Nature, whether “red in tooth and claw” or as the fount of all innocence, is an attempt to define man’s origins and purpose and humanity’s part in the natural world. With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick; Roger Scruton, Professor of Philosophy at the University of Buckingham; Karen Edwards, Lecturer in English at the University of Exeter.
episodes.summaryAI: Competing conceptions of nature and humanity's place within the natural world. Contrasts Byron's Romantic rapture at nature's sublime beauty with Hobbes's vision of life as perpetual war. Juxtaposes the Genesis narrative of paradise with Tennyson's nature red in tooth and claw. Philosophical and literary attempts to define human origins, purpose, and relationship to the natural environment, spanning theology, Enlightenment rationalism, Romanticism, and evolutionary thought. Connects philosophy, literature, theology, and environmental consciousness across Western intellectual history.

episodes.title: Nefertiti
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the woman who inspired one of the best known artefacts from ancient Egypt. The Bust of Nefertiti is multicoloured and symmetrical, about 49cm/18" high and, despite the missing left eye, still holds the gaze of onlookers below its tall, blue, flat topped headdress. Its discovery in 1912 in Amarna was kept quiet at first but its display in Berlin in the 1920s caused a sensation, with replicas sent out across the world. Ever since, as with Tutankhamun perhaps, the concrete facts about Nefertiti herself have barely kept up with the theories, the legends and the speculation, reinvigorated with each new discovery.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the woman who inspired one of the best known artefacts from ancient Egypt. The Bust of Nefertiti is multicoloured and symmetrical, about 49cm/18" high and, despite the missing left eye, still holds the gaze of onlookers below its tall, blue, flat topped headdress. Its discovery in 1912 in Amarna was kept quiet at first but its display in Berlin in the 1920s caused a sensation, with replicas sent out across the world. Ever since, as with Tutankhamun perhaps, the concrete facts about Nefertiti herself have barely kept up with the theories, the legends and the speculation, reinvigorated with each new discovery.
episodes.summaryAI: Nefertiti, the ancient Egyptian queen whose painted limestone bust, discovered in 1912 at Amarna and displayed in Berlin in the 1920s, became one of the most recognized artefacts from antiquity. The sensation the bust caused and its role in shaping modern perceptions of ancient Egypt. Sparse historical evidence about Nefertiti's life, her role in Akhenaten's religious revolution promoting Aten worship, and how theories and speculation have consistently outpaced verifiable facts about her reign and influence. Connects ancient Egyptian history, archaeology, and cultural reception.

episodes.title: Negative Numbers
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss negative numbers, a history of mystery and suspicion. In 1759 the British mathematician Francis Maseres wrote that negative numbers "darken the very whole doctrines of the equations and make dark of the things which are in their nature excessively obvious and simple". Because of their dark and mysterious nature, Maseres concluded that negative numbers did not exist, as did his contemporary, William Friend. However, other mathematicians were braver. They took a leap into the unknown and decided that negative numbers could be used during calculations, as long as they had disappeared upon reaching the solution. The history of negative numbers is one of stops and starts. The trailblazers were the Chinese who by 100 BC were able to solve simultaneous equations involving negative numbers. The Ancient Greeks rejected negative numbers as absurd, by 600 AD, the Indians had written the rules for the multiplication of negative numbers and 400 years later, Arabic mathematicians realised the importance of negative debt. But it wasn't until the Renaissance that European mathematicians finally began to accept and use these perplexing numbers. Why were negative numbers considered with such suspicion? Why were they such an abstract concept? And how did they finally get accepted? With Ian Stewart , Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick; Colva Roney-Dougal , Lecturer in Pure Mathematics at the University of St Andrews; Raymond Flood , Lecturer in Computing Studies and Mathematics at Kellogg College, Oxford.

episodes.summaryAI: Contested history of negative numbers across civilizations. Chinese mathematicians solved simultaneous equations with negatives by 100 BC. Greeks rejected the concept. Indians formalized multiplication rules by 600 AD. Arabic mathematicians recognized negative debt. European acceptance came only during the Renaissance despite practical utility. Why negative quantities seemed absurd or meaningless for centuries, connecting number theory to broader questions about mathematical abstraction, epistemology, and the cultural barriers to accepting counterintuitive mathematical concepts.

episodes.title: Neoplatonism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Neoplatonism, the school of thought founded in the 3rd century AD by the philosopher Plotinus. Born in Egypt, Plotinus was brought up in the Platonic tradition, studying and reinterpreting the works of the Greek thinker Plato. After he moved to Rome Plotinus became the most influential member of a group of thinkers dedicated to Platonic scholarship. The Neoplatonists - a term only coined in the nineteenth century - brought a new religious sensibility to bear on Plato's thought. They outlined a complex cosmology which linked the human with the divine, headed by a mysterious power which they called the One. Neoplatonism shaped early Christian, Jewish and Muslim religious scholarship, and remained a dominant force in European thought until the Renaissance

episodes.summaryAI: Neoplatonism, the philosophical school founded in the 3rd century AD by Plotinus, who was born in Egypt and reinterpreted Plato's thought into an elaborate metaphysical system. Cosmology centered on the One as the supreme principle from which all reality emanates, linking human souls with the divine through contemplation and ascent. Profound influence on early Christian, Jewish, and Islamic theology and scholarship, and its dominance as a philosophical framework from late antiquity through the Renaissance. Connects ancient philosophy, religious thought, and the transmission of classical learning.

episodes.title: Nero
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life of Nero (37-68 AD) who became Emperor at the age of 16. At first he was largely praised for his generosity yet became known for his debauched lifestyle, with allegations he started the Fire of Rome, watching the flames as he played the lyre. Christians saw him as their persecutor, an anti-Christ, and the number of the Beast in the Book of Revelation was thought to indicate Nero. He had confidence in his own artistry, took up acting (which then had a very low status) and, as revolts in the empire grew, killed himself after the Senate condemned him to die as a slave, on a cross.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life of Nero (37-68 AD) who became Emperor at the age of 16. At first he was largely praised for his generosity yet became known for his debauched lifestyle, with allegations he started the Fire of Rome, watching the flames as he played the lyre. Christians saw him as their persecutor, an anti-Christ, and the number of the Beast in the Book of Revelation was thought to indicate Nero. episodes.summaryAI: The Roman Emperor Nero (37-68 AD), who ascended to power at age 16. Initially praised reign, subsequent descent into debauched and tyrannical rule, and the allegations surrounding the Great Fire of Rome. Persecution of Christians, who identified him with the anti-Christ, and the theory that the biblical Number of the Beast encodes his name. Eventual suicide after the Senate condemned him. Enduring reputation as an archetype of imperial decadence and cruelty in Roman and Christian historical memory.

episodes.title: Neuroscience
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests examine the relationship between the mind and the brain as they discuss recent developments in Neuroscience. In the mid-19th century a doctor had a patient who had suffered a stroke. The patient was unable to speak save for one word. The word was ‘Tan’ which became his name. When Tan died, the doctor discovered damage to the left side of his brain and concluded that the ability to speak was housed there. This is how neuroscience used to work – by examining the dead or investigating the damaged – but now things have changed. Imaging machines and other technologies enable us to see the active brain in everyday life, to observe the activation of its cells and the mass firing of its neuron batteries. Our extraordinary new knowledge of how the brain works has challenged concepts of free will and consciousness and opened up new ways of understanding the brain. Yet these new ideas seem to conform to some old ideas such as Freudian Psychoanalysis. But what picture of the brain has emerged, how has our understanding of it changed and what are the implications for understanding that most mysterious and significant of all phenomena – the human mind?With Martin Conway, Professor of Psychology at the University of Leeds; Gemma Calvert, Professor of Applied Neuroimaging at WMG, University of Warwick and David Papineau, Professor of Philosophy of Science at King’s College London.

episodes.summaryAI: Development of neuroscience from 19th-century discoveries of speech localisation in the brain to modern neuroimaging technology that reveals the active brain in real time. Advances in brain science have challenged traditional conceptions of free will and consciousness. Surprising convergences between neuroscientific findings and some Freudian ideas about the unconscious mind. Broader philosophical implications of understanding human thought, decision-making, and behaviour through biological mechanisms. Connects neurology, psychology, philosophy of mind, and the history of brain science.

episodes.title: Neuroscience in the 20th century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests marvel at our brains and discuss how at the end of a century of research we still understand so little about how they work.Developments in the understanding of the brain represent one of the major leaps forward in science in the 20th century, and the research is gathering pace and intensity. It’s a subject which captures the imagination, particularly the search for consciousness whatever that might be, and brings together some of the newest technology and the oldest belief systems. What a piece of work is the brain - a grain-of-sand-sized piece contains one hundred thousand neurons, two-million axons and one billion synapses which all talk to each other. How far we have got with our understanding of the brain and what can it tell us about ourselves and the world we live in?With Professor Susan Greenfield, director of the Royal Institution, Professor of Pharmacology, Oxford University and Professor of Physics at Gresham College; Professor Vilayanur Ramachandran, Professor of Neuroscience and Psychology, Director of the Brain Perception Laboratory, University of California in San Diego and Professor at the Salk Institute.
