episodes.summaryAI: A century of brain research and the persistent limits of neuroscientific understanding. A grain-of-sand-sized piece of brain tissue contains approximately 100,000 neurons, 2 million axons, and 1 billion synapses, illustrating the organ's staggering complexity. The ongoing search for the neural basis of consciousness. How the newest imaging and computational technologies intersect with the oldest philosophical and religious questions about the nature of mind, awareness, and selfhood. Connects neuroscience, philosophy of consciousness, and the history of scientific investigation of the brain.

episodes.title: New Wars
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of modern warfare. In the early nineteenth century the Prussian General Karl von Clausewitz seemed to define war for all time when he called it “an act of violence intended to compel our opponent to fulfil our will” and “nothing but a continuation of politics with the admixture of other means”. But after the nuclear bomb, the Cold War and the brutal and perplexing recent wars in Africa and Eastern Europe does his definition still hold true? Or are we in a new era when the idea of a continuation of peacetime politics and the notion of a national will is increasingly irrelevant? Are the technologically billion dollar new wars, coupled with the wars on the ground which are more like crimes, revolutions or more organised violence than war, a way of following Clausewitz’s notion of war as a continuation of politics by other means or do they constitute something completely different?With Sir Michael Howard, Emeritus Professor of Modern History, Oxford University; Dr Mary Kaldor, Director of the Programme on Global Civil Society, London School of Economics; General Sir Michael Rose, former Commander of the United Nations Protection Force in Bosnia and author of Fighting for Peace: Lessons from Bosnia.

episodes.summaryAI: The changing nature of warfare from Clausewitz's classic definition of war as a continuation of politics by other means. How the nuclear bomb, Cold War deterrence, and brutal conflicts in Africa and Eastern Europe have challenged traditional frameworks. Whether modern warfare, combining billion-dollar military technology with ground-level ethnic violence and asymmetric combat, constitutes something fundamentally new or remains recognizable within established theories of armed conflict, state power, and political violence.

episodes.title: Nietzsche's Genealogy of Morality
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Nietzsche's On The Genealogy of Morality - A Polemic, which he published in 1887 towards the end of his working life and in which he considered the price humans have paid, and were still paying, to become civilised. In three essays, he argued that having a guilty conscience was the price of living in society with other humans. He suggested that Christian morality, with its consideration for others, grew as an act of revenge by the weak against their masters, 'the blond beasts of prey', as he calls them, and the price for that slaves' revolt was endless self-loathing. These and other ideas were picked up by later thinkers, perhaps most significantly by Sigmund Freud who further explored the tensions between civilisation and the individual.

episodes.summaryAI: Friedrich Nietzsche's On the Genealogy of Morality, published in 1887, investigates the psychological costs humans paid to become civilised. Nietzsche's account of guilty conscience as the price of social organization, and his argument that Christian morality originated as the revenge of the weak against aristocratic masters through a slave revolt in values. Nietzsche's influence on Freud's later work examining the tensions between the demands of civilisation and individual instinct. Connects moral philosophy, psychology, Christian ethics, and the critique of Western values.

episodes.title: Nihilism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of Nihilism. The nineteenth-century philosopher, Friedrich Nietzsche, wrote, “There can be no doubt that morality will gradually perish: this is the great spectacle in a hundred acts reserved for the next two centuries in Europe”. And, with chilling predictions like these, ‘Nihilism’ was born. The hard view that morals are pointless, loyalty is a weakness and ‘truths’ are illusory, has excited, confused and appalled western thinkers ever since. But what happened to Nietzsche’s revolutionary ideas about truth, morality and a life without meaning? Existentialism can claim lineage to Nietzsche, as can Post Modernism, but then so can Nazism. With so many interpretations, and claims of ownership from the left and the right, has anything positive come out of the great philosopher of ‘nothing’?With Rob Hopkins, Senior Lecturer in Philosophy, University of Birmingham; Professor Raymond Tallis, Doctor and Philosopher; Professor Catherine Belsey, University of Cardiff.

But what happened to Nietzsche’s revolutionary ideas about truth, morality and a life without meaning? Existentialism can claim lineage to Nietzsche, as can Post Modernism, but then so can Nazism. With so many interpretations, and claims of ownership from the left and the right, has anything positive come out of the great philosopher of ‘nothing’?With Rob Hopkins, Senior Lecturer in Philosophy, University of Birmingham; Professor Raymond Tallis, Doctor and Philosopher; Professor Catherine Belsey, University of Cardiff.
episodes.summaryAI: Nihilism as a philosophical stance, rooted in Nietzsche's prediction that conventional morality would collapse. The hard nihilist position that moral values are groundless and truths illusory. Diverse intellectual movements claiming nihilist lineage, including existentialism, postmodernism, and the appropriation by Nazism. Whether anything constructive has emerged from a philosophy centered on negation and nothingness, and how nihilism has shaped modern debates about meaning, ethics, and cultural authority. Connects moral philosophy, political ideology, and twentieth-century intellectual history.

episodes.title: Nikola Tesla
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Serbian-American inventor Nikola Tesla (1856-1943) and his role in the development of electrical systems towards the end of the nineteenth century. He made his name in New York in the contest over which current should flow into homes and factories in America. Some such as Edison backed direct current or DC while others such as Westinghouse backed alternating current or AC and Nikola Tesla’s invention of a motor that worked on AC swung it for the alternating system that went on to power the modern age. He ensured his reputation and ideas burnt brightly for the next decades, making him synonymous with the lone, genius inventor of the new science fiction.

episodes.summaryAI: Serbian-American inventor Nikola Tesla (1856-1943) and the pivotal contest between direct current and alternating current for electrifying homes and factories. Edison's backing of DC against Westinghouse's promotion of AC, with Tesla's invention of the AC induction motor proving decisive in establishing alternating current as the standard powering the modern electrical age. Tesla's iconic status as the archetypal lone genius inventor and his broader contributions to electrical engineering, radio technology, and the development of modern power systems.

episodes.title: Nineteen Eighty-Four
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss George Orwell's (1903-1950) final novel, published in 1949, set in a dystopian London which is now found in Airstrip One, part of the totalitarian superstate of Oceania which is always at war and where the protagonist, Winston Smith, works at the Ministry of Truth as a rewriter of history: 'Who controls the past,' ran the Party slogan, 'controls the future: who controls the present controls the past.' The influence of Orwell's novel is immeasurable, highlighting threats to personal freedom with concepts he named such as doublespeak, thoughtcrime, Room 101, Big Brother, memory hole and thought police.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss George Orwell's (1903-1950) final novel, published in 1949, set in a dystopian London which is now found in Airstrip One, part of the totalitarian superstate of Oceania which is always at war and where the protagonist, Winston Smith, works at the Ministry of Truth as a rewriter of history: 'Who controls the past,' ran the Party slogan, 'controls the future: who controls the present controls the past.' The influence of Orwell's novel is immeasurable, highlighting threats to personal freedom with concepts he named such as doublespeak, thoughtcrime, Room 101, Big Brother, memory hole and thought police.
episodes.summaryAI: George Orwell's 1949 dystopian novel set in a totalitarian superstate called Oceania, where London is part of Airstrip One. Protagonist Winston Smith works at the Ministry of Truth rewriting historical records. Explores mechanisms of authoritarian control including surveillance (Big Brother, thought police), language manipulation (doublespeak, Newspeak), ideological enforcement (thoughtcrime, Room 101), and institutional erasure of memory (memory hole). Reflects mid-twentieth-century anxieties about Stalinism, propaganda, and the relationship between political power, truth, and individual consciousness. Central to twentieth-century English literature and political philosophy.

episodes.title: Nizami Ganjavi
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the greatest romantic poets in Persian literature. Nizami Ganjavi (c1141–1209) is was born in the city of Ganja in what is now Azerbaijan and his popularity soon spread throughout the Persian-speaking lands and beyond. Nizami is best known for his Khamsa, a set of five epic poems that contains a famous retelling of the tragic love story of King Khosrow II (c570-628) and the Christian princess Shirin (unknown-628) and the legend of Layla and Majnun. Not only did he write romances: his poetry also displays a dazzling knowledge of philosophy, astronomy, botany and the life of Alexander the Great.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the greatest romantic poets in Persian literature. Nizami Ganjavi (c1141–1209) is was born in the city of Ganja in what is now Azerbaijan and his popularity soon spread throughout the Persian-speaking lands and beyond. Nizami is best known for his Khamsa, a set of five epic poems that contains a famous retelling of the tragic love story of King Khosrow II (c570-628) and the Christian princess Shirin (unknown-628) and the legend of Layla and Majnun. Not only did he write romances: his poetry also displays a dazzling knowledge of philosophy, astronomy, botany and the life of Alexander the Great.
episodes.summaryAI: Nizami Ganjavi (c.1141-1209), major Persian-language romantic poet born in Ganja in the Caucasus (modern Azerbaijan). Best known for the Khamsa (Quintet), five long narrative poems including the tragic romances of Khosrow and Shirin and Layla and Majnun. His verse integrates wide-ranging learning in philosophy, astronomy, botany, and legends of Alexander the Great. Bridges Persian literary tradition with broader Islamic cultural achievement, connecting courtly romance, ethical reflection, and encyclopedic knowledge within the medieval Islamic world's intellectual landscape.

episodes.title: North and South
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Elizabeth Gaskell's novel North and South, published in 1855 after serialisation in Dickens' Household Words magazine. It is the story of Margaret Hale, who was raised in the South in the New Forest and London's Harley Street, and then moves North to a smokey mill town, Milton, in Darkshire. As well as Margaret's emotional life and her growing sense of independence, the novel explores the new ways of living thrown up by industrialisation, and the relationships between 'masters and men'. Many of Margaret Hale's experiences echo Gaskell's own life, as she was born in Chelsea and later moved to Manchester, and the novel has become valued for its insights into social conflicts and the changing world in which Gaskell lived.

Many of Margaret Hale's experiences echo Gaskell's own life, as she was born in Chelsea and later moved to Manchester, and the novel has become valued for its insights into social conflicts and the changing world in which Gaskell lived.
episodes.summaryAI: Elizabeth Gaskell's 1855 novel examining industrialisation in mid-Victorian England through protagonist Margaret Hale's relocation from the rural South to the manufacturing town of Milton in northern England. Explores class tensions between factory owners and workers, labour relations, and new social structures created by industrial capitalism. Draws on Gaskell's own experience moving from Chelsea to Manchester. Addresses the cultural and economic divide between agrarian and industrial England, changing gender roles, and the social consequences of rapid urbanization and factory production in nineteenth-century Britain.

episodes.title: Nuclear Fusion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss nuclear fusion, the process that powers stars. In the 1920s physicists predicted that it might be possible to generate huge amounts of energy by fusing atomic nuclei together, a reaction requiring enormous temperatures and pressures. Today we know that this complex reaction is what keeps the Sun shining. Scientists have achieved fusion in the laboratory and in nuclear weapons; today it is seen as a likely future source of limitless and clean energy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss nuclear fusion, the process that powers stars. In the 1920s physicists predicted that it might be possible to generate huge amounts of energy by fusing atomic nuclei together, a reaction requiring enormous temperatures and pressures. Today we know that this complex reaction is what keeps the Sun shining. Scientists have achieved fusion in the laboratory and in nuclear weapons; today it is seen as a likely future source of limitless and clean energy.
episodes.summaryAI: Nuclear fusion, the process powering stars including the Sun, where atomic nuclei combine at extreme temperatures and pressures to release energy. Theoretical prediction emerged in the 1920s from advances in physics. The complex chain of fusion reactions sustaining stellar energy output. Achieved in laboratory settings and exploited in thermonuclear weapons (hydrogen bombs). Ongoing efforts to develop controlled fusion as a future source of virtually limitless clean energy. Connects stellar astrophysics, plasma physics, nuclear weapons technology, and energy policy.

episodes.title: Nuclear Physics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines one of the greatest scientific breakthroughs of the 20th century, and certainly the most controversial; the development of nuclear physics. Harnessing the enigmatic qualities of the atom’s tiny core brought us nuclear power and gave us The Bomb, a breakthrough with such far-reaching consequences that it moved the physicist Albert Einstein to say, “Had I known, I should have become a watch maker”.How can such outlandish power be released from such infinitesimal amounts of matter and what does the science of the nucleus tell us about how our universe is built? Nuclear technology provokes strong emotional and political reactions, but what are the plain facts behind its development as a science? With Jim Al-Khalili, Senior Lecturer in Physics at the University of Surrey; Christine Sutton, Particle Physicist and Lecturer in Physics at St Catherine’s College Oxford; John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy at the University of Sussex.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg examines one of the greatest scientific breakthroughs of the 20th century, and certainly the most controversial; the development of nuclear physics. Harnessing the enigmatic qualities of the atom’s tiny core brought us nuclear power and gave us The Bomb, a breakthrough with such far-reaching consequences that it moved the physicist Albert Einstein to say, “Had I known, I should have become a watch maker”.How can such outlandish power be released from such infinitesimal amounts of matter and what does the science of the nucleus tell us about how our universe is built? Nuclear technology provokes strong emotional and political reactions, but what are the plain facts behind its development as a science? With Jim Al-Khalili, Senior Lecturer in Physics at the University of Surrey; Christine Sutton, Particle Physicist and Lecturer in Physics at St Catherine’s College Oxford; John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy at the University of Sussex.
episodes.summaryAI: Development of nuclear physics from discovery of the atomic nucleus through harnessing nuclear energy. Explores how physicists unlocked the immense energy contained in the infinitesimally small atomic core, leading to nuclear power generation and nuclear weapons. Einstein's reflections on the moral consequences of atomic research. Covers nuclear structure, radioactivity, fission, and the relationship between mass and energy. Connects fundamental particle physics to twentieth-century military technology and energy production, raising questions about scientific responsibility and the dual-use nature of nuclear knowledge.

episodes.title: Oceanography
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the science of Oceanography. In 1870 Jules Verne described the deep ocean in 2,000 Leagues Under the Sea. He wrote: “The sea is an immense desert where man is never alone for he feels life, quivering around him on every side.” This was actually closer to the truth than the science of the time, when ‘Azoic Theory’ held sway and it was believed that nothing could exist below 600 metres. Now we estimate that there are more species in the deep ocean than in the rest of the planet put together, somewhere between 2 million and 100 million different species of organism are living on the ocean floor.Science has dispelled the idea that huge underground tunnels join our oceans together and the notion that giant Kraken lurk in the deep, but our seas still retain much of their mystery and there have been more men on the surface of the moon than at the bottom of the ocean. How should we understand the sea? With Margaret Deacon, visiting Research Fellow at Southampton Oceanography Centre and author of Scientists and the Sea, Tony Rice, Biological Oceanographer and author of Deep Ocean, Simon Schaffer, Reader in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge, and a fellow of Darwin College.

episodes.summaryAI: The science of oceanography from nineteenth-century misconceptions to modern deep-sea exploration. The Azoic Theory once held nothing lived below 600 metres, contradicted by discoveries of extraordinary biodiversity in the deep ocean exceeding that of all other habitats combined. Jules Verne's 1870 fictional descriptions proved more accurate than contemporary science. Covers ocean floor mapping, marine ecosystems, deep-sea biology, ocean currents, and the technological challenges of underwater exploration. Fewer humans have visited the ocean floor than the Moon, highlighting the frontier nature of ocean science.

episodes.title: Ockham's Razor
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the philosophical ideas of William Ockham including Ockham's Razor. In the small village of Ockham, near Woking in Surrey, stands a church. Made of grey stone, it has a pitched roof and an unassuming church tower but parts of it date back to the 13th century. This means they would have been standing when the village witnessed the birth of one of the greatest philosophers in Medieval Europe. His name was William and he became known as William of Ockham.William of Ockham’s ideas on human freedom and the nature of reality influenced Thomas Hobbes and helped fuel the Reformation. During a turbulent career he managed to offend the Chancellor of Oxford University, disagree with his own ecclesiastical order and get excommunicated by the Pope. He also declared that the authority of rulers derives from the people they govern and was one of the first people so to do. Ockham’s razor is the idea that philosophical arguments should be kept as simple as possible, something that Ockham himself practised severely on the theories of his predecessors. But why is William of Ockham significant in the history of philosophy, how did his turbulent life fit within the political dramas of his time and to what extent do we see his ideas in the work of later thinkers such as Thomas Hobbes and even Martin Luther?With Sir Anthony Kenny, philosopher and former Master of Balliol College, Oxford; Marilyn Adams, Regius Professor of Divinity at Oxford University; Richard Cross, Professor of Medieval Theology at Oriel College, Oxford

episodes.summaryAI: William of Ockham (c.1287-1347), major medieval Franciscan philosopher and theologian. Formulated Ockham's razor, the principle of parsimony holding that explanations should not multiply entities beyond necessity. His ideas on human freedom, the nature of universals, and nominalist metaphysics influenced later thinkers including Hobbes. Argued that political authority derives from the governed, anticipating social contract theory. Excommunicated for challenging papal authority. His work contributed to the intellectual currents fuelling the Protestant Reformation, connecting medieval philosophy, political theory, and theology.

episodes.title: Octavia Hill
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Victorian social reformer Octavia Hill.From the 1850s until her death in 1912, Octavia Hill was an energetic campaigner who did much to improve the lot of impoverished city dwellers. She was a pioneer of social housing who believed that there were better and more humane ways of arranging accommodation for the poor than through the state. Aided at first by her friend John Ruskin, the essayist and art critic, she bought houses and let them to the urban dispossessed. Octavia Hill provided an early model of social work, did much to preserve urban open spaces, and was the first to use the term 'green belt' to describe the rural areas around London. She was also one of the founders of the National Trust. Yet her vision of social reform, involving volunteers and private enterprise rather than central government, was often at odds with that of her contemporaries

episodes.summaryAI: Octavia Hill (1838-1912), Victorian social reformer who pioneered social housing in Britain by purchasing properties and letting them to the urban poor with financial backing from John Ruskin. First to use the term 'green belt' for urban green spaces. Co-founded the National Trust for preserving places of natural beauty and historic interest. Advocated reform through voluntary action and private enterprise rather than state intervention. Connects nineteenth-century urban poverty, housing reform, environmental conservation, philanthropic models, and debates about the role of government in social welfare.

episodes.title: Oedipus Rex
episodes.synopsis: Sophocles’ play Oedipus Rex begins with a warning: the murderer of the old king of Thebes, Laius, has never been identified or caught, and he’s still at large in the city. Oedipus is the current king of Thebes, and he sets out to solve the crime.
episodes.description: Sophocles’ play Oedipus Rex begins with a warning: the murderer of the old king of Thebes, Laius, has never been identified or caught, and he’s still at large in the city. Oedipus is the current king of Thebes, and he sets out to solve the crime.
episodes.summaryAI: Sophocles' tragedy Oedipus Rex (Oedipus Tyrannus), a foundational work of ancient Greek drama. King Oedipus investigates the unsolved murder of his predecessor Laius, only to discover he himself is the killer and has unwittingly fulfilled the prophecy of killing his father and marrying his mother. Explores themes of fate versus free will, the limits of human knowledge, dramatic irony, and the nature of truth. Central to Western literary tradition and later psychoanalytic theory (Freud's Oedipus complex). Fifth-century BCE Athenian theatre.

episodes.title: Oliver Goldsmith
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the renowned and versatile Irish writer Oliver Goldsmith (1728 - 1774). There is a memorial to him in Westminster Abbey’s Poet’s Corner written by Dr Johnson, celebrating Goldsmith's life as a poet, natural philosopher and historian. To this could be added ‘playwright’ and ‘novelist’ and ‘science writer’ and ‘pamphleteer’ and much besides, as Goldsmith explored so many different outlets for his talents. While he began on Grub Street in London, the centre for jobbing writers scrambling for paid work, he became a great populariser and compiler of new ideas and knowledge and achieved notable successes with poems such as The Deserted Village, his play She Stoops to Conquer and his short novel The Vicar of Wakefield.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the renowned and versatile Irish writer Oliver Goldsmith (1728 - 1774). There is a memorial to him in Westminster Abbey’s Poet’s Corner written by Dr Johnson, celebrating Goldsmith's life as a poet, natural philosopher and historian. episodes.summaryAI: Oliver Goldsmith (1728-1774), Irish-born writer of extraordinary literary range active in eighteenth-century London. Poet, playwright, novelist, natural philosopher, historian, and pamphleteer. Major works include the poem The Deserted Village, the comedy She Stoops to Conquer, and the novel The Vicar of Wakefield. Began career in Grub Street hack writing. Memorialised in Westminster Abbey. His output spans literature, popular science writing, and social commentary, connecting Augustan literary culture, Irish diaspora in London, and the commercialisation of eighteenth-century publishing.

episodes.title: Olympe de Gouges
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the French playwright who, in 1791, wrote The Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen. This was Olympe de Gouges (1748-93) and she was responding to The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen from 1789, the start of the French Revolution which, by excluding women from these rights, had fallen far short of its apparent goals. Where the latter declared ‘men are born equal’, she asserted ‘women are born equal to men,’ adding, ‘since women are allowed to mount the scaffold, they should also be allowed to stand in parliament and defend their rights’. Two years later this playwright, novelist, activist and woman of letters did herself mount the scaffold, two weeks after Marie Antoinette, for the crime of being open to the idea of a constitutional monarchy and, for two hundred years, her reputation died with her, only to be revived with great vigour in the last 40 years.

episodes.summaryAI: Olympe de Gouges (1748-1793), French playwright and political activist who authored the Declaration of the Rights of Woman and the Female Citizen in 1791, directly responding to the 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen which excluded women. Asserted women's natural equality with men. Executed by guillotine in 1793, two weeks after Marie Antoinette. Her reputation was suppressed for nearly two centuries before feminist scholarship revived it. Connects French Revolution politics, Enlightenment rights theory, early feminism, and women's exclusion from revolutionary citizenship.

episodes.title: On Liberty
episodes.synopsis: Journalist, author and historian Misha Glenny presents his first edition of In Our Time, succeeding Melvyn Bragg who retired from this role last summer. Misha and his guests discuss the landmark work On Liberty by John Stuart Mill, published in 1859 and the increasing recognition for his wife Harriet Taylor Mill's contribution. The subject matter of the essay is ‘civil or social liberty: the nature and limits of the power which can be legitimately exercised by society over the individual’ and it argues that the sole end for which mankind may interfere with the liberty of action of anyone is self-protection and even then only to prevent harm to others. This essay became enormously popular and a foundational text for liberalism.
episodes.description: Journalist, author and historian Misha Glenny presents his first edition of In Our Time, succeeding Melvyn Bragg who retired from this role last summer. Misha and his guests discuss the landmark work On Liberty by John Stuart Mill, published in 1859 and the increasing recognition for his wife Harriet Taylor Mill's contribution. The subject matter of the essay is ‘civil or social liberty: the nature and limits of the power which can be legitimately exercised by society over the individual’ and it argues that the sole end for which mankind may interfere with the liberty of action of anyone is self-protection and even then only to prevent harm to others. This essay became enormously popular and a foundational text for liberalism.
episodes.summaryAI: John Stuart Mill's On Liberty (1859), a foundational text of liberal political philosophy. Examines the nature and limits of power that society and the state may legitimately exercise over the individual. Articulates the harm principle: the sole justification for restricting individual liberty is preventing harm to others. Mill acknowledged significant intellectual contribution from his wife Harriet Taylor Mill. Addresses freedom of thought, expression, and individuality against democratic tyranny of the majority. Central to debates about civil liberties, censorship, paternalism, and the boundaries between public authority and private autonomy.

episodes.title: Optics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of optics. From telescopes to microscopes, from star-gazing to the intimacies of a magnified flea. As Galileo turned his telescope to the heavens in the early 1600s, Kepler began to formulate a theory of optics. The new and improving instruments went hand in hand with radical new ideas about how we see and what we see. Spectacles allowed scholars to study long into the evening (and into old age), while giant telescopes, up to 100 feet long, led to the discovery of planets and attempts to map the universe. The craze for optical trickery swept Europe with enthusiastic amateurs often providing valuable discoveries. But this new view of the world through a lens raised questions too – how much can you rely on the senses, on what you see? The further into space you can spy, the larger and more unmanageable the universe becomes. At the same time, the microscope was utterly transforming the world close at hand.So how did these developments inform ideas of knowledge? If new methods of scientific observation support an empirical approach, what does this mean for divine, innate reason?With Simon Schaffer, Professor in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge; Jim Bennett, Director of the Museum of the History of Science and Fellow of Linacre College at the University of Oxford; Emily Winterburn, Curator of Astronomy at the National Maritime Museum

episodes.summaryAI: History of optics from early modern period through the scientific revolution and beyond. Galileo's telescopic observations of the heavens, Kepler's formulation of optical theory, and the development of instruments including spectacles, giant telescopes, and microscopes. Discovery of new planets through improved telescopy. Optical illusions and trickery that swept through European culture, raising philosophical questions about the reliability of sensory perception. The microscope's transformation of understanding of the natural world at small scales. Connects physics of light, instrument technology, astronomy, biology, and epistemology.

episodes.title: Ordinary Language Philosophy
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Ordinary Language Philosophy, a school of thought which emerged in Oxford in the years following World War II. With its roots in the work of Ludwig Wittgenstein, Ordinary Language Philosophy is concerned with the meanings of words as used in everyday speech. Its adherents believed that many philosophical problems were created by the misuse of words, and that if such 'ordinary language' were correctly analysed, such problems would disappear. Philosophers associated with the school include some of the most distinguished British thinkers of the twentieth century, such as Gilbert Ryle and JL Austin.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Ordinary Language Philosophy, a school of thought which emerged in Oxford in the years following World War II. With its roots in the work of Ludwig Wittgenstein, Ordinary Language Philosophy is concerned with the meanings of words as used in everyday speech. Its adherents believed that many philosophical problems were created by the misuse of words, and that if such 'ordinary language' were correctly analysed, such problems would disappear. Philosophers associated with the school include some of the most distinguished British thinkers of the twentieth century, such as Gilbert Ryle and JL Austin.
episodes.summaryAI: Ordinary language philosophy, an analytical philosophical movement emerging at Oxford after World War II, rooted in the later work of Ludwig Wittgenstein. Central thesis that many traditional philosophical problems arise from misunderstandings or misuse of everyday language. Key figures include Gilbert Ryle and J.L. Austin. Emphasised careful attention to how words function in ordinary speech rather than constructing formal logical systems. Distinct from logical positivism and ideal language philosophy. Connects twentieth-century British philosophy, philosophy of language, epistemology, and the relationship between linguistic analysis and metaphysical questions.

episodes.title: Originality
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests explore the creative force of originality. How far is it to do with origins, how far with the combination of the discoveries of others, which were themselves based on the thoughts of others, into an ever-receding and replicating past? Is invention original? Is original important? Is tradition more interesting and the reworking of what is traditional of greater value than the search for idiosyncrasy? And did our notion of the original genius come as much out of a commercial imperative for individual copyright in the eighteenth century, as a romantic view of human nature which came in, perhaps not co-incidentally, at the same time? In 1800, in his preface to Lyrical Ballads, Wordsworth wrote "Every great and original writer, in proportion as he is great and original, must himself create the taste by which he is to be relished". But did the notion of originality begin with the Romantics in the 18th century, or has society always valued originality? Should we consider Shakespeare an innovator or a plagiarist?To what extent is originality about perception rather than conception and is originality a concept without meaning today?With John Deathridge, King Edward Professor of Music at King’s College London; Jonathan Rée, philosopher and author of Philosophical Tales; Professor Catherine Belsey, Chair of the Centre for Critical and Cultural Theory at Cardiff University

And did our notion of the original genius come as much out of a commercial imperative for individual copyright in the eighteenth century, as a romantic view of human nature which came in, perhaps not co-incidentally, at the same time? episodes.summaryAI: The concept of originality and its contested nature across creative and intellectual domains. Questions whether originality means genuine novelty or novel combination of existing ideas. Examines whether invention qualifies as original creation. The emergence of the notion of original genius in the eighteenth century, possibly driven by commercial imperatives for copyright protection. Shakespeare as case study: innovator or adapter of existing sources. Wordsworth's claim that great writers must create the taste by which they are appreciated. Connects aesthetics, intellectual property law, literary history, and philosophy of creativity.

episodes.title: Oscar Wilde
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines Oscar Wilde and the Aesthetes. In February 1895 Oscar Wilde was at the height of his powers, he was known on both sides of the Atlantic, he was feted in London society and celebrated in the West End where An Ideal Husband continued to play to packed houses as The Importance of Being Earnest opened to ecstatic reviews. “The man who can dominate a London dinner table can dominate the world” he is reputed to have said, and he seemed on course to do just that. But in April that year he was arrested for the crime of ‘gross indecency’ and his swift decent from omnipotence began; it ended five years later, when exiled and humiliated he died, famously ‘beyond his means’, in a Paris hotel.Wilde’s reputation spent a long time in the wilderness but just over a century after his death his stock is higher than ever before. Now that his rehabilitation seems complete how should we understand his legacy? Was Wilde a reactionary - the last of the romantics - or was he the midwife to modernism? Was there a coherent philosophy behind those beguiling epigrams or was there little more to aestheticism than dressing up and showing off? With Valentine Cunningham, Professor of English Language and Literature at Oxford University, Regenia Gagnier, Professor of English at the University of Exeter and Neil Sammells, Dean of Humanities at Bath Spa University and author of Wilde Style.

With Valentine Cunningham, Professor of English Language and Literature at Oxford University, Regenia Gagnier, Professor of English at the University of Exeter and Neil Sammells, Dean of Humanities at Bath Spa University and author of Wilde Style.
episodes.summaryAI: Oscar Wilde (1854-1900) and the Aesthetic movement in late Victorian Britain. At the peak of his career in 1895 with An Ideal Husband and The Importance of Being Earnest on the London stage, then arrested and imprisoned for gross indecency. Questions whether Wilde was the last Romantic or a precursor to literary modernism, and whether a coherent philosophical stance underpinned his wit and epigrams. Connects aestheticism, decadence, Victorian sexuality and morality, theatre history, the relationship between art and ethics, and transitions in nineteenth-century literary culture.

episodes.title: Ovid
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman poet Publius Ovidius Naso (43BC-17/18AD) who, as he described it, was destroyed by 'carmen et error', a poem and a mistake. His works have been preserved in greater number than any of the poets of his age, even Virgil, and have been among the most influential. The versions of many of the Greek and Roman myths we know today were his work, as told in his epic Metamorphoses and, together with his works on Love and the Art of Love, have inspired and disturbed readers from the time they were created. Despite being the most prominent poet in Augustan Rome at the time, he was exiled from Rome to Tomis on the Black Sea Coast where he remained until he died. It is thought that the 'carmen' that led to his exile was the Art of Love, Ars Amatoria, supposedly scandalising Augustus, but the 'error' was not disclosed.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman poet Publius Ovidius Naso (43BC-17/18AD) who, as he described it, was destroyed by 'carmen et error', a poem and a mistake. episodes.summaryAI: The Roman poet Publius Ovidius Naso (43 BC - 17/18 AD), whose works survive in greater quantity than any contemporary Latin poet. Best known for the Metamorphoses, a vast narrative poem retelling Greek and Roman myths through the theme of transformation. The Ars Amatoria (Art of Love) scandalised Emperor Augustus. Exiled to Tomis on the Black Sea for mysterious reasons involving a 'carmen' (poem) and an 'error.' Connects Augustan-era Roman literature, classical mythology, the politics of censorship, and the enduring influence of Latin poetry on Western literary tradition.

episodes.title: Owain Glyndwr
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life of the Welsh nobleman, also known as Owen Glendower, who began a revolt against Henry IV in 1400 which was at first very successful. Glyndwr (c1359-c1415) adopted the title Prince of Wales and established a parliament and his own foreign policy, until he was defeated by the future Henry V. Owain Glyndwr escaped and led guerilla attacks for several years but was never betrayed to the English, disappearing without trace.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life of the Welsh nobleman, also known as Owen Glendower, who began a revolt against Henry IV in 1400 which was at first very successful. Glyndwr (c1359-c1415) adopted the title Prince of Wales and established a parliament and his own foreign policy, until he was defeated by the future Henry V. Owain Glyndwr escaped and led guerilla attacks for several years but was never betrayed to the English, disappearing without trace.
episodes.summaryAI: Owain Glyndwr (c.1359-c.1415), Welsh nobleman who launched a major revolt against English rule under Henry IV beginning in 1400. Claimed the title Prince of Wales, established a Welsh parliament, and conducted independent foreign policy including alliances with France. Eventually defeated militarily by the future Henry V but escaped capture, led guerrilla resistance, was never betrayed, and disappeared without trace. Connects late medieval Welsh and English political history, national identity and sovereignty movements, the Lancastrian dynasty, and Anglo-Welsh relations in the fifteenth century.

episodes.title: Oxygen
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses the discovery of Oxygen by Joseph Priestley and Antoine Lavoisier. In the late 18th century Chemistry was the prince of the sciences – vital to the economy, it shaped how Europeans fought each other, ate with each other, what they built and the medicine they took. And then, in 1772, the British chemist, Joseph Priestley, stood in front of the Royal Society and reported on his latest discovery: “this air is of exalted nature…A candle burned in this air with an amazing strength of flame; and a bit of red hot wood crackled and burned with a prodigious rapidity. But to complete the proof of the superior quality of this air, I introduced a mouse into it; and in a quantity in which, had it been common air, it would have died in about a quarter of an hour; it lived at two different times, a whole hour, and was taken out quite vigorous.” For the British dissenting preacher, Joseph Priestley, and the French aristocrat, Antoine Lavoisier, Chemistry was full of possibilities and they pursued them for scientific and political ends. But they came to blows over oxygen because they both claimed to have discovered it, provoking a scientific controversy that rattled through the laboratories of France and England until well after their deaths. To understand their disagreement is to understand something about the nature of scientific discovery itself. With Simon Schaffer, Professor in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge; Jenny Uglow, Honorary Visiting Professor at the University of Warwick; Hasok Chang, Reader in Philosophy of Science at University College London.

episodes.summaryAI: The discovery of oxygen in the late eighteenth century by Joseph Priestley and Antoine Lavoisier. Priestley reported to the Royal Society an experiment producing air in which a candle burned with remarkable vigour and a mouse survived far longer than expected. Both Priestley and Lavoisier claimed priority, generating enduring controversy about the nature of scientific discovery, credit, and the distinction between isolating a substance and correctly interpreting its significance. Connects chemistry's transition from phlogiston theory to modern combustion theory, the scientific revolution, and philosophy of science.

episodes.title: P v NP
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the problem of P versus NP, which has a bearing on online security. There is a $1,000,000 prize on offer from the Clay Mathematical Institute for the first person to come up with a complete solution. At its heart is the question "are there problems for which the answers can be checked by computers, but not found in a reasonable time?" If the answer to that is yes, then P does not equal NP. However, if all answers can be found easily as well as checked, if only we knew how, then P equals NP. The area has intrigued mathematicians and computer scientists since Alan Turing, in 1936, found that it's impossible to decide in general whether an algorithm will run forever on some problems. Resting on P versus NP is the security of all online transactions which are currently encrypted: if it transpires that P=NP, if answers could be found as easily as checked, computers could crack passwords in moments.

episodes.summaryAI: The P versus NP problem, one of the Clay Mathematics Institute's millennium prize problems carrying a one-million-dollar reward. Asks whether problems whose solutions can be verified quickly by computers can also be solved quickly. If P does not equal NP, certain problems are inherently harder to solve than to check. The security of online cryptographic systems depends on this asymmetry. Rooted in Alan Turing's 1936 foundational work on computability and algorithms. If P equalled NP, computers could crack encryption almost instantly. Connects theoretical computer science, computational complexity, cryptography, and mathematical logic.

episodes.title: Paganism in the Renaissance
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss paganism in the Renaissance. For hundreds of years in the Middle Ages, the only way to read Ovid was through the prism of a Christian moralising text. Ovid's sensual tales of metamorphosis and pagan gods were presented as veiled allegories, and the famous story of Zeus descending to Danae in a shower of gold was explained as the soul receiving divine illumination. But in 1478 Botticelli finished Primavera, the first major project on a mythological theme for a thousand years, and by 1554 Titian completed a very different version of Danae - commissioned by a Cardinal, no less - where she expectantly awaits her union with Zeus in what is a nakedly sexual pose. What happened to bring the myths and eroticism of antiquity back into the culture of Europe? And how was it possible for a Church that was prosecuting for heresy, to exalt in pagan imagery, even in the Vatican itself?With Tom Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies, Birkbeck College, University of London; Charles Hope, Director of the Warburg Institute and Professor of the History of the Classical Tradition, University of London; Evelyn Welch, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary, University of London.

episodes.summaryAI: Revival of Greco-Roman pagan mythology and imagery in Renaissance European art and culture, from the medieval period through the sixteenth century. Ovid's mythological texts reinterpreted beyond Christian allegorical frameworks. Botticelli's Primavera (1478) as first major mythological painting in a millennium, Titian's erotically charged Danae (1554) commissioned by Catholic clergy. Tensions between Church authority prosecuting heresy and simultaneous papal patronage of pagan-themed art in the Vatican. Intersection of classical antiquity, Christian theology, artistic patronage, and cultural transformation in early modern Europe.

episodes.title: Pain
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss pain; something of which everyone has an individual experience. What causes it, how do we cope with it, what mechanisms are involved, what is the traditional view of pain and how is that being challenged today? Do we experience pain in the same way and how is emotional pain different from physical pain? What can our experience of pain tell us about ourselves and human consciousness? Is each individual human experience unique or are there experiences we can say apply across all of human consciousness? Is science a blunt instrument for examining subjective experience?With Patrick Wall, Professor of Physiology at St Thomas’ Hospital, London and author of Pain: The Science of Suffering; Semir Zeki, Professor of Neurobiology at University College, London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss pain; something of which everyone has an individual experience. What causes it, how do we cope with it, what mechanisms are involved, what is the traditional view of pain and how is that being challenged today? Do we experience pain in the same way and how is emotional pain different from physical pain? What can our experience of pain tell us about ourselves and human consciousness? Is each individual human experience unique or are there experiences we can say apply across all of human consciousness? Is science a blunt instrument for examining subjective experience?With Patrick Wall, Professor of Physiology at St Thomas’ Hospital, London and author of Pain: The Science of Suffering; Semir Zeki, Professor of Neurobiology at University College, London.
episodes.summaryAI: Neuroscience and philosophy of pain perception. Physiological mechanisms of pain signaling, neural pathways, and how the brain processes nociceptive data. Individual variation in pain experience and subjective thresholds. Distinction between physical pain and emotional suffering, their overlapping neural substrates. Pain as a window into consciousness and the hard problem of subjective experience. Limitations of scientific methodology in measuring and quantifying inherently subjective phenomena. Connections between sensory processing, psychology of suffering, and philosophy of mind.

episodes.title: Panpsychism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea that some kind of consciousness is present not just in our human brains but throughout the universe, right down to cells or even electrons. This is panpsychism and its proponents argue it offers a compelling alternative to those who say we are nothing but matter, like machines, and to those who say we are both matter and something else we might call soul. It is a third way. Critics argue panpsychism is implausible, an example of how not to approach this problem, yet interest has been growing widely in recent decades partly for the idea itself and partly in the broader context of understanding how consciousness arises.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea that some kind of consciousness is present not just in our human brains but throughout the universe, right down to cells or even electrons. This is panpsychism and its proponents argue it offers a compelling alternative to those who say we are nothing but matter, like machines, and to those who say we are both matter and something else we might call soul. It is a third way. Critics argue panpsychism is implausible, an example of how not to approach this problem, yet interest has been growing widely in recent decades partly for the idea itself and partly in the broader context of understanding how consciousness arises.
episodes.summaryAI: Panpsychism as a philosophical position on consciousness, proposing that mentality or proto-consciousness is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, extending to cells, atoms, and subatomic particles. Positioned as a third alternative between physicalist materialism and Cartesian mind-body dualism. Historical roots in ancient philosophy through contemporary philosophy of mind. Addresses the hard problem of consciousness and the combination problem of how micro-level experience aggregates into complex awareness. Recent resurgence of academic interest across philosophy and cognitive science.

episodes.title: Papal Infallibility
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss why, in 1870, the Vatican Council issued the decree ‘pastor aeternus’ which, among other areas, affirmed papal infallibility. It meant effectively that the Pope could not err in his teachings, an assertion with its roots in the early Church when the bishop of Rome advanced to being the first among equals, then overall head of the Christian Church in the West. The idea that the Pope could not err had been a double-edged sword from the Middle Ages, though; while it apparently conveyed great power, it also meant a Pope was constrained by whatever a predecessor had said. If a later Pope were to contradict an earlier Pope, then one of them must be wrong, and how could that be…if both were infallible?

It meant effectively that the Pope could not err in his teachings, an assertion with its roots in the early Church when the bishop of Rome advanced to being the first among equals, then overall head of the Christian Church in the West. The idea that the Pope could not err had been a double-edged sword from the Middle Ages, though; while it apparently conveyed great power, it also meant a Pope was constrained by whatever a predecessor had said. If a later Pope were to contradict an earlier Pope, then one of them must be wrong, and how could that be…if both were infallible?
episodes.summaryAI: Doctrine of papal infallibility formally defined at the First Vatican Council in 1870, declaring the Pope's ex cathedra teachings on faith and morals free from error. Historical development from the early Church through the gradual elevation of the Bishop of Rome to supreme authority over Western Christianity. Theological tensions and logical paradoxes when successive popes issue contradictory pronouncements. Relationship between papal authority, Church governance, doctrinal development, and ecclesiastical power structures within Catholic institutional history.

episodes.title: Parasitism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the relationship between parasites and hosts, where one species lives on or in another to the benefit of the parasite but at a cost to the host, potentially leading to disease or death of the host. Typical examples are mistletoe and trees, hookworms and vertebrates, cuckoos and other birds. In many cases the parasite species do so well in or on a particular host that they reproduce much faster and can adapt to changes more efficiently, and it is thought that almost half of all animal species have a parasitic stage in their lifetime. What techniques do hosts have to counter the parasites, and what impact do parasites have on the evolution of their hosts?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the relationship between parasites and hosts, where one species lives on or in another to the benefit of the parasite but at a cost to the host, potentially leading to disease or death of the host. Typical examples are mistletoe and trees, hookworms and vertebrates, cuckoos and other birds. In many cases the parasite species do so well in or on a particular host that they reproduce much faster and can adapt to changes more efficiently, and it is thought that almost half of all animal species have a parasitic stage in their lifetime. What techniques do hosts have to counter the parasites, and what impact do parasites have on the evolution of their hosts?
episodes.summaryAI: Parasitism as a pervasive biological relationship where one organism benefits at the expense of a host species. Examples spanning plants (mistletoe), invertebrates (hookworms), and birds (brood parasitism by cuckoos). Prevalence across animal kingdom with nearly half of species exhibiting parasitic life stages. Co-evolutionary dynamics between parasites and hosts including immune defenses, counter-adaptations, and evolutionary arms races. Impact on host population dynamics, behavior modification, and broader ecological and evolutionary processes. Connections between parasitology, evolutionary biology, and ecosystem functioning.

episodes.title: Pascal
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests begin a new series of the programme with a discussion of the French polymath Blaise Pascal. Born in 1623, Pascal was a brilliant mathematician and scientist, inventing one of the first mechanical calculators and making important discoveries about fluids and vacuums while still a young man. In his thirties he experienced a religious conversion, after which he devoted most of his attention to philosophy and theology. Although he died in his late thirties, Pascal left a formidable legacy as a scientist and pioneer of probability theory, and as one of seventeenth century Europe's greatest writers.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests begin a new series of the programme with a discussion of the French polymath Blaise Pascal. Born in 1623, Pascal was a brilliant mathematician and scientist, inventing one of the first mechanical calculators and making important discoveries about fluids and vacuums while still a young man. In his thirties he experienced a religious conversion, after which he devoted most of his attention to philosophy and theology. Although he died in his late thirties, Pascal left a formidable legacy as a scientist and pioneer of probability theory, and as one of seventeenth century Europe's greatest writers.
episodes.summaryAI: Blaise Pascal (1623-1662), French mathematician, physicist, inventor, philosopher, and theological writer. Mathematical contributions including probability theory foundations and work on combinatorics. Scientific discoveries concerning fluid dynamics, hydraulics, atmospheric pressure, and the existence of vacuums, challenging Aristotelian physics. Invention of the Pascaline mechanical calculator as early computing technology. Religious conversion to Jansenism leading to philosophical works including the Pensees and Provincial Letters. Pascal's Wager as foundational argument in philosophy of religion. Legacy spanning mathematics, experimental physics, engineering, and seventeenth-century French literature.

episodes.title: Pastoral Literature
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss pastoral literature.Come live with me and be my love, And we will all the pleasures prove That valleys, groves, hills, and fields, Woods or steepy mountain yields.And we will sit upon the rocks, Seeing the shepherds feed their flocks, By shallow rivers to whose falls Melodious birds sing madrigals. An entreaty from Christopher Marlowe's Passionate Shepherd to His Love - thought by many to be the crowning example of Elizabethan pastoral poetry. The traditions of pastoral poetry, literature and drama can be traced back to the third century BC and have principally offered a conventionalised picture of rural life, the naturalness and innocence of which is seen to contrast favourably with the corruption and artificialities of city and court life. Pastoral literature deals with tensions between nature and art, the real and the ideal, the actual and the mythical, and although pastoral works have been written from the point of view of shepherds or rustics, they have often been penned by highly sophisticated, urban poets and playwrights. But to what extent does pastoral literature represent a continuous yearning for a non-existent Golden Age of Innocence? How far did it evolve to reflect the social and political preoccupations of its times and what were the real meanings of its much used metaphors of town and country? With Helen Cooper, Professor of Medieval and Renaissance English at the University of Cambridge; Laurence Lerner, former Professor of English at the University of Sussex; Julie Sanders, Professor of English Literature and Drama at the University of Nottingham.

An entreaty from Christopher Marlowe's Passionate Shepherd to His Love - thought by many to be the crowning example of Elizabethan pastoral poetry. The traditions of pastoral poetry, literature and drama can be traced back to the third century BC and have principally offered a conventionalised picture of rural life, the naturalness and innocence of which is seen to contrast favourably with the corruption and artificialities of city and court life. Pastoral literature deals with tensions between nature and art, the real and the ideal, the actual and the mythical, and although pastoral works have been written from the point of view of shepherds or rustics, they have often been penned by highly sophisticated, urban poets and playwrights. But to what extent does pastoral literature represent a continuous yearning for a non-existent Golden Age of Innocence? How far did it evolve to reflect the social and political preoccupations of its times and what were the real meanings of its much used metaphors of town and country? With Helen Cooper, Professor of Medieval and Renaissance English at the University of Cambridge; Laurence Lerner, former Professor of English at the University of Sussex; Julie Sanders, Professor of English Literature and Drama at the University of Nottingham.
episodes.summaryAI: Pastoral literature as a Western literary tradition from Theocritus in third-century BC Greece through Virgil's Eclogues to Elizabethan English poetry. Idealized depictions of rural shepherds' lives contrasting with urban and courtly corruption. Central tensions between nature and artifice, simplicity and sophistication, the real and the ideal. Paradox of sophisticated urban poets composing nostalgic rural verse. Marlowe's Passionate Shepherd to His Love as exemplary Elizabethan pastoral. Genre's influence across poetry, drama, and prose romance spanning classical antiquity to early modern Europe.

episodes.title: Paul Dirac
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the theoretical physicist Dirac (1902-1984), whose achievements far exceed his general fame. To his peers, he was ranked with Einstein and, when he moved to America in his retirement, he was welcomed as if he were Shakespeare. Born in Bristol, he trained as an engineer before developing theories in his twenties that changed the understanding of quantum mechanics, bringing him a Nobel Prize in 1933 which he shared with Erwin Schrödinger. He continued to make deep contributions, bringing abstract maths to physics, beyond predicting anti-particles as he did in his Dirac Equation.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the theoretical physicist Dirac (1902-1984), whose achievements far exceed his general fame. To his peers, he was ranked with Einstein and, when he moved to America in his retirement, he was welcomed as if he were Shakespeare. Born in Bristol, he trained as an engineer before developing theories in his twenties that changed the understanding of quantum mechanics, bringing him a Nobel Prize in 1933 which he shared with Erwin Schrödinger. He continued to make deep contributions, bringing abstract maths to physics, beyond predicting anti-particles as he did in his Dirac Equation.
episodes.summaryAI: Paul Dirac (1902-1984), British theoretical physicist whose contributions to quantum mechanics rank alongside Einstein's work in modern physics. Engineering training informing his mathematical approach. Developed the Dirac equation in his twenties, unifying quantum mechanics with special relativity and predicting the existence of antimatter (positrons). Nobel Prize in Physics 1933 shared with Schrodinger. Pioneered quantum electrodynamics and quantum field theory. Brought rigorous abstract mathematical formalism into theoretical physics, transforming the discipline's methodology. Connections between atomic theory, particle physics, and mathematical foundations of modern physics.

episodes.title: Paul Erdős
episodes.synopsis: Paul Erdős (1913 – 1996) is one of the most celebrated mathematicians of the 20th century. During his long career, he made a number of impressive advances in our understanding of maths and developed whole new fields in the subject.
episodes.description: Paul Erdős (1913 – 1996) is one of the most celebrated mathematicians of the 20th century. During his long career, he made a number of impressive advances in our understanding of maths and developed whole new fields in the subject.
episodes.summaryAI: Paul Erdos (1913-1996), Hungarian mathematician renowned as one of the most prolific and celebrated mathematicians of the twentieth century. Itinerant lifestyle collaborating across institutions worldwide. Pioneering contributions to number theory, combinatorics, graph theory, set theory, and probabilistic methods in mathematics. Developed entirely new fields and mathematical techniques. The Erdos number system reflecting unprecedented breadth of collaboration. Connections between pure mathematics, discrete mathematics, and the social practice of mathematical research and collaboration.

episodes.title: Paul von Hindenburg
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and role of one of the most significant figures in early 20th Century German history. Paul von Hindenburg (1847-1934) had been famous since 1914 as the victorious commander at the Battle of Tannenberg against Russian invaders, soon burnishing this fame on the Western Front and Hindenburg was to claim he would have won there too, if enemies at home had not 'stabbed Germany in the back'. He won Germany’s Presidential election twice during the Weimar Republic, as a candidate of national unity and, while he gained his second term as a ‘stop Hitler’ candidate, President Hindenburg was to appoint Hitler as Chancellor and transfer some of his charisma onto him – a move so disastrous that Germans were later to ask if the myth of Hindenburg had always been an illusion.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and role of one of the most significant figures in early 20th Century German history. Paul von Hindenburg (1847-1934) had been famous since 1914 as the victorious commander at the Battle of Tannenberg against Russian invaders, soon burnishing this fame on the Western Front and Hindenburg was to claim he would have won there too, if enemies at home had not 'stabbed Germany in the back'. He won Germany’s Presidential election twice during the Weimar Republic, as a candidate of national unity and, while he gained his second term as a ‘stop Hitler’ candidate, President Hindenburg was to appoint Hitler as Chancellor and transfer some of his charisma onto him – a move so disastrous that Germans were later to ask if the myth of Hindenburg had always been an illusion.
episodes.summaryAI: Paul von Hindenburg (1847-1934), German military commander and statesman. Rose to national hero status through the 1914 Battle of Tannenberg on the Eastern Front in World War I. Promoted the Dolchstosslegende (stab-in-the-back myth) blaming civilians for Germany's Western Front defeat. Elected President of the Weimar Republic twice during the interwar period. His 1933 appointment of Adolf Hitler as Chancellor legitimized Nazi power by transferring Hindenburg's conservative military prestige to the Nazi movement. Intersection of German militarism, Weimar democracy, and the collapse into totalitarianism.

episodes.title: Pauli's Exclusion Principle
episodes.synopsis: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this fifth of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss a key figure from quantum mechanics.
episodes.description: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this fifth of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss a key figure from quantum mechanics.
episodes.summaryAI: Wolfgang Pauli's exclusion principle, a foundational rule of quantum mechanics stating that no two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously. Explains the structure of the periodic table, electron shell configurations, and chemical bonding properties of elements. Underpins understanding of atomic stability, the behavior of matter in extreme conditions such as white dwarf stars and neutron stars, and the distinction between fermions and bosons. Connections between quantum theory, atomic physics, chemistry, condensed matter physics, and astrophysics.

episodes.title: Penicillin
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928. It is said he noticed some blue-green penicillium mould on an uncovered petri dish at his hospital laboratory, and that this mould had inhibited bacterial growth around it. After further work, Fleming filtered a broth of the mould and called that penicillin, hoping it would be useful as a disinfectant. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain later shared a Nobel Prize in Medicine with Fleming, for their role in developing a way of mass-producing the life-saving drug. Evolutionary theory predicted the risk of resistance from the start and, almost from the beginning of this 'golden age' of antibacterials, scientists have been looking for ways to extend the lifespan of antibiotics.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928. It is said he noticed some blue-green penicillium mould on an uncovered petri dish at his hospital laboratory, and that this mould had inhibited bacterial growth around it. After further work, Fleming filtered a broth of the mould and called that penicillin, hoping it would be useful as a disinfectant. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain later shared a Nobel Prize in Medicine with Fleming, for their role in developing a way of mass-producing the life-saving drug. Evolutionary theory predicted the risk of resistance from the start and, almost from the beginning of this 'golden age' of antibacterials, scientists have been looking for ways to extend the lifespan of antibiotics.
episodes.summaryAI: Discovery and development of penicillin as the first major antibiotic. Alexander Fleming's 1928 observation of Penicillium mould inhibiting bacterial growth on a petri dish. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain's critical work mass-producing penicillin for clinical use during World War II, sharing the 1945 Nobel Prize with Fleming. Transformative impact on medicine, surgery, and infectious disease treatment. Evolutionary biology predicting antibiotic resistance from the outset. Growing crisis of antimicrobial resistance and scientific efforts to extend antibiotic efficacy. Connections between microbiology, pharmacology, public health, and evolutionary theory.

episodes.title: Perception and the Senses
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss perception: how the brain reacts to the mass of data continually crowding it. Barry Stein's laboratory at Wake Forest University in the United States found that the shape of a right angle drawn on the hand of a chimpanzee starts the visual part of the brain working, even when the shape has not been seen. It has also been discovered that babies learn by touch before they can properly make sense of visual data, and that the senses of smell and taste chemically combine to give us flavour.Perception is a tangled web of processes and so much of what we see, hear and touch is determined by our own expectations that it raises the question of whether we ever truly perceive what others do.What governs our perception of the world? And are we correct to distinguish between sight, sound, smell, touch and taste when they appear to influence each other so very much?With Richard Gregory, Senior Research Fellow in the Department of Experimental Psychology, Bristol University; David Moore, Director of the Medical Research Council Institute of Hearing Research, University of Nottingham; Gemma Calvert, Reader in Cognitive Neuroscience, University of Bath.

Barry Stein's laboratory at Wake Forest University in the United States found that the shape of a right angle drawn on the hand of a chimpanzee starts the visual part of the brain working, even when the shape has not been seen. It has also been discovered that babies learn by touch before they can properly make sense of visual data, and that the senses of smell and taste chemically combine to give us flavour.Perception is a tangled web of processes and so much of what we see, hear and touch is determined by our own expectations that it raises the question of whether we ever truly perceive what others do.What governs our perception of the world? And are we correct to distinguish between sight, sound, smell, touch and taste when they appear to influence each other so very much?With Richard Gregory, Senior Research Fellow in the Department of Experimental Psychology, Bristol University; David Moore, Director of the Medical Research Council Institute of Hearing Research, University of Nottingham; Gemma Calvert, Reader in Cognitive Neuroscience, University of Bath.
episodes.summaryAI: Neuroscience and philosophy of sensory perception. How the brain integrates and interprets data from multiple sensory modalities including vision, touch, smell, taste, and hearing. Cross-modal interactions such as tactile stimulation activating visual cortex. Developmental aspects of perception in infants, where haptic learning precedes visual processing. Multisensory integration in flavor perception combining olfactory and gustatory inputs. Questioning whether the traditional five-sense classification reflects neural reality. Subjective variation in perceptual experience between individuals. Connections between cognitive science, developmental psychology, consciousness studies, and philosophy of mind.

episodes.title: Pericles
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Pericles (495-429BC), the statesman who dominated the politics of Athens for thirty years, the so-called Age of Pericles, when the city’s cultural life flowered, its democracy strengthened as its empire grew, and the Acropolis was adorned with the Parthenon. In 431 BC he gave a funeral oration for those Athenians who had already died in the new war with Sparta which has been celebrated as one of the greatest speeches of all time, yet within two years he was dead from a plague made worse by Athenians crowding into their city to avoid attacks. Thucydides, the historian, knew him and was in awe of him, yet few shared that view until the nineteenth century, when they found much in Pericles to praise, an example for the Victorian age.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Pericles (495-429BC), the statesman who dominated the politics of Athens for thirty years, the so-called Age of Pericles, when the city’s cultural life flowered, its democracy strengthened as its empire grew, and the Acropolis was adorned with the Parthenon. In 431 BC he gave a funeral oration for those Athenians who had already died in the new war with Sparta which has been celebrated as one of the greatest speeches of all time, yet within two years he was dead from a plague made worse by Athenians crowding into their city to avoid attacks. Thucydides, the historian, knew him and was in awe of him, yet few shared that view until the nineteenth century, when they found much in Pericles to praise, an example for the Victorian age.
episodes.summaryAI: Pericles (c. 495-429 BC), dominant Athenian statesman during the classical golden age. Strengthened democratic institutions and expanded Athenian imperial power across the Aegean. Patron of monumental architecture including the Parthenon on the Acropolis. His funeral oration, recorded by Thucydides, articulated democratic ideals and became one of antiquity's most celebrated speeches. Death during the Athenian plague that devastated the city early in the Peloponnesian War. Intersection of Athenian democracy, Greek imperialism, classical art and architecture, rhetoric, and fifth-century BC Mediterranean political history.

episodes.title: Perpetual Motion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise of the idea of perpetual motion and its decline, in the 19th Century, with the Laws of Thermodynamics. For hundreds of years, some of the greatest names in science thought there might be machines that could power themselves endlessly. Leonardo Da Vinci tested the idea of a constantly-spinning wheel and Robert Boyle tried to recirculate water from a draining flask. Gottfried Leibniz supported a friend, Orffyreus, who claimed he had built an ever-rotating wheel. An increasing number of scientists voiced their doubts about perpetual motion, from the time of Galileo, but none could prove it was impossible. For scientists, the designs were a way of exploring the laws of nature. Others claimed their inventions actually worked, and promised a limitless supply of energy. It was not until the 19th Century that the picture became clearer, with the experiments of James Joule and Robert Mayer on the links between heat and work, and the establishment of the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics.

For hundreds of years, some of the greatest names in science thought there might be machines that could power themselves endlessly. Gottfried Leibniz supported a friend, Orffyreus, who claimed he had built an ever-rotating wheel.
