For scientists, the designs were a way of exploring the laws of nature. Others claimed their inventions actually worked, and promised a limitless supply of energy. It was not until the 19th Century that the picture became clearer, with the experiments of James Joule and Robert Mayer on the links between heat and work, and the establishment of the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics.
episodes.summaryAI: History of perpetual motion concepts from medieval engineering through nineteenth-century thermodynamics. Leonardo da Vinci's experimental tests with spinning wheels, Robert Boyle's attempts at self-circulating fluid systems, and Leibniz's endorsement of Johann Bessler (Orffyreus). Persistent failure to demonstrate perpetual motion machines despite centuries of attempts. Definitive refutation through nineteenth-century work by James Joule and Julius Robert von Mayer establishing the laws of thermodynamics, particularly conservation of energy and entropy. Intersection of mechanical engineering history, energy physics, and the development of foundational physical laws.

episodes.title: Persepolis
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the role of the great 'City of the Persians' founded by Darius I as the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire that stretched from the Indus Valley to Egypt and the coast of the Black Sea. It was known as the richest city under the sun and was a centre at which the Empire's subject peoples paid tribute to a succession of Achaemenid leaders, until the arrival of Alexander III of Macedon who destroyed it by fire supposedly in revenge for the burning of the Acropolis in Athens.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the role of the great 'City of the Persians' founded by Darius I as the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire that stretched from the Indus Valley to Egypt and the coast of the Black Sea. It was known as the richest city under the sun and was a centre at which the Empire's subject peoples paid tribute to a succession of Achaemenid leaders, until the arrival of Alexander III of Macedon who destroyed it by fire supposedly in revenge for the burning of the Acropolis in Athens.
episodes.summaryAI: Persepolis, ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire, founded by Darius I circa 515 BC. Center of the largest empire of the ancient world, stretching from the Indus Valley to Egypt and Libya. Architectural complex for receiving tribute from subject peoples, reflecting imperial ideology and multicultural artistic synthesis. Described as the richest city under the sun. Destruction by Alexander III of Macedon in 330 BC, reportedly in retaliation for Xerxes' burning of the Athenian Acropolis. Intersection of ancient Near Eastern civilization, imperial administration, monumental architecture, and Greco-Persian conflict.

episodes.title: Persuasion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Jane Austen’s last complete novel, which was published just before Christmas in 1817, five months after her death. It is the story of Anne Elliot, now 27 and (so we are told), losing her bloom, and of her feelings for Captain Wentworth who she was engaged to, 8 years before – an engagement she broke off under pressure from her father and godmother. When Wentworth, by chance, comes back into Anne Elliot's life, he is still angry with her and neither she nor Austen's readers can know whether it is now too late for their thwarted love to have a second chance.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Jane Austen’s last complete novel, which was published just before Christmas in 1817, five months after her death. It is the story of Anne Elliot, now 27 and (so we are told), losing her bloom, and of her feelings for Captain Wentworth who she was engaged to, 8 years before – an engagement she broke off under pressure from her father and godmother. When Wentworth, by chance, comes back into Anne Elliot's life, he is still angry with her and neither she nor Austen's readers can know whether it is now too late for their thwarted love to have a second chance.
episodes.summaryAI: Jane Austen's Persuasion, her last completed novel published posthumously in 1817. Story of Anne Elliot, aged twenty-seven, reunited with Captain Frederick Wentworth eight years after breaking their engagement under social pressure from her vain father Sir Walter and godmother Lady Russell. Themes of constancy versus prudence, class consciousness, naval meritocracy versus landed gentry, aging and lost opportunity, and the tension between individual feeling and social obligation. Austen's most mature exploration of women's limited autonomy in Regency-era English society. Connections between early nineteenth-century British literature, social hierarchy, and gender.

episodes.title: Peter Kropotkin
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Russian prince who became a leading anarchist and famous scientist. Kropotkin (1842 - 1921) was born into privilege, very much in the highest circle of Russian society as a pageboy for the Tsar, before he became a republican in childhood and dropped the title 'Prince'. While working in Siberia, he started reading about anarchism and that radicalised him further, as did his observations of Siberian villagers supporting each other without (or despite) a role for the State. He made a name for himself as a geographer but soon his politics landed him in jail in St Petersburg, from which he escaped to exile in England where he was fêted, with growing fame leading to lecture tours in the USA. His time in Siberia also inspired his ideas on the importance of mutual aid in evolution, a counter to the dominant idea from Darwin and Huxley that life was a gladiatorial combat in which only the fittest survived. Kropotkin became such a towering figure in public life that, returning to Russia, he was able to challenge Lenin without reprisal, and Lenin in turn permitted his enormous public funeral there, attended by 20,000 mourners.

Kropotkin (1842 - 1921) was born into privilege, very much in the highest circle of Russian society as a pageboy for the Tsar, before he became a republican in childhood and dropped the title 'Prince'. While working in Siberia, he started reading about anarchism and that radicalised him further, as did his observations of Siberian villagers supporting each other without (or despite) a role for the State. He made a name for himself as a geographer but soon his politics landed him in jail in St Petersburg, from which he escaped to exile in England where he was fêted, with growing fame leading to lecture tours in the USA. His time in Siberia also inspired his ideas on the importance of mutual aid in evolution, a counter to the dominant idea from Darwin and Huxley that life was a gladiatorial combat in which only the fittest survived. Kropotkin became such a towering figure in public life that, returning to Russia, he was able to challenge Lenin without reprisal, and Lenin in turn permitted his enormous public funeral there, attended by 20,000 mourners.
episodes.summaryAI: Peter Kropotkin (1842-1921), Russian aristocrat turned anarchist theorist and natural scientist. Renounced his princely title, embraced anarchism while conducting geographic research in Siberia. Made significant contributions to physical geography and glaciology. Developed mutual aid theory arguing that cooperation, not solely competition, drives evolution, directly challenging Social Darwinist interpretations of natural selection. Anarcho-communist political philosophy advocating decentralized voluntary communities. Challenged Lenin's Bolshevik authoritarianism without persecution. Intersection of evolutionary biology, political philosophy, anarchist thought, Russian revolutionary history, and geography.

episodes.title: Phenomenology
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss phenomenology, a style of philosophy developed by the German thinker Edmund Husserl in the first decades of the 20th century. Husserl's initial insights underwent a radical transformation in the work of his student Martin Heidegger, and played a key role in the development of French philosophy at the hands of writers like Emmanuel Levinas, Jean-Paul Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir and Maurice Merleau-Ponty.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss phenomenology, a style of philosophy developed by the German thinker Edmund Husserl in the first decades of the 20th century. Husserl's initial insights underwent a radical transformation in the work of his student Martin Heidegger, and played a key role in the development of French philosophy at the hands of writers like Emmanuel Levinas, Jean-Paul Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir and Maurice Merleau-Ponty.
episodes.summaryAI: Phenomenology as a philosophical movement founded by Edmund Husserl in early twentieth-century Germany. Husserl's method of bracketing natural attitudes to examine structures of consciousness and intentionality. Radical transformation by Martin Heidegger toward existential ontology and the question of Being. Transmission to French philosophy through Emmanuel Levinas, shaping Jean-Paul Sartre's existentialism, Simone de Beauvoir's existentialist feminism, and Maurice Merleau-Ponty's embodied perception theory. Connections between epistemology, ontology, philosophy of mind, existentialism, and twentieth-century continental European intellectual history.

episodes.title: Pheromones
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how members of the same species send each other invisible chemical signals to influence the way they behave. Pheromones are used by species across the animal kingdom in a variety of ways, such as laying trails to be followed, to raise the alarm, to scatter from predators, to signal dominance and to enhance attractiveness and, in honey bees, even direct development into queen or worker.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how members of the same species send each other invisible chemical signals to influence the way they behave. Pheromones are used by species across the animal kingdom in a variety of ways, such as laying trails to be followed, to raise the alarm, to scatter from predators, to signal dominance and to enhance attractiveness and, in honey bees, even direct development into queen or worker.
episodes.summaryAI: Pheromones as invisible chemical communication signals between members of the same species across the animal kingdom. Functions include trail-laying for navigation, alarm signaling, predator avoidance responses, dominance hierarchies, and sexual attractiveness. Role in social insect biology, particularly honey bees where pheromones determine caste development directing larvae toward queen or worker differentiation. Chemical ecology connecting intraspecific signaling to behavior, reproduction, social organization, and developmental biology. Intersection of biochemistry, animal behavior, evolutionary ecology, entomology, and endocrine-like regulatory mechanisms.

episodes.title: Philippa Foot
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most significant philosophers of the twentieth century, Philippa Foot (1920 - 2010). Her central question was, “Why be moral?” Drawing on Aristotle and Aquinas, Foot spent her life working through her instinct that there was something lacking in the prevailing philosophy of the 1950s and 1960s which held that values could only be subjective. Could there really be no objective response to the horrors of the concentration camps that she had seen on newsreels, no way of saying that such acts were morally wrong? Foot developed an ethics based on virtues, in which humans needed virtues to flourish as surely as plants needed light and water. While working through her ideas she explored applied ethics and the difference between doing something and letting it happen, an idea she illustrated with what became The Trolley Problem.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most significant philosophers of the twentieth century, Philippa Foot (1920 - 2010). Her central question was, “Why be moral?” Drawing on Aristotle and Aquinas, Foot spent her life working through her instinct that there was something lacking in the prevailing philosophy of the 1950s and 1960s which held that values could only be subjective. Could there really be no objective response to the horrors of the concentration camps that she had seen on newsreels, no way of saying that such acts were morally wrong? Foot developed an ethics based on virtues, in which humans needed virtues to flourish as surely as plants needed light and water. While working through her ideas she explored applied ethics and the difference between doing something and letting it happen, an idea she illustrated with what became The Trolley Problem.
episodes.summaryAI: Philippa Foot (1920-2010), British moral philosopher who challenged mid-twentieth-century moral subjectivism and emotivism dominant in analytic philosophy. Revived Aristotelian and Thomistic natural-law traditions to argue that moral judgments can be objectively grounded. Developed modern virtue ethics, proposing that humans need virtues to flourish just as biological organisms need favorable conditions. Created the Trolley Problem thought experiment, a foundational tool in applied ethics distinguishing acts from omissions, doing harm versus allowing harm. Her work bridges metaethics, normative theory, and practical moral reasoning.

episodes.title: Photosynthesis
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss photosynthesis, the process by which green plants and many other organisms use sunlight to synthesise organic molecules. Photosynthesis arose very early in evolutionary history and has been a crucial driver of life on Earth. In addition to providing most of the food consumed by organisms on the planet, it is also responsible for maintaining atmospheric oxygen levels, and is thus almost certainly the most important chemical process ever discovered.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss photosynthesis, the process by which green plants and many other organisms use sunlight to synthesise organic molecules. Photosynthesis arose very early in evolutionary history and has been a crucial driver of life on Earth. In addition to providing most of the food consumed by organisms on the planet, it is also responsible for maintaining atmospheric oxygen levels, and is thus almost certainly the most important chemical process ever discovered.
episodes.summaryAI: Photosynthesis, the biochemical process by which green plants, algae, and cyanobacteria convert sunlight into organic molecules using carbon dioxide and water. Arose very early in evolutionary history among prokaryotic organisms, fundamentally transforming Earth's atmosphere by generating and maintaining oxygen levels. Provides the primary energy source for nearly all food webs on Earth. Involves light-dependent reactions capturing photons and the Calvin cycle fixing carbon. Connects plant biology, biochemistry, atmospheric chemistry, evolutionary history, ecology, and global biogeochemical cycles including the carbon cycle and climate regulation.

episodes.title: Pi
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the most detailed number in nature. In the Bible's description of Solomon's temple it comes out as three, Archimedes calculated it to the equivalent of 14 decimal places and today's super computers have defined it with an extraordinary degree of accuracy to its first 1.4 trillion digits. It is the longest number in nature and we only need its first 32 figures to calculate the size of the known universe within the accuracy of one proton. We are talking about Pi, 3.14159 etc, the number which describes the ratio of a circle's diameter to its circumference. How has something so commonplace in nature been such a challenge for maths? And what does the oddly ubiquitous nature of Pi tell us about the hidden complexities of our world? With Robert Kaplan, co-founder of the Maths Circle at Harvard University, Eleanor Robson, Lecturer in the Department of History and Philosophy of Science at Cambridge University; and Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the most detailed number in nature. In the Bible's description of Solomon's temple it comes out as three, Archimedes calculated it to the equivalent of 14 decimal places and today's super computers have defined it with an extraordinary degree of accuracy to its first 1.4 trillion digits. It is the longest number in nature and we only need its first 32 figures to calculate the size of the known universe within the accuracy of one proton. We are talking about Pi, 3.14159 etc, the number which describes the ratio of a circle's diameter to its circumference. How has something so commonplace in nature been such a challenge for maths? And what does the oddly ubiquitous nature of Pi tell us about the hidden complexities of our world? With Robert Kaplan, co-founder of the Maths Circle at Harvard University, Eleanor Robson, Lecturer in the Department of History and Philosophy of Science at Cambridge University; and Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: History and mathematics of pi, the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter. Biblical approximation of three, Archimedes' geometric method yielding 14 decimal places, and modern supercomputer calculations reaching trillions of digits. Only 32 digits needed to calculate the size of the observable universe to the accuracy of one proton. Pi is irrational and transcendental, central to geometry, trigonometry, calculus, number theory, and mathematical analysis. Connects pure mathematics to applied physics, engineering, and the philosophical challenge of infinite non-repeating decimal representations.

episodes.title: Picasso's Guernica
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the context and impact of Pablo Picasso's iconic work, created soon after the bombing on 26th April 1937 that obliterated much of the Basque town of Guernica, and its people. The attack was carried out by warplanes of the German Condor Legion, joined by the Italian air force, on behalf of Franco's Nationalists. At first the Nationalists denied responsibility, blaming their opponents for creating the destruction themselves for propaganda purposes, but the accounts of journalists such as George Steer, and the prominence of Picasso's work, kept the events of that day under close scrutiny. Picasso's painting has gone on to become a symbol warning against the devastation of war.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the context and impact of Pablo Picasso's iconic work, created soon after the bombing on 26th April 1937 that obliterated much of the Basque town of Guernica, and its people. The attack was carried out by warplanes of the German Condor Legion, joined by the Italian air force, on behalf of Franco's Nationalists. At first the Nationalists denied responsibility, blaming their opponents for creating the destruction themselves for propaganda purposes, but the accounts of journalists such as George Steer, and the prominence of Picasso's work, kept the events of that day under close scrutiny. Picasso's painting has gone on to become a symbol warning against the devastation of war.
episodes.summaryAI: Pablo Picasso's Guernica (1937), monumental anti-war painting created in response to the bombing of the Basque town of Guernica by the German Condor Legion and Italian air forces supporting Franco's Nationalists during the Spanish Civil War. Nationalists initially denied responsibility for the attack. The painting became a universal symbol protesting the devastation of war and civilian suffering. Combines Cubist and Surrealist techniques in monochrome. Connects twentieth-century European political history, fascism, the Spanish Civil War, modern art movements, propaganda, and the relationship between art and political activism.

episodes.title: Pierre-Simon Laplace
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Laplace (1749-1827) who was a giant in the world of mathematics both before and after the French Revolution. He addressed one of the great questions of his age, raised but side-stepped by Newton: was the Solar System stable, or would the planets crash into the Sun, as it appeared Jupiter might, or even spin away like Saturn threatened to do? He advanced ideas on probability, long the preserve of card players, and expanded them out across science; he hypothesised why the planets rotate in the same direction; and he asked if the Universe was deterministic, so that if you knew everything about all the particles then you could predict the future. He also devised the metric system and reputedly came up with the name 'metre'.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Laplace (1749-1827) who was a giant in the world of mathematics both before and after the French Revolution. He addressed one of the great questions of his age, raised but side-stepped by Newton: was the Solar System stable, or would the planets crash into the Sun, as it appeared Jupiter might, or even spin away like Saturn threatened to do? He advanced ideas on probability, long the preserve of card players, and expanded them out across science; he hypothesised why the planets rotate in the same direction; and he asked if the Universe was deterministic, so that if you knew everything about all the particles then you could predict the future. He also devised the metric system and reputedly came up with the name 'metre'.
episodes.summaryAI: Pierre-Simon Laplace (1749-1827), French mathematician and astronomer who made foundational contributions to celestial mechanics, demonstrating the long-term stability of the Solar System. Advanced probability theory into a systematic mathematical framework. Proposed the nebular hypothesis explaining why planets orbit and rotate in the same direction. Raised the question of determinism in physics, positing a hypothetical intellect (Laplace's demon) that could predict all future states. Contributed to the development of the metric system. Bridges mathematics, astronomy, physics, philosophy of science, and Enlightenment-era French intellectual history.

episodes.title: Piers Plowman
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss William Langland's poem, written around 1370, about a man called Will who fell asleep on the Malvern Hills and dreamed of Piers the Plowman. This was a time between the Black Death and The Peasants’ Revolt, when Christians wanted to save their souls but doubted how best to do it - and had to live with that uncertainty. Some call this the greatest medieval poem in English, one offering questions not answers, and it can be as unsettling now as it was then.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss William Langland's poem, written around 1370, about a man called Will who fell asleep on the Malvern Hills and dreamed of Piers the Plowman. This was a time between the Black Death and The Peasants’ Revolt, when Christians wanted to save their souls but doubted how best to do it - and had to live with that uncertainty. Some call this the greatest medieval poem in English, one offering questions not answers, and it can be as unsettling now as it was then.
episodes.summaryAI: Piers Plowman, Middle English alliterative poem by William Langland, composed around 1370 during the period between the Black Death and the Peasants' Revolt in England. Structured as a dream vision following the narrator Will and the figure of Piers the Plowman. Explores Christian salvation, the corruption of clergy and secular institutions, social justice, labor, and the moral obligations of different social classes. Considered among the greatest medieval English poems alongside Chaucer. Connects fourteenth-century English literature, religious allegory, social criticism, medieval theology, and the crisis of faith amid plague and social upheaval.

episodes.title: Pitt-Rivers
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life and work of the Victorian anthropologist and archaeologist Augustus Pitt-Rivers. Over many years he amassed thousands of ethnographic and archaeological objects, some of which formed the founding collection of the Pitt Rivers Museum at Oxford University. Inspired by the work of Charles Darwin, Pitt-Rivers believed that human technology evolved in the same way as living organisms, and devoted much of his life to exploring this theory. He was also a pioneering archaeologist whose meticulous records of major excavations provided a model for later scholars.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life and work of the Victorian anthropologist and archaeologist Augustus Pitt-Rivers. Over many years he amassed thousands of ethnographic and archaeological objects, some of which formed the founding collection of the Pitt Rivers Museum at Oxford University. Inspired by the work of Charles Darwin, Pitt-Rivers believed that human technology evolved in the same way as living organisms, and devoted much of his life to exploring this theory. He was also a pioneering archaeologist whose meticulous records of major excavations provided a model for later scholars.
episodes.summaryAI: Augustus Pitt-Rivers (1827-1900), Victorian anthropologist and archaeologist who amassed thousands of ethnographic and archaeological objects, forming the founding collection of the Pitt Rivers Museum at Oxford. Influenced by Darwin, he organized artifacts typologically rather than geographically, arguing that human technology evolved incrementally like biological organisms. Pioneered systematic archaeological excavation methods emphasizing meticulous stratigraphic recording and documentation. Bridges nineteenth-century evolutionary theory, material culture studies, museum history, the development of archaeological methodology, Victorian imperialism, and the emergence of anthropology as a scientific discipline in Britain.

episodes.title: Plankton
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the tiny drifting organisms in the oceans that sustain the food chain for all the lifeforms in the water and so for the billions of people who, in turn, depend on the seas for their diet. In Earth's development, the plant-like ones among them, the phytoplankton, produced so much oxygen through photosynthesis that around half the oxygen we breathe today originated there. And each day as the sun rises, the animal ones, the zooplankton, sink to the depths of the seas to avoid predators in such density that they appear on ship sonars like a new seabed, only to rise again at night in the largest migration of life on this planet.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the tiny drifting organisms in the oceans that sustain the food chain for all the lifeforms in the water and so for the billions of people who, in turn, depend on the seas for their diet. In Earth's development, the plant-like ones among them, the phytoplankton, produced so much oxygen through photosynthesis that around half the oxygen we breathe today originated there. And each day as the sun rises, the animal ones, the zooplankton, sink to the depths of the seas to avoid predators in such density that they appear on ship sonars like a new seabed, only to rise again at night in the largest migration of life on this planet.
episodes.summaryAI: Plankton, the diverse community of tiny drifting organisms in the world's oceans forming the base of marine food chains. Phytoplankton are photosynthetic microorganisms producing approximately half of Earth's atmospheric oxygen. Zooplankton undertake diel vertical migration, sinking by day and rising at night in the largest mass migration of living organisms on Earth, detectable on sonar as a false seabed. Connects marine biology, oceanography, photosynthesis, atmospheric chemistry, carbon cycling, food web ecology, climate regulation, and the biogeochemical processes linking oceanic and atmospheric systems.

episodes.title: Plasma
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss plasma, the fourth state of matter after solid, liquid and gas. As over ninety-nine percent of all observable matter in the Universe is plasma, planets like ours, with so little plasma and so much solid, liquid and gas, appear all the more remarkable. On the grand scale, plasma is what the Sun is made from and, when we look into the night sky, almost everything we can see with the naked eye is made of plasma. On the smallest scale, here on Earth, scientists make plasma to etch the microchips on which we rely for so much. Plasma is in the fluorescent light bulbs above our heads and, in laboratories around the world, it is the subject of tests to create, one day, an inexhaustible and clean source of energy from nuclear fusion.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss plasma, the fourth state of matter after solid, liquid and gas. As over ninety-nine percent of all observable matter in the Universe is plasma, planets like ours, with so little plasma and so much solid, liquid and gas, appear all the more remarkable. On the grand scale, plasma is what the Sun is made from and, when we look into the night sky, almost everything we can see with the naked eye is made of plasma. On the smallest scale, here on Earth, scientists make plasma to etch the microchips on which we rely for so much. Plasma is in the fluorescent light bulbs above our heads and, in laboratories around the world, it is the subject of tests to create, one day, an inexhaustible and clean source of energy from nuclear fusion.
episodes.summaryAI: Plasma, the fourth state of matter distinct from solid, liquid, and gas, formed when sufficient energy ionizes atoms into free electrons and ions. Constitutes over 99 percent of observable matter in the universe, including stars and interstellar medium. The Sun is a plasma sphere. On Earth, plasma physics underpins fluorescent lighting, microchip fabrication through plasma etching, and experimental nuclear fusion energy research. Connects atomic physics, thermodynamics, astrophysics, stellar structure, electromagnetic phenomena, semiconductor manufacturing technology, and the pursuit of controlled fusion as an energy source.

episodes.title: Plate Tectonics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how the science of plate tectonics revolutionised our understanding of the planet on which we live. America is getting further away from Europe. This is not a political statement but a geological fact. Just as the Pacific is getting smaller, the Red Sea bigger, the Himalayas are still going up and one day the Horn of Africa will be a large island. This is the theory of plate tectonics, a revolutionary idea in 20th century geology that claimed the continents of Earth were dancing to the music of deep time. A dance of incredible slowness, yet powerful enough to throw up the mountains and pour away the oceans.Plate tectonics, the idea that the earth’s surface moved on a carpet of molten magma, constituted a genuine scientific revolution in geology. It explained why mountains appeared and why earth quakes occurred; it explained the curious distribution of fossils across the globe and finally solved the age old conundrum of why continents such as Africa and South America appeared to fit together like a giant jigsaw puzzle. Plate tectonics has made geologists, and many more besides, profoundly re-think what the Earth was, how it worked and how it related to all the things in it. With Richard Corfield, Visiting Senior Lecturer in Earth Sciences at the Open University; Joe Cann, Senior Fellow in the School of Earth and Environment at the University of Leeds; Lynne Frostick, Director of the Hull Environment Research Institute and Professor of Physical Geography at the University of Hull

Plate tectonics has made geologists, and many more besides, profoundly re-think what the Earth was, how it worked and how it related to all the things in it. With Richard Corfield, Visiting Senior Lecturer in Earth Sciences at the Open University; Joe Cann, Senior Fellow in the School of Earth and Environment at the University of Leeds; Lynne Frostick, Director of the Hull Environment Research Institute and Professor of Physical Geography at the University of Hull
episodes.summaryAI: Plate tectonics, the revolutionary twentieth-century geological theory explaining that Earth's lithosphere is divided into rigid plates moving on the asthenosphere. Accounts for continental drift, seafloor spreading, the formation of mountain ranges, earthquakes, volcanic activity, and the distribution of fossils across continents that once fit together. Builds on earlier continental drift hypotheses and mid-ocean ridge discoveries. Connects geology, geophysics, paleontology, oceanography, seismology, and Earth's deep-time history, fundamentally transforming understanding of the planet's surface processes, internal dynamics, and the long-term evolution of continents and ocean basins.

episodes.title: Plato's Atlantis
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Plato's account of the once great island of Atlantis out to the west, beyond the world known to his fellow Athenians, and why it disappeared many thousands of years before his time. There are no sources for this story other than Plato, and he tells it across two of his works, the Timaeus and the Critias, tantalizing his readers with evidence that it is true and clues that it is a fantasy. Atlantis, for Plato, is a way to explore what an ideal republic really is, and whether Athens could be (or ever was) one; to European travellers in the Renaissance, though, his story reflected their own encounters with distant lands, previously unknown to them, spurring generations of explorers to scour the oceans and in the hope of finding a lost world.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Plato's account of the once great island of Atlantis out to the west, beyond the world known to his fellow Athenians, and why it disappeared many thousands of years before his time. There are no sources for this story other than Plato, and he tells it across two of his works, the Timaeus and the Critias, tantalizing his readers with evidence that it is true and clues that it is a fantasy. Atlantis, for Plato, is a way to explore what an ideal republic really is, and whether Athens could be (or ever was) one; to European travellers in the Renaissance, though, his story reflected their own encounters with distant lands, previously unknown to them, spurring generations of explorers to scour the oceans and in the hope of finding a lost world.
episodes.summaryAI: Plato's account of Atlantis as told in his dialogues Timaeus and Critias, describing a powerful island civilization in the west that was destroyed. No independent sources corroborate the story. Plato likely used Atlantis as a philosophical device to explore the nature of ideal and corrupt republics. During the Renaissance, encounters with distant lands revived interest and spurred explorers to search for the lost civilization. Connects ancient Greek philosophy, political theory, utopian thought, the history of exploration, Renaissance intellectual culture, and the enduring interplay between myth and geography.

episodes.title: Plato's Gorgias
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of Plato's most striking dialogues, in which he addresses the real nature of power and freedom, and the relationship between pleasure and true self-interest. As he tests these ideas, Plato creates powerful speeches, notably from Callicles who claims that laws of nature trump man-made laws, that might is right, and that rules are made by weak people to constrain the strong in defiance of what is natural and proper. Gorgias is arguably the most personal of all of Plato's dialogues, with its hints of a simmering fury at the system in Athens that put his mentor Socrates to death, and where rhetoric held too much sway over people.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of Plato's most striking dialogues, in which he addresses the real nature of power and freedom, and the relationship between pleasure and true self-interest. As he tests these ideas, Plato creates powerful speeches, notably from Callicles who claims that laws of nature trump man-made laws, that might is right, and that rules are made by weak people to constrain the strong in defiance of what is natural and proper. Gorgias is arguably the most personal of all of Plato's dialogues, with its hints of a simmering fury at the system in Athens that put his mentor Socrates to death, and where rhetoric held too much sway over people.
episodes.summaryAI: Plato's Gorgias, a dialogue examining the real nature of power, freedom, rhetoric, and the relationship between pleasure and genuine self-interest. Features Callicles arguing that natural law supersedes human convention and that might is right, challenging Socratic ethics. Considered Plato's most personal dialogue, reflecting his anger at the Athenian political and legal system that condemned Socrates to death. Addresses whether rhetoric serves justice or merely enables manipulation. Connects ancient Greek philosophy, political theory, ethics, the nature of justice, the Socratic method, and Athenian democratic culture.

episodes.title: Plato's Republic
episodes.synopsis: Is it always better to be just than unjust? That is the central question of Plato's Republic, discussed here by Melvyn Bragg and guests. Writing in c380BC, Plato applied this question both to the individual and the city-state, considering earlier and current forms of government in Athens and potential forms, in which the ideal city might be ruled by philosophers. The Republic is arguably Plato's best known and greatest work, a dialogue between Socrates and his companions, featuring the allegory of the cave and ideas about immortality of the soul, the value of poetry to society, and democracy's vulnerability to a clever demagogue seeking tyranny.
episodes.description: Is it always better to be just than unjust? That is the central question of Plato's Republic, discussed here by Melvyn Bragg and guests. Writing in c380BC, Plato applied this question both to the individual and the city-state, considering earlier and current forms of government in Athens and potential forms, in which the ideal city might be ruled by philosophers. The Republic is arguably Plato's best known and greatest work, a dialogue between Socrates and his companions, featuring the allegory of the cave and ideas about immortality of the soul, the value of poetry to society, and democracy's vulnerability to a clever demagogue seeking tyranny.
episodes.summaryAI: Plato's Republic, foundational work of Western political philosophy and ethics asking whether it is always better to be just than unjust, applied to both individuals and the city-state. Examines forms of government including aristocracy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny. Proposes philosopher-kings as ideal rulers. Contains the Allegory of the Cave exploring knowledge versus illusion. Discusses the immortality of the soul, the value and danger of poetry, and democracy's vulnerability to demagogues seeking tyrannical power. Connects epistemology, metaphysics, ethics, political theory, education, and aesthetics in ancient Greek thought.

episodes.title: Plato's Symposium
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Plato's Symposium, one of the Greek philosopher's most celebrated works. Written in the 4th century BC, it is a dialogue set at a dinner party attended by a number of prominent ancient Athenians, including the philosopher Socrates and the playwright Aristophanes. Each of the guests speaks of Eros, or erotic love. This fictional discussion of the nature of love, how and why it arises and what it means to be in love, has had a significant influence on later thinkers, and is the origin of the modern notion of Platonic love.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Plato's Symposium, one of the Greek philosopher's most celebrated works. Written in the 4th century BC, it is a dialogue set at a dinner party attended by a number of prominent ancient Athenians, including the philosopher Socrates and the playwright Aristophanes. Each of the guests speaks of Eros, or erotic love. This fictional discussion of the nature of love, how and why it arises and what it means to be in love, has had a significant influence on later thinkers, and is the origin of the modern notion of Platonic love.
episodes.summaryAI: Plato's Symposium, a fourth-century BC philosophical dialogue set at an Athenian dinner party where guests deliver speeches on Eros and the nature of erotic love. Speakers include Aristophanes with his myth of divided souls and Socrates conveying Diotima's teaching on love as ascent toward the Form of Beauty. Origin of the modern concept of Platonic love as non-physical spiritual attachment. Connects ancient Greek philosophy, metaphysics, ethics, the philosophy of desire and beauty, Athenian social customs, and the literary form of the philosophical dialogue.

episodes.title: Pliny the Younger
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life and work of Pliny the Younger, famous for his letters. A prominent lawyer in Rome in the first century AD, Pliny later became governor of the province of Bithynia, on the Black Sea coast of modern Turkey. Throughout his career he was a prolific letter-writer, sharing his thoughts with great contemporaries including the historian Tacitus, and asking the advice of the Emperor Trajan. Pliny's letters offer fascinating insights into life in ancient Rome and its empire, from the mundane details of irrigation schemes to his vivid eyewitness account of the eruption of Vesuvius.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life and work of Pliny the Younger, famous for his letters. A prominent lawyer in Rome in the first century AD, Pliny later became governor of the province of Bithynia, on the Black Sea coast of modern Turkey. Throughout his career he was a prolific letter-writer, sharing his thoughts with great contemporaries including the historian Tacitus, and asking the advice of the Emperor Trajan. Pliny's letters offer fascinating insights into life in ancient Rome and its empire, from the mundane details of irrigation schemes to his vivid eyewitness account of the eruption of Vesuvius.
episodes.summaryAI: Pliny the Younger (c. 61-113 AD), prominent Roman lawyer, senator, and prolific letter-writer during the reigns of Domitian, Nerva, and Trajan. His published correspondence with Tacitus, Trajan, and others provides detailed insights into Roman daily life, legal practice, provincial governance, and social customs. Includes a famous eyewitness account of the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 AD and early Roman documentation of Christians. Connects Roman imperial history, Latin literature, epistolary tradition, natural disaster, Pompeii, Roman law and administration, and first-century Mediterranean society.

episodes.title: Pliny's Natural History
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Pliny's Natural History.Some time in the first century AD, the Roman scholar Pliny the Elder published his Naturalis Historia, or Natural History, an enormous reference work which attempted to bring together knowledge on every subject under the sun. The Natural History contains information on zoology, astronomy, geography, minerals and mining and - unusually for a work of this period - a detailed treatise on the history of classical art. It's a fascinating snapshot of the state of human knowledge almost two millennia ago.Pliny's 37-volume magnum opus is one of the most extensive works of classical scholarship to survive in its entirety, and was being consulted by scholars as late as the Renaissance. It had a significant influence on intellectual history, and has provided the template for every subsequent encyclopaedia
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Pliny's Natural History.Some time in the first century AD, the Roman scholar Pliny the Elder published his Naturalis Historia, or Natural History, an enormous reference work which attempted to bring together knowledge on every subject under the sun. The Natural History contains information on zoology, astronomy, geography, minerals and mining and - unusually for a work of this period - a detailed treatise on the history of classical art. It's a fascinating snapshot of the state of human knowledge almost two millennia ago.Pliny's 37-volume magnum opus is one of the most extensive works of classical scholarship to survive in its entirety, and was being consulted by scholars as late as the Renaissance. It had a significant influence on intellectual history, and has provided the template for every subsequent encyclopaedia
episodes.summaryAI: Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia (77 AD), an encyclopedic reference work compiling knowledge across zoology, botany, astronomy, geography, mineralogy, mining, medicine, and the history of classical art and sculpture. One of the most extensive works surviving from classical antiquity, it served as a template for subsequent encyclopedias throughout medieval and early modern periods. Reflects Roman attitudes toward the natural world and systematic knowledge organization. Connects natural history, ancient science, Roman intellectual culture, the encyclopedic tradition, classical art history, and the preservation and transmission of knowledge across civilizations.

episodes.title: Plutarch's Parallel Lives
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Greek biographer Plutarch (c46 AD-c120 AD) and especially his work 'Parallel Lives' which has shaped the way successive generations see the Classical world. Plutarch was clear that he was writing lives, not histories, and he wrote these very focussed accounts in pairs to contrast and compare the characters of famous Greeks and Romans, side by side, along with their virtues and vices. This focus on the inner lives of great men was to fascinate Shakespeare, who drew on Plutarch considerably when writing his Julius Caesar, Coriolanus, Timon of Athens and Antony and Cleopatra. While few followed his approach of setting lives in pairs, Plutarch's work was to influence countless biographers especially from the Enlightenment onwards.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Greek biographer Plutarch (c46 AD-c120 AD) and especially his work 'Parallel Lives' which has shaped the way successive generations see the Classical world. Plutarch was clear that he was writing lives, not histories, and he wrote these very focussed accounts in pairs to contrast and compare the characters of famous Greeks and Romans, side by side, along with their virtues and vices. This focus on the inner lives of great men was to fascinate Shakespeare, who drew on Plutarch considerably when writing his Julius Caesar, Coriolanus, Timon of Athens and Antony and Cleopatra. While few followed his approach of setting lives in pairs, Plutarch's work was to influence countless biographers especially from the Enlightenment onwards.
episodes.summaryAI: Plutarch (c. 46-120 AD), Greek biographer and moral philosopher from Chaeronea. His Parallel Lives pairs biographies of eminent Greeks and Romans to draw moral and political comparisons, shaping how successive generations understood the classical world. Directly influenced Shakespeare's Julius Caesar, Coriolanus, Timon of Athens, and Antony and Cleopatra. Shaped Enlightenment-era biographical writing and political thought. Connects ancient Greek and Roman history, biographical literature, comparative ethics, Renaissance and early modern reception of classical texts, and the transmission of classical ideals into Western political and literary culture.

episodes.title: Pocahontas
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life of Pocahontas, the Native American woman who to English eyes became a symbol of the New World. During the colonisation of Virginia in the first years of the seventeenth century, Pocahontas famously saved the life of an English prisoner, John Smith. Later captured, she converted to Christianity, married a settler and travelled to England where she was regarded as a curiosity. She died in 1617 at the age of 22 and was buried in Gravesend; her story has fascinated generations on both sides of the Atlantic, and has been reinterpreted and retold by many writers and artists.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life of Pocahontas, the Native American woman who to English eyes became a symbol of the New World. During the colonisation of Virginia in the first years of the seventeenth century, Pocahontas famously saved the life of an English prisoner, John Smith. Later captured, she converted to Christianity, married a settler and travelled to England where she was regarded as a curiosity. She died in 1617 at the age of 22 and was buried in Gravesend; her story has fascinated generations on both sides of the Atlantic, and has been reinterpreted and retold by many writers and artists.
episodes.summaryAI: Pocahontas (c. 1596-1617), Native American woman of the Powhatan confederacy in Virginia who became a symbolic figure in early Anglo-American colonial history. Reportedly saved the life of English colonist John Smith at Jamestown. Captured by English settlers, converted to Christianity, married tobacco planter John Rolfe, and traveled to England where she died at age 22 and was buried in Gravesend. Her story has been repeatedly reinterpreted by writers and artists. Connects early colonial American history, Native American-European contact, cultural mythology, religious conversion, and transatlantic exchange.

episodes.title: Polidori's The Vampyre
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the influential novella of John Polidori (1795-1821) published in 1819 and attributed first to Lord Byron (1788-1824) who had started a version of it in 1816 at the Villa Diodati in the Year Without A Summer. There Byron, his personal physician Polidori, Mary and Percy Shelley and Claire Clairmont had whiled away the weeks of miserable weather by telling ghost stories, famously giving rise to Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein'. Emerging soon after, 'The Vampyre' thrilled readers with its aristocratic Lord Ruthven who glutted his thirst with the blood of his victims, his status an abrupt change from the stories of peasant vampires of eastern and central Europe that had spread in the 18th Century with the expansion of the Austro-Hungarian empire. The connection with Lord Byron gave the novella a boost, and soon 'The Vampyre' spawned West End plays, penny dreadfuls such as 'Varney the Vampire', Bram Stoker’s 'Dracula', F.W Murnau's film 'Nosferatu A Symphony of Horror', and countless others.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the influential novella of John Polidori (1795-1821) published in 1819 and attributed first to Lord Byron (1788-1824) who had started a version of it in 1816 at the Villa Diodati in the Year Without A Summer. There Byron, his personal physician Polidori, Mary and Percy Shelley and Claire Clairmont had whiled away the weeks of miserable weather by telling ghost stories, famously giving rise to Mary Shelley's 'Frankenstein'. Emerging soon after, 'The Vampyre' thrilled readers with its aristocratic Lord Ruthven who glutted his thirst with the blood of his victims, his status an abrupt change from the stories of peasant vampires of eastern and central Europe that had spread in the 18th Century with the expansion of the Austro-Hungarian empire. The connection with Lord Byron gave the novella a boost, and soon 'The Vampyre' spawned West End plays, penny dreadfuls such as 'Varney the Vampire', Bram Stoker’s 'Dracula', F.W Murnau's film 'Nosferatu A Symphony of Horror', and countless others.
episodes.summaryAI: John Polidori's The Vampyre (1819), a novella featuring the aristocratic Lord Ruthven who preys on companions and drains their blood. Arose from the famous ghost story sessions at Villa Diodati in 1816 alongside Mary Shelley's Frankenstein. Initially misattributed to Lord Byron, on whom Lord Ruthven was partly modeled. Spawned a vast lineage of vampire fiction including West End theatrical adaptations, penny dreadfuls, Bram Stoker's Dracula, and Murnau's film Nosferatu. Connects Romantic-era literature, Gothic fiction, nineteenth-century popular culture, and the literary origins of the modern vampire genre.

episodes.title: Politeness
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea of Politeness. A new idea that stalked the land at the start of the eighteenth century in Britain, Politeness soon acquired a philosophy, a literature and even a society devoted to its thrall. It may seem to represent the very opposite now, but at that time, when Queen Anne was on the throne and The Spectator was in the coffee houses, politeness was part of a radical social revolution.How did the idea of politeness challenge the accepted norms of behaviour? How did a notion of how to behave affect the great wealth of eighteenth century culture? With Amanda Vickery, Reader in History at Royal Holloway, University of London; David Wootton, Professor of History at the University of York; John Mullan, Senior Lecturer in English at University College London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea of Politeness. A new idea that stalked the land at the start of the eighteenth century in Britain, Politeness soon acquired a philosophy, a literature and even a society devoted to its thrall. It may seem to represent the very opposite now, but at that time, when Queen Anne was on the throne and The Spectator was in the coffee houses, politeness was part of a radical social revolution.How did the idea of politeness challenge the accepted norms of behaviour? How did a notion of how to behave affect the great wealth of eighteenth century culture? With Amanda Vickery, Reader in History at Royal Holloway, University of London; David Wootton, Professor of History at the University of York; John Mullan, Senior Lecturer in English at University College London.
episodes.summaryAI: Politeness as a new cultural and philosophical concept in early eighteenth-century Britain during the reign of Queen Anne. Promoted through periodicals like the Spectator in coffee house culture, politeness encompassed refined manners, conversational civility, and engagement with philosophy, literature, and the arts. Represented a radical social revolution reshaping class interactions and public discourse beyond aristocratic circles. Connects eighteenth-century British social history, Enlightenment cultural values, the rise of print culture and periodicals, the public sphere, manners and social norms, and the relationship between behavior, taste, and societal transformation.

episodes.title: Politics in the 20th Century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg talks to Gore Vidal and Alan Clarke about the future of the nation-state; is the concept dead and buried? And what is the relationship between politics and morality - have salaciousness and self-righteousness taken over where seriousness of intent and a strong nerve left off, or was it ever thus? With Gore Vidal, American writer, commentator and author of The Smithsonian Institution; Alan Clarke, historian, politician and author of The Tories: Conservatives and the Nation State, 1922-97.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg talks to Gore Vidal and Alan Clarke about the future of the nation-state; is the concept dead and buried? And what is the relationship between politics and morality - have salaciousness and self-righteousness taken over where seriousness of intent and a strong nerve left off, or was it ever thus? With Gore Vidal, American writer, commentator and author of The Smithsonian Institution; Alan Clarke, historian, politician and author of The Tories: Conservatives and the Nation State, 1922-97.
episodes.summaryAI: Twentieth-century political transformation, examining the future viability of the nation-state as a governing unit. Explores the evolving relationship between politics and morality, questioning whether democratic governance has been degraded by scandal culture, media sensationalism, and moral posturing. Addresses tensions between political pragmatism and ethical principle, the role of public scrutiny in democratic accountability, and whether salaciousness and self-righteousness have displaced substantive policy debate. Touches on political philosophy, democratic theory, and the intersection of personal conduct with public authority in Western liberal democracies.

episodes.title: Pollination
episodes.synopsis: Since plants have to mate and produce offspring while rooted to the spot, they have to be pollinated – by wind, water, or animals – most commonly insects. They use a surprising array of tricks to attract pollinators: striking colours, iridescent light effects, and enticing scents, to name but a few.
episodes.description: Since plants have to mate and produce offspring while rooted to the spot, they have to be pollinated – by wind, water, or animals – most commonly insects. They use a surprising array of tricks to attract pollinators: striking colours, iridescent light effects, and enticing scents, to name but a few.
episodes.summaryAI: Plant reproductive biology focusing on pollination mechanisms and co-evolutionary relationships between plants and their pollinators. Covers the three primary pollination vectors: wind (anemophily), water (hydrophily), and animal-mediated transfer (zoophily). Examines how sessile plants evolved elaborate adaptations including striking flower coloration, iridescent light effects, and volatile chemical scents to attract insect, bird, and mammal pollinators. Addresses the ecological significance of pollination for terrestrial ecosystems, plant biodiversity, agricultural food production, and the co-evolutionary dynamics shaping flower morphology and pollinator behavior.

episodes.title: Pope
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of Alexander Pope. His enemies – who were numerous - described him as a hunchbacked toad, twisted in body, twisted in mind, but Alexander Pope is without doubt one of the greatest poets of the English language. His acerbic wit and biting satire were the scourge of politicians, fellow writers and most especially the critics. He was the first Englishman to make a living from his pen, free from the shackles of patronage and flattery. Indeed, his sharp tongue meant he couldn’t go out walking without his Great Dane and a pair of loaded pistols. He was a ferocious businessman too, striking tough deals with his publishers, ensuring he kept control of his work and was well-rewarded for it. So how did Pope manage to transform himself from a crippled outsider into a major cultural and moral authority? How did he shape our ideas about what a “modern author” is? Does his work still have resonances today or is it too firmly embedded in the politics, cultural life and rivalries of the period?With John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Jim McLaverty, Professor of English at Keele University; Valerie Rumbold, Reader in English Literature at Birmingham University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of Alexander Pope. His enemies – who were numerous - described him as a hunchbacked toad, twisted in body, twisted in mind, but Alexander Pope is without doubt one of the greatest poets of the English language. His acerbic wit and biting satire were the scourge of politicians, fellow writers and most especially the critics. He was the first Englishman to make a living from his pen, free from the shackles of patronage and flattery. Indeed, his sharp tongue meant he couldn’t go out walking without his Great Dane and a pair of loaded pistols. He was a ferocious businessman too, striking tough deals with his publishers, ensuring he kept control of his work and was well-rewarded for it. So how did Pope manage to transform himself from a crippled outsider into a major cultural and moral authority? How did he shape our ideas about what a “modern author” is? Does his work still have resonances today or is it too firmly embedded in the politics, cultural life and rivalries of the period?With John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Jim McLaverty, Professor of English at Keele University; Valerie Rumbold, Reader in English Literature at Birmingham University.
episodes.summaryAI: Alexander Pope, major English poet of the early eighteenth century, known for acerbic wit and biting verse satire targeting politicians, literary critics, and cultural pretension. First English writer to sustain himself financially through poetry alone, pioneering professional authorship through shrewd business dealings with publishers. Works include The Rape of the Lock, The Dunciad, and Essay on Man. Pope's mastery of the heroic couplet, his role in Augustan literary culture, his physical disability, eccentric personal habits, and lasting influence on English satirical and didactic poetry.

episodes.title: Pope Joan
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a story that circulated widely in the middle ages about a highly learned woman who lived in the ninth century, dressed as a man, travelled to Rome, and was elected Pope.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a story that circulated widely in the middle ages about a highly learned woman who lived in the ninth century, dressed as a man, travelled to Rome, and was elected Pope.
episodes.summaryAI: Medieval legend of Pope Joan, a highly learned woman allegedly living in the ninth century who disguised herself as a man, traveled to Rome, rose through the Church hierarchy, and was elected Pope. The story circulated widely across medieval Europe in chronicles, sermons, and popular culture from roughly the thirteenth century onward. Examines the origins, transmission, and credibility of the legend, its relationship to medieval attitudes toward women, gender, clerical authority, and ecclesiastical power. Touches on medieval historiography, the construction of historical myth, and Church institutional responses to the narrative.

episodes.title: Popper
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most important philosophers of the 20th century, Karl Popper whose ideas about science and politics robustly challenged the accepted ideas of the day. He strongly resisted the prevailing empiricist consensus that scientists' theories could be proved true.Popper wrote: “The more we learn about the world and the deeper our learning, the more conscious, specific and articulate will be our knowledge of what we do not know, our knowledge of our ignorance”. He believed that even when a scientific principle had been successfully and repeatedly tested, it was not necessarily true. Instead it had simply not proved false, yet! This became known as the theory of falsification.He called for a clear demarcation between good science, in which theories are constantly challenged, and what he called “pseudo sciences” which couldn't be tested. His debunking of such ideologies led some to describe him as the “murderer of Freud and Marx”. He went on to apply his ideas to politics, advocating an Open Society. His ideas influenced a wide range of politicians, from those close to Margaret Thatcher, to thinkers in the Eastern Communist bloc and South America.So how did Karl Popper change our approach to the philosophy of science? How have scientists and philosophers made use of his ideas? And how are his theories viewed today? Are we any closer to proving scientific principles are “true”?With John Worrall, Professor of Philosophy of Science at the London School of Economics; Anthony O'Hear, Weston Professor of Philosophy at Buckingham University; Nancy Cartwright, Professor of Philosophy at the LSE and the University of California
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most important philosophers of the 20th century, Karl Popper whose ideas about science and politics robustly challenged the accepted ideas of the day. He strongly resisted the prevailing empiricist consensus that scientists' theories could be proved true.Popper wrote: “The more we learn about the world and the deeper our learning, the more conscious, specific and articulate will be our knowledge of what we do not know, our knowledge of our ignorance”. He believed that even when a scientific principle had been successfully and repeatedly tested, it was not necessarily true. Instead it had simply not proved false, yet! This became known as the theory of falsification.He called for a clear demarcation between good science, in which theories are constantly challenged, and what he called “pseudo sciences” which couldn't be tested. His debunking of such ideologies led some to describe him as the “murderer of Freud and Marx”. He went on to apply his ideas to politics, advocating an Open Society. His ideas influenced a wide range of politicians, from those close to Margaret Thatcher, to thinkers in the Eastern Communist bloc and South America.So how did Karl Popper change our approach to the philosophy of science? How have scientists and philosophers made use of his ideas? And how are his theories viewed today? Are we any closer to proving scientific principles are “true”?With John Worrall, Professor of Philosophy of Science at the London School of Economics; Anthony O'Hear, Weston Professor of Philosophy at Buckingham University; Nancy Cartwright, Professor of Philosophy at the LSE and the University of California
episodes.summaryAI: Karl Popper, major twentieth-century philosopher of science and political theorist. Developed the theory of falsification as the criterion distinguishing genuine scientific inquiry from pseudo-science, challenging logical positivist claims that scientific theories could be verified as true. Applied this demarcation principle to critique Marxism and Freudian psychoanalysis as unfalsifiable. Advocated the concept of the Open Society against totalitarianism, influencing both Western conservative thinkers like Thatcher and Eastern bloc dissidents. Contributions span epistemology, philosophy of science, and political philosophy connecting scientific method to democratic governance.

episodes.title: Pragmatism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the American philosophy of pragmatism. A pragmatist "turns away from abstraction and insufficiency, from verbal solutions, from bad apriori reasons, from fixed principles, closed systems, and pretended absolutes and origins. He turns towards concreteness and adequacy, towards facts, towards action and towards power". A quote from William James' 1907 treatise Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking. William James, along with John Dewey and Charles Sanders Peirce, was the founder of an American philosophical movement which flowered during the last thirty years of the nineteenth century and the first twenty years of the 20th century. It purported that knowledge is only meaningful when coupled with action. Nothing is true or false - it either works or it doesn't. It was a philosophy which was deeply embedded in the reality of life, concerned firstly with the individual's direct experience of the world he inhabited. In essence, practical application was all. But how did Pragmatism harness the huge scientific leap forward that had come with Charles Darwin's ideas on evolution? And how did this dynamic new philosophy challenge the doubts expressed by the Sceptics about the nature and extent of knowledge? Did Pragmatism influence the economic and political ascendancy of America in the early 20th century? And did it also pave the way for the contemporary preoccupation with post-modernism? With A C Grayling, Professor of Applied Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London and a Fellow of St Anne's College, Oxford; Julian Baggini, editor of The Philosophers' Magazine; Miranda Fricker, Lecturer in Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the American philosophy of pragmatism. A pragmatist "turns away from abstraction and insufficiency, from verbal solutions, from bad apriori reasons, from fixed principles, closed systems, and pretended absolutes and origins. He turns towards concreteness and adequacy, towards facts, towards action and towards power". A quote from William James' 1907 treatise Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking. William James, along with John Dewey and Charles Sanders Peirce, was the founder of an American philosophical movement which flowered during the last thirty years of the nineteenth century and the first twenty years of the 20th century. It purported that knowledge is only meaningful when coupled with action. Nothing is true or false - it either works or it doesn't. It was a philosophy which was deeply embedded in the reality of life, concerned firstly with the individual's direct experience of the world he inhabited. In essence, practical application was all. But how did Pragmatism harness the huge scientific leap forward that had come with Charles Darwin's ideas on evolution? And how did this dynamic new philosophy challenge the doubts expressed by the Sceptics about the nature and extent of knowledge? Did Pragmatism influence the economic and political ascendancy of America in the early 20th century? And did it also pave the way for the contemporary preoccupation with post-modernism? With A C Grayling, Professor of Applied Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London and a Fellow of St Anne's College, Oxford; Julian Baggini, editor of The Philosophers' Magazine; Miranda Fricker, Lecturer in Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: Pragmatism as a distinctly American philosophical tradition, developed by Charles Sanders Peirce, William James, and John Dewey from the late nineteenth century onward. Central thesis that knowledge derives meaning only through practical consequences and action, rejecting abstract metaphysical truth claims. Pragmatists held that ideas are instruments judged by whether they work, not by correspondence to fixed reality. Drew on Darwinian evolutionary theory to ground epistemology in adaptive human experience. Examines pragmatism's relationship to American economic and cultural ascendancy, its anticipation of post-modernist thought, and its challenge to European rationalist philosophy.

episodes.title: Prayer
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the purpose and effects of prayer. Why do people pray? What did prayer ever do, the cry goes up, for those millions upon millions of non-combatants, civilians, children, innocents, whose lives have been ended by a savage variety of brutality? Do we pray for the benefit of God or for our own sake? Is it a good Christian weapon as Martin Luther defined it and as Mahatma Gandhi put it the most potent instrument of action; or is prayer simply the most essential form of self analysis? Or was Ovid right to see prayer as a way of changing the mind of God, when he wrote in The Art of Love, Even the Gods are moved by the voice of entreaty. People have prayed since the dawn of language - but why, and has it done us any good?With Professor Russell Stannard, physicist, religious writer and author of The God Experiment; Andrew Samuels, Jungian analyst and Professor of Analytical Psychology, University of Essex.

Is it a good Christian weapon as Martin Luther defined it and as Mahatma Gandhi put it the most potent instrument of action; or is prayer simply the most essential form of self analysis? episodes.summaryAI: The nature, purpose, and history of prayer across religious traditions and human experience. Examines prayer as spiritual communication with the divine, personal self-analysis, and instrument of moral action. Covers theological perspectives from Martin Luther (prayer as Christian practice), Gandhi (prayer as active force), and classical sources like Ovid (prayer as petition to change divine will). Addresses prayer's origins reaching back to earliest human language, its psychological and social dimensions, the tension between petition and contemplation, and its function in both organized religion and individual spiritual life across cultures.

episodes.title: President Ulysses S Grant
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the impact of Grant's presidency on Americans in the years after the Civil War in which he, with Lincoln, had led the Union Army to victory. His predecessor, Andrew Johnson, was prepared to let the Southern States decide for themselves which rights to allow freed slaves; Grant supported equal rights, and he used troops and Enforcement Acts to defeat the Ku klux Klan which was violently suppressing African Americans. In later years Grant was remembered mainly for the corruption scandals under his terms of office, and for his failure to support or protect Native Americans, but in more recent decades his support for reconstruction has prompted a reassessement.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the impact of Grant's presidency on Americans in the years after the Civil War in which he, with Lincoln, had led the Union Army to victory. His predecessor, Andrew Johnson, was prepared to let the Southern States decide for themselves which rights to allow freed slaves; Grant supported equal rights, and he used troops and Enforcement Acts to defeat the Ku klux Klan which was violently suppressing African Americans. In later years Grant was remembered mainly for the corruption scandals under his terms of office, and for his failure to support or protect Native Americans, but in more recent decades his support for reconstruction has prompted a reassessement.
episodes.summaryAI: Presidency of Ulysses S. Grant (1869-1877) during American Reconstruction following the Civil War. Contrasts Grant's approach with predecessor Andrew Johnson, who allowed Southern states to restrict freed slaves' rights. Grant actively supported African American civil rights, deploying federal troops and enforcing the Enforcement Acts to suppress the Ku Klux Klan. Addresses the corruption scandals that marred his administration, his failures in policy toward Native Americans, and the recent historiographical reassessment elevating his legacy for championing racial equality during Reconstruction. Covers nineteenth-century American political history, race relations, and federal power.

episodes.title: Prester John
episodes.synopsis: In the Middle Ages, Prester John was seen as the great hope for Crusaders struggling to hold on to, then regain, Jerusalem. He was thought to rule a lost Christian kingdom somewhere in the East and was ready to attack Muslim opponents with his enormous armies. There was apparent proof of Prester John's existence, in letters purportedly from him and in stories from travelers who claimed they had met, if not him, then people who had news of him. Most pointed to a home in the earthly paradise in the Indies, outside Eden, with fantastical animals and unimaginable riches. Later, Portuguese explorers thought they had found him in Ethiopia, despite the mystified denials of people there. Melvyn Bragg asks why the legend was so strongly believed for so long, and what facts helped sustain the myths.
episodes.description: In the Middle Ages, Prester John was seen as the great hope for Crusaders struggling to hold on to, then regain, Jerusalem. Later, Portuguese explorers thought they had found him in Ethiopia, despite the mystified denials of people there. episodes.summaryAI: Medieval European legend of Prester John, a supposedly powerful Christian king ruling a lost kingdom in the East, poised to ally with Crusaders against Muslim opponents. Examines the forged letters purportedly from Prester John circulating from the twelfth century, travelers' accounts reinforcing the myth, and geographical speculation locating his kingdom in the Indies or as an earthly paradise. Portuguese explorers later identified Ethiopia as the realm. Addresses why the legend persisted for centuries, its role in Crusade ideology, European geographic imagination, Christian eschatology, and the Age of Exploration's motivations.

episodes.title: Prime Numbers
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss prime numbers: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17 … This sequence of numbers goes on literally forever. Recently, a team of researchers in Missouri successfully calculated the highest prime number - it has 9.1 million digits. For nearly two and a half thousand years, since Euclid first described the prime numbers in his book Elements, mathematicians have struggled to write a rule to predict what comes next in the sequence. The Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler feared that it is "a mystery into which the human mind will never penetrate." But others have been more hopeful... In the middle of the nineteenth century, the German mathematician Bernhard Riemann discovered a connection between prime numbers and a complex mathematical function called the 'zeta function'. Ever since, mathematicians have laboured to prove the existence of this connection and reveal the rules behind the elusive sequence. What exactly are prime numbers and what secrets might they unlock about our understanding of atoms? What are the rules that may govern the prime sequence? And is it possible that the person who proves Riemann's Hypothesis may bring about the collapse of the world financial system? With Marcus du Sautoy, Professor of Mathematics and Fellow of Wadham College at the University of Oxford; Robin Wilson, Professor of Pure Mathematics at the Open University and Gresham Professor of Geometry; Jackie Stedall, Junior Research Fellow in the History of Mathematics at Queen's College, Oxford.
