episodes.summaryAI: English physician and polymath 1605 to 1682 based in Norwich. Religio Medici reconciled scientific inquiry with Christian faith, gaining European readership. Pseudodoxia Epidemica systematically debunked popular errors using empirical method, bridging natural philosophy and early scientific scepticism. Hydriotaphia meditated on funerary customs, mortality, and archaeological evidence. Major contributor to English prose style and vocabulary, coining words including electricity, indigenous, medical, and carnivorous. Straddles medicine, natural history, theology, rhetoric, and antiquarianism, exemplifying seventeenth-century learned culture before disciplinary specialisation.

episodes.title: Sir Thomas Wyatt
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss 'the greatest poet of his age', Thomas Wyatt (1503 -1542), who brought the poetry of the Italian Renaissance into the English Tudor world, especially the sonnet, so preparing the way for Shakespeare and Donne. As an ambassador to Henry VIII and, allegedly, too close to Anne Boleyn, he experienced great privilege under intense scrutiny. Some of Wyatt's poems, such as They Flee From Me That Sometime Did Me Seek, are astonishingly fresh and conversational and yet he wrote them under the tightest constraints, when a syllable out of place could have condemned him to the Tower.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss 'the greatest poet of his age', Thomas Wyatt (1503 -1542), who brought the poetry of the Italian Renaissance into the English Tudor world, especially the sonnet, so preparing the way for Shakespeare and Donne. As an ambassador to Henry VIII and, allegedly, too close to Anne Boleyn, he experienced great privilege under intense scrutiny. Some of Wyatt's poems, such as They Flee From Me That Sometime Did Me Seek, are astonishingly fresh and conversational and yet he wrote them under the tightest constraints, when a syllable out of place could have condemned him to the Tower.
episodes.summaryAI: English poet and diplomat 1503 to 1542 at court of Henry VIII. Introduced Italian Renaissance poetic forms into English, especially the Petrarchan sonnet, adapting it toward the English sonnet structure later perfected by Shakespeare. Served as ambassador to Spain and Holy Roman Empire. Poetry circulated in manuscript, published posthumously in Tottel's Miscellany 1557. Work reflects tensions between courtly self-expression and political danger under Tudor absolutism. Alleged connection to Anne Boleyn illustrates intersection of literary culture, political intrigue, and personal risk in early modern England.

episodes.title: Slavery and Empire
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss slavery and empire; two themes that run right through this country’s history. Britain’s imperial project dominated at least the last three centuries of our national life. Its advocates claim it was a civilising mission by which Britain spread enlightenment and improvement across the globe. Opponents have long seen it as a brutal business, with Britons cast as cruel oppressors out to exploit a conquered world. Is our imperial history so clear cut? What if Britons were themselves captives, either as prisoners of an imperial enterprise that sucked them in, generation after generation or, in some startling cases, as slaves to foreign peoples? Is slavery an inevitable part of empire: does it come with the territory? And how did Britain finally shake it off? With Linda Colley, School Professor of History, LSE; Catherine Hall, Professor of Modern British Social and Cultural History, University College London; Felipe Fernandez Armesto, Professorial Research Fellow, Queen Mary College London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss slavery and empire; two themes that run right through this country’s history. Britain’s imperial project dominated at least the last three centuries of our national life. Its advocates claim it was a civilising mission by which Britain spread enlightenment and improvement across the globe. Opponents have long seen it as a brutal business, with Britons cast as cruel oppressors out to exploit a conquered world. Is our imperial history so clear cut? What if Britons were themselves captives, either as prisoners of an imperial enterprise that sucked them in, generation after generation or, in some startling cases, as slaves to foreign peoples? Is slavery an inevitable part of empire: does it come with the territory? And how did Britain finally shake it off? With Linda Colley, School Professor of History, LSE; Catherine Hall, Professor of Modern British Social and Cultural History, University College London; Felipe Fernandez Armesto, Professorial Research Fellow, Queen Mary College London.
episodes.summaryAI: British imperial history over at least three centuries through the lens of slavery. Explores tension between civilising mission rhetoric and brutal exploitation of colonised peoples. Considers cases where Britons themselves were captives, complicating victim-perpetrator narratives. Asks whether slavery is structurally inherent to empire-building. Traces British abolition movement and its motivations, whether moral, economic, or political. Covers Atlantic slave trade, colonial governance, abolitionist campaigns, and legacy debates. Connects political philosophy of liberty, economic systems of forced labour, and moral reform movements across imperial and post-imperial periods.

episodes.title: Slime Moulds
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss slime mould, a basic organism that grows on logs, cowpats and compost heaps. Scientists have found difficult to categorise slime mould: in 1868, the biologist Thomas Huxley asked: ‘Is this a plant, or is it an animal? Is it both or is it neither?’ and there is a great deal scientists still don’t know about it.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss slime mould, a basic organism that grows on logs, cowpats and compost heaps. Scientists have found difficult to categorise slime mould: in 1868, the biologist Thomas Huxley asked: ‘Is this a plant, or is it an animal? Is it both or is it neither?’ and there is a great deal scientists still don’t know about it.
episodes.summaryAI: Slime moulds as organisms defying standard biological classification. Found on decaying organic matter including logs, cowpats, and compost. Huxley's 1868 inquiry asked whether they were plant, animal, both, or neither, highlighting fundamental problems in taxonomy. Modern biology still struggles to categorise them definitively, with species spanning multiple kingdoms. Exhibit remarkable behaviours including collective problem-solving without nervous systems. Much remains unknown about their life cycles and ecology. Relevant to biological classification theory, cell biology, evolutionary biology, and the philosophical boundaries between life's major domains.

episodes.title: Social Darwinism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Social Darwinism. After the publication of Charles Darwin's masterpiece On the Origin of Species in 1859, some thinkers argued that Darwin's ideas about evolution could also be applied to human society. One thinker particularly associated with this movement was Darwin's near-contemporary Herbert Spencer, who coined the phrase 'survival of the fittest'. He argued that competition among humans was beneficial, because it ensured that only the healthiest and most intelligent individuals would succeed. Social Darwinism remained influential for several generations, although its association with eugenics and later adoption as an ideological position by Fascist regimes ensured its eventual downfall from intellectual respectability.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Social Darwinism. After the publication of Charles Darwin's masterpiece On the Origin of Species in 1859, some thinkers argued that Darwin's ideas about evolution could also be applied to human society. One thinker particularly associated with this movement was Darwin's near-contemporary Herbert Spencer, who coined the phrase 'survival of the fittest'. He argued that competition among humans was beneficial, because it ensured that only the healthiest and most intelligent individuals would succeed. Social Darwinism remained influential for several generations, although its association with eugenics and later adoption as an ideological position by Fascist regimes ensured its eventual downfall from intellectual respectability.
episodes.summaryAI: Ideological movement applying Darwinian natural selection to human societies after Origin of Species 1859. Herbert Spencer coined survival of the fittest, arguing competitive struggle among humans drives social progress. Used to justify laissez-faire economics, racial hierarchies, and imperial expansion. Connected to eugenics movement promoting selective breeding. Adopted by Fascist ideologies in twentieth century, leading to its intellectual discrediting. Covers intersections of evolutionary biology with political philosophy, economics, ethics, and racial theory. Illustrates how scientific ideas are appropriated and distorted for social and political purposes across nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

episodes.title: Socrates
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Greek philosopher Socrates, acknowledged as one of the founders of Western philosophy. Born in 469 BC into the golden age of the city of Athens, he has profoundly influenced philosophy ever since. In fact, his impact is so profound that all the thinkers who went before are simply known as pre-Socratic.In person Socrates was deliberately irritating, he was funny and he was rude; he didn’t like democracy very much and spent quite a lot of time in shoe shops. He claimed he was on a mission from God to educate his fellow Athenians but has left us nothing in his own hand because he refused to write anything down. With Angie Hobbs, Associate Professor of Philosophy at Warwick University; David Sedley, Laurence Professor of Ancient Philosophy at Cambridge University; Paul Millett, Senior Lecturer in Classics at the University of Cambridge.

Born in 469 BC into the golden age of the city of Athens, he has profoundly influenced philosophy ever since. In fact, his impact is so profound that all the thinkers who went before are simply known as pre-Socratic.In person Socrates was deliberately irritating, he was funny and he was rude; he didn’t like democracy very much and spent quite a lot of time in shoe shops. He claimed he was on a mission from God to educate his fellow Athenians but has left us nothing in his own hand because he refused to write anything down. With Angie Hobbs, Associate Professor of Philosophy at Warwick University; David Sedley, Laurence Professor of Ancient Philosophy at Cambridge University; Paul Millett, Senior Lecturer in Classics at the University of Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Greek philosopher born Athens 469 BC, foundational figure dividing Western philosophy into pre-Socratic and post-Socratic periods. Practised dialectical method of questioning in public spaces including shoe shops, claiming divine mission. Challenged democratic assumptions and conventional wisdom through persistent interrogation. Deliberately wrote nothing, known entirely through others especially Plato and Xenophon. Personality combined humour, rudeness, and moral seriousness. Trial and execution in 399 BC for impiety and corrupting youth became defining moment in philosophical history. Central to ethics, epistemology, and the origins of Western rational inquiry.

episodes.title: Socrates in Prison
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Plato's Crito and Phaedo, his accounts of the last days of Socrates in prison in 399 BC as he waited to be executed by drinking hemlock. Both works show Socrates preparing to die in the way he had lived: doing philosophy. In the Crito, Plato shows Socrates arguing that he is duty bound not to escape from prison even though a bribe would open the door, while in the Phaedo his argument is for the immortality of the soul which, at the point of death, might leave uncorrupted from the 'prison' of his body, the one escape that truly mattered to Socrates. His example in his last days has proved an inspiration to thinkers over the centuries and in no small way has helped ensure the strength of his reputation.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Plato's Crito and Phaedo, his accounts of the last days of Socrates in prison in 399 BC as he waited to be executed by drinking hemlock. Both works show Socrates preparing to die in the way he had lived: doing philosophy. His example in his last days has proved an inspiration to thinkers over the centuries and in no small way has helped ensure the strength of his reputation.
episodes.summaryAI: Plato's dialogues Crito and Phaedo depicting Socrates' final days in Athenian prison 399 BC. In Crito, Socrates argues he is duty-bound to accept the state's verdict rather than escape despite available bribery, establishing foundational arguments about civic obligation and rule of law. In Phaedo, argues for immortality of the soul and its liberation from the body at death. These texts shaped Western philosophy of law, political obligation, metaphysics of the soul, and the ideal of the philosophical life. Influenced Stoic, Christian, and Enlightenment thinkers for centuries.

episodes.title: Solar Wind
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the flow of particles from the outer region of the Sun which we observe in the Northern and Southern Lights, interacting with Earth's magnetosphere, and in comet tails that stream away from the Sun regardless of their own direction. One way of defining the boundary of the solar system is where the pressure from the solar wind is balanced by that from the region between the stars, the interstellar medium. Its existence was suggested from the C19th and Eugene Parker developed the theory of it in the 1950s and it has been examined and tested by a series of probes in C20th up to today, with more planned.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the flow of particles from the outer region of the Sun which we observe in the Northern and Southern Lights, interacting with Earth's magnetosphere, and in comet tails that stream away from the Sun regardless of their own direction. One way of defining the boundary of the solar system is where the pressure from the solar wind is balanced by that from the region between the stars, the interstellar medium. Its existence was suggested from the C19th and Eugene Parker developed the theory of it in the 1950s and it has been examined and tested by a series of probes in C20th up to today, with more planned.
episodes.summaryAI: Stream of charged particles flowing from the Sun's outer atmosphere into interplanetary space. Observable effects include auroras at Earth's poles and the shaping of comet tails. Defines the heliosphere boundary where solar particle pressure balances interstellar medium. Eugene Parker developed theoretical framework in the 1950s predicting supersonic plasma flow. Investigated by successive space probes including Voyager missions approaching the heliopause. Covers solar physics, plasma physics, magnetohydrodynamics, planetary magnetospheres, and space weather effects on Earth's technology and climate. Connects stellar astrophysics to geophysics.

episodes.title: Solon the Lawgiver
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Solon, who was elected archon or chief magistrate of Athens in 594 BC: some see him as the father of Athenian democracy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Solon, who was elected archon or chief magistrate of Athens in 594 BC: some see him as the father of Athenian democracy.
episodes.summaryAI: Athenian statesman elected archon 594 BC during severe social and economic crisis. Implemented constitutional and legal reforms including debt cancellation, prohibition of debt slavery, and reorganisation of citizenship classes by wealth rather than birth. Some historians regard him as father of Athenian democracy, though his system was timocratic rather than fully democratic. His poetry survives as primary source for his political philosophy. Bridges aristocratic governance and popular sovereignty in ancient Greek political development. Relevant to history of law, democratic theory, economic reform, and ancient Greek political institutions.

episodes.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss William Blake's collection of illustrated poems "Songs of Innocence and of Experience." He published Songs of Innocence first in 1789 with five hand-coloured copies and, five years later, with additional Songs of Experience poems and the explanatory phrase "Shewing the Two Contrary States of the Human Soul." Blake drew on the street ballads and improving children's rhymes of the time, exploring the open and optimistic outlook of early childhood with the darker and more cynical outlook of adult life, in which symbols such as the Lamb belong to innocence and the Tyger to experience.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss William Blake's collection of illustrated poems "Songs of Innocence and of Experience." He published Songs of Innocence first in 1789 with five hand-coloured copies and, five years later, with additional Songs of Experience poems and the explanatory phrase "Shewing the Two Contrary States of the Human Soul." Blake drew on the street ballads and improving children's rhymes of the time, exploring the open and optimistic outlook of early childhood with the darker and more cynical outlook of adult life, in which symbols such as the Lamb belong to innocence and the Tyger to experience.
episodes.summaryAI: William Blake's complementary poetry collections: Songs of Innocence published 1789, combined with Songs of Experience 1794 showing two contrary states of the human soul. Hand-coloured illuminated printing with only five original copies of Innocence. Lamb symbolises innocence, Tyger experience. Drew on popular street ballads and children's rhymes, transforming simple forms into philosophical and theological exploration. Examines childhood, exploitation, institutionalised religion, and industrial society. Integrates visual art with poetry through unique relief etching technique. Connects Romantic literature, printmaking innovation, social criticism, and religious dissent in late eighteenth-century Britain.

episodes.title: Sources of Early Chinese History
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the sources for early Chinese history. The first attempts to make a record of historical events in China date from the Shang dynasty of the second millennium BC. The earliest surviving records were inscribed on bones or tortoise shells; in later centuries, chroniclers left detailed accounts on paper or silk. In the last hundred years, archaeologists have discovered a wealth of new materials, including a cache of previously unknown texts which were found in a sealed cave on the edge of the Gobi Desert. Such sources are are shedding new light on Chinese history, although interpreting ancient sources from the period before the invention of printing presents a number of challenges.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the sources for early Chinese history. In the last hundred years, archaeologists have discovered a wealth of new materials, including a cache of previously unknown texts which were found in a sealed cave on the edge of the Gobi Desert. Such sources are are shedding new light on Chinese history, although interpreting ancient sources from the period before the invention of printing presents a number of challenges.
episodes.summaryAI: Earliest Chinese historical records from Shang dynasty in second millennium BC. Oracle bone inscriptions on animal bones and tortoise shells represent first written sources. Later records on bamboo strips, silk, and paper. Major cache of texts discovered in sealed cave at Dunhuang on edge of Gobi Desert. Addresses challenges of interpreting ancient sources produced before invention of printing, including questions of authenticity, transmission, and editorial alteration. Covers Chinese historiographical traditions, archaeological methods, palaeography, and the relationship between material culture and textual evidence in reconstructing ancient East Asian civilisation.

episodes.title: Sovereignty
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the idea of Sovereignty, the authority of a state to govern itself and the relationship between the sovereign and the people. These ideas of external and internal sovereignty were imagined in various ways in ancient Greece and Rome, and given a name in 16th Century France by the philosopher and jurist Jean Bodin in his Six Books of the Commonwealth, where he said (in an early English translation) 'Maiestie or Soveraigntie is the most high, absolute, and perpetuall power over the citisens and subiects in a Commonweale: which the Latins cal Maiestatem, the Greeks akra exousia, kurion arche, and kurion politeuma; the Italians Segnoria, and the Hebrewes tomech shévet, that is to say, The greatest power to command.' Shakespeare also explored the concept through Richard II and the king's two bodies, Hobbes developed it in the 17th Century, and the idea of popular sovereignty was tested in the Revolutionary era in America and France.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the idea of Sovereignty, the authority of a state to govern itself and the relationship between the sovereign and the people. These ideas of external and internal sovereignty were imagined in various ways in ancient Greece and Rome, and given a name in 16th Century France by the philosopher and jurist Jean Bodin in his Six Books of the Commonwealth, where he said (in an early English translation) 'Maiestie or Soveraigntie is the most high, absolute, and perpetuall power over the citisens and subiects in a Commonweale: which the Latins cal Maiestatem, the Greeks akra exousia, kurion arche, and kurion politeuma; the Italians Segnoria, and the Hebrewes tomech shévet, that is to say, The greatest power to command.' Shakespeare also explored the concept through Richard II and the king's two bodies, Hobbes developed it in the 17th Century, and the idea of popular sovereignty was tested in the Revolutionary era in America and France.
episodes.summaryAI: Traces the concept of political sovereignty from ancient Greece and Rome through early modern Europe to revolutionary era. Jean Bodin first formally theorised sovereignty in sixteenth-century France during Wars of Religion. Shakespeare dramatised its tensions through Richard II's deposition. Hobbes developed absolutist sovereignty theory in Leviathan. Tested practically in American and French Revolutions, which redefined sovereign authority as residing in the people. Covers relationships between ruler and ruled, legitimacy of state power, social contract theory, constitutional development, and the philosophical foundations of modern political authority across Western political thought.

episodes.title: Space in Religion and Science
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of thought about space, and examines whether cyberspace has introduced a new concept of space in our world or if its roots are in Einsteinian physics. It would have seemed extraordinary to Dante or Newton, from their different perspectives, that at the end of the 20th century there would be learned scholars who would find no place for religion in the great schemes of thought and belief. In the 20th century our notions of physical space have been revolutionised. Einstein said that space was not a separate entity; we’ve probed and explored the outer reaches of our physical space with space flight, powerful telescopes and theoretical physics. But in the last 20 years, with the birth of the Internet, a virtual form of space has been introduced to us - cyberspace - where people can meet and communicate ideas; you sit at home, punch the keys and you can rove all over the world - the keyboard becomes a magic carpet. But does cyberspace introduce a new concept of space in our world? Or does it really have its roots in Einsteinian physics and even in Medieval theologyAccording to the science writer Margaret Wertheim, cyberspace - life on the surfing internet - gives us not only virtual reality, but a soul. Dr John Polkinghorne, the distinguished physicist and ordained priest in the C of E, is not happy with this news, but he does believe that religion is not destroyed by the new technology, and that latest theories in physics reinforce it. With The Reverend Dr John Polkinghorne, Fellow of Queen’s College, Cambridge and Canon Theologian of Liverpool; Margaret Wertheim, science writer and author of The Pearly Gates of Cyberspace: A History of Space from Dante to the Internet.

episodes.summaryAI: Traces conceptions of space from medieval theology through modern physics to digital culture. Einstein's relativity redefined space as inseparable from time and matter rather than independent entity. Space exploration and telescopic observation extended understanding of physical cosmic space. Birth of the Internet introduced cyberspace as conceptual domain. Asks whether cyberspace represents genuinely new spatial concept or derives from earlier frameworks in physics and medieval theology. Connects cosmology, theoretical physics, philosophy of space, religious metaphysics, and digital technology, examining how scientific and theological ideas about spatial reality have evolved and persisted.

episodes.title: Sparta
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Paul Cartledge, Edith Hall and Angie Hobbs discuss Sparta, the militaristic Ancient Greek city-state, and the political ideas it spawned.The isolated Ancient Greek city-state of Sparta was a ferocious opposite to the cosmopolitan port of Athens. Spartans were hostile to outsiders and rhetoric, to philosophy and change. Two and a half thousand years on, Sparta remains famous for its brutally rigorous culture of military discipline, as inculcated in its young men through communal living, and terrifying, licensed violence towards the Helots, the city-state's subjugated majority. Sparta and its cruelty was used as an argument against slavery by British Abolitionists in the early 1800s, before inspiring the Nazis in the 1930s and 1940s.Yet Sparta also produced poets of great skill: Tyrteaus wrote marching songs for the young men; Alcman wrote choral lyrics for the young women. Moreover, the city-state's rulers pioneered a radically egalitarian political system, and its ideals were invoked by Plato. Its inhabitants also prided themselves on their wit: we don't only derive the word 'spartan' from their culture, but the word 'laconic'. Paul Cartledge is AG Leventis Professor of Greek Culture and a Fellow of Clare College, University of Cambridge; Edith Hall is Professor of Classics and Drama at Royal Holloway, University of London; Angie Hobbs is Associate Professor of Philosophy and Senior Fellow in the Public Understanding of Philosophy at the University of Warwick.

Paul Cartledge is AG Leventis Professor of Greek Culture and a Fellow of Clare College, University of Cambridge; Edith Hall is Professor of Classics and Drama at Royal Holloway, University of London; Angie Hobbs is Associate Professor of Philosophy and Senior Fellow in the Public Understanding of Philosophy at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: Militaristic Greek city-state contrasting sharply with Athenian democratic and cultural model. Hostile to rhetoric, philosophy, and innovation. Maintained brutal system of licensed violence against enslaved Helot population. Paradoxically pioneered elements of political egalitarianism among citizen class and produced notable poets. Word laconic derives from Spartan brevity. Legacy appropriated diversely: British abolitionists invoked Sparta against slavery, while Nazis later claimed Spartan ideals. Covers ancient Greek political systems, military organisation, slavery, education, and the contested reception and ideological instrumentalisation of classical models across modern European history.

episodes.title: Spartacus
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life of Spartacus, the gladiator who led a major slave rebellion against the Roman Republic in the 1st century BC. He was an accomplished military leader, and the campaign he led contributed significantly to the instability of the Roman state in this period. Spartacus was celebrated by some ancient historians and reviled by others, and became a hero to revolutionaries in 19th-century Europe. Modern perceptions of his character have been influenced by Stanley Kubrick's 1960 film - but ancient sources give a rather more complex picture of Spartacus and the aims of his rebellion.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life of Spartacus, the gladiator who led a major slave rebellion against the Roman Republic in the 1st century BC. He was an accomplished military leader, and the campaign he led contributed significantly to the instability of the Roman state in this period. Spartacus was celebrated by some ancient historians and reviled by others, and became a hero to revolutionaries in 19th-century Europe. Modern perceptions of his character have been influenced by Stanley Kubrick's 1960 film - but ancient sources give a rather more complex picture of Spartacus and the aims of his rebellion.
episodes.summaryAI: Thracian gladiator who led major slave revolt against the Roman Republic in the first century BC, approximately 73 to 71 BC. Demonstrated accomplished military leadership defeating multiple Roman forces before final suppression by Crassus. Ancient historians both celebrated and condemned him. Became symbolic hero for nineteenth-century European revolutionary and socialist movements. Stanley Kubrick's 1960 film shaped modern popular perception, embedding Spartacus in Cold War political culture. Covers Roman slavery, gladiatorial institutions, Republican military history, and the ideological afterlife of ancient figures in modern revolutionary politics and cinema.

episodes.title: Spinoza
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses the Dutch Jewish Philosopher Spinoza. For the radical thinkers of the Enlightenment, he was the first man to have lived and died as a true atheist. For others, including Samuel Taylor Coleridge, he provides perhaps the most profound conception of God to be found in Western philosophy. He was bold enough to defy the thinking of his time, yet too modest to accept the fame of public office and he died, along with Socrates and Seneca, one of the three great deaths in philosophy. Baruch Spinoza can claim influence on both the Enlightenment thinkers of the 18th century and great minds of the 19th, notably Hegel, and his ideas were so radical that they could only be fully published after his death. But what were the ideas that caused such controversy in Spinoza’s lifetime, how did they influence the generations after, and can Spinoza really be seen as the first philosopher of the rational Enlightenment?With Jonathan Rée, historian and philosopher and Visiting Professor at Roehampton University; Sarah Hutton, Professor of English at the University of Wales, Aberystwyth; John Cottingham, Professor of Philosophy at the University of Reading.

For others, including Samuel Taylor Coleridge, he provides perhaps the most profound conception of God to be found in Western philosophy. He was bold enough to defy the thinking of his time, yet too modest to accept the fame of public office and he died, along with Socrates and Seneca, one of the three great deaths in philosophy. Baruch Spinoza can claim influence on both the Enlightenment thinkers of the 18th century and great minds of the 19th, notably Hegel, and his ideas were so radical that they could only be fully published after his death. But what were the ideas that caused such controversy in Spinoza’s lifetime, how did they influence the generations after, and can Spinoza really be seen as the first philosopher of the rational Enlightenment?With Jonathan Rée, historian and philosopher and Visiting Professor at Roehampton University; Sarah Hutton, Professor of English at the University of Wales, Aberystwyth; John Cottingham, Professor of Philosophy at the University of Reading.
episodes.summaryAI: Dutch Jewish philosopher Baruch Spinoza, seventeenth century, excommunicated from Amsterdam's Jewish community. Radical monist who identified God with Nature, interpreted variously as atheism by Enlightenment radicals and as profoundly theistic by Coleridge and German Romantics. Ethics published posthumously, using geometric method to argue for determinism, intellectual freedom, and unified substance. Influenced Hegel, German Idealism, and Enlightenment political thought. Died alongside Socrates and Seneca as one of philosophy's three exemplary deaths. Connects rationalist metaphysics, biblical criticism, political liberalism, and religious philosophy across early modern European intellectual history.

episodes.title: St Bartholomew's Day Massacre
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the infamous St Bartholomew’s Day Massacre. In Paris, in the high summer of 1572, a very unusual wedding was happening in the cathedral of Notre Dame. Henri, the young Huguenot King of Navarre, was marrying the King of France’s beloved sister, Margot, a Catholic. Theirs was a union designed to bring together the rival factions of France and finally end the French Wars of Religion. Paris was bustling with Huguenots and Catholics and, though the atmosphere was tense, the wedding went off without a hitch. And as they danced together at the Louvre, it seemed that the flower of French nobility had finally come together to bury its differences.That wasn’t to be: on St Bartholomew’s Day, four days after the ill-starred nuptials, so many Protestants were killed in the streets of Paris that the River Seine ran red with their blood. Was the wedding a trap? Who was to blame for the carnage and what impact did it have on the Reformation in Europe?With Diarmaid MacCulloch, Professor of the History of the Church at Oxford University and author of a new book: Reformation: Europe’s House Divided 1490-1700; Mark Greengrass, Professor of History at the University of Sheffield; Penny Roberts, Lecturer in History at the University of Warwick.

episodes.summaryAI: Mass killing of Huguenot Protestants in Paris beginning 24 August 1572. Marriage of Henri of Navarre to Marguerite de Valois intended to reconcile Catholic and Protestant factions after French Wars of Religion. Four days after the wedding, organised slaughter began, with bodies thrown into the Seine. Debate persists whether the wedding was deliberately planned as a trap. Event profoundly impacted the European Reformation, influencing political theory on resistance to tyranny, religious toleration, and sovereignty. Connects French political history, confessional conflict, early modern diplomacy, and the development of rights of resistance theory.

episodes.title: St Hilda
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 7th century saint, Hilda, or Hild as she would have been known then, wielded great religious and political influence in a volatile era. The monasteries she led in the north of England were known for their literacy and learning and produced great future leaders, including 5 bishops. The remains of a later abbey still stand in Whitby on the site of the powerful monastery she headed there. We gain most of our knowledge of Hilda's life from The Venerable Bede who wrote that she was 66 years in the world, living 33 years in the secular life and 33 dedicated to God. She was baptised alongside the king of Northumbria and with her royal connections, she was a formidable character. Bede writes: “Her prudence was so great that not only indifferent persons but even kings and princes asked and received her advice”. Hild and her Abbey at Whitby hosted the Synod which decided when Easter would be celebrated, following a dispute between different traditions. Her achievements are all the more impressive when we consider that Christianity was still in its infancy in Northumbria. So what contribution did she make to establishing Christianity in the north of England? How unusual was it for a woman to be such an important figure in the Church at the time? How did her double monastery of both men and women operate on a day-to-day basis? And how did she manage to convert a farmhand into England's first vernacular poet?With John Blair, Fellow in History at The Queen's College, Oxford; Rosemary Cramp, Emeritus Professor in Archaeology at Durham University; Sarah Foot, Professor of Early Medieval History at Sheffield University.

episodes.summaryAI: Seventh-century Anglo-Saxon abbess wielding exceptional religious and political authority in Northumbria. Founded and led double monastery of men and women at Whitby, known for literacy, learning, and producing five bishops. Hosted pivotal Synod of Whitby 664 AD deciding Roman versus Celtic dating of Easter, shaping English Christianity's alignment with Rome. Baptised alongside King Edwin of Northumbria. Converted farmhand Caedmon into England's first known vernacular poet. Covers early medieval English church governance, female religious authority, monastic education, the Celtic-Roman ecclesiastical debate, and origins of English literary culture.

episodes.title: St Paul
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Helen Bond, John Haldane and John Barclay discuss the influence of St Paul on the early Christian church and on Christian theology generally. St Paul joined the Christian church in a time of confusion and wonder. Jesus had been crucified and resurrected and the Christians believed they were living at the end of the world. Paul's impact on Christianity is vast: he imposed an identity on the early Christians and a coherent theology that thinkers from St Augustine to Martin Luther have grappled with. Crucially, Paul is responsible for changing Christianity from a Jewish reform movement into a separate and universal religion.Helen Bond is Senior Lecturer in the New Testament at the University of Edinburgh; John Haldane is Professor of Philosophy at the University of St Andrews; John Barclay is the Lightfoot Professor of Divinity at Durham University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Helen Bond, John Haldane and John Barclay discuss the influence of St Paul on the early Christian church and on Christian theology generally. St Paul joined the Christian church in a time of confusion and wonder. Jesus had been crucified and resurrected and the Christians believed they were living at the end of the world. Paul's impact on Christianity is vast: he imposed an identity on the early Christians and a coherent theology that thinkers from St Augustine to Martin Luther have grappled with. Crucially, Paul is responsible for changing Christianity from a Jewish reform movement into a separate and universal religion.Helen Bond is Senior Lecturer in the New Testament at the University of Edinburgh; John Haldane is Professor of Philosophy at the University of St Andrews; John Barclay is the Lightfoot Professor of Divinity at Durham University.
episodes.summaryAI: Paul of Tarsus, first-century Jewish Pharisee who experienced conversion on the road to Damascus and became the principal architect of early Christian theology. His epistles established core doctrines including justification by faith, the body of Christ as church, and salvation extended to Gentiles without requiring Mosaic law observance. Transformed Christianity from a Jewish reform movement into a distinct universal religion with coherent theological identity. Profoundly influenced Augustine's theology of grace and Luther's Protestant Reformation. Shaped Christian ethics, ecclesiology, and the relationship between law, faith, and redemption.

episodes.title: St Thomas Aquinas
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses the life, works and enduring influence of the medieval philosopher and theologian St Thomas Aquinas with Martin Palmer, John Haldane and Annabel Brett. St Thomas Aquinas' ideas remain at the heart of the official doctrine of the Roman Catholic Church today and inform philosophical debates on human rights, natural law and what constitutes a 'just war'.Martin Palmer is Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture; John Haldane is Professor of Philosophy at the University of St Andrews; Annabel Brett is Lecturer in History at Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg discusses the life, works and enduring influence of the medieval philosopher and theologian St Thomas Aquinas with Martin Palmer, John Haldane and Annabel Brett. St Thomas Aquinas' ideas remain at the heart of the official doctrine of the Roman Catholic Church today and inform philosophical debates on human rights, natural law and what constitutes a 'just war'.Martin Palmer is Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture; John Haldane is Professor of Philosophy at the University of St Andrews; Annabel Brett is Lecturer in History at Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Aquinas, thirteenth-century Dominican friar and scholastic philosopher-theologian who synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine. His Summa Theologica remains foundational to Roman Catholic teaching. Developed natural law theory grounding ethics in reason accessible to all, not only revelation. Articulated criteria for just war doctrine. His arguments for God's existence, the relationship between faith and reason, and hierarchical natural order influenced Western philosophy, jurisprudence, and human rights discourse. Engaged questions spanning metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, political philosophy, and the compatibility of classical philosophy with monotheistic theology.

episodes.title: States of Matter
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the science of matter and the states in which it can exist. Most people are familiar with the idea that a substance like water can exist in solid, liquid and gaseous forms. But as much as 99% of the matter in the universe is now believed to exist in a fourth state, plasma. Today scientists recognise a number of other exotic states or phases, such as glasses, gels and liquid crystals - many of them with useful properties that can be exploited.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the science of matter and the states in which it can exist. Most people are familiar with the idea that a substance like water can exist in solid, liquid and gaseous forms. But as much as 99% of the matter in the universe is now believed to exist in a fourth state, plasma. Today scientists recognise a number of other exotic states or phases, such as glasses, gels and liquid crystals - many of them with useful properties that can be exploited.
episodes.summaryAI: Classical states of matter: solid, liquid, gas, and plasma, distinguished by molecular arrangement, energy, and intermolecular forces. Plasma constitutes an estimated 99% of visible matter in the universe, found in stars and interstellar medium. Beyond classical states, exotic and intermediate forms include glasses with disordered solid structure, gels combining solid and liquid properties, and liquid crystals exhibiting directional molecular order exploited in display technologies. Phase transitions governed by temperature and pressure. Connects condensed matter physics, thermodynamics, materials science, and industrial applications of engineered states.

episodes.title: Stevie Smith
episodes.synopsis: In 1957 Stevie Smith published a poetry collection called Not Waving But Drowning – and its title poem gave us a phrase which has entered the language.
episodes.description: In 1957 Stevie Smith published a poetry collection called Not Waving But Drowning – and its title poem gave us a phrase which has entered the language.
episodes.summaryAI: Stevie Smith, twentieth-century British poet and novelist active from the 1930s through 1960s. Published Not Waving But Drowning in 1957, a poem whose title phrase entered common English usage as an expression of unrecognized distress mistaken for coping. Her verse combined dark humor, nursery-rhyme rhythms, and themes of loneliness, death, suburban isolation, and religious doubt. Distinctive illustrative doodles accompanied her poetry. Occupied an eccentric position in mid-century English literature, distinct from prevailing modernist and Movement poetry, bridging light verse and existential despair.

episodes.title: Stoicism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Stoicism, the third great philosophy of the Ancient World. It was founded by Zeno in the fourth century BC and flourished in Greece and then in Rome. Its ideals of inner solitude, forbearance in adversity and the acceptance of fate won many brilliant adherents and made it the dominant philosophy across the whole of the Ancient World. The ex-slave Epictetus said "Man is troubled not by events, but by the meaning he gives them". Seneca, the politician, declared that "Life without the courage for death is slavery". The stoic thoughts of Marcus Aurelius, the philosopher emperor, provided a rallying point for empire builders into the modern age.Stoicism influenced the Christian church, had a big effect on Shakespeare and Renaissance drama and may even have given the British their 'stiff upper lip', but it's a philosophy that was almost forgotten in the 20th century. Does it still have a legacy for us today?With Angie Hobbs, Lecturer in Philosophy, University of Warwick; Jonathan Rée, philosopher and historian; David Sedley, Laurence Professor of Ancient Philosophy, University of Cambridge.

Does it still have a legacy for us today?With Angie Hobbs, Lecturer in Philosophy, University of Warwick; Jonathan Rée, philosopher and historian; David Sedley, Laurence Professor of Ancient Philosophy, University of Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Stoicism, Hellenistic philosophical school founded by Zeno of Citium in Athens around 300 BC. Core tenets: virtue as sole good, rational acceptance of fate, mastery of emotions through reason, living according to nature and cosmic logos. Major figures span centuries from Chrysippus through Roman practitioners Seneca, Epictetus the former slave, and Emperor Marcus Aurelius. Dominated intellectual life across the Greco-Roman world. Stoic ethics and self-discipline influenced early Christian morality, Renaissance drama, Shakespeare's tragic heroes, and arguably British cultural attitudes toward emotional restraint. Connects ethics, metaphysics, logic, and political philosophy.

episodes.title: Strabo's Geographica
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Strabo's Geographica. Written almost exactly two thousand years ago by a Greek scholar living in Rome, the Geographica is an ambitious attempt to describe the entire world known to the Romans and Greeks at that time. Strabo seems to have based his book on accounts of distant lands given to him by contemporary travellers and imperial administrators, and on earlier works of scholarship by other Greek writers. One of the earliest systematic works of geography, Strabo's book offers a revealing insight into the state of ancient scholarship, and remained influential for many centuries after the author's death.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Strabo's Geographica. Written almost exactly two thousand years ago by a Greek scholar living in Rome, the Geographica is an ambitious attempt to describe the entire world known to the Romans and Greeks at that time. Strabo seems to have based his book on accounts of distant lands given to him by contemporary travellers and imperial administrators, and on earlier works of scholarship by other Greek writers. One of the earliest systematic works of geography, Strabo's book offers a revealing insight into the state of ancient scholarship, and remained influential for many centuries after the author's death.
episodes.summaryAI: Strabo's Geographica, composed circa 7 BC to 23 AD by a Greek scholar residing in Rome. Seventeen books attempting systematic description of the entire known world, from Iberia to India, covering physical geography, ethnography, political boundaries, climate, natural resources, and cultural practices. Drew on earlier Greek geographical tradition including Eratosthenes and Posidonius alongside travelers' firsthand accounts. One of the earliest comprehensive geographical treatises surviving from antiquity. Bridges natural philosophy, cartographic ambition, ethnographic observation, and Roman imperial knowledge of diverse peoples and territories.

episodes.title: Sturm und Drang
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the artistic movement known as Sturm und Drang.In the 1770s a small group of German writers started to produce plays, poetry and novels which were radically different from what had gone before. These writers were all young men, and they rejected the values of the Enlightenment, which they felt had robbed art of its spontaneity and feeling. Their work was passionate, ignored existing conventions and privileged the individual's free will above the constraints of society.The most prominent member of the movement was Johann von Goethe, whose novel The Sorrows of Young Werther became its most notable success, translated into more then thirty languages. Despite this and other successes including Schiller's play The Robbers, the Sturm und Drang disappeared almost as quickly as it had emerged; by the mid-1780s it was already a thing of the past
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the artistic movement known as Sturm und Drang.In the 1770s a small group of German writers started to produce plays, poetry and novels which were radically different from what had gone before. These writers were all young men, and they rejected the values of the Enlightenment, which they felt had robbed art of its spontaneity and feeling. Their work was passionate, ignored existing conventions and privileged the individual's free will above the constraints of society.The most prominent member of the movement was Johann von Goethe, whose novel The Sorrows of Young Werther became its most notable success, translated into more then thirty languages. episodes.summaryAI: Sturm und Drang, German literary movement of the 1770s to mid-1780s whose young writers rejected Enlightenment rationalism, neoclassical rules, and aristocratic restraint. Emphasized emotional intensity, individual genius, nature, and rebellion against social convention. Key figures include the young Goethe, whose Sorrows of Young Werther achieved international celebrity and triggered imitative suicides, and Schiller with The Robbers. Influenced by Rousseau and Herder's ideas on folk culture and national character. Brief but pivotal transition between Enlightenment classicism and Romanticism in German and European literary and philosophical history.

episodes.title: Suffragism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Krista Cowman, June Purvis and Julia Bush discuss suffragism, a name for the various movements to get the vote for women in the 19th and early-20th century. On the 4th June 1913 the Epsom Derby was underway. King George V was there watching his horse Anmer, ridden by Herbert Jones. Also watching was a young woman called Emily Davison. As the horses thundered towards the finish line, Emily Davison stepped through the barrier and threw herself in front of the King's horse and died of her injuries four days later. Davison was a suffragette, a campaigner for the woman's right to vote and her death is perhaps the most powerful image of that entire movement. Emmeline Pankhurst and her Suffragettes are famous for their militant campaign of suicide, violence and direct action, but Suffragism was a broader movement involving letter writing, reasoned argument, journalism and parliamentary petition - all played out across biology, medicine, law, psychology, politics and the military amidst the rising tide of democratic ideas.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Krista Cowman, June Purvis and Julia Bush discuss suffragism, a name for the various movements to get the vote for women in the 19th and early-20th century. On the 4th June 1913 the Epsom Derby was underway. King George V was there watching his horse Anmer, ridden by Herbert Jones. Also watching was a young woman called Emily Davison. As the horses thundered towards the finish line, Emily Davison stepped through the barrier and threw herself in front of the King's horse and died of her injuries four days later. Davison was a suffragette, a campaigner for the woman's right to vote and her death is perhaps the most powerful image of that entire movement. Emmeline Pankhurst and her Suffragettes are famous for their militant campaign of suicide, violence and direct action, but Suffragism was a broader movement involving letter writing, reasoned argument, journalism and parliamentary petition - all played out across biology, medicine, law, psychology, politics and the military amidst the rising tide of democratic ideas.
episodes.summaryAI: Women's suffrage movements in Britain and internationally during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Encompasses both constitutional suffragists using petition, journalism, public meetings, and parliamentary lobbying, and the militant suffragettes led by the Pankhurst family employing civil disobedience, hunger strikes, and property destruction. Emily Davison's death at the 1913 Epsom Derby became an iconic moment. Broader movement involved working-class and middle-class women, men allies, and colonial contexts. Connects political rights, democratic reform, gender equality, civil liberties, protest tactics, and the relationship between activism and legislative change.

episodes.title: Sun Tzu and The Art of War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas attributed to Sun Tzu (544-496BC, according to tradition), a legendary figure from the beginning of the Iron Age in China, around the time of Confucius. He may have been the historical figure Sun Wu, a military adviser at the court of King Helu of Wu (who reigned between about 514 and 496 BC), one of the kings in power in the Warring States period of Chinese history (6th - 5th century BC). Sun Tzu was credited as the author of The Art of War, a work on military strategy that soon became influential in China and then Japan both for its guidance on conducting and avoiding war and for its approach to strategy generally. After The Art of War was translated into European languages in C18th, its influence spread to military academies around the world.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas attributed to Sun Tzu (544-496BC, according to tradition), a legendary figure from the beginning of the Iron Age in China, around the time of Confucius. He may have been the historical figure Sun Wu, a military adviser at the court of King Helu of Wu (who reigned between about 514 and 496 BC), one of the kings in power in the Warring States period of Chinese history (6th - 5th century BC). Sun Tzu was credited as the author of The Art of War, a work on military strategy that soon became influential in China and then Japan both for its guidance on conducting and avoiding war and for its approach to strategy generally. After The Art of War was translated into European languages in C18th, its influence spread to military academies around the world.
episodes.summaryAI: The Art of War, ancient Chinese military treatise attributed to Sun Tzu, a possibly legendary strategist from the late Spring and Autumn period around the fifth century BC. Thirteen chapters covering strategy, terrain, espionage, deception, logistics, and the relationship between warfare and statecraft. Emphasizes winning without fighting, adaptability, intelligence gathering, and understanding adversary psychology. Central to Chinese and Japanese military thought for centuries. Translated into European languages in the eighteenth century, subsequently adopted by military academies worldwide. Connects military strategy, political philosophy, diplomacy, and organizational management theory.

episodes.title: Sunni and Shia Islam
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Amira Bennison, Robert Gleave and Hugh Kennedy discuss the split between the Sunni and the Shia. This schism came to dominate early Islam, and yet it did not spring at first from a deep theological disagreement, but rather from a dispute about who should succeed the Prophet Muhammad, and on what grounds. The supporters of the Prophet's cousin Ali argued for the hereditary principle; their opponents championed systems of selection. Ali's followers were to become the Shia; the supporters of selection were to become Sunnis.It is a story that takes us from Medina to Syria and on into Iraq, that takes in complex family loyalties, civil war and the killing at Karbala of the Prophet's grandson. Husayn has been commemorated as a martyr by the Shia ever since, and his death helped to formalise the divide as first a political and then a profoundly theological separation.Amira Bennison is Senior Lecturer in Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at the University of Cambridge; Robert Gleave is Professor of Arabic Studies at the University of Exeter; Hugh Kennedy is Professor of Arabic in the School of Oriental and African Studies at the University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Amira Bennison, Robert Gleave and Hugh Kennedy discuss the split between the Sunni and the Shia. This schism came to dominate early Islam, and yet it did not spring at first from a deep theological disagreement, but rather from a dispute about who should succeed the Prophet Muhammad, and on what grounds. The supporters of the Prophet's cousin Ali argued for the hereditary principle; their opponents championed systems of selection. Ali's followers were to become the Shia; the supporters of selection were to become Sunnis.It is a story that takes us from Medina to Syria and on into Iraq, that takes in complex family loyalties, civil war and the killing at Karbala of the Prophet's grandson. Husayn has been commemorated as a martyr by the Shia ever since, and his death helped to formalise the divide as first a political and then a profoundly theological separation.Amira Bennison is Senior Lecturer in Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at the University of Cambridge; Robert Gleave is Professor of Arabic Studies at the University of Exeter; Hugh Kennedy is Professor of Arabic in the School of Oriental and African Studies at the University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: The Sunni-Shia division in Islam originating in the seventh-century succession crisis following Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 AD. Ali ibn Abi Talib's supporters believed leadership belonged to the Prophet's family, forming the Shia tradition. Opponents accepted the elected caliphs, becoming Sunni. The massacre of Ali's son Husayn at Karbala in 680 became a defining moment of Shia martyrdom and identity. Not primarily a theological schism but a political dispute over legitimate authority that deepened over centuries into distinct legal schools, ritual practices, and communal identities across the Islamic world.

episodes.title: Superconductivity
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the discovery made in 1911 by the Dutch physicist Heike Kamerlingh Onnes (1853-1926). He came to call it Superconductivity and it is a set of physical properties that nobody predicted and that none, since, have fully explained. When he lowered the temperature of mercury close to absolute zero and ran an electrical current through it, Kamerlingh Onnes found not that it had low resistance but that it had no resistance. Later, in addition, it was noticed that a superconductor expels its magnetic field. In the century or more that has followed, superconductors have already been used to make MRI scanners and to speed particles through the Large Hadron Collider and they may perhaps bring nuclear fusion a little closer (a step that could be world changing).
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the discovery made in 1911 by the Dutch physicist Heike Kamerlingh Onnes (1853-1926). He came to call it Superconductivity and it is a set of physical properties that nobody predicted and that none, since, have fully explained. When he lowered the temperature of mercury close to absolute zero and ran an electrical current through it, Kamerlingh Onnes found not that it had low resistance but that it had no resistance. Later, in addition, it was noticed that a superconductor expels its magnetic field. In the century or more that has followed, superconductors have already been used to make MRI scanners and to speed particles through the Large Hadron Collider and they may perhaps bring nuclear fusion a little closer (a step that could be world changing).
episodes.summaryAI: Superconductivity, the phenomenon of zero electrical resistance and expulsion of magnetic fields discovered by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911 using mercury cooled near absolute zero. Explained theoretically by BCS theory in 1957 involving Cooper pairs of electrons. The Meissner effect causes magnetic field expulsion enabling levitation. Practical applications include MRI medical imaging, particle accelerators such as the Large Hadron Collider, and SQUID magnetometers. High-temperature superconductors discovered in the 1980s raised hopes for room-temperature materials. Potential future applications include lossless power transmission and nuclear fusion reactor magnets.

episodes.title: Surrealism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss surrealism. ‘Si vous aimez L’amour, vous aimerez Surrealisme!’. If you like Love, you’ll love Surrealism! Thus was the launch of the surrealist manifesto publicised in Paris in 1924. In that document the formerly Dadaist poet André Breton defined his new movement, “Surrealism is pure psychic automatism, by which it is intended to express…the real process of thought. It is the dictation of thought, free from any control by reason and of any aesthetic or moral preoccupation”.Surrealism is about sex, the unconscious, repression and desire and seems to carry more than a distant echo of the Doctor from Vienna. How much was their notion of ‘pure thought’ influenced by the writings of Sigmund Freud and the new technique of psychoanalysis being developed at the time? Did the surrealists manage to release the secrets and wonders of the human psyche, or was their wild foray into melting clocks, floating euphoniums and automatic writing simply a wasted journey into nonsense?With Dawn Adiss, Professor of Art History and Theory at the University of Essex; Malcolm Bowie, Marshal Foch Professor of French Literature at Oxford University and a fellow of All Souls College; the psychoanalyst Darian Leader

episodes.summaryAI: Surrealism, artistic and literary movement launched by Andre Breton's 1924 manifesto in Paris, defining it as pure psychic automatism expressing thought free from rational control. Deeply influenced by Freudian psychoanalysis, exploring the unconscious, dreams, desire, sexuality, and repression. Techniques included automatic writing, unexpected juxtapositions, and dreamlike imagery. Visual artists Dali, Magritte, and Ernst created iconic works. Literary contributions from Eluard, Aragon, and Breton himself. Extended into film, photography, and theater. Connected to Dada's anti-rational legacy. Influenced abstract expressionism, pop art, advertising, and broader twentieth-century visual culture.

episodes.title: Swift's A Modest Proposal
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most brilliant and shocking satires ever written in English – Jonathan Swift’s A Modest Proposal. Masquerading as an attempt to end poverty in Ireland once and for all, a Modest Proposal is a short pamphlet that draws the reader into a scheme for economic and industrial horror. Published anonymously but written by Jonathan Swift, A Modest Proposal lays bare the cruel presumptions, unchecked prejudice, the politics and the poverty of the 18th century, but it also reveals, perhaps more than anything else, the character and the mind of Swift himself.With John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Judith Hawley, Professor of 18th Century Literature at Royal Holloway, University of London and Ian McBride, Senior Lecturer in the History Department at King’s College London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most brilliant and shocking satires ever written in English – Jonathan Swift’s A Modest Proposal. Masquerading as an attempt to end poverty in Ireland once and for all, a Modest Proposal is a short pamphlet that draws the reader into a scheme for economic and industrial horror. Published anonymously but written by Jonathan Swift, A Modest Proposal lays bare the cruel presumptions, unchecked prejudice, the politics and the poverty of the 18th century, but it also reveals, perhaps more than anything else, the character and the mind of Swift himself.With John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Judith Hawley, Professor of 18th Century Literature at Royal Holloway, University of London and Ian McBride, Senior Lecturer in the History Department at King’s College London.
episodes.summaryAI: Jonathan Swift's A Modest Proposal, published 1729, a satirical pamphlet suggesting Irish poverty be solved by selling children of the poor as food for the wealthy. Masterpiece of sustained ironic voice adopting the detached economic reasoning of contemporary political arithmeticians. Exposed English colonial exploitation, Anglo-Irish prejudice, absentee landlordism, and the dehumanizing economic logic applied to Ireland. Reveals Swift's complex position as Anglo-Irish dean of St Patrick's Cathedral. Landmark in the history of satire, political rhetoric, and English prose style. Connects literature, colonial politics, economics, and ethics.

episodes.title: Symmetry
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss symmetry. Found in Nature - from snowflakes to butterflies - and in art in the music of Bach and the poems of Pushkin, symmetry is both aesthetically pleasing and an essential tool to understanding our physical world. The Greek philosopher Aristotle described symmetry as one of the greatest forms of beauty to be found in the mathematical sciences, while the French poet Paul Valery went further, declaring; “The universe is built on a plan, the profound symmetry of which is somehow present in the inner structure of our intellect”.The story of symmetry tracks an extraordinary shift from its role as an aesthetic model - found in the tiles in the Alhambra and Bach's compositions - to becoming a key tool to understanding how the physical world works. It provides a major breakthrough in mathematics with the development of group theory in the 19th century. And it is the unexpected breakdown of symmetry at sub-atomic level that is so tantalising for contemporary quantum physicists.So why is symmetry so prevalent and appealing in both art and nature? How does symmetry enable us to grapple with monstrous numbers? And how might symmetry contribute to the elusive Theory of Everything?With Fay Dowker, Reader in Theoretical Physics at Imperial College, London; Marcus du Sautoy, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford; Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss symmetry. Found in Nature - from snowflakes to butterflies - and in art in the music of Bach and the poems of Pushkin, symmetry is both aesthetically pleasing and an essential tool to understanding our physical world. The Greek philosopher Aristotle described symmetry as one of the greatest forms of beauty to be found in the mathematical sciences, while the French poet Paul Valery went further, declaring; “The universe is built on a plan, the profound symmetry of which is somehow present in the inner structure of our intellect”.The story of symmetry tracks an extraordinary shift from its role as an aesthetic model - found in the tiles in the Alhambra and Bach's compositions - to becoming a key tool to understanding how the physical world works. It provides a major breakthrough in mathematics with the development of group theory in the 19th century. And it is the unexpected breakdown of symmetry at sub-atomic level that is so tantalising for contemporary quantum physicists.So why is symmetry so prevalent and appealing in both art and nature? How does symmetry enable us to grapple with monstrous numbers? And how might symmetry contribute to the elusive Theory of Everything?With Fay Dowker, Reader in Theoretical Physics at Imperial College, London; Marcus du Sautoy, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford; Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: Symmetry as a concept spanning mathematics, physics, biology, and aesthetics. Aristotle identified it as fundamental to beauty. Mathematically formalized through group theory in the nineteenth century by Galois and others. In physics, Noether's theorem links symmetries to conservation laws. Symmetry principles underpin crystallography, molecular structure, and particle physics. Symmetry breaking at the subatomic level, including parity violation and the Higgs mechanism, revealed that fundamental laws are not perfectly symmetric, driving advances in quantum field theory. Connections across visual arts, architecture, natural forms, and abstract mathematical structures.

episodes.title: Tacitus and the Decadence of Rome
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman historian Tacitus who chronicled some of Rome’s most notorious emperors, including Nero and Caligula, and whose portrayal of Roman decadence influences the way we see Rome today. “The story I now commence is rich in vicissitudes, grim with warfare, torn by civil strife, a tale of horror even during times of peace”. So reads page one of The Histories by the Roman historian Tacitus and it doesn’t disappoint. Convinced that Rome was going to the dogs, Tacitus depicts a Rome which is a hotbed of sex and violence, of excessive wealth and senatorial corruption. His work is a pungent study in tyranny and decline that has influenced depictions of Rome, from Gibbon’s Decline and Fall to Robert Graves’ I, Claudius. But is it a true picture of the age or does Tacitus’ work present the tyranny and decadence of Rome at the expense of its virtues? And to what extent, when we look at the Roman Empire today, do we still see it through Tacitus' eyes?With Catharine Edwards, Professor of Classics and Ancient History at Birkbeck, University of London; Ellen O’Gorman, Senior Lecturer in Classics at the University of Bristol and Maria Wyke, Professor of Latin at University College London
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman historian Tacitus who chronicled some of Rome’s most notorious emperors, including Nero and Caligula, and whose portrayal of Roman decadence influences the way we see Rome today. “The story I now commence is rich in vicissitudes, grim with warfare, torn by civil strife, a tale of horror even during times of peace”. His work is a pungent study in tyranny and decline that has influenced depictions of Rome, from Gibbon’s Decline and Fall to Robert Graves’ I, Claudius. But is it a true picture of the age or does Tacitus’ work present the tyranny and decadence of Rome at the expense of its virtues? And to what extent, when we look at the Roman Empire today, do we still see it through Tacitus' eyes?With Catharine Edwards, Professor of Classics and Ancient History at Birkbeck, University of London; Ellen O’Gorman, Senior Lecturer in Classics at the University of Bristol and Maria Wyke, Professor of Latin at University College London
episodes.summaryAI: Tacitus, Roman historian of the late first and early second centuries AD whose Annals and Histories chronicle the Julio-Claudian and Flavian dynasties. Vivid portrayals of emperors Nero and Caligula established enduring images of Roman imperial decadence, corruption, tyranny, and moral decline. His rhetorical style combined moral judgment with political analysis of power under autocracy. Profoundly influenced later historiography including Gibbon's Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire and historical fiction such as Robert Graves' I Claudius. Connects Roman history, political philosophy, rhetoric, literary style, and Western reception of classical antiquity.

episodes.title: Tagore
episodes.synopsis: Rabindranath Tagore was the first non-European to win a Nobel Prize for Literature. He has been called one of the outstanding thinkers of the 20th century and the greatest poet India has ever produced. His Nobel followed publication of Gitanjali, his English version of some of his Bengali poems. WB Yeats and Ezra Pound were great supporters. Tagore was born in Calcutta in 1861 and educated partly in Britain; King George V knighted him, but Tagore renounced this in 1919 following the Amritsar Massacre. A key figure in Indian nationalism, Tagore became a friend of Gandhi, offering criticism as well as support. A polymath and progressive, Tagore painted, wrote plays, novels, short stories and many songs. The national anthems of India and Bangladesh are based on his poems.

Tagore was born in Calcutta in 1861 and educated partly in Britain; King George V knighted him, but Tagore renounced this in 1919 following the Amritsar Massacre. A key figure in Indian nationalism, Tagore became a friend of Gandhi, offering criticism as well as support. A polymath and progressive, Tagore painted, wrote plays, novels, short stories and many songs. The national anthems of India and Bangladesh are based on his poems.
episodes.summaryAI: Rabindranath Tagore, Bengali polymath and first non-European Nobel laureate in Literature in 1913 for Gitanjali, devotional poems he translated into English. Poet, novelist, playwright, composer, painter, and educational reformer who founded Visva-Bharati University. Initially championed in the West by Yeats and Pound. Renounced British knighthood after the 1919 Amritsar Massacre. Friend and intellectual counterpart to Gandhi despite philosophical differences. Composed the national anthems of both India and Bangladesh. Bridges Indian literary tradition, Bengali Renaissance, anticolonial nationalism, comparative religion, educational philosophy, and cross-cultural literary exchange.

episodes.title: Tang Era Poetry
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss two of China’s greatest poets, Li Bai and Du Fu, who wrote in the 8th century in the Tang Era. Li Bai (701-762AD) is known for personal poems, many of them about drinking wine, and for finding the enjoyment in life. Du Fu (712-770AD), a few years younger, is more of an everyman, writing in the upheaval of the An Lushan Rebellion (755-763AD). Together they have been a central part of Chinese culture for over a millennium, reflecting the balance between the individual and the public life, and one sign of their enduring appeal is that there is rarely agreement on which of them is the greater.

episodes.summaryAI: Tang dynasty poetry of eighth-century China, focusing on two supreme poets: Li Bai and Du Fu. Li Bai celebrated for exuberant personal lyrics on drinking, friendship, nature, and Daoist transcendence. Du Fu regarded as the poet-historian, documenting suffering during the devastating An Lushan Rebellion of 755-763 with Confucian moral seriousness. Tang era poetry represents the classical pinnacle of Chinese literary achievement, studied and memorized for over a millennium. Regulated verse forms perfected during this period. Connects Chinese literary history, Confucian and Daoist thought, political upheaval, and the cultural role of poetry in governance and identity.

episodes.title: Taste
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 18th century obsession with taste. In the mid 18th century the social commentator, George Coleman, decried the great fashion of his time: “Taste is at present the darling idol of the polite world…The fine ladies and gentlemen dress with Taste; the architects, whether Gothic or Chinese, build with Taste; the painters paint with Taste; critics read with Taste; and in short, fiddlers, players, singers, dancers, and mechanics themselves, are all the sons and daughters of Taste. Yet in this amazing super-abundancy of Taste, few can say what it really is, or what the word itself signifies.”From the pens of philosophers to the interior decor of the middle classes, the idea of good and bad taste shaped decisions about dress, wallpaper, furniture, architecture, literature and much more. The period saw an explosion in the taste industries - the origins of Chippendale furniture, Wedgwood pottery and Christie's auction house - and a similar growth in magazines and journals devoted to the new aesthetic, moral and social guidelines. But taste was also a battle ground that pitched old money against new, the city against the country and men against women. With Amanda Vickery, Reader in History at Royal Holloway, University of London; John Mullan, Professor of English at University College London; Jeremy Black, Professor of History at the University of Exeter
