episodes.summaryAI: The interstellar medium consists of vast clouds of gas and dust occupying the space between stars. Detected primarily through infrared and radio astronomy, these regions include molecular clouds, diffuse nebulae, and dark dust lanes. This material constitutes the raw substance from which new stars and planetary systems form through gravitational collapse and the debris into which dying stars disintegrate. Study of the cool universe reveals the chemical composition, temperature, and density conditions governing stellar and planetary formation, connecting astrophysics, cosmochemistry, and the origins of Earth and its elemental building blocks.

episodes.title: The Corn Laws
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Corn Laws. In 1815 the British Government passed legislation which artificially inflated the price of corn. The measure was supported by landowners but strongly opposed by manufacturers and the urban working class. In the 1830s the Anti-Corn Law League was founded to campaign for their repeal, led by the Radical Richard Cobden. The Conservative government of Sir Robert Peel finally repealed the laws in 1846, splitting his party in the process, and the resulting debate had profound consequences for the political and economic future of the country.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Corn Laws. In 1815 the British Government passed legislation which artificially inflated the price of corn. The measure was supported by landowners but strongly opposed by manufacturers and the urban working class. In the 1830s the Anti-Corn Law League was founded to campaign for their repeal, led by the Radical Richard Cobden. The Conservative government of Sir Robert Peel finally repealed the laws in 1846, splitting his party in the process, and the resulting debate had profound consequences for the political and economic future of the country.
episodes.summaryAI: The Corn Laws, introduced in 1815, imposed tariffs that artificially inflated British grain prices, protecting landed aristocratic interests. Manufacturers and the working class opposed the laws as driving up food costs and wages. The Anti-Corn Law League, led by Cobden and Bright, became a pioneering political pressure group. Repeal in 1846 under Peel split the Conservative Party and marked a decisive shift toward free trade. The episode reshaped British political alignments, advanced industrial capitalism, and established precedents for organized political lobbying and the relationship between economic policy and class interests.

episodes.title: The Covenanters
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the bonds that Scottish Presbyterians made between themselves and their monarchs in the 16th and 17th Centuries, to maintain their form of worship. These covenants bound James VI of Scotland to support Presbyterians yet when he became James I he was also expected to support episcopacy. That tension came to a head under Charles I who found himself on the losing side of a war with the Covenanters, who later supported Parliament before backing the future Charles II after he had pledged to support them. Once in power, Charles II failed to deliver the religious settlement the Covenanters wanted, and set about repressing them violently. Those who refused to renounce the covenants were persecuted in what became known as The Killing Times, as reflected in the image above.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the bonds that Scottish Presbyterians made between themselves and their monarchs in the 16th and 17th Centuries, to maintain their form of worship. These covenants bound James VI of Scotland to support Presbyterians yet when he became James I he was also expected to support episcopacy. That tension came to a head under Charles I who found himself on the losing side of a war with the Covenanters, who later supported Parliament before backing the future Charles II after he had pledged to support them. Once in power, Charles II failed to deliver the religious settlement the Covenanters wanted, and set about repressing them violently. Those who refused to renounce the covenants were persecuted in what became known as The Killing Times, as reflected in the image above.
episodes.summaryAI: The Covenanters were Scottish Presbyterians who from the sixteenth century made formal bonds (covenants) with monarchs to protect their form of worship. Tensions escalated under Charles I's attempts to impose episcopal church governance, triggering the Bishops' Wars. Covenanters initially supported Parliament during the English Civil War, then backed the future Charles II in exchange for religious guarantees. After the Restoration, Charles II repressed Covenanting communities during the Killing Times of the 1680s. The movement shaped Scottish religious identity, resistance to royal absolutism, and the broader relationship between church governance and political authority.

episodes.title: The Cult of Mithras
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the cult of Mithras, a mystery religion that existed in the Roman Empire from the 1st to the 4th centuries AD. Also known as the Mysteries of Mithras, its origins are uncertain. Academics have suggested a link with the ancient Vedic god Mitra and the Iranian Zoroastrian deity Mithra, but the extent and nature of the connection is a matter of controversy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the cult of Mithras, a mystery religion that existed in the Roman Empire from the 1st to the 4th centuries AD. Also known as the Mysteries of Mithras, its origins are uncertain. Academics have suggested a link with the ancient Vedic god Mitra and the Iranian Zoroastrian deity Mithra, but the extent and nature of the connection is a matter of controversy.
episodes.summaryAI: The Cult of Mithras was a mystery religion practiced across the Roman Empire from the first to fourth centuries AD. Worship centered on underground temples (mithraea) depicting Mithras slaying a bull (tauroctony). Membership was predominantly male and military. Scholars debate connections to the Vedic god Mitra and the Zoroastrian deity Mithra, with the extent of Iranian origins remaining controversial. Mithraic rituals involved initiation grades and communal meals. The cult's relationship to contemporary Christianity, its secretive nature, and its rapid disappearance raise questions about religious transmission, syncretism, and competition in late antiquity.

episodes.title: The Cultural Revolution
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Chairman Mao and the revolt he led within his own party from 1966, setting communists against each other, to renew the revolution that he feared had become too bourgeois and to remove his enemies and rivals. Universities closed and the students formed Red Guard factions to attack the 'four olds' - old ideas, culture, habits and customs - and they also turned on each other, with mass violence on the streets and hundreds of thousands of deaths. Over a billion copies of Chairman Mao’s Little Red Book were printed to support his cult of personality, before Mao himself died in 1976 and the revolution came to an end.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Chairman Mao and the revolt he led within his own party from 1966, setting communists against each other, to renew the revolution that he feared had become too bourgeois and to remove his enemies and rivals. Universities closed and the students formed Red Guard factions to attack the 'four olds' - old ideas, culture, habits and customs - and they also turned on each other, with mass violence on the streets and hundreds of thousands of deaths. Over a billion copies of Chairman Mao’s Little Red Book were printed to support his cult of personality, before Mao himself died in 1976 and the revolution came to an end.
episodes.summaryAI: The Cultural Revolution (1966-1976) was Chairman Mao Zedong's campaign to reassert ideological control within the Chinese Communist Party. Students organized into Red Guard factions attacking the so-called Four Olds: old customs, culture, habits, and ideas. Intellectuals, officials, and perceived class enemies suffered persecution, public humiliation, and mass violence, with hundreds of thousands killed. Over a billion copies of Mao's Little Red Book were distributed. The upheaval devastated education, cultural heritage, and governance. Ending with Mao's death in 1976, it reshaped Chinese politics, society, and collective memory for generations.

episodes.title: The Curies
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the scientific achievements of the Curie family. In 1903 Marie and Pierre Curie shared a Nobel Prize in Physics with Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity, a term which Marie coined. Marie went on to win a Nobel in Chemistry eight years later; remarkably, her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie would later share a Nobel with her husband Frédéric Joliot-Curie for their discovery that it was possible to create radioactive materials in the laboratory. The work of the Curies added immensely to our knowledge of fundamental physics and paved the way for modern treatments for cancer and other illnesses.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the scientific achievements of the Curie family. In 1903 Marie and Pierre Curie shared a Nobel Prize in Physics with Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity, a term which Marie coined. Marie went on to win a Nobel in Chemistry eight years later; remarkably, her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie would later share a Nobel with her husband Frédéric Joliot-Curie for their discovery that it was possible to create radioactive materials in the laboratory. The work of the Curies added immensely to our knowledge of fundamental physics and paved the way for modern treatments for cancer and other illnesses.
episodes.summaryAI: Radioactivity research by the Curie family across two generations of Nobel Prize-winning physicists and chemists. Pierre and Marie Curie shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Henri Becquerel for work on radioactivity, a term Marie coined. Marie Curie won a second Nobel in Chemistry for isolating radium and polonium. Daughter Irène Joliot-Curie and husband Frédéric shared a Nobel for synthesizing artificial radioactive isotopes. Contributions span atomic physics, nuclear chemistry, and medical applications including radiation-based cancer treatment. Family dynasty unique in scientific history.

episodes.title: The Danelaw
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the effective partition of England in the 880s after a century of Viking raids, invasions and settlements. Alfred of Wessex, the surviving Anglo-Saxon king and Guthrum, a Danish ruler, had fought each other to a stalemate and came to terms, with Guthrum controlling the land to the east (once he had agreed to convert to Christianity). The key strategic advantage the invaders had was the Viking ships which were far superior and enabled them to raid from the sea and up rivers very rapidly. Their Great Army had arrived in the 870s, conquering the kingdom of Northumbria and occupying York. They defeated the king of Mercia and seized part of his land. They killed the Anglo-Saxon king of East Anglia and gained control of his territory. It was only when a smaller force failed to defeat Wessex that the Danelaw came into being, leaving a lasting impact on the people and customs of that area.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the effective partition of England in the 880s after a century of Viking raids, invasions and settlements. Alfred of Wessex, the surviving Anglo-Saxon king and Guthrum, a Danish ruler, had fought each other to a stalemate and came to terms, with Guthrum controlling the land to the east (once he had agreed to convert to Christianity). The key strategic advantage the invaders had was the Viking ships which were far superior and enabled them to raid from the sea and up rivers very rapidly. Their Great Army had arrived in the 870s, conquering the kingdom of Northumbria and occupying York. They defeated the king of Mercia and seized part of his land. They killed the Anglo-Saxon king of East Anglia and gained control of his territory. It was only when a smaller force failed to defeat Wessex that the Danelaw came into being, leaving a lasting impact on the people and customs of that area.
episodes.summaryAI: Partition of ninth-century England between Anglo-Saxon and Viking-controlled territories following decades of Scandinavian invasion. The Great Heathen Army conquered Northumbria, seized York, defeated Mercia, and killed the king of East Anglia. Alfred of Wessex resisted and negotiated terms with the Viking leader Guthrum in the 880s, establishing the Danelaw boundary. Viking longships provided decisive military superiority. The Danelaw left lasting linguistic, legal, and place-name traces across northern and eastern England, reshaping settlement patterns, governance, and cultural identity in early medieval Britain.

episodes.title: The Davidian Revolution
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the impact of David I of Scotland (c1084-1153) on his kingdom and on neighbouring lands. The youngest son of Malcolm III, he was raised in exile in the Anglo-Norman court and became Earl of Huntingdon and Prince of Cumbria before claiming the throne in 1124. He introduced elements of what he had learned in England and, in the next decades, his kingdom saw new burghs, new monasteries, new ways of governing and the arrival of some very influential families, earning him the reputation of The Perfect King.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the impact of David I of Scotland (c1084-1153) on his kingdom and on neighbouring lands. The youngest son of Malcolm III, he was raised in exile in the Anglo-Norman court and became Earl of Huntingdon and Prince of Cumbria before claiming the throne in 1124. He introduced elements of what he had learned in England and, in the next decades, his kingdom saw new burghs, new monasteries, new ways of governing and the arrival of some very influential families, earning him the reputation of The Perfect King.
episodes.summaryAI: Reign and transformative influence of David I of Scotland, circa 1084 to 1153, youngest son of Malcolm III and Margaret. Raised at the Anglo-Norman English court, David introduced feudal landholding, established royal burghs as centers of trade, founded major monasteries including Melrose and Holyrood, and reformed Scottish governance along Anglo-Norman lines. His policies brought Continental religious orders, new coinage, and administrative structures that reshaped Scottish society. Known as the Perfect King, his reign marks a pivotal transition from Celtic to feudal kingship in medieval Scotland.

episodes.title: The Dead Sea Scrolls
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the revelatory collection of Biblical texts, legal documents, community rules and literary writings.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the revelatory collection of Biblical texts, legal documents, community rules and literary writings.
episodes.summaryAI: Collection of ancient Jewish manuscripts discovered near the Dead Sea, dating from the third century BCE to the first century CE. Includes the oldest known copies of Hebrew Bible texts, sectarian community rules such as the Rule of the Community, legal documents, apocalyptic writings, and literary compositions. Associated with the Qumran settlement and possibly the Essene sect. The scrolls illuminate Second Temple Judaism, the diversity of Jewish religious practice before rabbinic consolidation, textual transmission of biblical books, and the intellectual context from which early Christianity emerged.

episodes.title: The Death of Elizabeth I
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests John Guy, Clare Jackson and Helen Hackett discuss the death of Queen Elizabeth I and its immediate impact, as a foreign monarch became King in the face of plots and plague.By the spring of 1603, Elizabeth had been Queen for 44 years, and it was clear that she would leave no heir. Many feared that her death would spark insurrection, led perhaps by Puritans, perhaps by Catholics, possibly with the support of Spain. As it became clear that she was dying, Elizabeth's chief minister, Sir Robert Cecil, put into action his covert strategy to secure the succession of King James the Sixth of Scotland.What follows is a story of plots, plague and high politics, as a foreign monarch brought a thoroughly Continental approach to Kingship to the English throne. James's accession was widely welcomed, but his relationship with Cecil was initially tense, and his long procession south from Edinburgh attracted both celebration and criticism. His treatise on Kingship, published on his succession, became a bestseller in London - at least until an outbreak of plague, which also drove him from the capital not long after he arrived. His coronation was hurried through to circumvent plots against him, but his triumphal entry into London had to be delayed until a year after Elizabeth's death. And, as the high expectations which first greeted James were increasingly frustrated, the English started to invoke the ghost of their dead Queen to criticise their new ruler.John Guy is a Fellow of Clare College, University of Cambridge; Clare Jackson is Lecturer and Director of Studies in History at Trinity Hall, University of Cambridge; Helen Hackett is Reader in English at University College, London.

episodes.summaryAI: Political crisis surrounding the death of Elizabeth I in spring 1603 after a 44-year reign with no designated heir. Robert Cecil secretly arranged the succession of James VI of Scotland to the English throne. The transition involved court intrigue, competing plots, plague in London, and high-stakes diplomacy. James's accession united the English and Scottish crowns but his Continental style of kingship clashed with English expectations. The ghost of Elizabeth was repeatedly invoked by critics to measure and challenge the new Stuart monarch's legitimacy and governing approach.

episodes.title: The Death of Stars
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the abrupt transformation of stars after shining brightly for millions or billions of years, once they lack the fuel to counter the force of gravity. Those like our own star, the Sun, become red giants, expanding outwards and consuming nearby planets, only to collapse into dense white dwarves. The massive stars, up to fifty times the mass of the Sun, burst into supernovas, visible from Earth in daytime, and become incredibly dense neutron stars or black holes. In these moments of collapse, the intense heat and pressure can create all the known elements to form gases and dust which may eventually combine to form new stars, new planets and, as on Earth, new life.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the abrupt transformation of stars after shining brightly for millions or billions of years, once they lack the fuel to counter the force of gravity. Those like our own star, the Sun, become red giants, expanding outwards and consuming nearby planets, only to collapse into dense white dwarves. The massive stars, up to fifty times the mass of the Sun, burst into supernovas, visible from Earth in daytime, and become incredibly dense neutron stars or black holes. In these moments of collapse, the intense heat and pressure can create all the known elements to form gases and dust which may eventually combine to form new stars, new planets and, as on Earth, new life.
episodes.summaryAI: Stellar evolution and end states across different mass ranges. Sun-like stars exhaust hydrogen fuel, expand into red giants, shed outer layers as planetary nebulae, and collapse into white dwarfs. Stars above roughly eight solar masses undergo core collapse, triggering supernova explosions that synthesize heavy elements through nucleosynthesis, producing neutron stars or black holes depending on remnant mass. Supernovae disperse enriched gas and dust into the interstellar medium, seeding molecular clouds from which subsequent generations of stars, planetary systems, and ultimately biological chemistry originate. Connects nuclear physics to cosmic chemical evolution.

episodes.title: The Decadent Movement
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the British phase of a movement that spread across Europe in the mid-19th and early 20th centuries. Influenced by Charles Baudelaire and by Walter Pater, these Decadents rejected the mainstream Victorian view that art needed a moral purpose, and valued instead the intense sensations art provoked, celebrating art for art’s sake. Oscar Wilde was at its heart, Aubrey Beardsley adorned it with his illustrations and they, with others, provoked moral panic with their supposed degeneracy. After burning brightly, the movement soon lost its energy in Britain yet it has proved influential.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the British phase of a movement that spread across Europe in the mid-19th and early 20th centuries. Influenced by Charles Baudelaire and by Walter Pater, these Decadents rejected the mainstream Victorian view that art needed a moral purpose, and valued instead the intense sensations art provoked, celebrating art for art’s sake. Oscar Wilde was at its heart, Aubrey Beardsley adorned it with his illustrations and they, with others, provoked moral panic with their supposed degeneracy. After burning brightly, the movement soon lost its energy in Britain yet it has proved influential.
episodes.summaryAI: British Decadent movement from the mid-nineteenth to early twentieth century, a branch of the broader European aesthetic and literary revolt against Victorian moral conventions. Influenced by Charles Baudelaire's poetry and Walter Pater's emphasis on sensory experience. Central figures include Oscar Wilde and illustrator Aubrey Beardsley, associated with The Yellow Book. Advocated art for art's sake, rejecting didactic or morally instructive purposes for literature and visual art. Provoked public moral panic, notably during Wilde's trials. Intersects aestheticism, symbolism, fin-de-siècle culture, and debates over censorship and artistic freedom.

episodes.title: The Delphic Oracle
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Delphic Oracle, the most important source of prophecies in the ancient world. In central Greece, on the flank of Mount Parnassus, lies the ruined city of Delphi. For over a thousand years, between approximately 800 BC and 400 AD, this was the most sacred place in the ancient world. Its chief attraction was the Delphic Oracle, which predicted the future and offered petitioners advice.Travellers journeyed for weeks for a chance to ask the oracle a question. The answers, given by a mysterious priestess called the Pythia, were believed to come straight from the god Apollo. At the height of Greek civilisation the oracle was revered, and its opinion sought in some of the most significant conflicts of the age. Its activities were documented by historians including Xenophon and Plutarch, and it was regularly depicted in Greek tragedy, most famously Sophocles's masterpiece Oedipus the King
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Delphic Oracle, the most important source of prophecies in the ancient world. In central Greece, on the flank of Mount Parnassus, lies the ruined city of Delphi. For over a thousand years, between approximately 800 BC and 400 AD, this was the most sacred place in the ancient world. Its chief attraction was the Delphic Oracle, which predicted the future and offered petitioners advice.Travellers journeyed for weeks for a chance to ask the oracle a question. The answers, given by a mysterious priestess called the Pythia, were believed to come straight from the god Apollo. At the height of Greek civilisation the oracle was revered, and its opinion sought in some of the most significant conflicts of the age. Its activities were documented by historians including Xenophon and Plutarch, and it was regularly depicted in Greek tragedy, most famously Sophocles's masterpiece Oedipus the King
episodes.summaryAI: The oracle at Delphi on Mount Parnassus, the most authoritative prophetic institution in the ancient Greek and Roman world, active from approximately 800 BCE to 400 CE. The Pythia, a priestess, delivered prophecies attributed to Apollo. Consulted by individuals, city-states, and rulers on matters of war, colonization, law, and religion. Documented extensively by ancient writers including Xenophon and Plutarch. The sanctuary functioned as a Panhellenic religious center, housing treasuries and hosting the Pythian Games. Connects ancient religion, political decision-making, and classical historiography.

episodes.title: The Devil
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the Devil. In the Gospel according to John he is ‘a murderer from the beginning’, ‘a liar and the father of lies’, and Dante calls him ‘the ill Worm that pierces the world’s core’. But Milton’s description of him as a powerful rebel was so attractive that William Blake declared that Milton was ‘of the Devil’s party, without knowing it’. To ordinary folk the Devil has often been regarded as a trickster, a tempter, sometimes even a figure of fun rather than of fear. How did this contradictory character come into being? Why did it take so long for him to become an established figure in Christianity? And if the Devil did not exist, would we have had to invent him? With Martin Palmer, theologian and Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture; Alison Rowlands, Senior Lecturer in European History at the University of Essex; David Wootton, Professor of Intellectual History at Queen Mary, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the Devil. In the Gospel according to John he is ‘a murderer from the beginning’, ‘a liar and the father of lies’, and Dante calls him ‘the ill Worm that pierces the world’s core’. But Milton’s description of him as a powerful rebel was so attractive that William Blake declared that Milton was ‘of the Devil’s party, without knowing it’. To ordinary folk the Devil has often been regarded as a trickster, a tempter, sometimes even a figure of fun rather than of fear. How did this contradictory character come into being? Why did it take so long for him to become an established figure in Christianity? And if the Devil did not exist, would we have had to invent him? With Martin Palmer, theologian and Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture; Alison Rowlands, Senior Lecturer in European History at the University of Essex; David Wootton, Professor of Intellectual History at Queen Mary, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: The figure of the Devil across Christian theology, literature, and popular culture. In the Gospel of John, characterized as murderer and father of lies. Dante depicted Satan as a monstrous worm trapped in ice. Milton's Paradise Lost created a compelling rebel angel so attractive that Blake argued Milton sympathized with his creation. In folk tradition, the Devil appears as trickster and tempter rather than cosmic evil. Traces the historical and theological development of this contradictory figure from Hebrew Bible adversary through medieval demonology to Romantic literary reinterpretation.

episodes.title: The Diet of Worms
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Diet of Worms, an event that helped trigger the European Reformation. Nestled on a bend of the River Rhine, in the South West corner of Germany, is the City of Worms. It’s one of the oldest cities in central Europe; it still has its early city walls, its 11th century Romanesque cathedral and a 500-year-old printing industry, but in its centre is a statue of the monk, heretic and founder of the Protestant Reformation, Martin Luther. In 1521 Luther came to Worms to explain his attacks on the Catholic Church to the Holy Roman Emperor, Charles V, and the gathered dignitaries of the German lands. What happened at that meeting, called the Diet of Worms, tore countries apart, set nation against nation, felled kings and plunged dynasties into suicidal bouts of infighting. But why did Martin Luther risk execution to go to the Diet, what was at stake for the big players of medieval Europe and how did events at the Diet of Worms irrevocably change the history of Europe? With Diarmaid MacCulloch, Professor of the History of the Church at Oxford University; David Bagchi, Lecturer in the History of Christian Thought at the University of Hull; Reverend Dr Charlotte Methuen, Lecturer in Reformation History at the University of Oxford.

Nestled on a bend of the River Rhine, in the South West corner of Germany, is the City of Worms. What happened at that meeting, called the Diet of Worms, tore countries apart, set nation against nation, felled kings and plunged dynasties into suicidal bouts of infighting. But why did Martin Luther risk execution to go to the Diet, what was at stake for the big players of medieval Europe and how did events at the Diet of Worms irrevocably change the history of Europe? With Diarmaid MacCulloch, Professor of the History of the Church at Oxford University; David Bagchi, Lecturer in the History of Christian Thought at the University of Hull; Reverend Dr Charlotte Methuen, Lecturer in Reformation History at the University of Oxford.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1521 Diet of Worms, where Martin Luther appeared before Holy Roman Emperor Charles V to defend his theological writings attacking Catholic Church doctrine and papal authority. Luther's refusal to recant triggered political and religious upheaval across Europe, fracturing Western Christendom into Catholic and Protestant confessions. The resulting conflicts set nation against nation, contributed to the overthrow of rulers, and reshaped European geopolitics for centuries. Connects Reformation theology, imperial politics, church-state relations, and the emergence of confessional states in early modern European history.

episodes.title: The Dissolution of the Monasteries
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Henry VIII and the Dissolution of the Monasteries. Was Henry’s decision to destroy monastic culture in this country a tyrannical act of grand larceny or the pious destruction of a corrupt institution? When he was an old man, Michael Sherbrook remembered the momentous events of his youth: “All things of price were either spoiled, plucked away or defaced to the uttermost…it seemed that every person bent himself to filch and spoil what he could. Nothing was spared but the ox-houses and swincotes…” He was talking about the destruction of Roche Abbey, but it could have been Lewes or Fountains, Glastonbury, Tintern or Walsingham, names that haunt the religious past as their ruins haunt the landscape. These were the monasteries, suddenly and for many shockingly, destroyed during the reign of Henry VIII.The conflict was played out with a mix of violence, heroism, political manoeuvring and genuine theological disputation. But what was lost in terms of architecture, painting, treasure and in the religious habits of the monasteries themselves and of the common people who lived with them?With Diarmaid MacCulloch, Professor of the History of the Church at Oxford University; Diane Purkiss, Fellow and Tutor at Keble College, Oxford; George Bernard, Professor of Early Modern History at the University of Southampton
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Henry VIII and the Dissolution of the Monasteries. Was Henry’s decision to destroy monastic culture in this country a tyrannical act of grand larceny or the pious destruction of a corrupt institution? When he was an old man, Michael Sherbrook remembered the momentous events of his youth: “All things of price were either spoiled, plucked away or defaced to the uttermost…it seemed that every person bent himself to filch and spoil what he could. Nothing was spared but the ox-houses and swincotes…” He was talking about the destruction of Roche Abbey, but it could have been Lewes or Fountains, Glastonbury, Tintern or Walsingham, names that haunt the religious past as their ruins haunt the landscape. These were the monasteries, suddenly and for many shockingly, destroyed during the reign of Henry VIII.The conflict was played out with a mix of violence, heroism, political manoeuvring and genuine theological disputation. But what was lost in terms of architecture, painting, treasure and in the religious habits of the monasteries themselves and of the common people who lived with them?With Diarmaid MacCulloch, Professor of the History of the Church at Oxford University; Diane Purkiss, Fellow and Tutor at Keble College, Oxford; George Bernard, Professor of Early Modern History at the University of Southampton
episodes.summaryAI: Henry VIII's dissolution of English monasteries in the 1530s, transferring vast ecclesiastical property to the Crown. Debate persists whether this constituted tyrannical grand larceny of wealth or justified destruction of corrupt religious institutions. Major abbeys affected include Roche, Lewes, Fountains, and Glastonbury. The dissolution destroyed centuries of monastic culture, including medieval architecture, manuscript collections, paintings, liturgical treasures, and communal religious life. Reshaped English land ownership, redistributed wealth to a new gentry class, and permanently altered the physical and cultural landscape of England.

episodes.title: The Divine Right of Kings
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Divine Right of Kings. In Shakespeare’s Macbeth, the character Malcolm describes the magical healing powers of the king: “How he solicits heaven, Himself best knows; but strangely-visited people, All swoln and ulcerous, pitiful to the eye, The mere despair of surgery, he cures; Hanging a golden stamp about their necks, Put on with holy prayers...”The idea that a monarch could heal with his touch flowed from the idea that a king was sacred, appointed by God and above the judgement of earthly powers. It was called the Divine Right of Kings. The idea resided deep in the culture of 17th century Britain affecting the pomp of the Stuart Kings, the writings of Milton and Shakespeare and the political works of John Locke. It is a story that involves witches, regicide, scrofula, Macbeth, miraculous portraits and some of the greatest poetry in the English language. With Justin Champion, Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; Tom Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Birkbeck College, University of London; Clare Jackson, Lecturer and Director of Studies in History at Trinity Hall, Cambridge
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Divine Right of Kings. In Shakespeare’s Macbeth, the character Malcolm describes the magical healing powers of the king: “How he solicits heaven, Himself best knows; but strangely-visited people, All swoln and ulcerous, pitiful to the eye, The mere despair of surgery, he cures; Hanging a golden stamp about their necks, Put on with holy prayers...”The idea that a monarch could heal with his touch flowed from the idea that a king was sacred, appointed by God and above the judgement of earthly powers. It was called the Divine Right of Kings. The idea resided deep in the culture of 17th century Britain affecting the pomp of the Stuart Kings, the writings of Milton and Shakespeare and the political works of John Locke. It is a story that involves witches, regicide, scrofula, Macbeth, miraculous portraits and some of the greatest poetry in the English language. With Justin Champion, Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; Tom Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Birkbeck College, University of London; Clare Jackson, Lecturer and Director of Studies in History at Trinity Hall, Cambridge
episodes.summaryAI: The political theology of divine right of kings, the doctrine that monarchs receive authority directly from God and are accountable only to divine judgment. Central to Stuart dynasty claims in England and Scotland. Intersects with Shakespeare's Macbeth, witchcraft beliefs, the royal touch for scrofula, and miraculous royal portraits. Challenged by John Locke's social contract theory and Milton's republican arguments. The doctrine's arc encompasses regicide of Charles I, the English Civil War, and the Glorious Revolution, connecting political philosophy, sovereignty, religious legitimation, and constitutional development.

episodes.title: The Domesday Book
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Domesday Book, a vast survey of the land and property of much of England and Wales completed in 1086. Twenty years after the Battle of Hastings, William the Conqueror sent officials to most of his new territories to compile a list of land holdings and to gather information about settlements, the people who lived there and even their farm animals. Almost without parallel in European history, the resulting document was of immense importance for many centuries, and remains a central source for medieval historians.

Twenty years after the Battle of Hastings, William the Conqueror sent officials to most of his new territories to compile a list of land holdings and to gather information about settlements, the people who lived there and even their farm animals. episodes.summaryAI: The Domesday Book, a comprehensive survey of land ownership, agricultural resources, and property values across England and parts of Wales, completed in 1086. Commissioned by William the Conqueror twenty years after the Norman Conquest of 1066, royal officials systematically recorded tenants, livestock, ploughlands, mills, and woodland for taxation and administrative purposes. Almost without parallel in medieval European documentation. Serves as a foundational primary source for medieval economic history, feudal social structure, Norman governance, and the transformation of Anglo-Saxon England under Norman rule.

episodes.title: The Dreyfus Affair
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Robert Gildea, Ruth Harris and Robert Tombs discuss the Dreyfus Affair, the 1890s scandal which divided opinion in France for a generation.In 1894, a high-flying Jewish staff officer in the French Army, one Alfred Dreyfus, was convicted of spying for the Prussians. He was publicly humiliated: before a large Paris crowd, he was stripped of his badges of rank and his sword was ceremonially broken. Some of those watching shouted 'Down with Judas!' Then he was dispatched to Devil's Island. But when it emerged that Dreyfus was innocent, a scandal erupted which engulfed the Army, the Church and French society as a whole, exposing deep political rifts, and the nation's endemic anti-Semitism. It pitted Catholics against Republicans, provoked fighting in the streets, and led to the prosecution of the novelist Emile Zola, after his famous J'Accuse polemic against those protecting the real spy and so prolonging Dreyfus's suffering. The Affair became so divisive that it posed a serious threat to the French Republic itself. Finally, in 1905, it prompted the separation of Church and State. The scandal and the anti-Semitism at the heart of it cast a very long shadow. In 1945, when the ultra-nationalist one-time 'anti-Dreyfusard' Charles Maurras was convicted of collaborating with the Nazis, he reacted by declaring that his punishment was Dreyfus's revenge. Robert Gildea is Professor of Modern History at Oxford University; Ruth Harris is Lecturer in Modern History at Oxford University; Robert Tombs is Professor of French History at Cambridge University.

episodes.summaryAI: The Dreyfus Affair beginning in 1894, when Jewish French army officer Alfred Dreyfus was falsely convicted of espionage for Prussia. The scandal exposed deep antisemitism, military corruption, and political divisions within the French Third Republic. Emile Zola's open letter J'Accuse galvanized public opinion and the Dreyfusard movement. The affair polarized French society along lines of army, Catholic Church, republican secularism, and civil liberties. Directly contributed to the 1905 law separating Church and State in France. Connects antisemitism, military justice, press freedom, and republican political culture.

episodes.title: The Druids
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Druids, the priests of ancient Europe. Active in Ireland, Britain and Gaul, the Druids were first written about by Roman authors including Julius Caesar and Pliny, who described them as wearing white robes and cutting mistletoe with golden sickles. They were suspected of leading resistance to the Romans, a fact which eventually led to their eradication from ancient Britain. In the early modern era, however, interest in the Druids revived, and later writers reinvented and romanticised their activities. Little is known for certain about their rituals and beliefs, but modern archaeological discoveries have shed new light on them.

They were suspected of leading resistance to the Romans, a fact which eventually led to their eradication from ancient Britain. episodes.summaryAI: The Druids, priestly class of pre-Roman Celtic societies in Ireland, Britain, and Gaul. Classical sources including Julius Caesar and Pliny the Elder described rituals involving white robes, golden sickles, oak groves, and mistletoe. Romans suspected Druids of organizing resistance to imperial conquest. Very little is known with certainty due to absence of written records from the Druids themselves. Romanticized heavily during the early modern antiquarian revival and linked to Stonehenge. Modern archaeology and comparative Celtic studies provide new evidence about Iron Age religion, ritual practice, and social organization.

episodes.title: The Dutch East India Company
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie or VOC, known in English as the Dutch East India Company. The VOC dominated the spice trade between Asia and Europe for two hundred years, with the British East India Company a distant second. At its peak, the VOC had a virtual monopoly on nutmeg, mace, cloves and cinnamon, displacing the Portuguese and excluding the British, and were the only European traders allowed access to Japan.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie or VOC, known in English as the Dutch East India Company. The VOC dominated the spice trade between Asia and Europe for two hundred years, with the British East India Company a distant second. At its peak, the VOC had a virtual monopoly on nutmeg, mace, cloves and cinnamon, displacing the Portuguese and excluding the British, and were the only European traders allowed access to Japan.
episodes.summaryAI: The Dutch East India Company (VOC), chartered in 1602, which dominated the spice trade between Asia and Europe for roughly two centuries. Held virtual monopolies on nutmeg, mace, cloves, and cinnamon through military force and territorial control in the Indonesian archipelago. Displaced the Portuguese from key trading posts and excluded British competitors. Maintained the only European trading access to Tokugawa Japan at Dejima. Pioneered joint-stock corporate structure and early modern global commerce. Connects colonial expansion, mercantilism, Southeast Asian history, and the origins of modern capitalism.

episodes.title: The Earth's Core
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Earth's Core. The inner core is an extremely dense, solid ball of iron and nickel, the size of the Moon, while the outer core is a flowing liquid, the size of Mars. Thanks to the magnetic fields produced within the core, life on Earth is possible. The magnetosphere protects the Earth from much of the Sun's radiation and the flow of particles which would otherwise strip away the atmosphere. The precise structure of the core and its properties have been fascinating scientists from the Renaissance. Recent seismographs show the picture is even more complex than we might have imagined, with suggestions that the core is spinning at a different speed and on a different axis from the surface.

episodes.summaryAI: Structure and geophysics of Earth's core, comprising an extremely dense solid inner core of iron and nickel approximately the size of the Moon, surrounded by a liquid outer core roughly the size of Mars. Convective flow in the liquid outer core generates Earth's magnetic field through a geodynamo mechanism. The resulting magnetosphere shields the surface from solar radiation, making biological life possible. Evidence suggests the inner core may rotate at a slightly different rate or axis than the mantle. Connects planetary science, geomagnetism, seismology, and conditions for habitability.

episodes.title: The Earth's Origins
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg discusses the origin of the Earth. Ideas used to be very clear about its origins. Bishop Ussher, in 1654 arrived at an exact figure and specified it in his work Annalis Veteris et Novi Testamenti: He deduced that work on Planet Earth began at exactly 9am, on Monday 23rd October 4004 BC. The date was then printed in the margin of The Bible and preached from the pulpit, and right up to the nineteenth century to the left of ‘In The Beginning…’ was specified ‘Before Christ 4004’.Christian believers thought the creation story was solid as a rock…until the geologists arrived. First Hutton, then Smith, and then Lyell smashing away at orthodox belief in a way that made poor Ruskin quail, but in doing so they created a science. With Simon Winchester, author of The Map That Changed the World: the Tale of William Smith and the Birth of A Science; Cherry Lewis, geologist and author of The Dating Game: One Man’s Search for the Age of the Earth; John Cosgrove, Structural Geologist from the Royal School of Mines at Imperial College, London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg discusses the origin of the Earth. Ideas used to be very clear about its origins. Bishop Ussher, in 1654 arrived at an exact figure and specified it in his work Annalis Veteris et Novi Testamenti: He deduced that work on Planet Earth began at exactly 9am, on Monday 23rd October 4004 BC. The date was then printed in the margin of The Bible and preached from the pulpit, and right up to the nineteenth century to the left of ‘In The Beginning…’ was specified ‘Before Christ 4004’.Christian believers thought the creation story was solid as a rock…until the geologists arrived. First Hutton, then Smith, and then Lyell smashing away at orthodox belief in a way that made poor Ruskin quail, but in doing so they created a science. With Simon Winchester, author of The Map That Changed the World: the Tale of William Smith and the Birth of A Science; Cherry Lewis, geologist and author of The Dating Game: One Man’s Search for the Age of the Earth; John Cosgrove, Structural Geologist from the Royal School of Mines at Imperial College, London.
episodes.summaryAI: Historical development of ideas about Earth's age and geological origins. Bishop James Ussher calculated in 1654 that creation began on 23 October 4004 BCE, a date printed in Bible margins and widely accepted. Enlightenment and early modern geologists including James Hutton, William Smith, and Charles Lyell overturned biblical chronology through stratigraphic observation, fossil evidence, and uniformitarian principles. Their work established geology as an empirical science, extending Earth's history to vast timescales. Connects natural theology, biblical literalism, the history of science, deep time, and the foundations of modern earth sciences.

episodes.title: The East India Company
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the private trading company that helped forge the British Empire. At its peak, its influence stretched from western India to eastern China via the farthest reaches of the Indonesian archipelago. It had a fleet of 130 twelve hundred tonne ships and commanded an army of 200,000 troops that came to dominate the Indian subcontinent. It funded governments, toppled princes and generated spectacular amounts of money from trading textiles and spices. But this wasn’t an empire, it wasn’t even a state, it was a company. The East India Company, founded in 1600, lasted for 258 years before the British state gained full control of its activities. In that time it had redrawn the map of India, built an empire and reinvented the fashions and the foodstuffs of Britain. But how did the East India Company become so powerful? How did it change both India and Britain and how was the idea of a company running a country ever accepted by the British Crown?With Huw Bowen, Senior Lecturer in Economic and Social History at the University of Leicester; Linda Colley, School Professor of History at the London School of Economics; Maria Misra, Fellow and Tutor in Modern History at Keble College, Oxford.

episodes.summaryAI: The English East India Company, founded in 1600, operating for 258 years as a commercial and eventually political power across the Indian subcontinent. Commanded a fleet of 130 ships and a private army of 200,000 soldiers. Evolved from a trading enterprise into a governing authority that toppled Indian princes, collected taxes, administered territories, and reshaped the political map of South Asia. Influenced British culture through imported textiles, spices, tea, and foodstuffs. Connects mercantilism, colonial imperialism, British-Indian relations, corporate governance, and global trade networks in early modern history.

episodes.title: The Economic Consequences of the Peace
episodes.synopsis: In an extended version of the programme that was broadcast, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the influential book John Maynard Keynes wrote in 1919 after he resigned in protest from his role at the Paris Peace Conference. There the victors of World War One were deciding the fate of the defeated, especially Germany and Austria-Hungary, and Keynes wanted the world to know his view that the economic consequences would be disastrous for all. Soon Germany used his book to support their claim that the Treaty was grossly unfair, a sentiment that fed into British appeasement in the 1930s and has since prompted debate over whether Keynes had only warned of disaster or somehow contributed to it.
episodes.description: In an extended version of the programme that was broadcast, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the influential book John Maynard Keynes wrote in 1919 after he resigned in protest from his role at the Paris Peace Conference. episodes.summaryAI: John Maynard Keynes's 1919 book The Economic Consequences of the Peace, written after his resignation from the British delegation at the Paris Peace Conference. Keynes argued that the Versailles Treaty's punitive reparations imposed on Germany would cause economic catastrophe and political instability. Germany subsequently cited the book to claim the Treaty was unjust. The work influenced British appeasement policy toward Germany during the 1930s. Ongoing historiographical debate examines whether Keynes accurately predicted disaster or inadvertently undermined collective security. Connects economics, diplomacy, interwar European politics, and the origins of World War II.

episodes.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the text and context of The Book of the Dead, also known as the Book of Coming Forth by Day, the ancient Egyptian collections of spells which were intended to help the recently deceased navigate the underworld. They flourished under the New Kingdom from C16th BC until the end of the Ptolemaic era in C1st BC, and drew on much earlier traditions from the walls of pyramids and on coffin cases. Almost 200 spells survive, though no one collection contains all of them, and one of the best known surrounds the weighing of the heart, the gods' final judgement of the deceased's life.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the text and context of The Book of the Dead, also known as the Book of Coming Forth by Day, the ancient Egyptian collections of spells which were intended to help the recently deceased navigate the underworld. They flourished under the New Kingdom from C16th BC until the end of the Ptolemaic era in C1st BC, and drew on much earlier traditions from the walls of pyramids and on coffin cases. Almost 200 spells survive, though no one collection contains all of them, and one of the best known surrounds the weighing of the heart, the gods' final judgement of the deceased's life.
episodes.summaryAI: Ancient Egyptian funerary literature spanning roughly 1500 years from New Kingdom (c. 16th century BC) through the Ptolemaic era (c. 1st century BC). Collections of nearly 200 spells guiding the deceased through the underworld, drawing on older Pyramid Texts and Coffin Texts traditions. Central motif of the weighing of the heart before divine judges determined the soul's fate. Reflects Egyptian beliefs about death, afterlife, moral judgment, and the relationship between mortals and gods. Illustrates how religious ritual, magic, and written language intersected in ancient Egyptian culture.

episodes.title: The Electron
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss an atomic particle that's become inseparable from modernity. JJ Thomson discovered the electron 125 years ago, so revealing that atoms, supposedly the smallest things, were made of even smaller things. He pictured them inside an atomic ball like a plum pudding, with others later identifying their place outside the nucleus - and it is their location on the outer limit that has helped scientists learn so much about electrons and with electrons. We can use electrons to reveal the secrets of other particles and, while electricity exists whether we understand electrons or not, the applications of electricity and electrons grow as our knowledge grows. Many questions, though, remain unanswered.

episodes.summaryAI: Discovery of the electron by J.J. Thomson in 1897, establishing that atoms contain smaller subatomic particles. Thomson's plum pudding model proposed electrons embedded in positive charge. The electron's position on the outer boundary of atoms made it instrumental for probing atomic structure and identifying further particles. Electrical applications expanded alongside theoretical understanding. Connects particle physics, atomic theory, electromagnetism, and the history of scientific discovery. Ongoing questions about electron behavior link to quantum mechanics, wave-particle duality, and condensed matter physics.

episodes.title: The Emancipation of the Serfs
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 1861 declaration by Tsar Alexander II that serfs were now legally free of their landlords. Until then, over a third of Russians were tied to the land on which they lived and worked and in practice there was little to distinguish their condition from slavery. Russia had lost the Crimean War in 1855 and there had been hundreds of uprisings, prompting the Tsar to tell the nobles, "The existing condition of owning souls cannot remain unchanged. It is better to begin to destroy serfdom from above than to wait until that time when it begins to destroy itself from below." Reform was constrained by the Tsar's wish to keep the nobles on side and, for the serfs, tied by debt and law to the little land they were then allotted, the benefits were hard to see.

episodes.summaryAI: The 1861 emancipation of Russian serfs under Tsar Alexander II, ending legal bondage for over a third of the population. Preceded by Russia's defeat in the Crimean War and hundreds of peasant uprisings that exposed serfdom's economic and military liabilities. Alexander's reform aimed to abolish serfdom from above before revolution from below, but terms were constrained by the need to retain noble support. Connects to agrarian economics, autocratic governance, social stratification, nineteenth-century European reform movements, and Russia's uneven modernization trajectory.

episodes.title: The Empire of Mali
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Empire of Mali which flourished from 1200 to 1600 and was famous in the wider world for the wealth of rulers such as Mansa Musa. Mali was the largest empire in west Africa and for almost 400 years controlled the flow of gold from mines in the south up to the Mediterranean coast and across to the Middle East. These gold mines were the richest known deposits in the 14th Century and produced around half of the world's gold. When Mansa Musa journeyed to Cairo in 1324 as part of his Hajj, he distributed so much gold that its value depreciated by over 10%. Some of the mosques he built on his return survive, albeit rebuilt, such as the UNESCO World Heritage Site of the Great Mosque of Djenne.

Mali was the largest empire in west Africa and for almost 400 years controlled the flow of gold from mines in the south up to the Mediterranean coast and across to the Middle East. These gold mines were the richest known deposits in the 14th Century and produced around half of the world's gold. When Mansa Musa journeyed to Cairo in 1324 as part of his Hajj, he distributed so much gold that its value depreciated by over 10%. episodes.summaryAI: The Empire of Mali, West Africa's largest medieval polity, flourishing c. 1200-1600. Controlled trans-Saharan gold trade routes linking mines to Mediterranean and Middle Eastern markets. Mansa Musa's 1324 Hajj pilgrimage distributed such quantities of gold that its value depreciated by roughly ten percent in Cairo. The UNESCO-listed Great Mosque of Djenne survives as architectural evidence. Covers West African political organization, Islamic influence in sub-Saharan Africa, long-distance trade networks, medieval economic systems, and cultural exchange between Africa, the Arab world, and Europe.

episodes.title: The Enclosures of the 18th Century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the enclosure movement of the 18th and 19th centuries. In the early 19th century, the Northamptonshire poet John Clare took a good look at the countryside and didn’t like what he saw. He wrote: "Fence meeting fence in owners little boundsOf field and meadow, large as garden-grounds,In little parcels little minds to please,With men and flocks imprisoned, ill at ease."Enclosure means literally enclosing a field with a fence or a hedge to prevent others using it. This seemingly innocuous act triggered a revolution in land holding that dispossessed many, enriched a few but helped make the agricultural and industrial revolutions possible. It saw the dominance of private property as the model of ownership, as against the collective rights of previous generations. For some Enclosure underpinned the economic and agricultural development of Modern Britain. But it has also been a cause celebre for the political left ever since Karl Marx argued that enclosures created the industrialised working class and ushered in the capitalist society. What really happened during the era of 18th and 19th century enclosures? Who gained, who lost and what role did Enclosures play in the agricultural and industrial transformation of this country? With Rosemary Sweet, Director of the Centre for Urban History at the University of Leicester; Murray Pittock, Bradley Professor of English Literature at the University of Glasgow; Mark Overton, Professor of Economic and Social History at the University of Exeter.

episodes.summaryAI: Eighteenth-century English enclosure movement converting open fields and commons into private fenced or hedged holdings. Represented a revolution in land tenure that dispossessed many smallholders while enriching larger landowners. Created conditions enabling both the Agricultural Revolution through consolidated efficient farming and the Industrial Revolution by displacing rural laborers into urban wage work. Marx argued enclosures produced the industrialized proletariat. Raises questions about private property versus collective rights, class formation, rural social structure, parliamentary legislation, and the relationship between agrarian change and capitalist development.

episodes.title: The Encyclopédie
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the French encyclopédie, the European Enlightenment in book form. One of its editors, D’Alembert, described its mission as giving an overview of knowledge, as if gazing down on a vast labyrinth of all the branches of human ideas, observing where they separate or unite and even catching sight of the secret routes between them. It was a project that attracted some of the greatest thinkers of the Enlightenment - Voltaire, Rousseau and Diderot - striving to bring together all that was known of the world in one comprehensive encyclopaedia. No subject was too great or too small, so while Voltaire wrote of “fantasie” and “elegance”, Diderot rolled up his sleeves and got to grips with jam-making.The resulting Encyclopédie was a bestseller - running to 28 volumes over more than 20 years, amidst censorship, bans, betrayals and reprieves. It even got them excited on this side of the Channel, with subscribers including Oliver Goldsmith, Samuel Johnson and Charles Burney. So what drove these men to such lengths that they were prepared to risk ridicule, prison, even exile? How did the Encyclopédie embody the values of the Enlightenment? And what was its legacy – did it really fuel the French Revolution? With Judith Hawley, Senior Lecturer in English at Royal Holloway, University of London; Caroline Warman, Fellow and Tutor in French at Jesus College, Oxford; David Wootton, Anniversary Professor of History at the University of York.

It even got them excited on this side of the Channel, with subscribers including Oliver Goldsmith, Samuel Johnson and Charles Burney. With Judith Hawley, Senior Lecturer in English at Royal Holloway, University of London; Caroline Warman, Fellow and Tutor in French at Jesus College, Oxford; David Wootton, Anniversary Professor of History at the University of York.
episodes.summaryAI: The French Encyclopedie, a landmark Enlightenment project edited by Diderot with contributions from Voltaire, Rousseau, and other philosophes. Twenty-eight volumes published over twenty years amid censorship, official bans, and personal betrayals. Became a bestseller that systematized human knowledge across arts, sciences, and trades. Challenged religious authority and established knowledge hierarchies. Debate persists over whether it helped fuel the French Revolution. Connects Enlightenment philosophy, print culture, intellectual freedom, state censorship, the classification of knowledge, and the political power of ideas in eighteenth-century Europe.

episodes.title: The Enlightenment in Britain
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Enlightenment. In Germany it's called Aufklarung, in France it's the Siecle De Lumieres, and in Britain it's called the Age of Enlightenment. It's the period around the eighteenth century when an intellectual movement committed to science and opposed to superstition, embraced the greatest minds of Europe and America; Descartes, Kant, Leibniz, Montesquie, Diderot, Voltaire, Thomas Jefferson, Benjamin Franklin. But where are all the British thinkers? According to the Encyclopaedia Britannica, 'ideas concerning God, reason, nature and man were synthesised into a world view that gained wide assent and that instigated revolutionary developments in art, philosophy and politics'. Some historians in the past have claimed that The Enlightenment passed these islands by, but in his new book Enlightenment: Britain and The Creation of The Modern World, Roy Porter says The Enlightenment was British first, and that the modern world started here. With Roy Porter, Professor in the Social History of Medicine, Wellcome Trust Centre of University College London, Linda Colley, Leverhulme Research Professor and School Professor of History, London School of Economics; Jeremy Black, Professor of History at Exeter University.

episodes.summaryAI: The nature and extent of Enlightenment thought in Britain, debating whether modernity originated there before continental European developments. Roy Porter's argument that the modern world began in Britain, contrasted with the continental tradition of Descartes, Kant, and Leibniz. Raises the question of why British thinkers receive less recognition in standard Enlightenment narratives. Empiricism and scientific method opposed to superstition and dogma. Covers the relationship between British intellectual culture, natural philosophy, religious toleration, commercial society, and the broader European Enlightenment of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

episodes.title: The Enlightenment in Scotland
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Scottish Enlightenment of the 18th century. In 1696 the Edinburgh student, Thomas Aitkenhead, claimed theology was "a rhapsody of feigned and ill invented nonsense". He was hanged for his trouble - just one victim of a repressive religious society called the Scottish Kirk. Yet within 60 years Scotland was transformed by the ideas sweeping the continent in what we call the Enlightenment. This Scottish Enlightenment emerged on a broad front. From philosophy to farming it championed empiricism, questioned religion and debated reason. It was crowned by the philosophical brilliance of David Hume and by Adam Smith – the father of modern economics. But what led to this ‘Scottish Miracle’, was it an indigenous phenomenon or did it depend on influence from abroad? It profoundly influenced the American revolutionaries and the British Empire, but what legacy does it have for Scotland today?With Professor Tom Devine, Director of the Research Institute of Irish and Scottish Studies at the University of Aberdeen; Karen O’Brien, Reader in English and American Literature at the University of Warwick; Alexander Broadie, Professor of Logic and Rhetoric at the University of Glasgow.

episodes.summaryAI: The Scottish Enlightenment of the mid-eighteenth century, a rapid intellectual transformation from a society that hanged student Thomas Aitkenhead for blasphemy in 1696 to a center of philosophical innovation within sixty years. Key figures include David Hume in philosophy and Adam Smith in political economy. Scottish Enlightenment ideas influenced American revolutionary thought, British imperial governance, and modern social science. Connects moral philosophy, empiricism, political economy, university culture, Presbyterian religious context, and the transmission of ideas across the Atlantic world.

episodes.title: The Epic
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the epic. In his essay 'Why the novel matters', DH Lawrence argued that the novel contained all aspects of life. Perhaps better placed to make that claim is the epic. From tackling questions of identity, history, warfare, mortality and the ways of the Gods to narrating tales of magic and supernatural creatures, it was the Greek and Roman poems of Homer and Virgil that underpinned and explained the position of men in the world. And it was these narratives of heroic actions and grand deeds that were to form a template from which many future epics would be constructed from Chaucer's Troilus and Cressayde to Milton’s Paradise Lost. But who are the heroes of these epics? To what extent was the classical epic a political project, a means of creating a founding myth for empire? How did the Renaissance revive the form and how successful were writers such as Milton in rendering the Christian story an epic? And what does the novel owe to the epic?With, John Carey, Emeritus Professor of English Literature at Oxford University; Karen Edwards, Lecturer in English at Exeter University; Oliver Taplin, Professor of Classical Languages and Literature at the University of Oxford.

episodes.summaryAI: The literary genre of epic poetry from its classical origins in Homer and Virgil through later adaptations by Chaucer and Milton. Epic addresses foundational questions of identity, collective history, warfare, heroism, and mortality. Examination of whether classical epic served political purposes legitimizing power. The genre provided a template for narrative ambition across centuries. Relationship between epic and the rise of the novel as a successor form. Covers oral tradition, literary form, classical Greek and Roman literature, medieval and early modern English poetry, and the evolution of narrative genres.

episodes.title: The Etruscan Civilisation
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Etruscan civilisation.Around 800 BC a sophisticated civilisation began to emerge in the area of Italy now known as Tuscany. The Etruscans thrived for the next eight hundred years, extracting and trading copper and developing a sophisticated culture. They were skilled soldiers, architects and artists, and much of their handiwork survives today. They are also believed to have given us the alphabet, an innovation they imported from Greece. Eventually the Etruscan civilisation was absorbed into that of Rome, but not before it had profoundly influenced Roman art and religion, and even its politics

episodes.summaryAI: Etruscan civilization emerging around 800 BC in present-day Tuscany, thriving for approximately eight centuries before absorption into the Roman state. Skilled in military arts, architecture, and visual arts. Credited with transmitting the Greek alphabet to the Italian peninsula. Despite political absorption, Etruscans profoundly influenced Roman art, religious practices, and political institutions. Connects pre-Roman Italian cultures, Mediterranean trade networks, the transmission of writing systems, archaeological evidence for ancient societies, and the cultural foundations underlying Roman civilization.

episodes.title: The Eunuch
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the history and significance of eunuchs, castrated men who were a common feature of many civilisations for at least three thousand years. Eunuchs were typically employed as servants in royal households in the ancient Middle East, China and classical antiquity. In some civilisations they were used as administrators or senior military commanders, sometimes achieving high office. The tradition lingered until surprisingly recently, with castrated singers remaining a feature of Vatican choirs until the nineteenth century, while the last Chinese eunuch of the imperial court died in 1996.

episodes.summaryAI: The history of eunuchs across civilizations spanning over three thousand years. Castrated men served in royal households of the ancient Middle East, China, and classical Mediterranean world, sometimes rising to positions of significant political power. Practice extended to the Vatican's castrati singers through the nineteenth century. The last Chinese imperial eunuch died in 1996. Covers court politics, gender and sexuality, slavery and servitude, religious institutions, cross-cultural comparison of governance structures, and the intersection of bodily modification with social hierarchy and political authority.

episodes.title: The Evolution of Crocodiles
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the remarkable diversity of the animals that dominated life on land in the Triassic, before the rise of the dinosaurs in the Jurassic, and whose descendants are often described wrongly as 'living fossils'. For tens of millions of years, the ancestors of alligators and Nile crocodiles included some as large as a bus, some running on two legs like a T Rex and some that lived like whales. They survived and rebounded from a series of extinction events but, while the range of habitats of the dinosaur descendants such as birds covers much of the globe, those of the crocodiles have contracted, even if the animals themselves continue to evolve today as quickly as they ever have.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the remarkable diversity of the animals that dominated life on land in the Triassic, before the rise of the dinosaurs in the Jurassic, and whose descendants are often described wrongly as 'living fossils'. For tens of millions of years, the ancestors of alligators and Nile crocodiles included some as large as a bus, some running on two legs like a T Rex and some that lived like whales. They survived and rebounded from a series of extinction events but, while the range of habitats of the dinosaur descendants such as birds covers much of the globe, those of the crocodiles have contracted, even if the animals themselves continue to evolve today as quickly as they ever have.
episodes.summaryAI: Evolutionary history of crocodilians from their Triassic origins when ancestral forms dominated terrestrial ecosystems before dinosaurs rose to prominence. Ancient relatives included species as large as buses and bipedal runners, revealing far greater morphological diversity than modern forms suggest. Crocodilians survived multiple mass extinction events. Challenges the common misconception that they are living fossils by demonstrating continued rapid evolutionary change. Connects paleontology, adaptive radiation, mass extinction biology, reptilian physiology, ecological niche occupation, and the relationship between morphological conservatism and underlying genetic evolution.

episodes.title: The Evolution of Horses
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origins of horses, from their dog sized ancestors to their proliferation in the New World until hunted to extinction, their domestication in Asia and their development since. The genetics of the modern horse are the most studied of any animal, after humans, yet it is still uncertain why they only have one toe on each foot when their wider family had more, or whether speed or stamina has been more important in their evolution. What is clear, though, is that when humans first chose to ride horses, as well as eat them, the future of both species changed immeasurably.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origins of horses, from their dog sized ancestors to their proliferation in the New World until hunted to extinction, their domestication in Asia and their development since. The genetics of the modern horse are the most studied of any animal, after humans, yet it is still uncertain why they only have one toe on each foot when their wider family had more, or whether speed or stamina has been more important in their evolution. What is clear, though, is that when humans first chose to ride horses, as well as eat them, the future of both species changed immeasurably.
episodes.summaryAI: Evolutionary history of horses from small dog-sized Eocene ancestors through proliferation in the New World, extinction there likely due to human hunting, and subsequent domestication in Central Asia. Horses possess the most studied genetics of any non-human animal. The reduction to a single toe remains scientifically unexplained. Domestication of horses transformed human civilization through warfare, agriculture, transportation, and trade. Connects paleontology, mammalian evolution, megafaunal extinction, animal domestication, human-animal co-evolution, and the impact of mounted mobility on social and military history.

episodes.title: The Evolution of Lungs
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the evolution of lungs and of the first breaths, which can be traced back 400 million years to when animal life spread from rock pools and swamps onto land, as some fish found an evolutionary advantage in getting their oxygen from air rather than water. Breathing with lungs may have started with fish filling their mouths with air and forcing it down into sacs in their chests, like the buccal pumping that frogs do now, and slowly their swimming muscles adapted to work their lungs like bellows.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the evolution of lungs and of the first breaths, which can be traced back 400 million years to when animal life spread from rock pools and swamps onto land, as some fish found an evolutionary advantage in getting their oxygen from air rather than water. Breathing with lungs may have started with fish filling their mouths with air and forcing it down into sacs in their chests, like the buccal pumping that frogs do now, and slowly their swimming muscles adapted to work their lungs like bellows.
episodes.summaryAI: Evolution of respiratory lungs over approximately 400 million years as vertebrate life transitioned from aquatic to terrestrial environments. Early fish species developed capacity to extract oxygen from air rather than water in shallow rock pools. Buccal pumping mechanisms seen in modern amphibians represent an intermediate stage. Swimming musculature was repurposed to operate lungs as bellows in more derived terrestrial vertebrates. Connects comparative anatomy, vertebrate paleontology, the water-to-land transition, respiratory physiology, natural selection, and the evolutionary co-option of existing structures for novel functions.

episodes.title: The Evolution of Teeth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss theories about the origins of teeth in vertebrates, and what we can learn from sharks in particular and their ancestors. Great white sharks can produce up to 100,000 teeth in their lifetimes. For humans, it is closer to a mere 50 and most of those have to last from childhood. Looking back half a billion years, though, the ancestors of sharks and humans had no teeth in their mouths at all, nor jaws. They were armoured fish, sucking in their food. The theory is that either their tooth-like scales began to appear in mouths as teeth, or some of their taste buds became harder. If we knew more about that, and why sharks can regenerate their teeth, then we might learn how humans could grow new teeth in later lives.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss theories about the origins of teeth in vertebrates, and what we can learn from sharks in particular and their ancestors. Great white sharks can produce up to 100,000 teeth in their lifetimes. For humans, it is closer to a mere 50 and most of those have to last from childhood. Looking back half a billion years, though, the ancestors of sharks and humans had no teeth in their mouths at all, nor jaws. They were armoured fish, sucking in their food. The theory is that either their tooth-like scales began to appear in mouths as teeth, or some of their taste buds became harder.
