episodes.summaryAI: The Korean Empire (1897-1910) marked Korea's brief assertion of sovereign modernity after five centuries of Joseon dynasty rule. King Gojong proclaimed himself Emperor, claiming equal status with neighboring emperors of China, Russia, and Japan. The government pursued modernization including infrastructure, legal reform, and diplomatic outreach. However, geopolitical pressures from Japan and Russia, internal factional struggles, and limited military capacity undermined independence. Japan's victory in the Russo-Japanese War sealed Korea's fate. Formal annexation came in 1910, ending an era of attempted transformation between traditional monarchy and modern nation-state.

episodes.title: The KT Boundary
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the KT Boundary. Across the entire planet, where it hasn't been eroded or destroyed in land movements, there is a thin grey line. In Italy it is 1 cm thick, in America it stretches to three centimetres, but it is all the same thin grey line laid into the rock some 65 million years ago and it bears witness to a cataclysmic event experienced only once in Earth's history. It is called the KT Boundary and geologists believe it is the clue to the death of the dinosaurs and the ultimate reason why mammals and humans inherited the Earth.But exactly what did happen 65 million years ago? How was this extraordinary line created across the Earth and does it really hold the key to the death of the dinosaurs?With Simon Kelley, Head of Department in the Department of Earth Sciences, Open University, Jane Francis, Professor of Palaeoclimatology, University of Leeds; Mike Benton, Professor of Vertebrate Palaeontology in the Department of Earth Sciences, University of Bristol.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the KT Boundary. Across the entire planet, where it hasn't been eroded or destroyed in land movements, there is a thin grey line. In Italy it is 1 cm thick, in America it stretches to three centimetres, but it is all the same thin grey line laid into the rock some 65 million years ago and it bears witness to a cataclysmic event experienced only once in Earth's history. It is called the KT Boundary and geologists believe it is the clue to the death of the dinosaurs and the ultimate reason why mammals and humans inherited the Earth.But exactly what did happen 65 million years ago? How was this extraordinary line created across the Earth and does it really hold the key to the death of the dinosaurs?With Simon Kelley, Head of Department in the Department of Earth Sciences, Open University, Jane Francis, Professor of Palaeoclimatology, University of Leeds; Mike Benton, Professor of Vertebrate Palaeontology in the Department of Earth Sciences, University of Bristol.
episodes.summaryAI: The KT boundary is a thin sedimentary layer dating to approximately 66 million years ago, marking the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction that eliminated non-avian dinosaurs and roughly 75 percent of species. Luis and Walter Alvarez identified anomalous iridium concentrations in the boundary clay in Italy, suggesting an extraterrestrial impact. The Chicxulub crater in Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula was later confirmed as the impact site. Debate continues over whether volcanic activity from the Deccan Traps contributed. The extinction enabled mammalian diversification, ultimately leading to human evolution.

episodes.title: The Kuiper Belt
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Kuiper Belt, a vast region of icy objects at the fringes of our Solar System, beyond Neptune, in which we find the dwarf planet Pluto and countless objects left over from the origins of the solar system, some of which we observe as comets. It extends from where Neptune is, which is 30 times further out than the Earth is from the Sun, to about 500 times the Earth-Sun distance. It covers an immense region of space and it is the part of the Solar System that we know the least about, because it is so remote from us and has been barely detectable by Earth-based telescopes until recent decades. Its existence was predicted before it was known, and study of the Kuiper Belt, and how objects move within it, has led to a theory that there may be a 9th planet far beyond Neptune.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Kuiper Belt, a vast region of icy objects at the fringes of our Solar System, beyond Neptune, in which we find the dwarf planet Pluto and countless objects left over from the origins of the solar system, some of which we observe as comets. It extends from where Neptune is, which is 30 times further out than the Earth is from the Sun, to about 500 times the Earth-Sun distance. It covers an immense region of space and it is the part of the Solar System that we know the least about, because it is so remote from us and has been barely detectable by Earth-based telescopes until recent decades. Its existence was predicted before it was known, and study of the Kuiper Belt, and how objects move within it, has led to a theory that there may be a 9th planet far beyond Neptune.
episodes.summaryAI: The Kuiper Belt is a vast disc of icy bodies extending from Neptune's orbit at 30 astronomical units to roughly 50 AU, with scattered objects reaching much farther. It contains dwarf planets including Pluto, Eris, Makemake, and Haumea, along with countless smaller objects representing primordial material from solar system formation. Short-period comets originate here. The belt's structure, including resonant populations and a sharp outer edge, provides evidence about planetary migration. Gravitational anomalies in distant object orbits have prompted hypotheses about a possible undiscovered ninth planet.

episodes.title: The Lancashire Cotton Famine
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Cotton Famine in Lancashire from 1861-65. The Famine followed the blockade of Confederate Southern ports during the American Civil War which stopped the flow of cotton into mills in Britain and Europe. Reports at the time told of starvation, mass unemployment and migration. Abraham Lincoln wrote, "I know and deeply deplore the sufferings which the working-men of Manchester, and in all Europe, are called to endure in this crisis." While the full cause and extent of the Famine in Lancashire are disputed, the consequences of this and the cotton blockade were far reaching.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Cotton Famine in Lancashire from 1861-65. The Famine followed the blockade of Confederate Southern ports during the American Civil War which stopped the flow of cotton into mills in Britain and Europe. Reports at the time told of starvation, mass unemployment and migration. Abraham Lincoln wrote, "I know and deeply deplore the sufferings which the working-men of Manchester, and in all Europe, are called to endure in this crisis." While the full cause and extent of the Famine in Lancashire are disputed, the consequences of this and the cotton blockade were far reaching.
episodes.summaryAI: The Lancashire Cotton Famine (1861-1865) resulted from the Union naval blockade of Confederate ports during the American Civil War, cutting raw cotton supply to British textile mills. Lancashire's economy, dependent on cotton processing, suffered mass unemployment, starvation, and emigration affecting over 400,000 workers. Despite hardship, many workers supported abolition. Lincoln acknowledged their sacrifice. Relief efforts pioneered public welfare approaches. The crisis exposed dangers of industrial monoculture, accelerated cotton sourcing from India and Egypt, and reshaped global commodity networks connecting American slavery, British industry, and imperial trade.

episodes.title: The Late Devonian Extinction
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the devastating mass extinctions of the Late Devonian Period, roughly 370 million years ago, when around 70 percent of species disappeared. Scientists are still trying to establish exactly what happened, when and why, but this was not as sudden as when an asteroid hits Earth. The Devonian Period had seen the first trees and soils and it had such a diversity of sea life that it’s known as the Age of Fishes, some of them massive and armoured, and, in one of the iconic stages in evolution, some of them moving onto land for the first time. One of the most important theories for the first stage of this extinction is that the new soils washed into oceans, leading to algal blooms that left the waters without oxygen and suffocated the marine life.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the devastating mass extinctions of the Late Devonian Period, roughly 370 million years ago, when around 70 percent of species disappeared. Scientists are still trying to establish exactly what happened, when and why, but this was not as sudden as when an asteroid hits Earth. The Devonian Period had seen the first trees and soils and it had such a diversity of sea life that it’s known as the Age of Fishes, some of them massive and armoured, and, in one of the iconic stages in evolution, some of them moving onto land for the first time. One of the most important theories for the first stage of this extinction is that the new soils washed into oceans, leading to algal blooms that left the waters without oxygen and suffocated the marine life.
episodes.summaryAI: Late Devonian mass extinction approximately 370 million years ago eliminated roughly 70 percent of species over an extended period, distinguishing it from sudden impact-driven events like the end-Cretaceous. Occurred during the Age of Fishes when early forests and terrestrial soils first developed. Leading hypothesis links new soil formation and terrestrial plant colonization to nutrient runoff into shallow seas, triggering massive algal blooms that caused marine anoxia. Primarily devastated marine ecosystems including reef-building organisms, brachiopods, and armored fish. Connects paleobotany, soil science, oceanography, and deep-time biodiversity loss.

episodes.title: The Later Romantics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the poetry, the tragedy and the idealism of the Later Romantics. There must have been something extraordinary about the early 19th century, when six of the greatest poets in the English language were all writing. William Blake was there and Wordsworth and Coleridge had established themselves as the main players in British poetry, when the youthful trio of Byron, Shelley and Keats erupted – if not straight onto the public stage, then at least onto the literary scene. The great chronicler of the age was William Hazlitt, whose romantic maxim was: “Happy are they who live in the dream of their own existence and see all things in the light of their own minds; who walk by faith and hope; to whom the guiding star of their youth still shines from afar and into whom the spirit of the world has not yet entered…the world has no hand on them.” How fitting an epitaph is that for the three great poets who all died tragically young? What were the ideals that drove them and how did their unconventional lifestyles infect the poetry they left behind?With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick; Robert Woof, Director of the Wordsworth Trust; Jennifer Wallace, Director of Studies in English at Peterhouse, Cambridge.

There must have been something extraordinary about the early 19th century, when six of the greatest poets in the English language were all writing. William Blake was there and Wordsworth and Coleridge had established themselves as the main players in British poetry, when the youthful trio of Byron, Shelley and Keats erupted – if not straight onto the public stage, then at least onto the literary scene. The great chronicler of the age was William Hazlitt, whose romantic maxim was: “Happy are they who live in the dream of their own existence and see all things in the light of their own minds; who walk by faith and hope; to whom the guiding star of their youth still shines from afar and into whom the spirit of the world has not yet entered…the world has no hand on them.” How fitting an epitaph is that for the three great poets who all died tragically young? What were the ideals that drove them and how did their unconventional lifestyles infect the poetry they left behind?With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick; Robert Woof, Director of the Wordsworth Trust; Jennifer Wallace, Director of Studies in English at Peterhouse, Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Byron, Shelley, and Keats as central figures of later English Romanticism in the early nineteenth century. Their poetry emphasized imagination, intense emotion, individual liberty, and rebellion against social convention. All three died young, reinforcing Romantic ideals of the doomed artist. Their unconventional lives—political exile, radical atheism, erotic transgression—directly shaped poetic themes and forms. Distinct from first-generation Romantics like Wordsworth and Coleridge in political radicalism and continental influence. Connections span literary aesthetics, political philosophy, biography, and the cultural history of early nineteenth-century Britain and Europe.

episodes.title: The Laws of Motion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Newton’s Laws of Motion. In 1687 Isaac Newton attempted to explain the movements of everything in the universe, from a pea rolling on a plate to the position of the planets. It was a brilliant, vaultingly ambitious and fiendishly complex task; it took him three sentences. These are the three laws of motion with which Newton founded the discipline of classical mechanics and conjoined a series of concepts - inertia, acceleration, force, momentum and mass - by which we still describe the movement of things today. Newton’s laws have been refined over the years – most famously by Einstein - but they were still good enough, 282 years after they were published, to put Neil Armstrong on the Moon. With Simon Schaffer, Professor in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge and Fellow of Darwin College; Raymond Flood, University Lecturer in Computing Studies and Mathematics and Senior Tutor at Kellogg College, University of Oxford; Rob Iliffe, Professor of Intellectual History and History of Science at the University of Sussex.

Newton’s laws have been refined over the years – most famously by Einstein - but they were still good enough, 282 years after they were published, to put Neil Armstrong on the Moon. With Simon Schaffer, Professor in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge and Fellow of Darwin College; Raymond Flood, University Lecturer in Computing Studies and Mathematics and Senior Tutor at Kellogg College, University of Oxford; Rob Iliffe, Professor of Intellectual History and History of Science at the University of Sussex.
episodes.summaryAI: Newton's three laws of motion published in Principia Mathematica 1687 established classical mechanics: inertia, the relationship between force mass and acceleration, and action-reaction pairs. Unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics under universal principles. Foundational concepts include momentum, gravitational force, and deterministic prediction of motion. Remained sufficient for engineering and space travel including the 1969 Moon landing. Later refined by Einstein's special and general relativity at extreme speeds and gravitational fields. Connects physics, mathematics, astronomy, engineering, and the philosophy and history of science.

episodes.title: The Library at Nineveh
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Library at Nineveh, a treasure house of Assyrian ideas from the 7th Century BC. In 1849 a young English adventurer called Henry Layard started digging into a small hill on the banks of the River Tigris in Northern Iraq. Underneath it he found the ancient city of Nineveh. Layard unearthed extraordinary things - wonderful carved reliefs, ancient palace rooms and great statues of winged bulls. He also found a collection of clay tablets, broken up, jumbled around and sitting on the floor of a toilet. It was the remnants of a library and although Layard didn’t know it at the time, it was one of the greatest archaeological finds ever made.Conceived to house the sum of all human knowledge the library was built in the 7th century BC as the grand Assyrian Empire entered its last years. The clay tablets have proved to be a window into all aspects of Assyrian life, its literature, politics, religion and medicine – practises that are both deeply alien to us and alluringly familiar. With Eleanor Robson, Senior Lecturer at Cambridge University and Vice-Chair of the British Institute for the Study of Iraq; Karen Radner, Lecturer in the Ancient Near Eastern History at University College London; Andrew George, Professor of Babylonian at the School of Oriental and African Studies at the University of London

episodes.summaryAI: Austen Henry Layard's 1849 excavation of Nineveh in modern Iraq uncovered the royal library of the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal, built seventh century BC. Thousands of cuneiform clay tablets preserved Mesopotamian literature, science, medicine, law, and religious texts including the Epic of Gilgamesh. Represented an ancient attempt to compile comprehensive human knowledge. Provides a window into Assyrian imperial administration, daily life, and intellectual culture. Connects archaeology, ancient Southwest Asian civilizations, cuneiform studies, library history, and the preservation and organization of knowledge.

episodes.title: The Library of Alexandria
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Library at Alexandria. Founded by King Ptolemy in the 3rd century BC the library was the first attempt to collect all the knowledge of the ancient world in one place. Scholars including Archimedes and Euclid came to study its grand array of papyri. the legacy of the library is with us today, not just in the ideas it stored and the ideas it seeded but also in the way it organised knowledge and the tools developed for dealing with it. It still influences the things we know and the way we know them to this day.With Simon Goldhill, Professor of Greek at the University of Cambridge; Matthew Nicholls, Lecturer in Classics at the University of Reading; Serafina Cuomo, Reader in Roman History at Birkbeck College, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Library at Alexandria. Founded by King Ptolemy in the 3rd century BC the library was the first attempt to collect all the knowledge of the ancient world in one place. Scholars including Archimedes and Euclid came to study its grand array of papyri. the legacy of the library is with us today, not just in the ideas it stored and the ideas it seeded but also in the way it organised knowledge and the tools developed for dealing with it. It still influences the things we know and the way we know them to this day.With Simon Goldhill, Professor of Greek at the University of Cambridge; Matthew Nicholls, Lecturer in Classics at the University of Reading; Serafina Cuomo, Reader in Roman History at Birkbeck College, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: The Library of Alexandria founded under Ptolemy I and expanded by successors in third-century-BC Egypt. Aspired to collect all written knowledge, housing hundreds of thousands of scrolls. Scholars including Archimedes, Euclid, and Eratosthenes worked there, advancing mathematics, geometry, astronomy, and geography. Pioneered cataloguing through Callimachus's Pinakes and textual criticism methods. Its gradual decline involved multiple destructions over centuries. Legacy shaped how knowledge is organized, preserved, and transmitted. Connects Hellenistic civilization, the history of science, bibliography, and institutions for the collection and dissemination of knowledge.

episodes.title: The Life of Stars
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life cycle of stars. In his poem Bright Star John Keats wrote, "Bright Star, would I were steadfast as thou art". For Keats the stars were symbols of eternity- they were beautiful and ordered and unchanging - but modern astronomy tells a very different story. Stars, like everything else in the universe, are subject to change. They are born among vast swirls of gas and dust and they die in the stunning explosions we call supernovae. They create black holes and neutron stars and, in the very beginning of the universe, they forged the elements from which all life is made. But how do stars keep burning for millions of years, why do they self-destruct with such ferocity and what will happen to the universe when they all go out?With Paul Murdin, Senior Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge; Janna Levin, Advanced Fellow in Theoretical Physics in the Department of Applied Mathematics & Theoretical Physics at the University of Cambridge; Phil Charles, Professor of Astronomy at Southampton University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life cycle of stars. In his poem Bright Star John Keats wrote, "Bright Star, would I were steadfast as thou art". For Keats the stars were symbols of eternity- they were beautiful and ordered and unchanging - but modern astronomy tells a very different story. Stars, like everything else in the universe, are subject to change. They are born among vast swirls of gas and dust and they die in the stunning explosions we call supernovae. They create black holes and neutron stars and, in the very beginning of the universe, they forged the elements from which all life is made. But how do stars keep burning for millions of years, why do they self-destruct with such ferocity and what will happen to the universe when they all go out?With Paul Murdin, Senior Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge; Janna Levin, Advanced Fellow in Theoretical Physics in the Department of Applied Mathematics & Theoretical Physics at the University of Cambridge; Phil Charles, Professor of Astronomy at Southampton University.
episodes.summaryAI: Stellar evolution from formation in nebulae of gas and dust through main-sequence hydrogen fusion to death as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes depending on mass. Massive stars end in supernovae that synthesize and disperse heavy elements essential for planets and life, connecting stellar physics to biochemistry. Covers nuclear fusion processes, the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, stellar classification, and ultimate fate of stars. Addresses the long-term cosmological future when all stars exhaust fuel. Spans astrophysics, nuclear physics, cosmology, and the chemical origins of matter.

episodes.title: The Lindisfarne Gospels
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Lindisfarne Gospels. In 597 Pope Gregory the Great ordered that a mission of monks be sent from Rome to convert Britain to its own brand of Christianity - lest it be submerged by the pagan beliefs of the Anglo-Saxon overlords. Just over 100 years later, the Lindisfarne Gospels were produced - lavish and ornate manuscripts, central to the story of how Britain came to be unified by the flag of the Roman Church – and they came to embody a set of beliefs and ideas that dominated Britain for a thousand years. Was the Rome mission in the 6th century the only strand of Christianity to sweep through Britain? Why did Northumbria become a key battleground for ideological dispute? How successful were the Lindisfarne Gospels in unifying the different strands of Christianity? To what extent did they serve as a founding statement of Christian identity in Britain?With Dr Michelle Brown, Curator of Illuminated Manuscripts at the British Library and author of A Guide to Western Historical Scripts: From Antiquity to 1600; Dr Richard Gameson, Reader in Medieval History at Kent University and editor of St Augustine and the Conversion of England; Professor Clare Lees, Professor of Medieval Literature at King's College London and author of Tradition and Belief: Religious Writing in Late Anglo-Saxon England.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Lindisfarne Gospels. In 597 Pope Gregory the Great ordered that a mission of monks be sent from Rome to convert Britain to its own brand of Christianity - lest it be submerged by the pagan beliefs of the Anglo-Saxon overlords. Just over 100 years later, the Lindisfarne Gospels were produced - lavish and ornate manuscripts, central to the story of how Britain came to be unified by the flag of the Roman Church – and they came to embody a set of beliefs and ideas that dominated Britain for a thousand years. episodes.summaryAI: The Lindisfarne Gospels produced around 700 AD on Holy Island in Northumbria, following Pope Gregory's 597 mission to convert Anglo-Saxon Britain. Lavishly illuminated manuscript blending Celtic, Anglo-Saxon, and Mediterranean artistic traditions. Central to the process by which the Roman Church unified disparate Anglo-Saxon kingdoms under shared Christian beliefs and ecclesiastical structures. Reflects insular manuscript art, early medieval literacy, monasticism, and religious devotion. Shaped British cultural and political identity for a millennium. Connects early medieval history, Christianity, manuscript illumination, and the decorative arts.

episodes.title: The Long March
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a foundation story for China as it was reshaped under Mao Zedong. In October 1934, around ninety thousand soldiers of the Red Army broke out of a siege in Jiangxi in the south east of the country, hoping to find a place to regroup and rebuild. They were joined by other armies, and this turned into a very long march to the west and then north, covering thousands of miles of harsh and hostile territory, marshes and mountains, pursued by forces of the ruling Kuomintang for a year. Mao Zedong was among the marchers and emerged at the head of them, and he ensured the officially approved history of the Long March would be an inspiration and education for decades to come.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a foundation story for China as it was reshaped under Mao Zedong. In October 1934, around ninety thousand soldiers of the Red Army broke out of a siege in Jiangxi in the south east of the country, hoping to find a place to regroup and rebuild. They were joined by other armies, and this turned into a very long march to the west and then north, covering thousands of miles of harsh and hostile territory, marshes and mountains, pursued by forces of the ruling Kuomintang for a year. Mao Zedong was among the marchers and emerged at the head of them, and he ensured the officially approved history of the Long March would be an inspiration and education for decades to come.
episodes.summaryAI: The Long March of 1934-1935 when approximately 90,000 Chinese Communist Red Army soldiers broke a Nationalist siege in Jiangxi province and trekked thousands of miles westward then northward to Yan'an in Shaanxi. Mao Zedong consolidated leadership during the march, displacing Soviet-trained rivals at the Zunyi Conference. Enormous casualties from combat, disease, and terrain. Became the founding myth of the People's Republic, shaping official Communist Party historiography and revolutionary ideology for decades. Connects Chinese civil war, communist political theory, military strategy, and twentieth-century Asian history.

episodes.title: The Lunar Society
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Lunar Society. In the late 18th century, with the ascendant British Empire centred on London, a small group of friends met at a house on the crossroads outside Birmingham and applied their minds to the problems of the age. Between them they managed to launch the Industrial Revolution, discover oxygen, harness the power of steam and pioneer the theory of evolution. They were the Lunar Society, a gathering of free and fertile minds centred on the remarkable quartet of Matthew Boulton, James Watt, Joseph Priestly and Erasmus Darwin. The potter Josiah Wedgwood, another member, summed up the ethos of this group when he said that they were ‘living in an age of miracles in which anything could be achieved’.But how did the Lunar Society operate? What was the blend of religious dissent, entrepreneurial spirit and intellectual adventure that proved so fertile and how did their discoveries permanently change the shape and character of this country?With Simon Schaffer, Reader in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge; Jenny Uglow, Honorary Visiting Professor at the University of Warwick and author of The Lunar Men: The Friends who Made the Future; Peter Jones, Professor of French History at the University of Birmingham.

In the late 18th century, with the ascendant British Empire centred on London, a small group of friends met at a house on the crossroads outside Birmingham and applied their minds to the problems of the age. The potter Josiah Wedgwood, another member, summed up the ethos of this group when he said that they were ‘living in an age of miracles in which anything could be achieved’.But how did the Lunar Society operate? What was the blend of religious dissent, entrepreneurial spirit and intellectual adventure that proved so fertile and how did their discoveries permanently change the shape and character of this country?With Simon Schaffer, Reader in History and Philosophy of Science at the University of Cambridge; Jenny Uglow, Honorary Visiting Professor at the University of Warwick and author of The Lunar Men: The Friends who Made the Future; Peter Jones, Professor of French History at the University of Birmingham.
episodes.summaryAI: The Lunar Society of Birmingham, an informal late-eighteenth-century group of industrialists, scientists, and intellectuals meeting monthly near the full moon. Members included Matthew Boulton, James Watt, Joseph Priestley, Erasmus Darwin, and Josiah Wedgwood. Collectively advanced steam engine technology, discovered oxygen, pioneered evolutionary thought, and developed industrial manufacturing processes. Embodied Enlightenment ideals connecting natural philosophy to practical application, catalyzing the Industrial Revolution. Intersects chemistry, engineering, biological theory, industrial technology, political radicalism, and the social history of science in eighteenth-century Britain.

episodes.title: The Mabinogion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the eleven stories of Celtic mythology and Arthurian romance known as The Mabinogion, most of which were told and retold for generations before being written down in C14th. Among them are stories of Pwyll and Rhiannon and their son Pryderi, of Culhwch and Olwen, of the dream of the Emperor Macsen, of Lludd and Llefelys, of magic and giants and imagined history. With common themes but no single author, they project an image of the Island of Britain before the Anglo-Saxons and Normans and before Edward I's conquest of Wales. They came to new prominence, worldwide, from C19th with the translation into English by Lady Charlotte Guest aided by William Owen Pughe.

episodes.summaryAI: The Mabinogion comprises eleven medieval Welsh prose tales combining Celtic mythology, Arthurian romance, and legendary history. Transmitted orally for generations before being written down in the fourteenth century. Includes the Four Branches of the Mabinogi featuring Pwyll and Rhiannon, the tale of Culhwch and Olwen, and Arthurian narratives. Projects an image of pre-Anglo-Saxon, pre-Norman Britain. Translated into English by Lady Charlotte Guest in the nineteenth century, bringing Welsh literary tradition to wider audiences. Connects medieval literature, Celtic mythology, Arthurian legend, oral tradition, and Welsh cultural identity.

episodes.title: The Magna Carta
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Nicholas Vincent, David Carpenter and Michael Clanchy discuss the Magna Carta, the oft-proclaimed foundation of English liberties.The Magna Carta has been cited ever since its issue in 1215 as a foundation stone of English liberties. It includes clauses of universal justice, some of which are still on the statute book, but also sorted out the fishing rights in the upper Thames. Whether Magna Carta is a genuine proclamation of universal liberty or a hotchpotch of baronial self-interest has been debated ever since. Melvyn and his guests examine the ideas contained within it, assess their legacy and find out what really happened all those years ago in a tent in Runnymede.

episodes.summaryAI: Magna Carta sealed in 1215 between King John and rebel barons at Runnymede. Established principles including rule of law, limits on royal taxation without consent, and protections against arbitrary imprisonment. Some clauses remain on the English statute book. Also addressed practical matters like fish weirs on the Thames. Debated as genuine foundation of universal liberty versus pragmatic assertion of baronial self-interest. Influenced subsequent constitutional developments including habeas corpus, English Bill of Rights, and American Constitution. Connects medieval English history, constitutional law, political theory, and the philosophy of rights.

episodes.title: The Mamluks
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Mamluks, who ruled Egypt and Syria from about 1250 to 1517. Originally slave soldiers who managed to depose their masters, they went on to repel the Mongols and the Crusaders to become the dominant force in the medieval Islamic Middle Eastern world. Although the Mamluks were renowned as warriors, under their rule art, crafts and architecture blossomed. Little known by many in the West today, the Mamluks remained in power for almost 300 years until they were eventually overthrown by the Ottomans.

episodes.summaryAI: The Mamluk Sultanate ruled Egypt and Syria from approximately 1250 to 1517. Originally Turkic and Circassian slave soldiers who overthrew their Ayyubid masters. Successfully repelled Mongol invasions at Ain Jalut 1260 and expelled the last Crusader strongholds from the Levant. Presided over a flourishing of Islamic art, architecture, metalwork, and manuscript illumination. Unique political system where power passed between military elites rather than dynastically. Eventually conquered by the Ottoman Empire. Connects Islamic history, medieval military organization, Crusades, Mongol expansion, and Islamic decorative arts and architecture.

episodes.title: The Manhattan Project
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the race to build an atom bomb in the USA during World War Two. Before the war, scientists in Germany had discovered the potential of nuclear fission and scientists in Britain soon argued that this could be used to make an atom bomb, against which there could be no defence other than to own one. The fear among the Allies was that, with its head start, Germany might develop the bomb first and, unmatched, use it on its enemies. The USA took up the challenge in a huge engineering project led by General Groves and Robert Oppenheimer and, once the first bomb had been exploded at Los Alamos in July 1945, it appeared inevitable that the next ones would be used against Japan with devastating results.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the race to build an atom bomb in the USA during World War Two. Before the war, scientists in Germany had discovered the potential of nuclear fission and scientists in Britain soon argued that this could be used to make an atom bomb, against which there could be no defence other than to own one. The fear among the Allies was that, with its head start, Germany might develop the bomb first and, unmatched, use it on its enemies. The USA took up the challenge in a huge engineering project led by General Groves and Robert Oppenheimer and, once the first bomb had been exploded at Los Alamos in July 1945, it appeared inevitable that the next ones would be used against Japan with devastating results.
episodes.summaryAI: The Manhattan Project was the secret American-led program to develop atomic weapons during World War II, driven by fear that Nazi Germany might build a nuclear bomb first following the 1938 discovery of nuclear fission. Led by General Leslie Groves and physicist J. Robert Oppenheimer. First successful test at Trinity site near Los Alamos in July 1945. Atomic bombs subsequently dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Involved unprecedented mobilization of physics, chemistry, and engineering. Connects nuclear physics, military technology, wartime diplomacy, ethical debates about weapons of mass destruction, and twentieth-century geopolitics.

episodes.title: The Mariana Trench
episodes.synopsis: Misha Glenny and guests discuss one of the wonders of the natural world. In 1875 in the western Pacific, the crew of HMS Challenger discovered the Mariana Trench which turned out to be deeper than Everest is high, by two kilometres. Trenches like Mariana form when one tectonic plate slips under another and heads down and there are around fifty of them globally. While at one time some thought it was too dark and deep for life there and others wildly imagined monsters, the truth has turned out to be much more surprising.
episodes.description: Misha Glenny and guests discuss one of the wonders of the natural world. Trenches like Mariana form when one tectonic plate slips under another and heads down and there are around fifty of them globally. While at one time some thought it was too dark and deep for life there and others wildly imagined monsters, the truth has turned out to be much more surprising.
episodes.summaryAI: The Mariana Trench in the western Pacific, discovered in 1875 by HMS Challenger, reaching nearly eleven kilometres depth—deeper than Mount Everest is tall. Formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Mariana Plate at a convergent tectonic boundary. Part of the broader system of oceanic trenches ringing the Pacific. Once presumed lifeless due to extreme pressure, darkness, and cold, but exploration revealed diverse organisms including extremophile microbes, amphipods, and fish adapted to hadal conditions. Connects plate tectonics, oceanography, deep-sea biology, and the history of scientific exploration.

episodes.title: The May Fourth Movement
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the violent protests in China on 4th May 1919 over the nation's humiliation in the Versailles Treaty after World War One. China had supported the Allies, sending workers to dig trenches, and expected to regain the German colonies on its territory, but the Allies and China's leaders chose to give that land to Japan instead. To protestors, this was a travesty and reflected much that was wrong with China, with its corrupt leaders, division by warlords, weakness before Imperial Europe and outdated ideas and values. The movement around 4th May has since been seen as a watershed in China’s development in the 20th century, not least as some of those connected with the movement went on to found the Communist Party of China a few years later.

episodes.summaryAI: The May Fourth Movement erupted on 4 May 1919 when Chinese students violently protested the Treaty of Versailles, which transferred former German concessions in Shandong province to Japan despite China's support for the Allied cause. Sparked broader cultural and intellectual revolution advocating science, democracy, vernacular literature, and rejection of Confucian tradition. Watershed moment in twentieth-century Chinese political development. Several movement participants later founded the Chinese Communist Party in 1921. Connects international diplomacy after World War I, Chinese nationalism, anti-imperialism, cultural modernization, and the origins of Chinese communism.

episodes.title: The Maya Civilization
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Maya Civilization, developed by the Maya people, which flourished in central America from around 250 AD in great cities such as Chichen Itza and Uxmal with advances in mathematics, architecture and astronomy. Long before the Spanish Conquest in the 16th Century, major cities had been abandoned for reasons unknown, although there are many theories including overpopulation and changing climate. The hundreds of Maya sites across Belize, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras and Mexico raise intriguing questions about one of the world's great pre-industrial civilizations.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Maya Civilization, developed by the Maya people, which flourished in central America from around 250 AD in great cities such as Chichen Itza and Uxmal with advances in mathematics, architecture and astronomy. Long before the Spanish Conquest in the 16th Century, major cities had been abandoned for reasons unknown, although there are many theories including overpopulation and changing climate. The hundreds of Maya sites across Belize, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras and Mexico raise intriguing questions about one of the world's great pre-industrial civilizations.
episodes.summaryAI: Maya civilization flourished in Mesoamerica from approximately 250 AD through the Spanish Conquest, spanning present-day Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, El Salvador, and southern Mexico. Major cities including Chichen Itza, Uxmal, Tikal, and Palenque featured monumental architecture including pyramids and palaces. Achievements in mathematics including the concept of zero, sophisticated astronomical observation and calendar systems, hieroglyphic writing, and elaborate religious cosmology. Many major cities mysteriously abandoned during the Terminal Classic period before European contact. Connects pre-Columbian American civilizations, architecture, mathematics, astronomy, writing systems, and agricultural practices.

episodes.title: The Measurement of Time
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the measurement of time. Early civilisations used the movements of heavenly bodies to tell the time, but even in the ancient world more sophisticated timekeeping devices such as waterclocks were known. The development of mechanical clocks in Europe emerged in the medieval period when monks used such devices to sound an alarm to signal it was the hour to pray, although these clocks did not tell them the time. For hundreds of years clocks were inaccurate and it proved hard to remedy the problems, let alone settle on a standard time that the country should follow. It was with the advent of the railways that time finally became standardised in Britain in the mid-19th century and only in 1884 that Greenwich became the prime meridian of the world. Atomic clocks now mark the passing of the days, hours, and minutes and they are capable of keeping time to a second in 15 million years
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the measurement of time. The development of mechanical clocks in Europe emerged in the medieval period when monks used such devices to sound an alarm to signal it was the hour to pray, although these clocks did not tell them the time. episodes.summaryAI: The measurement of time from ancient water clocks and sundials through medieval mechanical clocks developed by monks for regulating prayer schedules. Clocks remained inaccurate for centuries until pendulum and spring mechanisms improved precision. Railways drove standardization of local times across Britain in the nineteenth century. The 1884 International Meridian Conference established Greenwich as the prime meridian for global timekeeping. Modern atomic clocks achieve accuracy to one second in fifteen million years. Connects horology, physics, astronomy, monastic life, industrial transportation, and international standardization of measurement.

episodes.title: The Measurement Problem in Physics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the deepest problems in contemporary physics. It’s called the measurement problem and it emerged from the flurry of activity in the early 20th century that gave rise to Quantum Mechanics. If the most famous fruit in physics is an apple, the most famous animal in physics is a cat. Schrödinger’s cat is named after Edwin Schrödinger, a theoretical physicist who in the early 20th century helped to develop the radical theories of Quantum Mechanics. The cat does not actually exist – it is the subject of a thought experiment – in which the rules of quantum mechanics make it appear both dead and alive at the same time.The problem of a cat that is both dead and alive illustrates the challenges of quantum physics and at the heart of this apparent absurdity is a thing called the measurement problem.The measurement problem arises because we don’t really understand how the atoms that constitute our world behave. They are fundamentally mysterious to us, even shocking, and they defy our attempts to measure and make sense of them. Possible solutions range from the existence of multiple realities to the rather more mundane possibility of an error in our mathematics - but a solution, if found, could transform our understanding of reality. With Basil Hiley, Emeritus Professor of Physics at Birkbeck, University of London, Simon Saunders, Reader in Philosophy of Physics and University Lecturer in Philosophy of Science at the University of Oxford; Roger Penrose, Emeritus Rouse Ball Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford

The cat does not actually exist – it is the subject of a thought experiment – in which the rules of quantum mechanics make it appear both dead and alive at the same time.The problem of a cat that is both dead and alive illustrates the challenges of quantum physics and at the heart of this apparent absurdity is a thing called the measurement problem.The measurement problem arises because we don’t really understand how the atoms that constitute our world behave. They are fundamentally mysterious to us, even shocking, and they defy our attempts to measure and make sense of them. episodes.summaryAI: The measurement problem in quantum mechanics concerns how physical systems transition from superposition states—where particles exist in multiple states simultaneously, as in the Schrodinger's cat thought experiment—to definite outcomes upon observation. Central to debates about the nature of reality at subatomic scales. Proposed solutions include the Copenhagen interpretation emphasizing wavefunction collapse, Everett's many-worlds interpretation positing branching realities, decoherence theory, and hidden-variable approaches. Resolution could transform understanding of consciousness, determinism, and physical reality. Connects quantum physics, philosophy of science, epistemology, and the foundations of mathematics and logic.

episodes.title: The Medici
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Medici family, who dominated Florence's political and cultural life for three centuries. The House of Medici came to prominence in Italy in the fifteenth century as a result of the wealth they had built up through banking. With the rise of Cosimo de' Medici, they became Florence's most powerful and influential dynasty, effectively controlling the city's government. Their patronage of the arts turned Florence into a leading centre of the Renaissance and the Medici Bank was one of the most successful institutions of its day. As well as producing four popes, members of the House of Medici married into various European royal families.

episodes.summaryAI: The Medici family dominated Florence for approximately three centuries from the fifteenth century onward. Wealth derived from international banking, particularly under Cosimo de' Medici who established political control without formal office. Their patronage of artists including Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo transformed Florence into the center of the Italian Renaissance. Produced four popes and married into European royal families including the French monarchy through Catherine and Marie de' Medici. Connects Renaissance art and architecture, banking and finance, Italian political history, Church history, and European dynastic politics.

episodes.title: The Medieval University
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the medieval universities.In the 11th and 12th centuries a new type of institution started to appear in the major cities of Europe. The first universities were those of Bologna and Paris; within a hundred years similar educational organisations were springing up all over the continent. The first universities based their studies on the liberal arts curriculum, a mix of seven separate disciplines derived from the educational theories of Ancient Greece. The universities provided training for those intending to embark on careers in the Church, the law and education. They provided a new focus for intellectual life in Europe, and exerted a significant influence on society around them. And the university model proved so robust that many of these institutions and their medieval innovations still exist today

The first universities were those of Bologna and Paris; within a hundred years similar educational organisations were springing up all over the continent. The first universities based their studies on the liberal arts curriculum, a mix of seven separate disciplines derived from the educational theories of Ancient Greece. The universities provided training for those intending to embark on careers in the Church, the law and education. They provided a new focus for intellectual life in Europe, and exerted a significant influence on society around them. And the university model proved so robust that many of these institutions and their medieval innovations still exist today
episodes.summaryAI: Medieval universities emerged in eleventh- and twelfth-century Europe, with Bologna specializing in law and Paris in theology as the earliest foundations. Adopted the liberal arts curriculum of the trivium (grammar, rhetoric, logic) and quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music) rooted in ancient Greek educational tradition. Served primarily to train clergy, lawyers, and teachers. Developed institutional structures including faculties, degrees, and academic freedom that persist today. Spread across Europe through royal and papal charters. Connects medieval European history, educational organization, the classical intellectual tradition, Church influence, and the preservation of knowledge.

episodes.title: The Metaphysical Poets
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Metaphysical poets, a diffuse group of 17th century writers including John Donne, Andrew Marvell and George Herbert. Mourning the death of a good friend in 1631, the poet Thomas Carew declared: “The Muses' garden, with pedantic weeds O'erspread, was purg'd by thee; the lazy seeds Of servile imitation thrown away, And fresh invention planted.”The gardener in question was a poet, John Donne, and from his fresh invention blossomed a group of 17th century writers called the metaphysical poets. Concerned with sex and death, with science and empire, the metaphysical poets challenged the conventions of Elizabethan poetry with drama and with wit. And they showed that English, like Italian and French, was capable of true poetry.Unashamedly modern, they were saluted by another great modernist, T.S. Eliot, who admired their genius for imagery, the freshness of their language and the drama of their poetic character. But what do we mean by metaphysical poetry, how did it reflect an age of drama and discovery and do poets as different as John Donne, Andrew Marvell and George Herbert really belong together in the canon of English literature? With Tom Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Birkbeck College, University of London; Julie Sanders, Professor of English Literature and Drama at the University of Nottingham; and Tom Cain, Professor of Early Modern Literature at the University of Newcastle upon Tyne

episodes.summaryAI: Seventeenth-century English metaphysical poetry, principally John Donne, Andrew Marvell, and George Herbert. These poets broke from Elizabethan lyric conventions through dramatic voice, intellectual wit, and elaborate conceits fusing disparate ideas. Recurrent themes include erotic love, mortality, religious devotion, and the impact of new science and colonial expansion on poetic imagination. T.S. Eliot's twentieth-century critical revival championed their unified sensibility, arguing they thought and felt simultaneously through imagery. Connections to Baroque aesthetics, Protestant devotional literature, and the relationship between scientific revolution and literary form.

episodes.title: The Mexican Revolution
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Mexican Revolution.In 1908 the President of Mexico, Porfirio Diaz, gave an interview to an American journalist. He was 77 and had ruled the country in autocratic fashion for over thirty years. He discussed the country's economic development and spoke of his intention to retire to his country estate after overseeing a transition to multiparty democracy.Things did not turn out quite like that. Two years later Diaz was toppled by a popular uprising. It was the beginning of a tumultuous decade in which different factions fought for supremacy, and power changed hands many times. The conflict completely changed the face of the country, and resulted in the emergence of Mexico's most celebrated folk hero: Emiliano Zapata
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Mexican Revolution.In 1908 the President of Mexico, Porfirio Diaz, gave an interview to an American journalist. He was 77 and had ruled the country in autocratic fashion for over thirty years. He discussed the country's economic development and spoke of his intention to retire to his country estate after overseeing a transition to multiparty democracy.Things did not turn out quite like that. episodes.summaryAI: The Mexican Revolution beginning 1910 with the overthrow of long-ruling dictator Porfirio Diaz. A decade of civil war among competing factions including constitutionalists, agrarian reformers, and regional warlords. Emiliano Zapata championed indigenous land redistribution in Morelos; Pancho Villa led northern guerrilla forces. The 1917 constitution established land reform, labor rights, and limits on foreign ownership. The revolution transformed Mexican politics, class structure, national identity, and artistic culture, influencing muralism and indigenismo movements across Latin America.

episodes.title: The Mexican-American War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn and guests discuss the 1846-48 conflict after which the United States of Mexico lost half its territory to the United States of America. The US gained land covered by the states of Texas, Utah, California, New Mexico, Nevada, Arizona and part of Colorado. The outcome had a profound impact on Native Americans and led to civil war in defeated Mexico. It also raised the question of whether slavery would be legal in this acquired territory - something that would only be resolved in the US Civil War, which this victory hastened.
episodes.description: Melvyn and guests discuss the 1846-48 conflict after which the United States of Mexico lost half its territory to the United States of America. The US gained land covered by the states of Texas, Utah, California, New Mexico, Nevada, Arizona and part of Colorado. The outcome had a profound impact on Native Americans and led to civil war in defeated Mexico. It also raised the question of whether slavery would be legal in this acquired territory - something that would only be resolved in the US Civil War, which this victory hastened.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1846-1848 war between the United States and Mexico, triggered by disputed Texas annexation and border claims along the Rio Grande. American victory transferred roughly half of Mexico's territory, including California, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, and Arizona. Devastating consequences for Native American peoples already inhabiting ceded lands. The acquisition of vast western territory intensified the slavery debate in Congress, contributing directly to the Compromise of 1850 and ultimately the American Civil War. Connections to Manifest Destiny ideology, US expansionism, and Mexican national memory.

episodes.title: The Microscope
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the development of the microscope, an instrument which has revolutionised our knowledge of the world and the organisms that inhabit it. In the seventeenth century the pioneering work of two scientists, the Dutchman Antonie van Leeuwenhoek and Robert Hooke in England, revealed the teeming microscopic world that exists at scales beyond the capabilities of the naked eye.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the development of the microscope, an instrument which has revolutionised our knowledge of the world and the organisms that inhabit it. In the seventeenth century the pioneering work of two scientists, the Dutchman Antonie van Leeuwenhoek and Robert Hooke in England, revealed the teeming microscopic world that exists at scales beyond the capabilities of the naked eye.
episodes.summaryAI: The development of the microscope and its transformation of natural knowledge from the seventeenth century onward. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek's single-lens instruments revealed microorganisms, spermatozoa, and blood cells; Robert Hooke's Micrographia introduced the concept of biological cells. The microscope opened an entirely unseen world beneath the threshold of naked-eye observation, revolutionizing biology, medicine, and material science. Connections to the scientific revolution, optics, lens-grinding craft, germ theory, histology, and the philosophical implications of invisible realms underlying visible nature.

episodes.title: The Mind/Body Problem
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the mind/body problem in philosophy. At the start of René Descartes' Sixth Meditation he writes: "there is a great difference between mind and body, inasmuch as body is by nature always divisible, and mind is entirely indivisible. For when I consider the mind, or myself in so far as I am merely a thinking thing, I am unable to distinguish many parts within myself; I understand myself to be something quite single and complete. Although the whole mind seems to be united to the whole body, I recognize that if a foot or an arm or any other part of the body is cut off nothing has thereby been taken away from the mind".This thinking is the basis of what's known as 'Cartesian dualism', Descartes' attempt to address one of the central questions in philosophy, the mind/body problem: is the mind part of the body, or the body part of the mind? If they are distinct, then how do they interact? And which of the two is in charge?With Anthony Grayling, Reader in Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London; Julian Baggini, editor of The Philosophers' Magazine; Sue James, Professor of Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London.

And which of the two is in charge?With Anthony Grayling, Reader in Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London; Julian Baggini, editor of The Philosophers' Magazine; Sue James, Professor of Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: The mind-body problem in philosophy, centered on Cartesian dualism. Descartes argued mind is indivisible thinking substance, body is divisible extended substance, creating the question of how two fundamentally different substances interact. Competing positions include occasionalism, parallelism, epiphenomenalism, identity theory, functionalism, and eliminative materialism. The problem raises questions about consciousness, free will, mental causation, and personal identity. Connections to neuroscience, philosophy of mind, cognitive science, artificial intelligence, and debates over whether subjective experience can be reduced to physical brain processes.

episodes.title: The Ming Voyages
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Ming Voyages. In 1405 a Chinese admiral, Zheng He, set sail with an enormous fleet of ships carrying more than 27,000 people. This was the first of seven voyages of discovery which took Zheng and his ships all over the known world, from India to the Gulf of Persia and as far as East Africa. They took Chinese goods, evidence of the might of the Ming Empire, to the people they visited; and they also returned to China with treasure from the places they visited, and exotic items including a live giraffe. These seven voyages were an expression of the might of the Ming Dynasty; but they were regarded by some Chinese courtiers as a wasteful extravagance, and after internal disputes they came to an end in 1433. These extraordinary journeys live on in the imagination and the historical record - and had a profound effect on China's relationship with the rest of the world
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Ming Voyages. In 1405 a Chinese admiral, Zheng He, set sail with an enormous fleet of ships carrying more than 27,000 people. episodes.summaryAI: The seven maritime expeditions of Admiral Zheng He between 1405 and 1433 under the Ming Dynasty. Enormous treasure fleets carrying up to 27,000 personnel sailed to Southeast Asia, India, the Persian Gulf, and the East African coast. These voyages projected Chinese imperial power, established tributary relationships, and facilitated trade in porcelain, silk, and spices. Internal political disputes between court eunuchs and Confucian bureaucrats led to abrupt termination. China's subsequent maritime withdrawal profoundly shaped its isolationist trajectory, contrasting with contemporaneous European Age of Exploration.

episodes.title: The Minoan Civilisation
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Minoan Civilisation.In 1900 the British archaeologist Arthur Evans began excavating some ancient ruins at Knossos on the island of Crete. He uncovered an enormous palace complex which reminded him of the mythical labyrinth of King Minos. Evans had in fact discovered the remnants of a Bronze Age society; in honour of Crete's legendary king he named it the Minoan Civilisation.The Minoans flourished for twelve centuries, and their civilisation was at its height around three and a half thousand years ago, when they built elaborate palaces all over the island. They were sophisticated builders and artists, and appear to have invented one of the world's earliest writing systems. Since Evans's discoveries a hundred years ago, we have learnt much about Minoan society, religion and culture - but much still remains mysterious
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Minoan Civilisation.In 1900 the British archaeologist Arthur Evans began excavating some ancient ruins at Knossos on the island of Crete. episodes.summaryAI: Minoan civilization on Bronze Age Crete, flourishing roughly 2700-1450 BCE, named by archaeologist Arthur Evans after the mythical King Minos following his 1900 excavation of Knossos. Elaborate palace complexes at Knossos, Phaistos, and Malia featured advanced architecture, plumbing, and vivid frescoes. Minoans developed Linear A script, still undeciphered, and possibly influenced later Mycenaean Linear B. A thalassocratic trading society connected to Egypt and the Near East. Destruction causes debated, including volcanic eruption of Thera and Mycenaean conquest. Much remains enigmatic.

episodes.title: The Mokrani Revolt
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the revolt that broke out in 1871 in Algeria against French rule, spreading over hundreds of miles and countless towns and villages before being brutally suppressed. It began with the powerful Cheikh Mokrani and his family and was taken up by hundreds of thousands, becoming the last major revolt there before Algeria’s war of independence in 1954. In the wake of its swift suppression though came further waves of French migrants to settle on newly confiscated lands, themselves displaced by French defeat in Europe and the loss of Alsace-Lorraine, and their arrival only increased tensions. The Mokrani Revolt came to be seen as a watershed between earlier Ottoman rule and full national identity, an inspiration to nationalists in the 1950s.

episodes.summaryAI: The 1871 Mokrani Revolt in French colonial Algeria, led by Cheikh Mokrani and involving hundreds of thousands of Algerian rebels across Kabylia and beyond. The largest anticolonial insurrection between the initial French conquest and the 1954-1962 war of independence. Triggered partly by France's defeat in the Franco-Prussian War and local grievances over land dispossession. Brutal French military suppression followed by mass confiscation of tribal lands and accelerated European settler colonization. Connections to North African resistance movements, colonial land policy, and the long trajectory toward Algerian independence.

episodes.title: The Monarchy
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the British monarchy. In the last two hundred and fifty years, we’ve beheaded one king, exiled another, hired a distant German-speaking dynasty to fill the monarch’s role, and then mocked and ignored them, suffered a mad man and then a lavish sensualist, threatened a young queen, and then, over a century ago, invented a pageantry which brought majesty to a monarchy which is now tilting at the twenty first century against many and mighty odds. How has the monarchy survived since the execution of Charles the First two hundred and fifty years ago and what relevance does it have in a devolved Britain?With Professor David Cannadine, Director of the Institute of Historical Research, London and former Lecturer in History and Fellow, Christ’s College, Cambridge; Bea Campbell, sociologist, journalist and author of Diana, Princess of Wales.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the British monarchy. In the last two hundred and fifty years, we’ve beheaded one king, exiled another, hired a distant German-speaking dynasty to fill the monarch’s role, and then mocked and ignored them, suffered a mad man and then a lavish sensualist, threatened a young queen, and then, over a century ago, invented a pageantry which brought majesty to a monarchy which is now tilting at the twenty first century against many and mighty odds. How has the monarchy survived since the execution of Charles the First two hundred and fifty years ago and what relevance does it have in a devolved Britain?With Professor David Cannadine, Director of the Institute of Historical Research, London and former Lecturer in History and Fellow, Christ’s College, Cambridge; Bea Campbell, sociologist, journalist and author of Diana, Princess of Wales.
episodes.summaryAI: The British monarchy's survival and adaptation over 250 years since the execution of Charles I in 1649. Key constitutional crises include the Glorious Revolution deposing James II, installation of the Hanoverian dynasty, and gradual transition from governing power to ceremonial role. The monarchy persisted through civil war, republican sentiment, empire, democratization, and modern media scrutiny. Questions of constitutional function, royal prerogative, public funding, and cultural symbolism. Connections to parliamentary sovereignty, the unwritten British constitution, hereditary privilege, and debates over republicanism versus tradition.

episodes.title: The Moon
episodes.synopsis: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this first pick, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the origins, science and mythology of the moon.
episodes.description: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this first pick, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the origins, science and mythology of the moon.
episodes.summaryAI: The Moon's origins, physical science, and cultural mythology. Scientific accounts include the giant impact hypothesis proposing a Mars-sized body struck early Earth. Lunar geology encompasses maria, highlands, craters, and tidal locking. The Moon governs Earth's tides and stabilizes axial tilt, influencing climate and biological rhythms. Mythological associations span many cultures: Greek Selene and Artemis, Roman Luna, and lunar deities in Mesopotamian, Hindu, and East Asian traditions. Connections to astronomy, space exploration, the Apollo missions, calendrical systems, and the poetic imagination.

episodes.title: The Morant Bay Rebellion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rebellion that broke out in Jamaica on 11th October 1865 when Paul Bogle (1822-65) led a protest march from Stony Gut to the courthouse in nearby Morant Bay. There were many grounds for grievance that day and soon anger turned to bloodshed. Although the British had abolished slavery 30 years before, the plantation owners were still dominant and the conditions for the majority of people on Jamaica were poor. The British governor suppressed this rebellion brutally and soon people in Jamaica lost what right they had to rule themselves. Some in Britain, like Charles Dickens, supported the governor's actions while others, like Charles Darwin, wanted him tried for murder.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rebellion that broke out in Jamaica on 11th October 1865 when Paul Bogle (1822-65) led a protest march from Stony Gut to the courthouse in nearby Morant Bay. episodes.summaryAI: The Morant Bay Rebellion of October 1865 in Jamaica, led by Paul Bogle. Three decades after emancipation, formerly enslaved people remained impoverished under a plantation-dominated economy. Bogle's protest march escalated into violence; Governor Edward Eyre declared martial law and executed hundreds, including George William Gordon. The event divided Victorian Britain: Charles Dickens supported Eyre's actions while Charles Darwin and John Stuart Mill demanded prosecution. The crisis prompted dissolution of the Jamaican Assembly and imposition of Crown Colony rule. Connections to post-emancipation inequality, colonial governance, and racial justice.

episodes.title: The Mughal Empire
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Mughal Empire which, at its height, stretched from Bengal in the East to Gujarat in the West, and from Lahore in the North to Madras in the South. It covered the whole of present day northern India, Pakistan, Afghanistan and Bangladesh, and became famous for the Taj Mahal, the Koh-i-Noor and the Peacock Throne. In 1631 a Dutch naturalist Johannes de Laet published his account of the vast Empire, “the nobles live in indescribable luxury and extravagance, caring only to indulge themselves whilst they can, in every kind of pleasure. Their greatest magnificence is in their women’s quarters, for they marry three or four wives or sometimes more”.But were they really the opulent despots of European imagination? If so, how did they maintain such a vast territory? And to what extent was the success of the British Raj a legacy of their rule? With Sanjay Subrahmanyam, Professor of Indian History and Culture at the University of Oxford; Susan Stronge, Curator in the Asian Department of the Victoria and Albert Museum, London; Chandrika Kaul, Lecturer in Imperial History at the University of St Andrews.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Mughal Empire which, at its height, stretched from Bengal in the East to Gujarat in the West, and from Lahore in the North to Madras in the South. Their greatest magnificence is in their women’s quarters, for they marry three or four wives or sometimes more”.But were they really the opulent despots of European imagination? With Sanjay Subrahmanyam, Professor of Indian History and Culture at the University of Oxford; Susan Stronge, Curator in the Asian Department of the Victoria and Albert Museum, London; Chandrika Kaul, Lecturer in Imperial History at the University of St Andrews.
episodes.summaryAI: The Mughal Empire spanning the Indian subcontinent from the early sixteenth to mid-nineteenth century, ruling from Bengal to Gujarat and Lahore to the Deccan. Founded by Babur, consolidated by Akbar's administrative reforms and religious tolerance policies. Famous monuments include the Taj Mahal; treasures include the Koh-i-Noor diamond and Peacock Throne. Sophisticated revenue systems, Mansabdari military-bureaucratic hierarchy, and syncretic Indo-Islamic cultural production in art, architecture, and literature. Gradual decline enabled East India Company expansion. Legacy shaped British colonial administration and modern South Asian identity.

episodes.title: The Multiverse
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests will be leaving the studio, the planet and indeed, the universe to take a tour of the Multiverse. If you look up the word ‘universe’ in the Oxford English Dictionary you will find the following definition: “The whole of created or existing things regarded collectively; all things (including the earth, the heavens, and all the phenomena of space) considered as constituting a systematic whole.” That sounds fairly comprehensive as a description of everything, but for an increasing number of physicists and cosmologists the universe is not enough. They talk of a multiverse – literally many universes – to explain aspects of their theory, the character of the universe and the riddle of our existence within it. Indeed, compared to the scope and complexity of the multiverse, the whole of our known reality may be as a speck of sand upon a beach.The idea of a multiverse is still controversial, some argue that it isn’t even science, because it is based on an idea that we may never be able to prove or even see. But what might a multiverse be like, why are physicists and cosmologists increasingly interested in it and is it really scientific to discuss the existence of universes we may never know anything With Martin Rees, President of the Royal Society and Professor of Cosmology and Astrophysics at the University of Cambridge; Fay Dowker, Reader in Theoretical Physics at Imperial College; Bernard Carr, Professor of Mathematics and Astronomy at Queen Mary, University of London

If you look up the word ‘universe’ in the Oxford English Dictionary you will find the following definition: “The whole of created or existing things regarded collectively; all things (including the earth, the heavens, and all the phenomena of space) considered as constituting a systematic whole.” That sounds fairly comprehensive as a description of everything, but for an increasing number of physicists and cosmologists the universe is not enough.
