However, with the split he inadvertently created us - lonely creatures forever searching for our other halves. Aristophanes explained to Socrates, “human nature was originally one and we were a whole, and the desire and pursuit of the whole is what we call love”. This is one version of love that still seems to have strange echoes in the culture of today, but how has the Western understanding of the Philosophy of Love developed since Plato? Has it always been about finding our ‘other half’? With Professor Roger Scruton, author of many books including Sexual Desire; Angie Hobbes, lecturer in philosophy at Warwick University; Thomas Docherty, Professor of English at the University of Kent.
episodes.summaryAI: Western conceptions of love trace from Plato's Symposium, where Aristophanes mythologises humans as once-whole beings split by Zeus, forever seeking reunification. This idea of love as completing a missing half has persisted powerfully. Subsequent thinkers reshaped the concept: Ovid emphasised erotic pursuit, medieval courtly love idealised unattainable devotion, Christian theology elevated agape over eros, and Enlightenment philosophy connected love to autonomy and rational choice. Modern perspectives draw on psychoanalysis, existentialism, and feminism, questioning whether love is discovery, construction, or biological drive.

episodes.title: The Philosophy of Solitude
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the philosophy of solitude. The state of being alone can arise for many different reasons: imprisonment, exile or personal choice. It can be prompted by religious belief, personal necessity or a philosophical need for solitary contemplation. Many thinkers have dealt with the subject, from Plato and Aristotle to Hannah Arendt. It's a philosophical tradition that takes in medieval religious mystics, the work of Montaigne and Adam Smith, and the great American poets of solitude Thoreau and Emerson.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the philosophy of solitude. The state of being alone can arise for many different reasons: imprisonment, exile or personal choice. It can be prompted by religious belief, personal necessity or a philosophical need for solitary contemplation. Many thinkers have dealt with the subject, from Plato and Aristotle to Hannah Arendt. It's a philosophical tradition that takes in medieval religious mystics, the work of Montaigne and Adam Smith, and the great American poets of solitude Thoreau and Emerson.
episodes.summaryAI: Solitude spans a spectrum from enforced isolation in imprisonment to voluntary withdrawal for contemplation. Plato and Aristotle debated whether the examined life requires retreat from society. Medieval Christian mystics pursued solitary communion with God, while Montaigne retired to his tower library for self-reflection through essays. Thoreau's Walden experiment tested deliberate simplicity, and Emerson championed self-reliance through independent thought. Hannah Arendt distinguished solitude from loneliness, arguing productive inner dialogue requires chosen aloneness. Religious, philosophical, and literary traditions reveal solitude as essential to self-knowledge.

episodes.title: The Phoenicians
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Phoenicians. The Greek historian Herodotus wrote about a people from the Levant who were accomplished sailors and traders, and who taught the Greeks their alphabet. He called them the Phoenicians, the Greek word for purple, although it is not known what they called themselves. By about 700 BC they were trading all over the Mediterranean, taking Egyptian and Syrian goods as far as Spain and North Africa. Although they were hugely influential in the ancient world, they left few records of their own; some contemporary scholars believe that the Phoenicians were never a unified civilisation but a loose association of neighbouring city-states.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Phoenicians. The Greek historian Herodotus wrote about a people from the Levant who were accomplished sailors and traders, and who taught the Greeks their alphabet. He called them the Phoenicians, the Greek word for purple, although it is not known what they called themselves. By about 700 BC they were trading all over the Mediterranean, taking Egyptian and Syrian goods as far as Spain and North Africa. Although they were hugely influential in the ancient world, they left few records of their own; some contemporary scholars believe that the Phoenicians were never a unified civilisation but a loose association of neighbouring city-states.
episodes.summaryAI: The Phoenicians were Levantine coastal peoples centred on city-states including Tyre, Sidon, and Byblos who became the ancient Mediterranean's foremost seafarers and traders. By 700 BC their commercial networks stretched from the Levant to Iberia, founding Carthage and other colonies. They transmitted their alphabet to the Greeks, forming the basis of Western writing systems. Phoenicians left remarkably few records in their own voice, and they may never have constituted a unified political entity, instead functioning as independent trading cities sharing language, religion, and maritime culture.

episodes.title: The Photon
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the photon, one of the most enigmatic objects in the Universe. Generations of scientists have struggled to understand the nature of light. In the late nineteenth century it seemed clear that light was an electromagnetic wave. But the work of physicists including Planck and Einstein shed doubt on this theory. Today scientists accept that light can behave both as a wave and a particle, the latter known as the photon. Understanding light in terms of photons has enabled the development of some of the most important technology of the last fifty years.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the photon, one of the most enigmatic objects in the Universe. Generations of scientists have struggled to understand the nature of light. In the late nineteenth century it seemed clear that light was an electromagnetic wave. But the work of physicists including Planck and Einstein shed doubt on this theory. Today scientists accept that light can behave both as a wave and a particle, the latter known as the photon. Understanding light in terms of photons has enabled the development of some of the most important technology of the last fifty years.
episodes.summaryAI: The photon is the quantum of electromagnetic radiation, embodying wave-particle duality at the heart of modern physics. Planck's quantisation of energy and Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect overturned classical wave theory, establishing that light travels in discrete packets. Photons are massless bosons mediating the electromagnetic force. Understanding their behaviour enabled lasers, fibre-optic communications, photovoltaic cells, LED lighting, medical imaging, and quantum computing. Photon science bridges fundamental particle physics and the applied technologies that define contemporary life.

episodes.title: The Physics of Reality
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the physics of reality. When Quantum Mechanics was developed in the early 20th century reality changed forever. In the quantum world particles could be in two places at once, they disappeared for no reason and reappeared in unpredictable locations, they even acted differently according to whether we were watching them. It was so shocking that Erwin Schrodinger, one of the founders of Quantum Theory, said "I don’t like it and I'm sorry I ever had anything to do with it." He even developed an experiment with a cat to show how absurd it was. Quantum Theory was absurd, it disagreed with the classical physics of Newton and Einstein and it clashed with our experience of the everyday world. Footballs do not disappear without reason, cats do not split into two and shoes do not act differently when we are not looking at them. Or do they? Eighty years later we are still debating whether the absurd might actually be true. But why are features of quantum physics not seen in our experience of everyday reality? Can the classical and quantum worlds be reconciled, and why should reality make sense to us? With Roger Penrose, Emeritus Rouse Ball Professor of Mathematics, Oxford University; Fay Dowker, Lecturer in Theoretical Physics, Queen Mary, University of London; Tony Sudbery, Professor of Mathematics, University of York.

It was so shocking that Erwin Schrodinger, one of the founders of Quantum Theory, said "I don’t like it and I'm sorry I ever had anything to do with it." He even developed an experiment with a cat to show how absurd it was. Quantum Theory was absurd, it disagreed with the classical physics of Newton and Einstein and it clashed with our experience of the everyday world. Footballs do not disappear without reason, cats do not split into two and shoes do not act differently when we are not looking at them. Or do they? Eighty years later we are still debating whether the absurd might actually be true. But why are features of quantum physics not seen in our experience of everyday reality? Can the classical and quantum worlds be reconciled, and why should reality make sense to us? With Roger Penrose, Emeritus Rouse Ball Professor of Mathematics, Oxford University; Fay Dowker, Lecturer in Theoretical Physics, Queen Mary, University of London; Tony Sudbery, Professor of Mathematics, University of York.
episodes.summaryAI: Quantum mechanics reveals that subatomic particles can exist in superposition, occupy multiple positions simultaneously, tunnel through barriers, and behave differently under observation. Schrodinger's cat paradox dramatises the absurdity of applying quantum rules to everyday objects. The measurement problem and decoherence help explain why macroscopic reality appears classical: interactions with surrounding environments rapidly collapse quantum superpositions. Interpretations range from Copenhagen's observer-dependent collapse to many-worlds branching universes. Understanding why footballs do not vanish remains one of physics' deepest questions about the boundary between quantum and classical domains.

episodes.title: The Physics of Time
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the physics of time. When writing the Principia Mathematica, Isaac Newton declared his hand on most of the big questions in physics. He outlined the nature of space, explained the motions of the planets and conceived the operation of gravity. He also laid down the law on time declaring: “Absolute, true, and mathematical time, of itself and from its own nature, flows equably without relation to anything external.” For Newton time was absolute and set apart from the universe, but with the theories of Albert Einstein time became more complicated; it could be squeezed and distorted and was different in different places.Time is integral to our experience of things but we find it very difficult to think about. It may not even exist and yet seems written into the existence of absolutely everything. With Jim Al-Khalili, Professor of Theoretical Physics and Chair in the Public Engagement in Science at the University of Surrey; Monica Grady, Professor of Planetary and Space Sciences at the Open University and Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the physics of time. When writing the Principia Mathematica, Isaac Newton declared his hand on most of the big questions in physics. He outlined the nature of space, explained the motions of the planets and conceived the operation of gravity. He also laid down the law on time declaring: “Absolute, true, and mathematical time, of itself and from its own nature, flows equably without relation to anything external.” For Newton time was absolute and set apart from the universe, but with the theories of Albert Einstein time became more complicated; it could be squeezed and distorted and was different in different places.Time is integral to our experience of things but we find it very difficult to think about. It may not even exist and yet seems written into the existence of absolutely everything. With Jim Al-Khalili, Professor of Theoretical Physics and Chair in the Public Engagement in Science at the University of Surrey; Monica Grady, Professor of Planetary and Space Sciences at the Open University and Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: Newton conceived time as absolute and universal, flowing uniformly regardless of events. Einstein's relativity shattered this, showing time dilates with velocity and warps near massive objects, making it inseparable from space. Thermodynamics provides time's arrow through entropy, yet fundamental equations work identically forwards and backwards. Quantum gravity theories question whether time is fundamental or emergent. Time is integral to human consciousness and experience, woven into causality and the structure of the universe, yet its ultimate nature remains among the deepest unresolved problems in physics.

episodes.title: The Physiocrats
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Physiocrats, an important group of economic thinkers in eighteenth-century France. The Physiocrats believed that the land was the ultimate source of all wealth, and crucially that markets should not be constrained by governments. Their ideas were important not just to economists but to the course of politics in France. Later they influenced the work of Adam Smith, who called Physiocracy "perhaps the nearest approximation to the truth that has yet been published upon the subject of political economy."
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Physiocrats, an important group of economic thinkers in eighteenth-century France. The Physiocrats believed that the land was the ultimate source of all wealth, and crucially that markets should not be constrained by governments. Their ideas were important not just to economists but to the course of politics in France. Later they influenced the work of Adam Smith, who called Physiocracy "perhaps the nearest approximation to the truth that has yet been published upon the subject of political economy."
episodes.summaryAI: The Physiocrats were an influential school of eighteenth-century French economists led by Francois Quesnay. They argued that land and agriculture constituted the sole source of genuine wealth, with manufacturing and commerce merely transforming existing value. Their doctrine of laissez-faire opposed mercantilist restrictions, insisting governments should not constrain markets. Quesnay's Tableau Economique modelled economic circulation as a system. Adam Smith, deeply influenced by their thinking, called Physiocracy the nearest approximation to truth yet published on political economy, though he ultimately broadened the theory of productive labour beyond agriculture.

episodes.title: The Picts
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss The Picts and, to mark our twentieth season, that discussion takes place in front of a student audience at the University of Glasgow, many of them studying this topic. According to Bede writing c731AD, the Picts, with the English, Britons, Scots and Latins, formed one of the five nations of Britain, 'an island in the ocean formerly called Albion'. The Picts is now a label given to the people who lived in Scotland north of the Forth-Clyde line from about 300 AD to 900 AD, from the time of the Romans to the time of the Vikings. They left intricately carved stones, such as the one above with a bull motif, from Burghead, Moray, Scotland, but there are relatively few other traces. Who were they, and what happened to them? And what has been learned in the last twenty years, through archaeology?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss The Picts and, to mark our twentieth season, that discussion takes place in front of a student audience at the University of Glasgow, many of them studying this topic. According to Bede writing c731AD, the Picts, with the English, Britons, Scots and Latins, formed one of the five nations of Britain, 'an island in the ocean formerly called Albion'. The Picts is now a label given to the people who lived in Scotland north of the Forth-Clyde line from about 300 AD to 900 AD, from the time of the Romans to the time of the Vikings. episodes.summaryAI: The Picts inhabited Scotland north of the Forth-Clyde line from roughly 300 to 900 AD. They left intricately carved symbol stones featuring animals, geometric patterns, and Christian crosses, yet relatively few written records survive. Roman sources named them, and they resisted imperial expansion. Their political structures, language origins, and cultural practices remain debated. By the ninth century, Pictish identity merged with Gaelic-speaking Scots under Kenneth MacAlpin's dynasty. Recent archaeological discoveries including settlements, metalwork, and monastic sites have significantly expanded understanding of this enigmatic early medieval people.

episodes.title: The Pilgrim Fathers
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Pilgrim Fathers and their 1620 voyage to the New World on the Mayflower. Every year on the fourth Thursday in November, Americans go home to their families and sit down to a meal. It’s called Thanksgiving and it echoes a meal that took place nearly 400 years ago, when a group of religious exiles from Lincolnshire sat down, after a brutal winter, to celebrate their first harvest in the New World. They celebrated it in company with the American Indians who had helped them to survive.These settlers are called the Pilgrim Fathers. They were not the first and certainly not the largest of the early settlements but their Plymouth colony has retained a hold on the American imagination which the larger, older, violent and money-driven settlement of Jamestown has not.With Kathleen Burk, Professor of Modern and Contemporary History at University College London; Harry Bennett, Reader in History and Head of Humanities at the University of Plymouth; Tim Lockley, Associate Professor of History at the University of Warwick
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Pilgrim Fathers and their 1620 voyage to the New World on the Mayflower. Every year on the fourth Thursday in November, Americans go home to their families and sit down to a meal. It’s called Thanksgiving and it echoes a meal that took place nearly 400 years ago, when a group of religious exiles from Lincolnshire sat down, after a brutal winter, to celebrate their first harvest in the New World. They celebrated it in company with the American Indians who had helped them to survive.These settlers are called the Pilgrim Fathers. They were not the first and certainly not the largest of the early settlements but their Plymouth colony has retained a hold on the American imagination which the larger, older, violent and money-driven settlement of Jamestown has not.With Kathleen Burk, Professor of Modern and Contemporary History at University College London; Harry Bennett, Reader in History and Head of Humanities at the University of Plymouth; Tim Lockley, Associate Professor of History at the University of Warwick
episodes.summaryAI: In 1620, a group of religious separatists from Lincolnshire and surrounding areas sailed aboard the Mayflower to establish Plymouth Colony in Massachusetts. These Pilgrims had previously fled to Leiden seeking freedom to worship outside the Church of England. Their Mayflower Compact established a framework for self-governance. The celebrated Thanksgiving tradition echoes a harvest feast shared with Wampanoag people. Though Plymouth was neither the first nor largest English settlement in North America, the Pilgrim Fathers became central to American founding mythology, symbolising religious liberty and democratic self-determination.

episodes.title: The Plague of Justinian
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the plague that broke out in Constantinople 541AD, in the reign of Emperor Justinian. According to the historian Procopius, writing in Byzantium at the time, this was a plague by which the whole human race came near to being destroyed, embracing the whole world, and blighting the lives of all mankind. The bacterium behind the Black Death has since been found on human remains from that time, and the symptoms described were the same, and evidence of this plague has since been traced around the Mediterranean and from Syria to Britain and Ireland. The question of how devastating it truly was, though, is yet to be resolved.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the plague that broke out in Constantinople 541AD, in the reign of Emperor Justinian. According to the historian Procopius, writing in Byzantium at the time, this was a plague by which the whole human race came near to being destroyed, embracing the whole world, and blighting the lives of all mankind. The bacterium behind the Black Death has since been found on human remains from that time, and the symptoms described were the same, and evidence of this plague has since been traced around the Mediterranean and from Syria to Britain and Ireland. The question of how devastating it truly was, though, is yet to be resolved.
episodes.summaryAI: In 541 AD, bubonic plague struck Constantinople during Emperor Justinian's reign. The historian Procopius described catastrophic mortality threatening civilisation's survival. Caused by Yersinia pestis, the same bacterium responsible for the fourteenth-century Black Death, the pandemic spread across the Mediterranean world and reached as far as Britain and Syria. Archaeological evidence including mass graves and ancient DNA confirms its geographic range. Scholars debate its true demographic and economic impact: some argue it fundamentally weakened the Byzantine Empire, while others question whether mortality figures were as devastating as literary sources suggest.

episodes.title: The Planets
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss our knowledge of the planets in both our and other solar systems. Tucked away in the outer Western Spiral arm of the Milky Way is a middle aged star, with nine, or possibly ten orbiting planets of hugely varying sizes. Roughly ninety-two million miles and third in line from that central star is our own planet Earth, in thrall to our Sun, just one of the several thousand million stars that make up the Galaxy.Ever since Galileo and Copernicus gave us a scientific model of our own solar system, we have assumed that somewhere amongst the myriad stars there must be other orbiting planets, but it took until 1995 to find one. ‘51 Pegasus A’ was discovered in the Pegasus constellation and was far bigger and far closer to its sun than any of our existing theories could have predicted. Since then 121 new planets have been found. And now it is thought there may be more planets in the skies than there are stars.What causes a planet to form? How do you track one down? And how likely is there to be another one out there with properties like the Earth’s?With Paul Murdin, Senior Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy in Cambridge; Hugh Jones, planet hunter and Reader in Astrophysics at Liverpool John Moores University; Carolin Crawford, Royal Society Research Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy in Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss our knowledge of the planets in both our and other solar systems. Tucked away in the outer Western Spiral arm of the Milky Way is a middle aged star, with nine, or possibly ten orbiting planets of hugely varying sizes. Roughly ninety-two million miles and third in line from that central star is our own planet Earth, in thrall to our Sun, just one of the several thousand million stars that make up the Galaxy.Ever since Galileo and Copernicus gave us a scientific model of our own solar system, we have assumed that somewhere amongst the myriad stars there must be other orbiting planets, but it took until 1995 to find one. ‘51 Pegasus A’ was discovered in the Pegasus constellation and was far bigger and far closer to its sun than any of our existing theories could have predicted. Since then 121 new planets have been found. And now it is thought there may be more planets in the skies than there are stars.What causes a planet to form? How do you track one down? And how likely is there to be another one out there with properties like the Earth’s?With Paul Murdin, Senior Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy in Cambridge; Hugh Jones, planet hunter and Reader in Astrophysics at Liverpool John Moores University; Carolin Crawford, Royal Society Research Fellow at the Institute of Astronomy in Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Planetary science encompasses bodies orbiting our Sun and stars throughout the galaxy. The 1995 discovery of 51 Pegasi b, a hot Jupiter far larger and closer to its star than existing models predicted, revolutionised understanding of planetary formation and migration. Subsequent detection methods including radial velocity, transit photometry, and direct imaging have revealed thousands of exoplanets with extraordinary diversity: super-Earths, mini-Neptunes, and planets in habitable zones. Statistical extrapolation suggests planets may outnumber stars in the Milky Way, transforming questions about planetary formation, atmospheric composition, and potential habitability.

episodes.title: The Poincaré Conjecture
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Poincaré Conjecture. The great French mathematician Henri Poincaré declared: “The scientist does not study mathematics because it is useful; he studies it because he delights in it, and he delights in it because it is beautiful. If nature were not beautiful, it would not be worth knowing and life would not be worth living. And it is because simplicity, because grandeur, is beautiful that we preferably seek simple facts, sublime facts, and that we delight now to follow the majestic course of the stars.” Poincaré’s ground-breaking work in the 19th and early 20th century has indeed led us to the stars and the consideration of the shape of the universe itself. He is known as the father of topology – the study of the properties of shapes and how they can be deformed. His famous Conjecture in this field has been causing mathematicians sleepless nights ever since. He is also credited as the Father of Chaos Theory.So how did this great polymath change the way we understand the world and indeed the universe? Why did his conjecture remain unproved for almost a century? And has it finally been cracked?With June Barrow-Green, Lecturer in the History of Mathematics at the Open University; Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick; Marcus du Sautoy, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Poincaré Conjecture. The great French mathematician Henri Poincaré declared: “The scientist does not study mathematics because it is useful; he studies it because he delights in it, and he delights in it because it is beautiful. If nature were not beautiful, it would not be worth knowing and life would not be worth living. And it is because simplicity, because grandeur, is beautiful that we preferably seek simple facts, sublime facts, and that we delight now to follow the majestic course of the stars.” Poincaré’s ground-breaking work in the 19th and early 20th century has indeed led us to the stars and the consideration of the shape of the universe itself. He is known as the father of topology – the study of the properties of shapes and how they can be deformed. His famous Conjecture in this field has been causing mathematicians sleepless nights ever since. He is also credited as the Father of Chaos Theory.So how did this great polymath change the way we understand the world and indeed the universe? Why did his conjecture remain unproved for almost a century? And has it finally been cracked?With June Barrow-Green, Lecturer in the History of Mathematics at the Open University; Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick; Marcus du Sautoy, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Oxford.
episodes.summaryAI: Henri Poincare, a foundational figure in mathematics and physics, pioneered topology and formulated his famous conjecture: that every simply connected, closed three-dimensional manifold is homeomorphic to a three-sphere. This problem resisted proof for nearly a century, tormenting generations of mathematicians. Poincare also laid groundwork for chaos theory by discovering sensitive dependence on initial conditions in dynamical systems. In 2003, Grigori Perelman proved the conjecture using Ricci flow techniques developed by Richard Hamilton, subsequently declining both the Fields Medal and the Clay Millennium Prize.

episodes.title: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the republic that emerged from the union of the Kingdom of Poland and Grand Duchy of Lithuania in the 14th Century. At first this was a personal union, similar to that of James I and VI in Britain, but this was formalised in 1569 into a vast republic, stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea. Kings and princes from across Europe would compete for parliament to elect them King and Grand Duke, and the greatest power lay with the parliaments. When the system worked well, the Commonwealth was a powerhouse, and it was their leader Jan Sobieski who relieved the siege of Vienna in 1683, defeating the Ottomans. Its neighbours exploited its parliament's need for unanimity, though, and this contributed to its downfall. Austria, Russia and Prussia divided its territory between them from 1772, before the new, smaller states only emerged in the 20th Century.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the republic that emerged from the union of the Kingdom of Poland and Grand Duchy of Lithuania in the 14th Century. At first this was a personal union, similar to that of James I and VI in Britain, but this was formalised in 1569 into a vast republic, stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea. Kings and princes from across Europe would compete for parliament to elect them King and Grand Duke, and the greatest power lay with the parliaments. When the system worked well, the Commonwealth was a powerhouse, and it was their leader Jan Sobieski who relieved the siege of Vienna in 1683, defeating the Ottomans. Its neighbours exploited its parliament's need for unanimity, though, and this contributed to its downfall. Austria, Russia and Prussia divided its territory between them from 1772, before the new, smaller states only emerged in the 20th Century.
episodes.summaryAI: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth originated from dynastic ties in the fourteenth century and was formalised by the Union of Lublin in 1569, creating one of Europe's largest states stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea. Its political system concentrated power in the Sejm parliament and the nobility, with elected kings holding limited authority. Jan III Sobieski's relief of the 1683 Siege of Vienna marked a military high point. Internal paralysis from the liberum veto weakened the state, enabling Austria, Russia, and Prussia to partition it progressively from 1772 until its elimination in 1795.

episodes.title: The Poor Laws
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, from 1834, poor people across England and Wales faced new obstacles when they could no longer feed or clothe themselves, or find shelter. Parliament, in line with the ideas of Jeremy Bentham and Thomas Malthus, feared hand-outs had become so attractive, they stopped people working to support themselves, and encouraged families to have more children than they could afford. To correct this, under the New Poor Laws it became harder to get any relief outside a workhouse, where families would be separated, husbands from wives, parents from children, sisters from brothers. Many found this regime inhumane, while others protested it was too lenient, and it lasted until the twentieth century.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss how, from 1834, poor people across England and Wales faced new obstacles when they could no longer feed or clothe themselves, or find shelter. Parliament, in line with the ideas of Jeremy Bentham and Thomas Malthus, feared hand-outs had become so attractive, they stopped people working to support themselves, and encouraged families to have more children than they could afford. To correct this, under the New Poor Laws it became harder to get any relief outside a workhouse, where families would be separated, husbands from wives, parents from children, sisters from brothers. Many found this regime inhumane, while others protested it was too lenient, and it lasted until the twentieth century.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act fundamentally restructured English welfare provision. Parliament, influenced by Malthusian economics and the belief that outdoor relief discouraged labour, replaced local parish assistance with a centralised workhouse system. Conditions inside workhouses were deliberately harsh: families were separated, diets restricted, and regimes punitive to deter all but the most desperate. The system provoked widespread opposition, inspiring literary protest including Dickens's Oliver Twist. Poor Law institutions persisted into the twentieth century, only fully dismantled with the creation of the welfare state after 1945.

episodes.title: The Prelude
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the greatest long poems in the English language – The Prelude. Begun in Northern Germany during the terrible winter of 1798 by a young and dreadfully homesick William Wordsworth, The Prelude was to be his masterpiece - an epic retelling of his own life and the foundation stone of English Romanticism. In language of aching beauty wordsworth expressed thoughts about memory, identity, nature and experience familiar to anybody who has walked alone among the hills. With Rosemary Ashton, Quain Professor of English Language and Literature at University College London; Stephen Gill, University Professor of English Literature and Fellow of Lincoln College, Oxford; Emma Mason, Senior Lecturer in English at the University of Warwick.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the greatest long poems in the English language – The Prelude. Begun in Northern Germany during the terrible winter of 1798 by a young and dreadfully homesick William Wordsworth, The Prelude was to be his masterpiece - an epic retelling of his own life and the foundation stone of English Romanticism. In language of aching beauty wordsworth expressed thoughts about memory, identity, nature and experience familiar to anybody who has walked alone among the hills. With Rosemary Ashton, Quain Professor of English Language and Literature at University College London; Stephen Gill, University Professor of English Literature and Fellow of Lincoln College, Oxford; Emma Mason, Senior Lecturer in English at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: William Wordsworth began The Prelude during a homesick winter in Germany in 1798, developing it over decades into his masterpiece. This autobiographical epic in blank verse traces the growth of the poet's mind from childhood in the Lake District through Cambridge, revolutionary France, and spiritual crisis to imaginative restoration. It stands as a foundation stone of English Romanticism, pioneering the sustained exploration of memory, identity, nature, and subjective experience in language of extraordinary lyrical beauty. Published posthumously in 1850, it redefined the possibilities of long-form personal poetry.

episodes.title: The Proton
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the discovery and growing understanding of the Proton, formed from three quarks close to the Big Bang and found in the nuclei of all elements. The positive charges they emit means they attract the fundamental particles of negatively charged electrons, an attraction that leads to the creation of atoms which in turn leads to chemistry, biology and life itself. The Sun (in common with other stars) is a fusion engine that turn protons by a series of processes into helium, emitting energy in the process, with about half of the Sun's protons captured so far. Hydrogen atoms, stripped of electrons, are single protons which can be accelerated to smash other nuclei and have applications in proton therapy. Many questions remain, such as why are electrical charges for protons and electrons so perfectly balanced?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the discovery and growing understanding of the Proton, formed from three quarks close to the Big Bang and found in the nuclei of all elements. The positive charges they emit means they attract the fundamental particles of negatively charged electrons, an attraction that leads to the creation of atoms which in turn leads to chemistry, biology and life itself. The Sun (in common with other stars) is a fusion engine that turn protons by a series of processes into helium, emitting energy in the process, with about half of the Sun's protons captured so far. Hydrogen atoms, stripped of electrons, are single protons which can be accelerated to smash other nuclei and have applications in proton therapy. Many questions remain, such as why are electrical charges for protons and electrons so perfectly balanced?
episodes.summaryAI: Protons, each composed of three quarks bound by the strong nuclear force, formed within moments of the Big Bang and constitute the nuclei of all chemical elements. Their positive charge attracts electrons, creating atoms and enabling chemistry, biology, and life itself. In stellar cores, hydrogen fusion converts protons into helium, powering stars including the Sun. Proton stability underpins the persistence of matter across cosmic time. Practical applications include proton therapy for cancer treatment, where precisely targeted beams destroy tumours while minimising damage to surrounding tissue.

episodes.title: The Putney Debates
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Putney Debates. For several weeks in late 1647, after the defeat of King Charles I in the first hostilities of the Civil War, representatives of the New Model Army and the radical Levellers met in a church in Putney to debate the future of England. There was much to discuss: who should be allowed to vote, civil liberties and religious freedom. The debates were inconclusive, but the ideas aired in Putney had a considerable influence on centuries of political thought.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Putney Debates. For several weeks in late 1647, after the defeat of King Charles I in the first hostilities of the Civil War, representatives of the New Model Army and the radical Levellers met in a church in Putney to debate the future of England. There was much to discuss: who should be allowed to vote, civil liberties and religious freedom. The debates were inconclusive, but the ideas aired in Putney had a considerable influence on centuries of political thought.
episodes.summaryAI: In late 1647, following the defeat of Charles I, representatives of the New Model Army and the Leveller movement convened at St Mary's Church, Putney, to debate England's constitutional future. Colonel Rainsborough argued for universal manhood suffrage while Cromwell and Ireton defended property qualifications for voting. Discussions encompassed civil liberties, religious toleration, and the sovereignty of the people. Though the debates ended inconclusively and the Leveller programme was ultimately suppressed, their radical articulation of democratic principles profoundly influenced subsequent centuries of political thought about representation and rights.

episodes.title: The Ramayana
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Ramayana, the ancient Hindu epic which is regarded as one of the greatest works of world literature. Its importance in Indian culture has been compared to that of the Iliad and Odyssey in the West, and it’s still seen as a sacred text by Hindus today.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Ramayana, the ancient Hindu epic which is regarded as one of the greatest works of world literature. Its importance in Indian culture has been compared to that of the Iliad and Odyssey in the West, and it’s still seen as a sacred text by Hindus today.
episodes.summaryAI: The Ramayana stands among the greatest works of world literature, comparable in stature to Homer's Iliad and Odyssey. This ancient Hindu epic narrates the adventures of Prince Rama, encompassing themes of duty, devotion, honour, and the cosmic struggle between good and evil. Composed in Sanskrit and attributed to the sage Valmiki, it has shaped Indian culture, art, theatre, and religious practice for millennia. Unlike many ancient texts that survive only as scholarly artefacts, the Ramayana remains a living sacred scripture for hundreds of millions of Hindus today.

episodes.title: The Rapture
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas developed by the Anglican priest John Nelson Darby (1800-1882), drawn from his reading of scripture, in which Jesus would suddenly take His believers up into the air, and those left behind would suffer on Earth until He returned with His church to rule for a thousand years before Final Judgement. Some believers would look for signs that civilization was declining, such as wars and natural disasters, or for new Roman Empires that would harbour the Antichrist, and from these predict the time of the Rapture. Darby helped establish the Plymouth Brethren, and later his ideas were picked up in the Scofield Reference Bible (1909) and soon became influential, particularly in the USA.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas developed by the Anglican priest John Nelson Darby (1800-1882), drawn from his reading of scripture, in which Jesus would suddenly take His believers up into the air, and those left behind would suffer on Earth until He returned with His church to rule for a thousand years before Final Judgement. Some believers would look for signs that civilization was declining, such as wars and natural disasters, or for new Roman Empires that would harbour the Antichrist, and from these predict the time of the Rapture. Darby helped establish the Plymouth Brethren, and later his ideas were picked up in the Scofield Reference Bible (1909) and soon became influential, particularly in the USA.
episodes.summaryAI: The Rapture doctrine originated with John Nelson Darby, an Anglican priest who taught that Jesus would suddenly gather true believers into the air before a period of catastrophic tribulation afflicted those left behind. Darby helped found the Plymouth Brethren and spread his dispensationalist theology through extensive preaching. The 1909 Scofield Reference Bible embedded his interpretive framework into widely read scripture, encouraging believers to read current events as signs of civilisational decline heralding Christ's return. This theology became enormously influential in American evangelical Protestantism, shaping attitudes toward prophecy, geopolitics, and end-times expectation.

episodes.title: The Renaissance
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Renaissance, which was first given its role as the birth place of modern man by the nineteenth century historian Jacob Burckhardt. At the start of his immensely influential Civilisation of the Renaissance in Italy, he wrote “In the Middle Ages both sides of human consciousness - that which was turned within as that which was turned without - lay dreaming or half awake beneath a common veil. The veil was woven of faith, illusion, childish prepossession, through which the world and history were seen clad in strange hues…In Italy this veil first melted into air” But is the Renaissance really a cultural miracle, and is it fair to think of medieval thought as being ‘obscured by a veil’? Should we even call the period around the fifteenth century the Renaissance when the very word implies that culture, for a thousand years, has been dead? What if our idea of the Renaissance is completely wrong? With Francis Ames-Lewis, Professor of History of Art, Birkbeck College; Peter Burke, Professor of Cultural History and Fellow of Emmanuel College, University of Cambridge; Dr Evelyn Welch, Reader in the History of Art, University of Sussex.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Renaissance, which was first given its role as the birth place of modern man by the nineteenth century historian Jacob Burckhardt. At the start of his immensely influential Civilisation of the Renaissance in Italy, he wrote “In the Middle Ages both sides of human consciousness - that which was turned within as that which was turned without - lay dreaming or half awake beneath a common veil. Should we even call the period around the fifteenth century the Renaissance when the very word implies that culture, for a thousand years, has been dead? What if our idea of the Renaissance is completely wrong? With Francis Ames-Lewis, Professor of History of Art, Birkbeck College; Peter Burke, Professor of Cultural History and Fellow of Emmanuel College, University of Cambridge; Dr Evelyn Welch, Reader in the History of Art, University of Sussex.
episodes.summaryAI: The Renaissance is conventionally framed as the rebirth of classical learning that launched modernity, a narrative established by Jacob Burckhardt who argued that in Italy the veil of medieval faith and collective identity first dissolved, revealing the autonomous individual. This interpretation raises persistent questions: whether the period truly represents a cultural miracle or a more gradual transformation, whether medieval thought was genuinely obscured or already sophisticated in its own terms, and whether the label Renaissance itself distorts understanding by imposing a sharp rupture where continuity also existed.

episodes.title: The Restoration
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Restoration. On 29th May 1660, on his thirtieth birthday, Charles II rode into London on horseback and was restored to the thrones of England and Wales, of Scotland and of Ireland. A ‘golden age’ descended on a people that had been ravaged by civil war, religious division, Cromwellian tyranny and puritanical laws: suddenly the theatres were re-opened, Christmas was celebrated once again, all Orange-sellers were beautiful and peace and prosperity reigned across the land. Or at least that’s one version of the Restoration story. But despite the architecture of Wren, the literature of Dryden, and the philosophy of Hobbes, can an era that is suffused in Plague and in Fire, and culminates in something called The Glorious Revolution, ever really have had it so good?With Dr Mark Goldie, lecturer in History, Churchill College, University of Cambridge; Richard Ollard author of The Image of the King: Charles I and Charles II; Dr Clare Jackson, lecturer and Director of Studies in History, Trinity Hall, Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Restoration. On 29th May 1660, on his thirtieth birthday, Charles II rode into London on horseback and was restored to the thrones of England and Wales, of Scotland and of Ireland. A ‘golden age’ descended on a people that had been ravaged by civil war, religious division, Cromwellian tyranny and puritanical laws: suddenly the theatres were re-opened, Christmas was celebrated once again, all Orange-sellers were beautiful and peace and prosperity reigned across the land. Or at least that’s one version of the Restoration story. But despite the architecture of Wren, the literature of Dryden, and the philosophy of Hobbes, can an era that is suffused in Plague and in Fire, and culminates in something called The Glorious Revolution, ever really have had it so good?With Dr Mark Goldie, lecturer in History, Churchill College, University of Cambridge; Richard Ollard author of The Image of the King: Charles I and Charles II; Dr Clare Jackson, lecturer and Director of Studies in History, Trinity Hall, Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: The Restoration began on 29 May 1660 when Charles II rode into London, ending the austere Commonwealth period. Theatres reopened, Christmas celebrations resumed, and cultural life flourished through the architecture of Christopher Wren, the literature of John Dryden, and the philosophy of Thomas Hobbes. Yet this apparent golden age also witnessed catastrophe: the Great Plague of 1665 and the Great Fire of London in 1666. The era's political and religious tensions persisted beneath the surface, ultimately culminating in the Glorious Revolution of 1688, which replaced Stuart absolutism with constitutional monarchy.

episodes.title: The Riddle of the Sands
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discusses the prescient thriller ‘The Riddle of the Sands’ about the decline Anglo-German relations before the First World War. In 1903 an Englishman called Charles Caruthers went sailing in the North Sea and stumbled upon a German military plot. The cunning plan was to invade the British Isles from the Frisian Islands using special barges. The plucky Caruthers foiled the plot and returned to his sailing holiday.This is not history but fiction, an immensely popular book called ‘The Riddle of the Sands’ by Erskine Childers. It was a prescient vision of two nations soon to fight the First World War but it went against the spirit of the previous century. Brits and Germans had fought together at Waterloo and had influenced profoundly each other’s thought and art. They even shared a royal family. Yet somehow victory at Waterloo and the shared glories of Romanticism became the mutual tragedy of the Somme.With Richard Evans, Professor of Modern History at the University of Cambridge; Rosemary Ashton, Quain Professor of English Language and Literature at University College London and Tim Blanning, Professor of Modern European history at The University of Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discusses the prescient thriller ‘The Riddle of the Sands’ about the decline Anglo-German relations before the First World War. In 1903 an Englishman called Charles Caruthers went sailing in the North Sea and stumbled upon a German military plot. The plucky Caruthers foiled the plot and returned to his sailing holiday.This is not history but fiction, an immensely popular book called ‘The Riddle of the Sands’ by Erskine Childers. It was a prescient vision of two nations soon to fight the First World War but it went against the spirit of the previous century. Brits and Germans had fought together at Waterloo and had influenced profoundly each other’s thought and art. They even shared a royal family. Yet somehow victory at Waterloo and the shared glories of Romanticism became the mutual tragedy of the Somme.With Richard Evans, Professor of Modern History at the University of Cambridge; Rosemary Ashton, Quain Professor of English Language and Literature at University College London and Tim Blanning, Professor of Modern European history at The University of Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Erskine Childers's 1903 novel The Riddle of the Sands depicted a German plot to invade Britain, proving remarkably prescient about the conflict that erupted eleven years later. The book highlights the tragic irony of Anglo-German relations: the two nations had fought together at Waterloo, shared deep Romantic intellectual traditions, and were linked through intermarried royal families. Yet within a generation this kinship dissolved into the mutual slaughter of the Somme. Childers's thriller captured the moment when familiar allies became existential enemies, making it both gripping fiction and haunting historical document.

episodes.title: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
episodes.synopsis: In this 900th edition of the programme, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the best known and most influential of the poems of the Romantic movement. Samuel Taylor Coleridge (1772-1834) wrote The Rime of the Ancient Mariner in 1798 after discussions with his friend Wordsworth. He refined it for the rest of his life, and it came to define him, a foreshadowing of his opium-addicted, lonely wandering and deepening sense of guilt. The poem tells of a sailor compelled to tell and retell the story of a terrible voyage in his youth, this time as guests are heading to a wedding party, where he stoppeth one of three.
episodes.description: In this 900th edition of the programme, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the best known and most influential of the poems of the Romantic movement. Samuel Taylor Coleridge (1772-1834) wrote The Rime of the Ancient Mariner in 1798 after discussions with his friend Wordsworth. He refined it for the rest of his life, and it came to define him, a foreshadowing of his opium-addicted, lonely wandering and deepening sense of guilt. The poem tells of a sailor compelled to tell and retell the story of a terrible voyage in his youth, this time as guests are heading to a wedding party, where he stoppeth one of three.
episodes.summaryAI: Samuel Taylor Coleridge composed The Rime of the Ancient Mariner in 1798 following conversations with William Wordsworth, then revised it for the rest of his life. The poem tells of a sailor cursed after killing an albatross, compelled to wander and retell the story of his terrible voyage. It became the work that defined Coleridge, eerily foreshadowing his own trajectory of opium addiction, restless wandering, and deepening guilt. Its supernatural imagery, haunting musicality, and exploration of transgression and penance made it a landmark of English Romantic poetry.

episodes.title: The Roman Arena
episodes.synopsis: Misha Glenny and guests discuss the countless venues across the Roman Empire which for over five hundred years drew the biggest crowds both in the Republic and under the Emperors. The shows there delighted the masses who knew, no matter how low their place in society, they were much better off than the gladiators about to fight or the beasts to be slaughtered. Some of the Roman elites were disgusted, seeing this popular entertainment as morally corrupting and un-Roman. Moral degradation was a less immediate concern though than the overspill of violence. There was a constant threat of gladiators being used as a private army and while those of the elite wealthy enough to stage the shows hoped to win great prestige, they risked disappointing a crowd which could quickly become a mob and turn on them.
episodes.description: Misha Glenny and guests discuss the countless venues across the Roman Empire which for over five hundred years drew the biggest crowds both in the Republic and under the Emperors. The shows there delighted the masses who knew, no matter how low their place in society, they were much better off than the gladiators about to fight or the beasts to be slaughtered. Some of the Roman elites were disgusted, seeing this popular entertainment as morally corrupting and un-Roman. Moral degradation was a less immediate concern though than the overspill of violence. There was a constant threat of gladiators being used as a private army and while those of the elite wealthy enough to stage the shows hoped to win great prestige, they risked disappointing a crowd which could quickly become a mob and turn on them.
episodes.summaryAI: Arenas operated across the Roman Empire for over five hundred years, staging gladiatorial combat, animal hunts, and public executions. The spectacles entertained vast crowds who could take grim comfort that their own lot was better than that of those fighting and dying below. Some Roman elites condemned the shows as morally corrupting, yet the games served essential political functions: emperors used them to demonstrate power and generosity. Gladiators also posed dangers, potentially serving as private armies for ambitious patrons, while arena crowds themselves could turn into volatile mobs.

episodes.title: The Roman Empire's Collapse in the 5th century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the causes of the fall of the Roman Empire. Edward Gibbon wrote of its decline, "While that great body was invaded by open violence, or undermined by slow decay, a pure and humble religion gently insinuated itself into the minds of men, grew up in silence and obscurity, derived new vigour from opposition, and finally erected the triumphant banner of the cross on the ruins of the Capitol."But how far is the growth of Christianity implicated in the destruction of the great culture of Rome? How critical were the bawdy incursions of the Ostrogoths, the Visigoths and the Vandals to the fall of the Roman Empire? Should we even be talking in terms of blame and decline at all?St Augustine wrote about the fall of the Roman Empire in the fifth century AD, Edward Gibbon famously tackled it in the eighteenth and it is a question that preoccupies us today.With Charlotte Roueché, historian of late antiquity at Kings College London; David Womersley, Fellow and Tutor at Jesus College, Oxford and editor of Edward Gibbon’s The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire; Richard Alston, Lecturer in Classics at Royal Holloway, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the causes of the fall of the Roman Empire. Edward Gibbon wrote of its decline, "While that great body was invaded by open violence, or undermined by slow decay, a pure and humble religion gently insinuated itself into the minds of men, grew up in silence and obscurity, derived new vigour from opposition, and finally erected the triumphant banner of the cross on the ruins of the Capitol."But how far is the growth of Christianity implicated in the destruction of the great culture of Rome? How critical were the bawdy incursions of the Ostrogoths, the Visigoths and the Vandals to the fall of the Roman Empire? Should we even be talking in terms of blame and decline at all?St Augustine wrote about the fall of the Roman Empire in the fifth century AD, Edward Gibbon famously tackled it in the eighteenth and it is a question that preoccupies us today.With Charlotte Roueché, historian of late antiquity at Kings College London; David Womersley, Fellow and Tutor at Jesus College, Oxford and editor of Edward Gibbon’s The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire; Richard Alston, Lecturer in Classics at Royal Holloway, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: Edward Gibbon famously suggested that while the Roman Empire was invaded by open violence and undermined by slow decay, Christianity quietly insinuated itself into its foundations. The fifth-century collapse raises enduring questions about causation: how far Christianity redirected Roman energies from civic defence toward otherworldly concerns, how decisive the migrations of Ostrogoths, Visigoths, and Vandals actually were, and whether decline and fall is even the right framework. Some historians see transformation rather than collapse, with Roman institutions and culture persisting in altered forms across successor kingdoms.

episodes.title: The Roman Republic
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise and eventual downfall of the Roman Republic which survived for 500 years.Around 550 BC, Lucretia, the daughter of an aristocrat, was raped by the son of Tarquin, the King of Rome. Lucretia told her family what had happened to her and then in front of them, killed herself from shame. The Roman historian Livy describes what was believed to have happened next:"Brutus, while the others were absorbed in grief; drew out the knife from Lucretia's wound, and holding it up, dripping with gore, exclaimed, "By this blood, most chaste until a prince wronged it, I swear, and I take you, gods, to witness, that I will pursue Lucius Tarquinius Superbus and his wicked wife and all his children, with sword, with fire, aye with whatsoever violence I may; and that I will suffer neither them nor any other to be king in Rome!". The King was duly expelled from the city and the Roman Republic was founded and lasted for 500 years. But in what form did this republic evolve, what were its values and ideals and what ultimately caused the end of the world’s first true experiment in constitutional government?With Greg Woolf, Professor of Ancient History at St Andrews University; Catherine Steel, Lecturer in Classics at the University of Glasgow; Tom Holland, historian and author of Rubicon: the Triumph and Tragedy of the Roman Republic.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise and eventual downfall of the Roman Republic which survived for 500 years.Around 550 BC, Lucretia, the daughter of an aristocrat, was raped by the son of Tarquin, the King of Rome. Lucretia told her family what had happened to her and then in front of them, killed herself from shame. The King was duly expelled from the city and the Roman Republic was founded and lasted for 500 years. But in what form did this republic evolve, what were its values and ideals and what ultimately caused the end of the world’s first true experiment in constitutional government?With Greg Woolf, Professor of Ancient History at St Andrews University; Catherine Steel, Lecturer in Classics at the University of Glasgow; Tom Holland, historian and author of Rubicon: the Triumph and Tragedy of the Roman Republic.
episodes.summaryAI: The Roman Republic was founded around 550 BC after the rape of Lucretia provoked the expulsion of King Tarquin, replacing monarchy with elected government. Over five centuries it developed a sophisticated constitutional system balancing consuls, senate, and popular assemblies, governed by ideals of civic virtue, public service, and legal authority. Rome expanded from a single city to master of the Mediterranean world. The Republic's eventual collapse into civil war and dictatorship ended what can be considered the first true constitutional government, raising enduring questions about the vulnerabilities of representative systems.

episodes.title: The Romantics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideals, exponents and legacy of Romanticism. In the space of a few years around the start of the nineteenth century the Romantic period gave us: Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Burns, two Shelleys, Keats, De Quincey, Carlyle, Byron, Scott… the list goes on and on. And the poems: The Rhyme of the Ancient Mariner, Ode to a Nightingale, Tintern Abbey, Ozymandias, Don Juan… they make up some of the best known and most enjoyed works of literature in the English language. How do we explain what seems to be an extraordinary explosion of talent? Were the Romantics really a movement with their own philosphy and ideals? And when its adherents often died so tragically young, and its poems often seem so steeped in nostalgia and so wrapped in the transcendental, is Romanticism really good for you in a modern world? With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English, University of Liverpool; Rosemary Ashton, Professor of English, University College London; Nicholas Roe, Professor of English, University of St Andrews.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideals, exponents and legacy of Romanticism. In the space of a few years around the start of the nineteenth century the Romantic period gave us: Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Burns, two Shelleys, Keats, De Quincey, Carlyle, Byron, Scott… the list goes on and on. And the poems: The Rhyme of the Ancient Mariner, Ode to a Nightingale, Tintern Abbey, Ozymandias, Don Juan… they make up some of the best known and most enjoyed works of literature in the English language. How do we explain what seems to be an extraordinary explosion of talent? Were the Romantics really a movement with their own philosphy and ideals? And when its adherents often died so tragically young, and its poems often seem so steeped in nostalgia and so wrapped in the transcendental, is Romanticism really good for you in a modern world? With Jonathan Bate, Professor of English, University of Liverpool; Rosemary Ashton, Professor of English, University College London; Nicholas Roe, Professor of English, University of St Andrews.
episodes.summaryAI: The early nineteenth century produced an extraordinary concentration of poetic talent in Britain: Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Burns, the Shelleys, Keats, and Byron. Whether they constituted a coherent movement remains debatable, given their diverse temperaments, politics, and aesthetics. They shared an emphasis on imagination, emotion, nature, and individual experience as counterweights to Enlightenment rationalism and industrial change. Many died tragically young, and their poetry is steeped in nostalgia and longing. This raises the question of whether Romanticism, rooted in loss and yearning, remains a productive sensibility for the modern world.

episodes.title: The Rosetta Stone
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most famous museum objects in the world, shown in the image above in replica, and dating from around 196 BC. It is a damaged, dark granite block on which you can faintly see three scripts engraved: Greek at the bottom, Demotic in the middle and Hieroglyphs at the top. Napoleon’s soldiers found it in a Mamluk fort at Rosetta on the Egyptian coast, and soon realised the Greek words could be used to unlock the hieroglyphs. It was another 20 years before Champollion deciphered them, becoming the first to understand the hieroglyphs since they fell out of use 1500 years before and so opening up the written culture of ancient Egypt to the modern age.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most famous museum objects in the world, shown in the image above in replica, and dating from around 196 BC. It is a damaged, dark granite block on which you can faintly see three scripts engraved: Greek at the bottom, Demotic in the middle and Hieroglyphs at the top. Napoleon’s soldiers found it in a Mamluk fort at Rosetta on the Egyptian coast, and soon realised the Greek words could be used to unlock the hieroglyphs. It was another 20 years before Champollion deciphered them, becoming the first to understand the hieroglyphs since they fell out of use 1500 years before and so opening up the written culture of ancient Egypt to the modern age.
episodes.summaryAI: The Rosetta Stone was discovered in 1799 within a Mamluk fort in the Nile Delta, bearing the same decree inscribed in three scripts: ancient Greek, Demotic, and hieroglyphs. Jean-Francois Champollion used the Greek text as a key to decipher hieroglyphic writing in 1822, achieving what no one had managed since the script fell out of use roughly fifteen hundred years earlier. This breakthrough unlocked the entire written culture of ancient Egypt, transforming thousands of inscriptions, papyri, and tomb texts from mute decoration into readable historical records.

episodes.title: The Royal Society
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the formation of the Royal Society. In the 17th century the natural philosopher Francis Bacon heralded the new age of science. The frontispiece to his 1620 edition of the Instauratio Magna depicted a galleon travelling between the metaphorical Pillars of Hercules thought to lie at the Strait of Gibraltar and believed to mark the end of the known world. The image encapsulated Bacon's desire to sail beyond the limits set by Aristotle and the curriculum of the Ancient universities towards the new continent of science. Bacon imagined practical scientists engaged in a collaborative effort to expand knowledge of the natural world. But it was not until the turbulence of the Civil War and Commonwealth years had passed that such a group of scientists would gather together in London for this purpose, and form the Royal Society. Amongst its members were Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke, Christopher Wren and Isaac Newton, who explicitly rejected dogma and insisted on practical experimentation and observation. How was the Royal Society formed against a backdrop of religious and political strife? What was it about the way this group of men worked that allowed each individual to flourish in his own field? How successful was the Royal Society in disseminating the benefits of experimental science and what is its enduring legacy? With Stephen Pumfrey, Senior Lecturer in the History of Science at the University of Lancaster; Lisa Jardine, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary, University of London; Michael Hunter, Professor of History at Birkbeck, University of London.

The frontispiece to his 1620 edition of the Instauratio Magna depicted a galleon travelling between the metaphorical Pillars of Hercules thought to lie at the Strait of Gibraltar and believed to mark the end of the known world. The image encapsulated Bacon's desire to sail beyond the limits set by Aristotle and the curriculum of the Ancient universities towards the new continent of science. Bacon imagined practical scientists engaged in a collaborative effort to expand knowledge of the natural world. But it was not until the turbulence of the Civil War and Commonwealth years had passed that such a group of scientists would gather together in London for this purpose, and form the Royal Society. Amongst its members were Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke, Christopher Wren and Isaac Newton, who explicitly rejected dogma and insisted on practical experimentation and observation. How was the Royal Society formed against a backdrop of religious and political strife? What was it about the way this group of men worked that allowed each individual to flourish in his own field? episodes.summaryAI: The Royal Society emerged in seventeenth-century England from Francis Bacon's ambition to sail beyond the intellectual limits set by Aristotle. Fellows including Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke, Christopher Wren, and Isaac Newton rejected received dogma and insisted that knowledge must rest on systematic experimentation and observation. The Society formed amid the turbulence of civil war, religious conflict, and political upheaval, yet created an enduring institutional framework for collaborative empirical inquiry. Its legacy lies in establishing the principle that scientific claims require reproducible evidence, a foundation for modern research practice worldwide.

episodes.title: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 1
episodes.synopsis: As part of the BBC's year of science programming, Melvyn Bragg looks at the history of the oldest scientific learned society of them all: the Royal Society. Melvyn travels to Wadham College, Oxford, where under the shadow of the English Civil War, the young Christopher Wren and friends experimented in the garden of their inspirational college warden, John Wilkins. Back in London, as Charles II is brought to the throne from exile, the new Society is formally founded one night in Gresham College. When London burns six years later, it is two of the key early Fellows of the Society who are charged with its rebuilding. And, as Melvyn finds out, in the secret observatory in The Monument to the fire, it is science which flavours their plans.
episodes.description: As part of the BBC's year of science programming, Melvyn Bragg looks at the history of the oldest scientific learned society of them all: the Royal Society. Melvyn travels to Wadham College, Oxford, where under the shadow of the English Civil War, the young Christopher Wren and friends experimented in the garden of their inspirational college warden, John Wilkins. Back in London, as Charles II is brought to the throne from exile, the new Society is formally founded one night in Gresham College. When London burns six years later, it is two of the key early Fellows of the Society who are charged with its rebuilding. And, as Melvyn finds out, in the secret observatory in The Monument to the fire, it is science which flavours their plans.
episodes.summaryAI: Under the shadow of the English Civil War, a group of natural philosophers gathered at Wadham College, Oxford, where Christopher Wren and colleagues pursued experimental inquiry. The Society was formally established at Gresham College in London, gaining a royal charter from Charles II. When the Great Fire of 1666 devastated London, two Fellows were charged with rebuilding the city, an undertaking that included embedding a secret astronomical observatory within the Monument to the fire. This founding period united intellectual ambition with practical reconstruction, establishing the Society's dual commitment to pure knowledge and public benefit.

episodes.title: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 2
episodes.synopsis: As part of the BBC's year of science programming, Melvyn Bragg looks at the history of the oldest scientific learned society of them all: the Royal Society. Programme two begins in the coffee house Isaac Newton and the fellows of the early 18th century frequented. At the Royal Observatory, Greenwich, we learn how Newton's feud with the Astronomer Royal John Flamsteed tested the lines between government-funded research and public access. In the age of exploration, senior fellows accompany naval expeditions, such as Cook's expedition to Tahiti and subsequent discovery of Australia. International relations are fostered between scientists such as Benjamin Franklin, whose house in London serves as live-in lab and de facto American embassy. By the end of the century the President, Sir Joseph Banks, successfully embeds the Royal Society in the imperial bureaucratic hub of the new Somerset House. But while senior fellows concentrated on foreign fields, a more radical, dissident science and manufacturing base wrought the Industrial Revolution right under their noses.

episodes.summaryAI: The Royal Society's eighteenth-century history encompassed bitter rivalries and global expeditions. Isaac Newton feuded destructively with Astronomer Royal John Flamsteed over astronomical data and credit. Captain Cook's voyage to Tahiti to observe the transit of Venus exemplified the Society's expanding scientific reach. Benjamin Franklin used his London house as both laboratory and diplomatic embassy, linking American and British science. Joseph Banks, as long-serving president, embedded the Society at Somerset House and made it a pillar of establishment science, even as the Industrial Revolution transformed Britain largely without the Society's direct involvement.

episodes.title: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 3
episodes.synopsis: As part of the BBC's year of science programming, Melvyn Bragg looks at the history of the oldest scientific learned society of them all: the Royal Society. The 19th century blooms scientifically with numerous alternative, specialist learned societies and associations, all threatening the Royal Society's pre-eminence. Attempts to reform the membership criteria - marking scientific leadership's painful transition from patronage to expertise - are troubled, and organisations such as the British Association for the Advancement of Science (now the BSA) excite and enliven scientific discourse outside of London. Science becomes a realistic career and a path of improvement, and by the time HG Wells writes science fiction at the end of the 19th century, there are sufficient numbers of interested, informed readers to suggest that Edwardian society contained the beginnings of a scientific society.

episodes.summaryAI: During the nineteenth century the Royal Society faced challenges to its pre-eminence as specialist learned societies proliferated across disciplines. Internal reform shifted membership criteria from aristocratic patronage to demonstrated scientific expertise, professionalising the institution. The founding of the British Association for the Advancement of Science created a parallel body oriented toward public engagement. Science increasingly became a realistic career rather than a gentleman's pursuit. By century's end, writers like H.G. Wells cultivated a broad readership hungry for scientific ideas, reflecting how profoundly the culture of knowledge had been democratised beyond the Society's original elite circle.

episodes.title: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 4
episodes.synopsis: As part of the BBC's year of science programming, Melvyn Bragg looks at the history of the oldest scientific learned society of them all: the Royal Society. The horrors of the First World War were a shocking indictment of the power of science. Picking up the thread at this hiatus in scientific optimism, this programme, recorded in the current home of the Royal Society in Carlton House Terrace in London, looks at the more subtle, discreet role the Society played in the 20th century, such as secretly arranging for refugee scientists to flee Germany, co-ordinating international scientific missions during the Cold War and quietly distributing government grant money to fund the brightest young researchers in the land. As ever more important scientific issues face the world and Britain today, the programme asks how well placed the Royal Society is to take an important lead in the future.

As ever more important scientific issues face the world and Britain today, the programme asks how well placed the Royal Society is to take an important lead in the future.
episodes.summaryAI: The First World War delivered a shocking indictment of the destructive power that science could unleash. In its aftermath, the Royal Society navigated an era of political crisis and global conflict. It secretly arranged for refugee scientists to flee Nazi Germany, preserving intellectual talent that would prove decisive in subsequent decades. During the Cold War the Society coordinated international scientific missions that maintained channels of communication across ideological divides. It also took on the role of distributing government research grants, becoming a critical intermediary between state funding and scientific inquiry in twentieth-century Britain.

episodes.title: The Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam. In 1859 the poet Edward FitzGerald published a long poem based on the verses of the 11th-century Persian scholar Omar Khayyam. Not a single copy was sold in the first few months after the work's publication, but after it came to the notice of members of the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood it became enormously influential. Although only loosely based on the original, the Rubaiyat made Khayyam the best-known Eastern poet in the English-speaking world. FitzGerald's version is itself one of the most admired works of Victorian literature, praised and imitated by many later writers.

In 1859 the poet Edward FitzGerald published a long poem based on the verses of the 11th-century Persian scholar Omar Khayyam.
