episodes.summaryAI: In 1859 Edward FitzGerald published a free poetic translation of quatrains attributed to the eleventh-century Persian mathematician and poet Omar Khayyam. Initially not a single copy sold, and the remaindered pamphlet was discovered by chance by the Pre-Raphaelites, who championed it enthusiastically. The Rubaiyat became enormously popular, celebrated for its sensuous imagery, philosophical scepticism, and carpe diem spirit. FitzGerald's version made Khayyam the best-known Eastern poet in the English-speaking world, though scholars debate how faithfully it represents the Persian originals versus FitzGerald's own Victorian sensibility.

episodes.title: The Russo-Japanese War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the conflict between Russia and Japan from February 1904 to September 1905, which gripped the world and had a profound impact on both countries. Wary of Russian domination of Korea, Japan attacked the Russian Fleet at Port Arthur and the ensuing war gave Russia a series of shocks, including the loss of their Baltic Fleet after a seven month voyage, which reverberated in the 1905 Revolution. Meanwhile Japan, victorious, advanced its goal of making Europe and America more wary in East Asia, combining rapid military modernisation and Samurai traditions when training its new peasant conscripts. The US-brokered peace failed to require Russia to make reparations, which became a cause of Japanese resentment towards the US.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the conflict between Russia and Japan from February 1904 to September 1905, which gripped the world and had a profound impact on both countries. Wary of Russian domination of Korea, Japan attacked the Russian Fleet at Port Arthur and the ensuing war gave Russia a series of shocks, including the loss of their Baltic Fleet after a seven month voyage, which reverberated in the 1905 Revolution. episodes.summaryAI: The Russo-Japanese War lasted from February 1904 to September 1905, beginning when Japan attacked the Russian fleet at Port Arthur. Russia's humiliating loss of its Baltic Fleet after an epic voyage halfway around the world reverberated into the 1905 Revolution. Japan's victory demonstrated that rapid modernisation could be combined with traditional warrior values rooted in Samurai culture, shocking Western assumptions about Asian military capability. The American-brokered Treaty of Portsmouth ended the war but failed to require Russian reparations, generating lasting Japanese resentment that shaped subsequent imperial ambitions in East Asia.

episodes.title: The Sack of Rome 1527
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the infamous assault of an army of the Holy Roman Emperor on the city of Rome in 1527. The troops soon broke through the walls of this holy city and, with their leader shot dead early on, they brought death and destruction to the city on an epic scale. Later writers compared it to the fall of Carthage or Jerusalem and soon the mass murder, torture, rape and looting were followed by disease which was worsened by starvation and opened graves. It has been called the end of the High Renaissance, a conflict between north and south, between Lutherans and Catholics, and a fulfilment of prophecy of divine vengeance and, perhaps more persuasively, a consequence of military leaders not feeding or paying their soldiers other than by looting.

episodes.summaryAI: In 1527 the army of Holy Roman Emperor Charles V assaulted Rome after their commander was shot dead early in the attack, leaving troops leaderless and unchecked. The soldiers, many unpaid for months, unleashed mass destruction that contemporaries compared to the ancient falls of Carthage and Jerusalem. The devastation has been called the end of the High Renaissance, scattering artists and intellectuals who had gathered under papal patronage. The sack demonstrated the catastrophic consequences of failing to pay mercenary armies and marked a decisive shift in Italian political and cultural power.

episodes.title: The Safavid Dynasty
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Safavid Dynasty, rulers of the Persian empire between the 16th and 18th centuries.In 1501 Shah Ismail, a boy of fifteen, declared himself ruler of Azerbaijan. Within a year he had expanded his territory to include most of Persia, and founded a ruling dynasty which was to last for more than two hundred years. At the peak of their success the Safavids ruled over a vast territory which included all of modern-day Iran. They converted their subjects to Shi'a Islam, and so created the religious identity of modern Iran - although they were also often ruthless in their suppression of Sunni practices. They thrived on international trade, and their capital Isfahan, rebuilt by the visionary Shah Abbas, became one of the most magnificent cities in the world. Under Safavid rule Persia became a cultural centre, producing many great artists and thinkers

episodes.summaryAI: The Safavid dynasty ruled Persia from the sixteenth to eighteenth centuries, beginning when the boy Shah Ismail declared himself ruler of Azerbaijan in 1501 and rapidly expanded his domain across most of Persia. The Safavids' most consequential act was converting their subjects to Shi'a Islam, creating the religious identity that defines modern Iran. Their capital Isfahan became one of the world's most magnificent cities, renowned for its mosques, bridges, and gardens. The dynasty fostered great artists, poets, and philosophers, establishing a distinctive Persian cultural synthesis that endured long after Safavid political power collapsed.

episodes.title: The Salem Witch Trials
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the outbreak of witch trials in Massachusetts in 1692-3, centred on Salem, which led to the execution of twenty people, with more dying in prison before or after trial. Some were men, including Giles Corey who died after being pressed with heavy rocks, but the majority were women. At its peak, around 150 people were suspected of witchcraft, including the wife of the governor who had established the trials. Many of the claims of witchcraft arose from personal rivalries in an area known for unrest, but were examined and upheld by the courts at a time of mass hysteria, belief in the devil, fear of attack by Native Americans and religious divisions.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the outbreak of witch trials in Massachusetts in 1692-3, centred on Salem, which led to the execution of twenty people, with more dying in prison before or after trial. Some were men, including Giles Corey who died after being pressed with heavy rocks, but the majority were women. episodes.summaryAI: The Salem witch trials of 1692-93 in colonial Massachusetts resulted in twenty executions and additional prison deaths. Accusations emerged from intersecting pressures: personal rivalries among Salem Village families, Puritan theological beliefs about satanic presence, frontier anxieties about Native American warfare, factional tensions between agrarian villagers and mercantile Salem Town, and mass hysteria amplified by spectral evidence doctrine. The trials exposed failures in colonial legal procedure, particularly reliance on accusation over evidence. Their legacy shaped American legal protections, church-state separation debates, and became a lasting metaphor for politically motivated persecution.

episodes.title: The Samurai
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Gregory Irvine, Nicola Liscutin and Angus Lockyer discuss the history of the Samurai and the role of their myth in Japanese national identity.The Samurai have a fearsome historical reputation as a suicidally brave caste of Japanese warriors. During World War Two, kamikaze pilots were photographed climbing into their cockpits with Samurai swords, encapsulating the way the myth of the Samurai's martial ethos kept its power long after their heyday. But the Samurai's role in Japanese culture is much more complex than that. They were deeply engaged with Zen Buddhism and Noh Theatre, and sponsored haiku poetry. After their role in Japan's century of civil war, ending in the early 1600s, they became part of the country's civil service. A 250-year peace toppled them into identity crisis.In the 19th century, with the arrival of the West, they played an important role in the establishment of a Japanese nation-state, not least by restoring the Emperor to power. And in the 20th century the mythological version of the Samurai, designed in part for Western consumption, became integral to a newly forged national identity.Nicola Liscutin is Programme Director of Japanese Studies at Birkbeck College, University of London; Gregory Irvine is Senior Curator Japan at the Victoria and Albert Museum; Angus Lockyer is Lecturer in Japanese History and Chair of the Japan Research Centre at the School of Oriental and African Studies, University of London.

After their role in Japan's century of civil war, ending in the early 1600s, they became part of the country's civil service. A 250-year peace toppled them into identity crisis.In the 19th century, with the arrival of the West, they played an important role in the establishment of a Japanese nation-state, not least by restoring the Emperor to power. And in the 20th century the mythological version of the Samurai, designed in part for Western consumption, became integral to a newly forged national identity.Nicola Liscutin is Programme Director of Japanese Studies at Birkbeck College, University of London; Gregory Irvine is Senior Curator Japan at the Victoria and Albert Museum; Angus Lockyer is Lecturer in Japanese History and Chair of the Japan Research Centre at the School of Oriental and African Studies, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: The samurai emerged as a Japanese warrior caste during medieval feudal warfare, bound by codes of loyalty and martial discipline. They became deeply engaged with Zen Buddhism, Noh theatre, tea ceremony, and haiku poetry, linking military culture to aesthetic refinement. After the civil wars, Tokugawa unification transformed samurai into bureaucratic civil servants during 250 years of peace, creating an identity crisis as warriors without war. In the nineteenth century samurai drove the Meiji Restoration, helping establish Japan as a modern nation-state. Twentieth-century bushido mythology became integral to Japanese national identity.

episodes.title: The Sassanid Empire
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Sassanian Empire. Founded around 226 AD, in Persia, the Sassanian Empire lasted over 400 years as a grand imperial rival to Rome. In modern day Iran, just down the road from the ancient Persian capital of Persepolis, there is a picture carved into a rock. It depicts a king, triumphant on horseback, facing two defeated enemies. This is no pair of petty princes, they are Roman Emperors - Philip and Valerian - and the king towering above them is Shapur I of the Sassanian Empire. So complete was his victory that Shapur is reputed to have used Valerian as a footstool when mounting his horse. This super-power traded goods from Constantinople to Beijing, handed regular defeats to the Roman army and only fell to the Islamic conquests of the 7th century. It still influences Persian identity to this day. But what was the culture and the literature of the empire, its structure and organisation? And what was its role in the great geopolitical game played out between the decaying empires in late antiquity?With Hugh Kennedy, Professor of Arabic in the Faculty of Languages and Cultures at the School of Oriental and African Studies; Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis, Curator of Iranian and Islamic Coins in the British Museum; James Howard-Johnston, University Lecturer in Byzantine Studies at the University of Oxford.

episodes.summaryAI: The Sassanid Empire, founded circa 226 AD in Persia, lasted over four centuries as the principal rival to Rome and Byzantium. Shapur I defeated and captured Roman Emperor Valerian, symbolizing Persian military dominance. The empire administered vast trade networks stretching from Constantinople to Beijing along Silk Road routes. Zoroastrianism served as state religion, shaping imperial governance and cultural identity. The Sassanids fell only to the seventh-century Islamic conquests. Their administrative structures, artistic traditions, and cultural legacy profoundly influenced subsequent Islamic civilization and continue shaping Persian national identity.

episodes.title: The Schism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss events surrounding the medieval division of the Christian Church. In 1054, Cardinal Humbert stormed into the Cathedral of Constantinople and charged down the aisle. In his hand was a Papal Bull – a deed of excommunication - and he slammed it down onto the altar. As he swept out of the startled church, the Papal Legate and his entourage stopped at the door and symbolically shook the sullied dust of Eastern Christianity from their Catholic boots. The Pope of Rome had decreed that the Patriarch of Constantinople was denied his place in heaven, and soon afterwards the Patriarch excommunicated the Pope in return.It was the culmination of an argument over a single word in the Nicene Creed - but after a thousand years of being one Church, so began a permanent rift.But what were the real underlying reasons behind the split, what were its effects and why did it take until December 1965 for the excommunications to be finally revoked?With Henrietta Leyser, medieval historian and Fellow of St Peter’s College, Oxford; Norman Housley, Professor of Medieval History at the University of Leicester; Jonathan Shepard, editor of the Cambridge History of the Byzantine Empire.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss events surrounding the medieval division of the Christian Church. In 1054, Cardinal Humbert stormed into the Cathedral of Constantinople and charged down the aisle. In his hand was a Papal Bull – a deed of excommunication - and he slammed it down onto the altar. As he swept out of the startled church, the Papal Legate and his entourage stopped at the door and symbolically shook the sullied dust of Eastern Christianity from their Catholic boots. The Pope of Rome had decreed that the Patriarch of Constantinople was denied his place in heaven, and soon afterwards the Patriarch excommunicated the Pope in return.It was the culmination of an argument over a single word in the Nicene Creed - but after a thousand years of being one Church, so began a permanent rift.But what were the real underlying reasons behind the split, what were its effects and why did it take until December 1965 for the excommunications to be finally revoked?With Henrietta Leyser, medieval historian and Fellow of St Peter’s College, Oxford; Norman Housley, Professor of Medieval History at the University of Leicester; Jonathan Shepard, editor of the Cambridge History of the Byzantine Empire.
episodes.summaryAI: The Great Schism of 1054 permanently divided Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox communions. Cardinal Humbert placed a papal bull of excommunication on the altar of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople; Patriarch Michael Cerularius reciprocated by excommunicating the papal legation. The immediate theological dispute centered on the filioque clause added to the Nicene Creed, but deeper tensions involved papal authority claims, liturgical differences, and political rivalry between Rome and Constantinople. After nearly a millennium as one church, the split shaped European and Middle Eastern religious geography. Mutual excommunications were only revoked in 1965.

episodes.title: The School of Athens
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss The School of Athens – the fresco painted by the Italian Renaissance painter, Raphael, for Pope Julius II’s private library in the Vatican. The fresco depicts some of the most famous philosophers of ancient times, including Aristotle and Plato, engaged in discussion amidst the splendour of a classical Renaissance chamber. It is considered to be one of the greatest images in Western art not only because of Raphael’s skill as a painter, but also his ability to have created an enduring image that continues to inspire philosophical debate today. Raphael captured something essential about the philosophies of these two men, but he also revealed much about his own time. That such a pagan pair could be found beside a Pope in private tells of the complexity of intellectual life at the time when classical learning was reborn in what we now call the Renaissance.With Angie Hobbs, Associate Professor in Philosophy at the University of Warwick; Valery Rees, Renaissance scholar and senior member of the Language Department at the School of Economic Science; Jill Kraye, Professor of the History of Renaissance Philosophy and Librarian at the Warburg Institute at the University of London
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss The School of Athens – the fresco painted by the Italian Renaissance painter, Raphael, for Pope Julius II’s private library in the Vatican. The fresco depicts some of the most famous philosophers of ancient times, including Aristotle and Plato, engaged in discussion amidst the splendour of a classical Renaissance chamber. It is considered to be one of the greatest images in Western art not only because of Raphael’s skill as a painter, but also his ability to have created an enduring image that continues to inspire philosophical debate today. Raphael captured something essential about the philosophies of these two men, but he also revealed much about his own time. That such a pagan pair could be found beside a Pope in private tells of the complexity of intellectual life at the time when classical learning was reborn in what we now call the Renaissance.With Angie Hobbs, Associate Professor in Philosophy at the University of Warwick; Valery Rees, Renaissance scholar and senior member of the Language Department at the School of Economic Science; Jill Kraye, Professor of the History of Renaissance Philosophy and Librarian at the Warburg Institute at the University of London
episodes.summaryAI: Raphael's School of Athens, painted circa 1509-11 as a fresco in Pope Julius II's Vatican library (Stanza della Segnatura), depicts Plato, Aristotle, and dozens of ancient Greek philosophers, mathematicians, and scientists in an idealized architectural setting. The composition embodies Renaissance humanism's synthesis of classical learning with Christian patronage. Central figures represent competing philosophical traditions: Platonic idealism and Aristotelian empiricism. The fresco reveals how Renaissance intellectuals positioned themselves as heirs to Greco-Roman knowledge, reconciling pagan philosophy with papal authority and contemporary artistic ambition.

episodes.title: The Science of Glass
episodes.synopsis: While glass items have been made for at least 5,000 years, scientists are yet to explain, conclusively, what happens when the substance it's made from moves from a molten state to its hard, transparent phase. It is said to be one of the great unsolved problems in physics. While apparently solid, the glass retains certain properties of a liquid. At times, ways of making glass have been highly confidential; in Venice in the Middle Ages, disclosure of manufacturing techniques was a capital offence. Despite the complexity and mystery of the science of glass, glass technology has continued to advance from sheet glass to crystal glass, optical glass and prisms, to float glasses, chemical glassware, fibre optics and metal glasses.
episodes.description: While glass items have been made for at least 5,000 years, scientists are yet to explain, conclusively, what happens when the substance it's made from moves from a molten state to its hard, transparent phase. It is said to be one of the great unsolved problems in physics. While apparently solid, the glass retains certain properties of a liquid. episodes.summaryAI: Glass has been manufactured for over five thousand years, yet the transition from molten liquid to hard transparent solid remains one of physics' great unsolved problems. Unlike crystalline solids, glass molecules lack ordered structure, challenging conventional definitions of solid and liquid states. Venetian Murano glassmakers guarded secrets under penalty of death. Modern innovations include float glass manufacturing, fiber optics enabling global telecommunications, and metallic glasses with extraordinary strength. Glass science bridges condensed matter physics, materials engineering, chemistry, and optical technology, connecting ancient craft traditions to cutting-edge research in amorphous materials.

episodes.title: The Scientific Method
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the evolution of the Scientific Method, the systematic and analytical approach to scientific thought. In 1620 the great philosopher and scientist Francis Bacon published the Novum Organum, a work outlining a new system of thought which he believed should inform all enquiry into the laws of nature. Philosophers before him had given their attention to the reasoning that underlies scientific enquiry; but Bacon's emphasis on observation and experience is often seen today as giving rise to a new phenomenon: the scientific method.The scientific method, and the logical processes on which it is based, became a topic of intense debate in the seventeenth century, and thinkers including Isaac Newton, Thomas Huxley and Karl Popper all made important contributions. Some of the greatest discoveries of the modern age were informed by their work, although even today the term 'scientific method' remains difficult to define
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the evolution of the Scientific Method, the systematic and analytical approach to scientific thought. In 1620 the great philosopher and scientist Francis Bacon published the Novum Organum, a work outlining a new system of thought which he believed should inform all enquiry into the laws of nature. Philosophers before him had given their attention to the reasoning that underlies scientific enquiry; but Bacon's emphasis on observation and experience is often seen today as giving rise to a new phenomenon: the scientific method.The scientific method, and the logical processes on which it is based, became a topic of intense debate in the seventeenth century, and thinkers including Isaac Newton, Thomas Huxley and Karl Popper all made important contributions. Some of the greatest discoveries of the modern age were informed by their work, although even today the term 'scientific method' remains difficult to define
episodes.summaryAI: Francis Bacon's Novum Organum (1620) established inductive reasoning from systematic observation and experiment as the foundation for natural philosophy, challenging Aristotelian deductive logic. Newton demonstrated mathematical laws derived from empirical evidence. Huxley championed scientific method against religious authority in Victorian debates. Popper introduced falsifiability as the criterion distinguishing science from pseudoscience. Despite these contributions, the term scientific method remains contested among philosophers of science, with debates over induction, paradigm shifts, and theory-ladenness of observation. This methodological framework underpins the greatest discoveries of the modern age across all natural sciences.

episodes.title: The Scientist
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the origin of the concept and historical role of the scientist. The word "science" first appeared in the English language in 1340 and ever since its meaning has been in a state of flux. The notion of "the scientist" has had a similarly evolving history. For some, "the scientist" does not truly appear until after the Renaissance, others put its emergence much later than that. When did the words and concepts we recognise today take on their contemporary meaning? How has the role of the scientist, and our understanding of it, changed? Has science always been a rival to religion, or was it once an ally? And how has the scientist been perceived by the wider world – as a modern saint, the "priest of reason", or as a terrifying and amoral menace - the "mad scientist" of film and literature? With John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy, University of Sussex; Patricia Fara, Lecturer on the History and Philosophy of Science, Cambridge University; Hugh Pennington, Head of the Department of Medical Microbiology, University of Aberdeen.

For some, "the scientist" does not truly appear until after the Renaissance, others put its emergence much later than that. When did the words and concepts we recognise today take on their contemporary meaning? How has the role of the scientist, and our understanding of it, changed? And how has the scientist been perceived by the wider world – as a modern saint, the "priest of reason", or as a terrifying and amoral menace - the "mad scientist" of film and literature? With John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy, University of Sussex; Patricia Fara, Lecturer on the History and Philosophy of Science, Cambridge University; Hugh Pennington, Head of the Department of Medical Microbiology, University of Aberdeen.
episodes.summaryAI: The word science first appeared in English around 1340, and the concept of the scientist as a distinct social role evolved over centuries from natural philosopher to professional researcher. Changing perceptions cast scientists alternately as priests of reason during the Enlightenment or dangerous experimenters in Romantic and modern imagination. The relationship between science and religion shifted across periods, from medieval complementarity through Galileo's conflict to Darwin's controversies. Questions of scientific authority, institutional organization, public trust, and the cultural image of the scientist connect epistemology, sociology of knowledge, and intellectual history.

episodes.title: The Scriblerus Club
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Scriblerus Club. The 18th century Club included some of the most extraordinary and vivid satirists ever to have written in the English language. We are given giants and midgets, implausible unions with Siamese twins, diving competitions into the open sewer of Fleet-ditch, and Olympic-style pissing competitions: "Who best can send on high/The salient spout, far streaming to the sky". But these exotic images were part of an attempt by Pope, Swift and their cadres to show a world in terrible decline: "Religion, blushing, veils her sacred fires,And unawares Morality expires.Nor public flame, nor private dares to shine;Nor human spark is left, nor glimpse divine!Lo! Thy dread empire, Chaos! Is restored:Light dies before thy uncreating word".So wrote Alexander Pope in his great mock epic verse, The Dunciad. Who were the Scriblerans? And what in eighteenth century society had driven them to such disdain and despair?With John Mullan, Senior Lecturer in English, University College London; Judith Hawley, Senior Lecturer in English, Royal Holloway, University of London; Marcus Walsh, Kenneth Allott Professor of English Literature, University of Liverpool.

Thy dread empire, Chaos! Is restored:Light dies before thy uncreating word".So wrote Alexander Pope in his great mock epic verse, The Dunciad. Who were the Scriblerans? episodes.summaryAI: The Scriblerus Club, active in early eighteenth-century London, united Alexander Pope, Jonathan Swift, John Gay, John Arbuthnot, Thomas Parnell, and Robert Harley in collaborative satirical writing. Their fictional pedant Martinus Scriblerus targeted false learning, literary pretension, and intellectual vanity. The group's influence produced major works including Gulliver's Travels, The Beggar's Opera, and The Dunciad, which depicted civilization's decline through grotesque imagery of giants, midgets, and degradation. Their Augustan satire connected literary criticism, political commentary, and philosophical skepticism about human progress and Enlightenment optimism.

episodes.title: The Second Barons' War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the years of bloody conflict that saw Simon de Montfort (1205-65) become the most powerful man in England, with Henry III as his prisoner. With others, he had toppled Henry in 1258 in a secret, bloodless coup and established provisions for more parliaments with broader representation, for which he was later known as the Father of the House of Commons. When Henry III regained power in 1261, Simon de Montfort rallied forces for war, with victory at Lewes in 1264 and defeat and dismemberment in Evesham the year after. Although praised for supporting parliaments, he also earned a reputation for unleashing dark, violent forces in English politics and, infamously, his supporters murdered hundreds of Jewish people in London and elsewhere.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the years of bloody conflict that saw Simon de Montfort (1205-65) become the most powerful man in England, with Henry III as his prisoner. With others, he had toppled Henry in 1258 in a secret, bloodless coup and established provisions for more parliaments with broader representation, for which he was later known as the Father of the House of Commons. When Henry III regained power in 1261, Simon de Montfort rallied forces for war, with victory at Lewes in 1264 and defeat and dismemberment in Evesham the year after. Although praised for supporting parliaments, he also earned a reputation for unleashing dark, violent forces in English politics and, infamously, his supporters murdered hundreds of Jewish people in London and elsewhere.
episodes.summaryAI: The Second Barons' War (1264-67) saw Simon de Montfort challenge Henry III's royal authority in England. De Montfort led a bloodless coup in 1258, imposing the Provisions of Oxford requiring regular parliaments and baronial oversight of governance. After capturing Henry III at the Battle of Lewes in 1264, de Montfort summoned parliaments including borough representatives, earning him the title Father of the House of Commons. His defeat and death at Evesham in 1265 ended the revolt but established lasting precedents for parliamentary representation and constitutional limits on English monarchy.

episodes.title: The Second Law of Thermodynamics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Second Law of Thermodynamics which can be very simply stated like this: "Energy spontaneously tends to flow from being concentrated in one place to becoming diffused and spread out". It was first formulated – derived from ideas first put forward by Lord Kelvin - to explain how a steam engine worked, it can explain why a cup of tea goes cold if you don't drink it and how a pan of water can be heated to boil an egg.But its application has been found to be rather grander than this. The Second Law is now used to explain the big bang, the expansion of the cosmos and even suggests our inexorable passage through time towards the 'heat death' of the universe. It's been called the most fundamental law in all of science, and CP Snow in his Two Cultures wrote: "Not knowing the Second Law of Thermodynamics is like never having read a work of Shakespeare".What is the Second Law? What are its implications for time and energy in the universe, and does it tend to be refuted by the existence of life and the theory of evolution?With John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy at the University of Sussex; Peter Atkins, Professor of Chemistry at Oxford University; Monica Grady, Head of Petrology and Meteoritics at the Natural History Museum.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Second Law of Thermodynamics which can be very simply stated like this: "Energy spontaneously tends to flow from being concentrated in one place to becoming diffused and spread out". It was first formulated – derived from ideas first put forward by Lord Kelvin - to explain how a steam engine worked, it can explain why a cup of tea goes cold if you don't drink it and how a pan of water can be heated to boil an egg.But its application has been found to be rather grander than this. The Second Law is now used to explain the big bang, the expansion of the cosmos and even suggests our inexorable passage through time towards the 'heat death' of the universe. It's been called the most fundamental law in all of science, and CP Snow in his Two Cultures wrote: "Not knowing the Second Law of Thermodynamics is like never having read a work of Shakespeare".What is the Second Law? What are its implications for time and energy in the universe, and does it tend to be refuted by the existence of life and the theory of evolution?With John Gribbin, Visiting Fellow in Astronomy at the University of Sussex; Peter Atkins, Professor of Chemistry at Oxford University; Monica Grady, Head of Petrology and Meteoritics at the Natural History Museum.
episodes.summaryAI: The second law of thermodynamics states that energy in closed systems tends to disperse from concentrated to diffused states, increasing entropy. Originally formulated to explain steam engine efficiency by Carnot, Clausius, and Kelvin, the law governs why heat flows from hot to cold, why time has direction, and why perpetual motion machines are impossible. Modern applications extend to cosmology, explaining the Big Bang's low-entropy initial state and the universe's expansion toward heat death. C.P. Snow argued that ignorance of this law equals never having read Shakespeare, marking it as fundamental to scientific literacy.

episodes.title: The Seventh Seal
episodes.synopsis: In the 1000th edition of In Our Time, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss arguably the most celebrated film of the Swedish director Ingmar Bergman (1918-2007). It begins with an image that, once seen, stays with you for the rest of your life: the figure of Death playing chess with a Crusader on the rocky Swedish shore. The release of this film in 1957 brought Bergman fame around the world. We see Antonius Block, the Crusader, realising he can’t beat Death but wanting to prolong this final game for one last act, without yet knowing what that act might be. As he goes on a journey through a plague ridden world, his meeting with a family of jesters and their baby offers him some kind of epiphany.
episodes.description: In the 1000th edition of In Our Time, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss arguably the most celebrated film of the Swedish director Ingmar Bergman (1918-2007). It begins with an image that, once seen, stays with you for the rest of your life: the figure of Death playing chess with a Crusader on the rocky Swedish shore. episodes.summaryAI: Ingmar Bergman's 1957 film The Seventh Seal depicts a medieval knight, Antonius Block, playing chess with Death on a plague-ravaged Swedish shore after returning from the Crusades. Block prolongs the game seeking meaning and one final meaningful act before dying. The film explores existential themes of faith, doubt, mortality, and the search for God's silence amid suffering. Bergman drew on medieval iconography, morality plays, and Scandinavian artistic traditions. The Seventh Seal became a landmark of European art cinema, influencing subsequent explorations of death and spirituality in film.

episodes.title: The Shimabara Rebellion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Christian uprising in Japan and its profound and long-term consequences.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Christian uprising in Japan and its profound and long-term consequences.
episodes.summaryAI: The Shimabara Rebellion of 1637-38 was a major Christian uprising in southwestern Japan, centered in the Shimabara Peninsula and Amakusa Islands of Kyushu. Persecuted Japanese Christians and overtaxed peasants revolted against Tokugawa authority. The rebellion's suppression resulted in approximately 37,000 deaths and had profound long-term consequences: the Tokugawa shogunate expelled all Portuguese, virtually eliminated Christianity from Japan, imposed strict maritime isolation (sakoku), and restricted foreign contact to limited Dutch and Chinese trade at Nagasaki. These policies shaped Japan's closed society for over two centuries.

episodes.title: The Siege of Malta, 1565
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the event of which Voltaire, two hundred years later, said 'nothing was more well known'. In 1565, Suleiman the Magnificent, the Ottoman leader, sent a great fleet west to lay siege to Malta and capture it for his empire. Victory would mean control of trade across the Mediterranean and a base for attacks on Spain, Sicily and southern Italy, even Rome. It would also mean elimination of Malta's defenders, the Knights Hospitaller, driven by the Ottomans from their base in Rhodes in 1522 and whose raids on his shipping had long been a thorn in his side. News of the Great Siege of Malta spread fear throughout Europe, though that turned to elation when, after four months of horrific fighting, the Ottomans withdrew, undermined by infighting between their leaders and the death of the highly-valued admiral, Dragut. The Knights Hospitaller had shown that Suleiman's forces could be contained, and their own order was reinvigorated.

In 1565, Suleiman the Magnificent, the Ottoman leader, sent a great fleet west to lay siege to Malta and capture it for his empire. episodes.summaryAI: The 1565 Siege of Malta pitted Suleiman the Magnificent's Ottoman fleet against the Knights Hospitaller defending the island. The four-month siege saw intense fighting at Fort St. Elmo, Birgu, and Senglea, with heavily outnumbered defenders under Grand Master Jean de Valette resisting repeated Ottoman assaults. Relief forces from Sicily eventually helped force Ottoman withdrawal. The siege marked the first major demonstration that Suleiman's forces could be contained, shifting Mediterranean power dynamics. It halted Ottoman westward expansion in the central Mediterranean and preserved Christian control of a strategic naval position between Europe and North Africa.

episodes.title: The Siege of Munster
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Diarmaid MacCulloch, Lucy Wooding and Charlotte Methuen discuss the Siege of Munster in 1534-35.In the early 16th century, the Protestant Reformation revolutionised Christian belief. But one radical group of believers stood out. The Anabaptists rejected infant baptism and formal clergy, and believed that all goods should be held in common. They were also convinced that the Second Coming was imminent.In 1534, in the north-western German city of Munster, a group of Anabaptists attempted to establish the 'New Jerusalem', ready for the Last Days before the coming Apocalypse. But the city was besieged by its ousted Prince-Bishop, and under the reign of its self-appointed King, a 25-year-old Dutchman called Jan van Leyden, it descended into tyranny. Books were burned, dissenters were executed and women were forced to marry. As starvation spread, King Jan lived in luxury with his 16 wives. The horrors of Munster have resonated through the European memory ever since. Diarmaid MacCulloch is Professor of the History of the Church at the University of Oxford; Charlotte Methuen is University Research Lecturer in Ecclesiastical History at the University of Oxford and Lecturer in Church History and Liturgy at Ripon College Cuddesdon; Lucy Wooding is Lecturer in Early Modern History at King's College, London.

Books were burned, dissenters were executed and women were forced to marry. As starvation spread, King Jan lived in luxury with his 16 wives. episodes.summaryAI: The 1534-35 Siege of Munster saw radical Anabaptists seize the Westphalian city and establish a theocratic New Jerusalem under Jan Matthys and later Jan van Leyden, who crowned himself king. The regime abolished private property, burned books, imposed forced polygamy with van Leyden taking sixteen wives, and prepared for the anticipated Apocalypse. The ousted Prince-Bishop besieged the city, causing mass starvation while leadership lived lavishly. The city's fall brought brutal reprisals. The episode discredited radical Anabaptism, strengthened arguments against religious enthusiasm, and shaped Reformation-era debates about church authority and civil order.

episodes.title: The Siege of Orléans
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Siege of Orléans, when Joan of Arc came to the rescue of France and routed the English army with the help of God. The perfidious English then burnt her as a heretic in Rouen marketplace. At least that's the story we're told but the truth involves the murky world of French court politics, labyrinthine dynastic claims, mass religious hysteria and English military and political incompetenceLooking back on the events that followed, the Duke of Bedford wrote to King Henry VI and declared “all things prospered for you till the time of the siege of Orleans, taken in hand God knoweth by what advice”.But what happened at the siege of Orleans, did Joan of Arc really rescue the city and how significant was the battle in changing the course of the 100 Years' War and the subsequent histories of England and France?With Anne Curry, Professor of Medieval History at the University of Southampton; Malcolm Vale, Fellow and Tutor in History at St John’s College, Oxford; Matthew Bennett, Senior Lecturer at the Royal Military Academy, Sandhurst.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Siege of Orléans, when Joan of Arc came to the rescue of France and routed the English army with the help of God. The perfidious English then burnt her as a heretic in Rouen marketplace. At least that's the story we're told but the truth involves the murky world of French court politics, labyrinthine dynastic claims, mass religious hysteria and English military and political incompetenceLooking back on the events that followed, the Duke of Bedford wrote to King Henry VI and declared “all things prospered for you till the time of the siege of Orleans, taken in hand God knoweth by what advice”.But what happened at the siege of Orleans, did Joan of Arc really rescue the city and how significant was the battle in changing the course of the 100 Years' War and the subsequent histories of England and France?With Anne Curry, Professor of Medieval History at the University of Southampton; Malcolm Vale, Fellow and Tutor in History at St John’s College, Oxford; Matthew Bennett, Senior Lecturer at the Royal Military Academy, Sandhurst.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1429 Siege of Orleans was a pivotal engagement in the Hundred Years' War between England and France. Joan of Arc, a young peasant claiming divine visions, led French forces to break the English siege, reversing momentum in a conflict that had devastated France for decades. The Duke of Bedford acknowledged that English fortunes declined after Orleans. The relief galvanized French national sentiment, led to the coronation of Charles VII at Reims, and initiated a series of French victories. Joan's role raises questions about military leadership, religious inspiration, and the construction of national heroism in medieval warfare.

episodes.title: The Siege of Paris 1870-71
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the siege of Paris during the Franco-Prussian war and the social unrest that followed, as the French capital was cut off from the rest of the country and food was scarce. When the French government surrendered Paris to the Prussians, power gravitated to the National Guard in the city and to radical socialists, and a Commune established in March 1871 with the red flag replacing the trilcoleur. The French government sent in the army and, after bloody fighting, the Communards were defeated by the end of May 1871.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the siege of Paris during the Franco-Prussian war and the social unrest that followed, as the French capital was cut off from the rest of the country and food was scarce. When the French government surrendered Paris to the Prussians, power gravitated to the National Guard in the city and to radical socialists, and a Commune established in March 1871 with the red flag replacing the trilcoleur. The French government sent in the army and, after bloody fighting, the Communards were defeated by the end of May 1871.
episodes.summaryAI: The Siege of Paris during the Franco-Prussian War (1870-71) saw the Prussian army encircle the French capital after Napoleon III's defeat at Sedan. Months of isolation brought starvation and civilian suffering. The French government's surrender provoked radical opposition: the National Guard and socialist factions seized power, establishing the Paris Commune in March 1871 under the red flag. The Commune implemented radical democratic and labor reforms before the French army's bloody reconquest in late May, killing thousands of Communards. The Commune became a foundational symbol for socialist and revolutionary movements worldwide.

episodes.title: The Siege of Tenochtitlan
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Siege of Tenochtitlan. In 1521 the Spanish conquistador Hernan Cortes led an army of Spanish and native forces against the city of Tenochtitlan, the spectacular island capital of the Aztec civilisation. At first Cortes had been welcomed by the Aztec leader, Moctezuma, and he and his men were treated like kings. But their friendship proved short-lived, and soon celebrations turned into vicious fighting. After a prolonged siege and fierce battle, in which many thousands died, the city finally fell. This major confrontation between Old and New Worlds precipitated the downfall of the Aztec Empire, and marked a new phase in European colonisation of the Americas
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Siege of Tenochtitlan. In 1521 the Spanish conquistador Hernan Cortes led an army of Spanish and native forces against the city of Tenochtitlan, the spectacular island capital of the Aztec civilisation. At first Cortes had been welcomed by the Aztec leader, Moctezuma, and he and his men were treated like kings. But their friendship proved short-lived, and soon celebrations turned into vicious fighting. After a prolonged siege and fierce battle, in which many thousands died, the city finally fell. This major confrontation between Old and New Worlds precipitated the downfall of the Aztec Empire, and marked a new phase in European colonisation of the Americas
episodes.summaryAI: The 1521 Siege of Tenochtitlan saw Hernan Cortes lead Spanish conquistadors and indigenous allies against the Aztec island capital in Lake Texcoco. Initially welcomed by Emperor Moctezuma, Spanish presence deteriorated into violent conflict. After expulsion during the Noche Triste, Cortes regrouped with Tlaxcalan and other native forces, launching a prolonged siege using brigantines on the lake. The city's fall precipitated the collapse of the Aztec Empire and inaugurated a new phase of European colonization in the Americas, transforming demographic, cultural, and political structures across Mesoamerica through conquest, disease, and forced conversion.

episodes.title: The Siege of Vienna
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Andrew Wheatcroft, Claire Norton and Jeremy Black discuss the Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683, when the Ottoman Empire tried to capture the capital city of the Hapsburg monarchs. The ensuing tale of blood and drama helped define the boundaries of Europe. In June 1683, a man called Kara Mustafa made a journey to Vienna. That a Muslim Turk should come to a Catholic city was not unusual, but Kara Mustafa did so at the head of the Ottoman Army. Vienna was the capital of the Hapsburg Empire and he intended to take it. The ensuing siege has been held responsible for many things, from the invention of the croissant to the creation of Viennese coffee. But most importantly, it has come to be seen as a clash of civilisations, one that helped to define a series of boundaries, between Europe and Asia, Christian and Muslim, Hapsburg and Ottoman, that influence the view between Vienna and Istanbul to this day. But to see the siege as a defining moment in east/west relations may be to read back into history an idea that was not true at the time.Claire Norton is Lecturer in History at St Mary's University College, London; Andrew Wheatcroft is Professor of International Publishing at Stirling University; Jeremy Black is Professor of History at the University of Exeter.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Andrew Wheatcroft, Claire Norton and Jeremy Black discuss the Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683, when the Ottoman Empire tried to capture the capital city of the Hapsburg monarchs. The ensuing tale of blood and drama helped define the boundaries of Europe. In June 1683, a man called Kara Mustafa made a journey to Vienna. That a Muslim Turk should come to a Catholic city was not unusual, but Kara Mustafa did so at the head of the Ottoman Army. Vienna was the capital of the Hapsburg Empire and he intended to take it. The ensuing siege has been held responsible for many things, from the invention of the croissant to the creation of Viennese coffee. But most importantly, it has come to be seen as a clash of civilisations, one that helped to define a series of boundaries, between Europe and Asia, Christian and Muslim, Hapsburg and Ottoman, that influence the view between Vienna and Istanbul to this day. But to see the siege as a defining moment in east/west relations may be to read back into history an idea that was not true at the time.Claire Norton is Lecturer in History at St Mary's University College, London; Andrew Wheatcroft is Professor of International Publishing at Stirling University; Jeremy Black is Professor of History at the University of Exeter.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1683 Siege of Vienna saw the Ottoman Empire under Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa attempt to capture the Habsburg capital, representing the deepest Ottoman penetration into central Europe. A relief force led by Polish King Jan III Sobieski decisively defeated the Ottoman army, marking a turning point in Ottoman-European power relations. The siege defined emerging boundaries between Christian Europe and the Islamic Ottoman world, shaping geopolitical identities for centuries. Cultural legacies reportedly include the croissant and Viennese coffee culture. The Ottoman retreat initiated Habsburg reconquest of Hungary and long-term Ottoman territorial decline.

episodes.title: The Sikh Empire
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise of the Sikh Empire at the end of the 18th Century under Ranjit Singh, pictured above, who unified most of the Sikh kingdoms following the decline of the Mughal Empire. He became Maharaja of the Punjab at Lahore in 1801, capturing Amritsar the following year. His empire flourished until 1839, after which a decade of unrest ended with the British annexation. At its peak, the Empire covered the Punjab and stretched from the Khyber Pass in the west to the edge of Tibet in the east, up to Kashmir and down to Mithankot on the Indus River. Ranjit Singh is still remembered as "The Lion of the Punjab."
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise of the Sikh Empire at the end of the 18th Century under Ranjit Singh, pictured above, who unified most of the Sikh kingdoms following the decline of the Mughal Empire. He became Maharaja of the Punjab at Lahore in 1801, capturing Amritsar the following year. His empire flourished until 1839, after which a decade of unrest ended with the British annexation. At its peak, the Empire covered the Punjab and stretched from the Khyber Pass in the west to the edge of Tibet in the east, up to Kashmir and down to Mithankot on the Indus River. Ranjit Singh is still remembered as "The Lion of the Punjab."
episodes.summaryAI: The Sikh Empire rose in late eighteenth-century Punjab under Maharaja Ranjit Singh, who unified fragmented Sikh kingdoms after Mughal imperial decline. Proclaimed Maharaja of Punjab in 1801, Ranjit Singh captured Amritsar and the Golden Temple, building a powerful multiethnic state stretching from the Khyber Pass to the borders of Tibet and from Sindh to Kashmir. The empire maintained a modernized army trained by European officers. Following Ranjit Singh's death in 1839, internal instability led to two Anglo-Sikh Wars and British annexation of Punjab by 1849, incorporating it into the expanding British Indian empire.

episodes.title: The Silk Road
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Tim Barrett, Naomi Standen and Frances Wood discuss the Silk Road, the trade routes which spanned Asia for over a thousand years, carrying Buddhism to China and paper-making and gunpowder westwards.In 1900, a Taoist monk came upon a cave near the Chinese town of Dunhuang. Inside, he found thousands of ancient manuscripts. They revealed a vast amount of evidence about the so-called Silk Road: the great trade routes which had stretched from Central Asia, through desert oases, to China, throughout the first millennium.Besides silk, the Silk Road helped the dispersion of writing and paper-making, coinage and gunpowder, and it was along these trade routes that Buddhism reached China from India. The history of these transcontinental links reveals a dazzlingly complex meeting and mingling of civilisations, which lasted for well over a thousand years
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Tim Barrett, Naomi Standen and Frances Wood discuss the Silk Road, the trade routes which spanned Asia for over a thousand years, carrying Buddhism to China and paper-making and gunpowder westwards.In 1900, a Taoist monk came upon a cave near the Chinese town of Dunhuang. Inside, he found thousands of ancient manuscripts. They revealed a vast amount of evidence about the so-called Silk Road: the great trade routes which had stretched from Central Asia, through desert oases, to China, throughout the first millennium.Besides silk, the Silk Road helped the dispersion of writing and paper-making, coinage and gunpowder, and it was along these trade routes that Buddhism reached China from India. The history of these transcontinental links reveals a dazzlingly complex meeting and mingling of civilisations, which lasted for well over a thousand years
episodes.summaryAI: Ancient network of overland and maritime trade routes linking China to the Mediterranean across Central Asia, active for over a millennium. Merchants exchanged silk, spices, precious metals, and glassware, but the routes also transmitted religions, technologies, and diseases. Buddhism traveled eastward into China and Japan; papermaking and gunpowder moved westward, transforming European warfare and literacy. Oasis cities like Samarkand and Dunhuang became cosmopolitan centers. A sealed cave near Dunhuang preserved thousands of manuscripts in multiple languages, revealing the extraordinary cultural interchange these corridors sustained.

episodes.title: The Sino-Japanese War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Sino-Japanese War of 1937-45. After several years of rising tension, and the Japanese occupation of Manchuria, full-scale war between Japan and China broke out in the summer of 1937. The Japanese captured many major Chinese ports and cities, but met with fierce resistance, despite internal political divisions on the Chinese side. When the Americans entered the war following the bombing of Pearl Harbor, the Japanese found themselves fighting on several fronts simultaneously, and finally capitulated in August 1945. This notoriously brutal conflict left millions dead and had far-reaching consequences for international relations in Asia.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Sino-Japanese War of 1937-45. When the Americans entered the war following the bombing of Pearl Harbor, the Japanese found themselves fighting on several fronts simultaneously, and finally capitulated in August 1945. episodes.summaryAI: Full-scale conflict between Imperial Japan and China from 1937 to 1945, following Japan's 1931 seizure of Manchuria. Japanese forces captured major ports, cities, and railways, committing atrocities including the Nanjing massacre. Chinese Nationalist and Communist forces mounted fierce resistance across vast territory, trading space for time. After Pearl Harbor, the war merged into the broader Pacific theatre with American involvement. Casualties numbered in the tens of millions, predominantly Chinese civilians. The war weakened Nationalist authority, strengthened the Communist movement, and reshaped the political order across East and Southeast Asia.

episodes.title: The Sistine Chapel
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the astonishing work of Michelangelo (1477-1564) in this great chapel in the Vatican, firstly the ceiling with images from Genesis (of which the image above is a detail) and later The Last Judgement on the altar wall. For the Papacy, Michelangelo's achievement was a bold affirmation of the spiritual and political status of the Vatican, of Rome and of the Catholic Church. For the artist himself, already famous as the sculptor of David in Florence, it was a test of his skill and stamina, and of the potential for art to amaze which he realised in his astonishing mastery of the human form.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the astonishing work of Michelangelo (1477-1564) in this great chapel in the Vatican, firstly the ceiling with images from Genesis (of which the image above is a detail) and later The Last Judgement on the altar wall. For the Papacy, Michelangelo's achievement was a bold affirmation of the spiritual and political status of the Vatican, of Rome and of the Catholic Church. For the artist himself, already famous as the sculptor of David in Florence, it was a test of his skill and stamina, and of the potential for art to amaze which he realised in his astonishing mastery of the human form.
episodes.summaryAI: Papal chapel in Vatican City whose ceiling and altar wall bear Michelangelo's defining Renaissance masterworks. Commissioned by Julius II in 1508, the ceiling depicts nine Genesis scenes from the Creation to Noah, peopled with prophets and sibyls rendered in revolutionary foreshortening. Decades later Clement VII commissioned The Last Judgement on the altar wall, a turbulent vision of salvation and damnation. The project tested a sculptor's capacity for monumental painting, following his triumph with David. The frescoes affirmed papal authority and represent an astonishing command of anatomy, color, and compositional drama.

episodes.title: The Social Contract
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Social Contract and ask a foundational question of political philosophy – by what authority does a government govern? “Man was born free and he is everywhere in chains”. So begins Jean Jacques Rousseau’s great work on the Social Contract. Rousseau was trying to understand why a man would give up his natural freedoms and bind himself to the rule of a prince or a government. But the idea of the social contract - that political authority is held through a contract with those to be ruled - began before Rousseau with the work of John Locke, Hugo Grotius and even Plato. We explore how an idea that burgeoned among the 17th century upheavals of the English civil war and then withered in the face of modern capitalist society still influences our attitude to government today. With Melissa Lane, Senior University Lecturer in History at Cambridge University; Susan James, Professor of Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London; Karen O’Brien, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Social Contract and ask a foundational question of political philosophy – by what authority does a government govern? “Man was born free and he is everywhere in chains”. So begins Jean Jacques Rousseau’s great work on the Social Contract. Rousseau was trying to understand why a man would give up his natural freedoms and bind himself to the rule of a prince or a government. But the idea of the social contract - that political authority is held through a contract with those to be ruled - began before Rousseau with the work of John Locke, Hugo Grotius and even Plato. We explore how an idea that burgeoned among the 17th century upheavals of the English civil war and then withered in the face of modern capitalist society still influences our attitude to government today. With Melissa Lane, Senior University Lecturer in History at Cambridge University; Susan James, Professor of Philosophy at Birkbeck College, University of London; Karen O’Brien, Professor of English Literature at the University of Warwick.
episodes.summaryAI: Philosophical tradition examining the legitimacy and origin of governmental authority over individuals. Roots appear in Plato and were developed by Grotius, Hobbes, Locke, and most famously Rousseau, who declared man born free yet everywhere in chains. The English Civil War and its constitutional upheavals gave the question urgent practical force in the seventeenth century. Thinkers debated whether people surrender natural rights for security, consent tacitly or explicitly, and may reclaim sovereignty from tyrants. The concept underpins modern constitutionalism, democratic theory, and ongoing disputes about the proper limits of state power.

episodes.title: The Song of Roland
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss an early masterpiece of French epic poetry, from the 12th Century. It is a reimagining of Charlemagne’s wars in Spain in the 8th Century in which Roland, his most valiant knight, chooses death before dishonour, guarding the army’s rear from a pagan ambush as it heads back through the Roncesvalles Pass in the Pyrenees. If he wanted to, Roland could blow on his oliphant, his elephant tusk horn, to summon help by calling back Charlemagne's army, but according to his values that would bring shame both on him and on France, and he would rather keep killing pagans until he is the last man standing and the last to die.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss an early masterpiece of French epic poetry, from the 12th Century. It is a reimagining of Charlemagne’s wars in Spain in the 8th Century in which Roland, his most valiant knight, chooses death before dishonour, guarding the army’s rear from a pagan ambush as it heads back through the Roncesvalles Pass in the Pyrenees. If he wanted to, Roland could blow on his oliphant, his elephant tusk horn, to summon help by calling back Charlemagne's army, but according to his values that would bring shame both on him and on France, and he would rather keep killing pagans until he is the last man standing and the last to die.
episodes.summaryAI: Twelfth-century Old French chanson de geste recounting a reimagined episode from Charlemagne's Iberian campaigns. Roland, commanding the rearguard at Roncevaux Pass, faces overwhelming pagan ambush yet refuses to blow his horn for reinforcements, choosing death before dishonor. His companion Oliver urges pragmatism; Roland insists on valor. The poem explores tensions between feudal loyalty, personal pride, and military prudence. Composed for oral performance, it fused historical memory of the 778 Basque attack with crusading ideology, becoming the foundational epic of French literary tradition and a model for medieval heroic narrative.

episodes.title: The Sonnet
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Sonnet, the most enduring form in the poet’s armoury. For over five hundred years its fourteen lines have exercised poetic minds from Petrarch and Shakespeare, to Milton, Wordsworth and Heaney. It has inspired the duelling verse of ‘sonneteering’, encapsulated the political perspectives of Cromwell and Kennedy and most of all it has provided a way to meditate upon love.Dante Gabriel Rossetti called it “the moment’s monument”. What is it about the Sonnet that has inspired poets to bind themselves by its strictures again and again? With Sir Frank Kermode, author of many books including Shakespeare’s Language; Phillis Levin, Poet in Residence and Professor of English at Hofstra University; Jonathan Bate, King Alfred Professor of English at the University of Liverpool.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Sonnet, the most enduring form in the poet’s armoury. For over five hundred years its fourteen lines have exercised poetic minds from Petrarch and Shakespeare, to Milton, Wordsworth and Heaney. It has inspired the duelling verse of ‘sonneteering’, encapsulated the political perspectives of Cromwell and Kennedy and most of all it has provided a way to meditate upon love.Dante Gabriel Rossetti called it “the moment’s monument”. What is it about the Sonnet that has inspired poets to bind themselves by its strictures again and again? With Sir Frank Kermode, author of many books including Shakespeare’s Language; Phillis Levin, Poet in Residence and Professor of English at Hofstra University; Jonathan Bate, King Alfred Professor of English at the University of Liverpool.
episodes.summaryAI: Fourteen-line poetic form that has endured for over five centuries as a vehicle for concentrated thought and feeling. Originating with Petrarch in Italy, it was adapted by Shakespeare into the English quatrain-couplet structure, then reshaped by Milton, Wordsworth, and Heaney. Its tight formal constraints demand compression, making it suited to argument, meditation, and paradox. Poets have used sonnets to address love, mortality, politics from Cromwell to Kennedy, and questions of artistic purpose. The form's versatility and discipline ensure its continued centrality in the Western poetic tradition.

episodes.title: The Soul
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Soul. In his poem ‘Sailing to Byzantium’ WB Yeats wrote:An aged man is but a paltry thing, A tattered coat upon a stick, unlessSoul clap its hands and sing, and louder singFor every tatter in its mortal dress. For Plato it was the immortal seat of reason, for Aristotle it could be found in plants and animals and was the essence of every being - but it died when the body died. For some it is the fount of creativity, for others the spark of God in man. What is the soul made of and where does it live? Is it the key to our individuality as humans? And when we die will our souls find paradise or purgatory, rebirth, resurrection or simply annihilation? With Richard Sorabji, Gresham Professor of Rhetoric at Gresham College; Ruth Padel, poet and author; Martin Palmer, Theologian and Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture.

In his poem ‘Sailing to Byzantium’ WB Yeats wrote:An aged man is but a paltry thing, A tattered coat upon a stick, unlessSoul clap its hands and sing, and louder singFor every tatter in its mortal dress. For Plato it was the immortal seat of reason, for Aristotle it could be found in plants and animals and was the essence of every being - but it died when the body died. For some it is the fount of creativity, for others the spark of God in man. What is the soul made of and where does it live? Is it the key to our individuality as humans? And when we die will our souls find paradise or purgatory, rebirth, resurrection or simply annihilation? With Richard Sorabji, Gresham Professor of Rhetoric at Gresham College; Ruth Padel, poet and author; Martin Palmer, Theologian and Director of the International Consultancy on Religion, Education and Culture.
episodes.summaryAI: Concept spanning philosophy, theology, and psychology concerned with an immaterial essence animating living beings. Plato held the soul immortal, rational, and separable from the body. Aristotle broadened it to a principle of life present in plants and animals as well as humans. Religious traditions identify the soul as a divine spark, the seat of moral agency, and the entity facing judgment after death, whether destined for paradise, purgatory, rebirth, or annihilation. Debates persist over its substance, its relation to consciousness, and whether neuroscience can account for what the soul was invoked to explain.

episodes.title: The South Sea Bubble
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss The South Sea Bubble, the speculation mania in early 18th-century England which ended in the financial ruin of many of its investors. The South Sea Company was founded in 1711 with a view to restructuring government debt and restoring public credit. The company would ostensibly trade with South America, hence its name; and indeed, it did trade in slaves for the Spanish market even after the Bubble burst in 1720.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss The South Sea Bubble, the speculation mania in early 18th-century England which ended in the financial ruin of many of its investors. The South Sea Company was founded in 1711 with a view to restructuring government debt and restoring public credit. The company would ostensibly trade with South America, hence its name; and indeed, it did trade in slaves for the Spanish market even after the Bubble burst in 1720.
episodes.summaryAI: Speculative financial mania in early eighteenth-century England centered on the South Sea Company, founded in 1711 to restructure national debt through a monopoly on trade with Spanish South America. The company's actual commerce included the slave trade under the Asiento contract. Shares soared on extravagant promises, drawing investors from all social ranks. When the bubble burst in 1720, fortunes were destroyed and public trust shattered. Parliamentary investigations exposed bribery and fraud reaching into government. The crisis prompted early financial regulation and became a lasting cautionary emblem of speculative excess and corporate deception.

episodes.title: The Spanish Armada
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Spanish Armada. On May 28th, 1588, a fleet of a hundred and fifty-one Spanish ships set out from Lisbon, bound for England. Its mission was to transport a huge invasion force across the Channel: the Spanish King, Philip II, was determined to remove Elizabeth from the throne and return the English to the Catholic fold. Two months later the mighty Spanish Armada was sighted off the coast of Cornwall. Bad weather, poor planning and spirited English resistance defeated the Spaniards: after a brief battle the remnants of their fleet fled. This tale of religious dispute, shifting political alliance and naval supremacy has entered our folklore - although some historians argue it changed nothing
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Spanish Armada. On May 28th, 1588, a fleet of a hundred and fifty-one Spanish ships set out from Lisbon, bound for England. Its mission was to transport a huge invasion force across the Channel: the Spanish King, Philip II, was determined to remove Elizabeth from the throne and return the English to the Catholic fold. episodes.summaryAI: In May 1588, Philip II of Spain dispatched 151 warships from Lisbon to overthrow Elizabeth I, suppress Protestantism, and restore England to Catholic allegiance. The Armada planned to rendezvous with the Duke of Parma's invasion army in the Netherlands. English squadrons under Howard and Drake harassed the fleet through the Channel, while fireships scattered it at Calais. Storms drove surviving vessels around Scotland and Ireland, wrecking dozens. Poor coordination, rigid tactics, and savage weather combined to defeat the enterprise. The outcome bolstered English naval confidence, weakened Spanish prestige, and shaped the Protestant settlement of northern Europe.

episodes.title: The Spanish Civil War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Spanish Civil War which was a defining war of the twentieth century. It was a brutal conflict that polarised Spain, pitting the Left against the Right, the anti-clericals against the Church, the unions against the landed classes and the Republicans against the Monarchists. It was a bloody war which saw, in the space of just three years, the murder and execution of 350,000 people. It was also a conflict which soon became internationalised, becoming a battleground for the forces of Fascism and Communism as Europe itself geared up for war.But what were the roots of the Spanish Civil War? To what extent did Franco prosecute the war as a religious crusade? How did Franco institutionalise his victory after the war? And has Spain fully come to terms with its past?With Paul Preston, Principe de Asturias Professor of Contemporary Spanish History at the London School of Economics; Helen Graham, Professor of Spanish History at Royal Holloway, University of London; Dr Mary Vincent, Senior Lecturer in the Department of History at Sheffield University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Spanish Civil War which was a defining war of the twentieth century. It was a brutal conflict that polarised Spain, pitting the Left against the Right, the anti-clericals against the Church, the unions against the landed classes and the Republicans against the Monarchists. It was a bloody war which saw, in the space of just three years, the murder and execution of 350,000 people. It was also a conflict which soon became internationalised, becoming a battleground for the forces of Fascism and Communism as Europe itself geared up for war.But what were the roots of the Spanish Civil War? To what extent did Franco prosecute the war as a religious crusade? How did Franco institutionalise his victory after the war? And has Spain fully come to terms with its past?With Paul Preston, Principe de Asturias Professor of Contemporary Spanish History at the London School of Economics; Helen Graham, Professor of Spanish History at Royal Holloway, University of London; Dr Mary Vincent, Senior Lecturer in the Department of History at Sheffield University.
episodes.summaryAI: Conflict from 1936 to 1939 that divided Spain along ideological, religious, and class lines: Republicans against Nationalists, anti-clericals against the Church, trade unions against landed elites, left against right. Approximately 350,000 people were killed through combat, massacre, and political execution. The war became an international proxy struggle as Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy supported Franco's Nationalists while the Soviet Union aided the Republic and international volunteers joined both sides. Franco's victory established a Catholic authoritarian regime lasting decades. The war foreshadowed the broader European confrontation between fascism, communism, and democracy.

episodes.title: The Spanish Inquisition
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Spanish Inquisition, the defenders of medieval orthodoxy. The word ‘Inquisition’ has its roots in the Latin word 'inquisito' which means inquiry. The Romans used the inquisitorial process as a form of legal procedure employed in the search for evidence. Once Rome's religion changed to Christianity under Constantine, it retained the inquisitorial trial method but also developed brutal means of dealing with heretics who went against the doctrines of the new religion. Efforts to suppress religious freedom were initially ad hoc until the establishment of an Office of Inquisition in the Middle Ages, founded in response to the growing Catharist heresy in South West France. The Spanish Inquisition set up in 1478 surpassed all Inquisitorial activity that had preceded it in terms of its reach and length. For 350 years under Papal Decree, Jews, then Muslims and Protestants were put through the Inquisitional Court and condemned to torture, imprisonment, exile and death. How did the early origins of the Inquisition in Medieval Europe spread to Spain? What were the motivations behind the systematic persecution of Jews, Muslims and Protestants? And what finally brought about an end to the Spanish Inquisition 350 years after it had first been decreed? With John Edwards, Research Fellow in Spanish at the University of Oxford; Alexander Murray, Emeritus Fellow in History at University College, Oxford;Michael Alpert, Emeritus Professor in Modern and Contemporary History of Spain at the University of Westminster
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Spanish Inquisition, the defenders of medieval orthodoxy. The word ‘Inquisition’ has its roots in the Latin word 'inquisito' which means inquiry. The Romans used the inquisitorial process as a form of legal procedure employed in the search for evidence. Once Rome's religion changed to Christianity under Constantine, it retained the inquisitorial trial method but also developed brutal means of dealing with heretics who went against the doctrines of the new religion. Efforts to suppress religious freedom were initially ad hoc until the establishment of an Office of Inquisition in the Middle Ages, founded in response to the growing Catharist heresy in South West France. The Spanish Inquisition set up in 1478 surpassed all Inquisitorial activity that had preceded it in terms of its reach and length. For 350 years under Papal Decree, Jews, then Muslims and Protestants were put through the Inquisitional Court and condemned to torture, imprisonment, exile and death. How did the early origins of the Inquisition in Medieval Europe spread to Spain? What were the motivations behind the systematic persecution of Jews, Muslims and Protestants?
