episodes.summaryAI: Ecclesiastical tribunal established in 1478 under Ferdinand and Isabella that operated for roughly 350 years, surpassing all earlier inquisitions in scope and duration. Initially targeting converted Jews suspected of secretly practicing Judaism, it expanded to prosecute Muslims, Protestants, blasphemers, and other perceived heretics. Methods included denunciation, secret testimony, torture, imprisonment, exile, public execution, and burning at the stake. Its spread reflected the drive for religious uniformity during the Reconquista and early modern state consolidation. Enlightenment pressures and liberal political movements gradually eroded its authority until formal abolition in the early nineteenth century.

episodes.title: The Speed of Light
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the speed of light. Scientists and thinkers have been fascinated with the speed of light for millennia. Aristotle wrongly contended that the speed of light was infinite, but it was the 17th Century before serious attempts were made to measure its actual velocity – we now know that it’s 186,000 miles per second. Then in 1905 Einstein’s Special Theory of Relativity predicted that nothing can travel faster than the speed of light. This then has dramatic effects on the nature of space and time. It’s been thought the speed of light is a constant in Nature, a kind of cosmic speed limit, now the scientists aren’t so sure. With John Barrow, Professor of Mathematical Sciences and Gresham Professor of Astronomy at Cambridge University; Iwan Morus, Senior Lecturer in the History of Science at The University of Wales, Aberystwyth; Jocelyn Bell Burnell, Visiting Professor of Astrophysics at Oxford University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the speed of light. Scientists and thinkers have been fascinated with the speed of light for millennia. Aristotle wrongly contended that the speed of light was infinite, but it was the 17th Century before serious attempts were made to measure its actual velocity – we now know that it’s 186,000 miles per second. Then in 1905 Einstein’s Special Theory of Relativity predicted that nothing can travel faster than the speed of light. This then has dramatic effects on the nature of space and time. It’s been thought the speed of light is a constant in Nature, a kind of cosmic speed limit, now the scientists aren’t so sure. With John Barrow, Professor of Mathematical Sciences and Gresham Professor of Astronomy at Cambridge University; Iwan Morus, Senior Lecturer in the History of Science at The University of Wales, Aberystwyth; Jocelyn Bell Burnell, Visiting Professor of Astrophysics at Oxford University.
episodes.summaryAI: Fundamental physical constant governing the propagation of electromagnetic radiation through vacuum, measured at approximately 186,000 miles per second. Aristotle considered light instantaneous; seventeenth-century experiments by Roemer and others first demonstrated its finite velocity. Einstein's special relativity established it as an absolute cosmic speed limit, yielding dramatic consequences: time dilation, length contraction, and mass-energy equivalence. No object with mass can reach it; information cannot exceed it. Yet contemporary physicists probe edge cases involving quantum entanglement, expanding spacetime, and exotic media, questioning whether the limit is as inviolable as once believed.

episodes.title: The Statue of Liberty
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Statue of Liberty."Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free”. With these words, inscribed inside her pedestal, the Statue of Liberty has welcomed immigrants to America since 1903. But the Statue of Liberty is herself an immigrant, born in Paris she was shipped across the Atlantic in 214 separate crates, a present to the Americans from the French. She is a token of friendship forged in the fire of twin revolutions, finessed by thinkers like Alexis de Tocqueville and expressed in the shared language of liberty. But why was this colossal statue built, who built it and what did liberty mean to the Frenchmen who created her and the Americans who received her?With Robert Gildea, Professor of Modern History at Oxford University; Kathleen Burk, Professor of Modern Contemporary History at University College London; John Keane, Professor of Politics at the University of Westminster
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Statue of Liberty."Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free”. With these words, inscribed inside her pedestal, the Statue of Liberty has welcomed immigrants to America since 1903. But the Statue of Liberty is herself an immigrant, born in Paris she was shipped across the Atlantic in 214 separate crates, a present to the Americans from the French. She is a token of friendship forged in the fire of twin revolutions, finessed by thinkers like Alexis de Tocqueville and expressed in the shared language of liberty. But why was this colossal statue built, who built it and what did liberty mean to the Frenchmen who created her and the Americans who received her?With Robert Gildea, Professor of Modern History at Oxford University; Kathleen Burk, Professor of Modern Contemporary History at University College London; John Keane, Professor of Politics at the University of Westminster
episodes.summaryAI: Colossal neoclassical sculpture on Liberty Island in New York Harbor, conceived in France and presented as a gift commemorating the friendship forged during the American and French revolutions. Designed by Frederic Auguste Bartholdi with an iron framework by Gustave Eiffel, the statue was shipped across the Atlantic in 214 crates and dedicated in 1886. Emma Lazarus's sonnet inscribed on its pedestal welcomed the tired, the poor, and the huddled masses yearning to breathe free. The monument became an enduring symbol of immigration, democratic aspiration, and transatlantic ideals of liberty.

episodes.title: The Sublime
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a transcendental idea that took hold on the Age of Enlightenment. When the English essayist John Hall translated the work of an obscure Roman thinker into English, he could hardly have known the ferment it would cause; for the work of Longinus introduced late 17th century Britain to the idea of the sublime – an idea that stalked the proceeding century. Longinus wrote, “As if instinctively, our soul is lifted up by the true sublime; it takes a proud flight, and is filled with joy and vaunting, as though it had itself produced what it had heard”.He was talking about the power of language, but in the 18th century the idea was set for a broader stage as British artists, poets, philosophers and scientists grappled with the sublime and adapted it to great swathes of the intellectual and physical landscape. What drove the great minds of the age to invest so much in the defining of the state of awe?With Janet Todd, Professor of English Literature at the University of Glasgow; Annie Janowitz, Professor of Romantic Poetry at Queen Mary, University of London; Peter de Bolla, Lecturer in English at the University of Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a transcendental idea that took hold on the Age of Enlightenment. When the English essayist John Hall translated the work of an obscure Roman thinker into English, he could hardly have known the ferment it would cause; for the work of Longinus introduced late 17th century Britain to the idea of the sublime – an idea that stalked the proceeding century. Longinus wrote, “As if instinctively, our soul is lifted up by the true sublime; it takes a proud flight, and is filled with joy and vaunting, as though it had itself produced what it had heard”.He was talking about the power of language, but in the 18th century the idea was set for a broader stage as British artists, poets, philosophers and scientists grappled with the sublime and adapted it to great swathes of the intellectual and physical landscape. What drove the great minds of the age to invest so much in the defining of the state of awe?With Janet Todd, Professor of English Literature at the University of Glasgow; Annie Janowitz, Professor of Romantic Poetry at Queen Mary, University of London; Peter de Bolla, Lecturer in English at the University of Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Aesthetic and philosophical category concerned with experiences of overwhelming awe, grandeur, and terror. Ancient roots lie in Longinus, who described the soul uplifted by powerful rhetoric. Eighteenth-century British thinkers including Burke and Kant distinguished the sublime from the beautiful, associating it with vastness, danger, and the limits of comprehension. Poets, painters, and philosophers explored how mountains, storms, and oceans evoke feelings exceeding rational control. The concept extended from landscape aesthetics to broader questions about the power of language, the nature of artistic genius, and why human beings seek out and invest meaning in experiences of awe.

episodes.title: The Sun
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Sun. The object that gives the Earth its light and heat is a massive ball of gas and plasma 93 million miles away. Thanks to the nuclear fusion reactions taking place at its core, the Sun has been shining for four and a half billion years. Its structure, and the processes that keep it burning, have fascinated astronomers for centuries. After the invention of the telescope it became apparent that the Sun is not a placid, steadily shining body but is subject to periodic changes in its appearance and eruptions of dramatic violence, some of which can affect us here on Earth. Recent space missions have revealed fascinating new insights into our nearest star.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Sun. The object that gives the Earth its light and heat is a massive ball of gas and plasma 93 million miles away. Thanks to the nuclear fusion reactions taking place at its core, the Sun has been shining for four and a half billion years. Its structure, and the processes that keep it burning, have fascinated astronomers for centuries. After the invention of the telescope it became apparent that the Sun is not a placid, steadily shining body but is subject to periodic changes in its appearance and eruptions of dramatic violence, some of which can affect us here on Earth. Recent space missions have revealed fascinating new insights into our nearest star.
episodes.summaryAI: Main-sequence star at the center of the solar system, a massive sphere of hydrogen and helium plasma approximately 93 million miles from Earth. Nuclear fusion in its core converts hydrogen to helium, releasing energy that has sustained it for 4.5 billion years. The Sun exhibits periodic cycles of activity including sunspots, solar flares, and coronal mass ejections capable of disrupting terrestrial communications and power grids. Recent space missions including the Parker Solar Probe have revealed new details about the solar wind, the corona's extreme temperature, and magnetic field dynamics that continue to challenge astrophysical models.

episodes.title: The Taiping Rebellion
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Taiping Rebellion.In 1850 a Chinese Christian convert, Hong Xiuquan, proclaimed himself leader of a new dynasty, the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom. He and his followers marched against the ruling Qing dynasty, gathering huge support as they went. The ensuing civil war lasted fourteen years; around twenty million people lost their lives in a conflict which eventually involved European as well as Chinese soldiers. The Taiping Rebellion was arguably the most important event to befall China in the 19th century. Chinese nationalists and communists alike have been profoundly influenced by it, and historians believe it shaped modern China in the same way as the First World War shaped modern Europe.Rana MitterProfessor of the History and Politics of Modern China at the University of OxfordFrances WoodHead of the Chinese Section at the British LibraryJulia LovellLecturer in Chinese History at Birkbeck, University of London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Taiping Rebellion.In 1850 a Chinese Christian convert, Hong Xiuquan, proclaimed himself leader of a new dynasty, the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom. He and his followers marched against the ruling Qing dynasty, gathering huge support as they went. The ensuing civil war lasted fourteen years; around twenty million people lost their lives in a conflict which eventually involved European as well as Chinese soldiers. The Taiping Rebellion was arguably the most important event to befall China in the 19th century. Chinese nationalists and communists alike have been profoundly influenced by it, and historians believe it shaped modern China in the same way as the First World War shaped modern Europe.Rana MitterProfessor of the History and Politics of Modern China at the University of OxfordFrances WoodHead of the Chinese Section at the British LibraryJulia LovellLecturer in Chinese History at Birkbeck, University of London.
episodes.summaryAI: Massive civil war in Qing China lasting from 1850 to 1864, ignited when Hong Xiuquan, a failed examination candidate and Christian convert, proclaimed a Heavenly Kingdom and a new dynasty. The Taiping forces captured Nanjing and controlled vast swathes of southern China. An estimated twenty million people perished through combat, famine, and epidemic. The rebellion exposed Qing administrative weakness, empowered regional military commanders, and set precedents for revolutionary upheaval. Arguably the most consequential nineteenth-century Chinese event, it reshaped the country's political trajectory as profoundly as the First World War transformed Europe.

episodes.title: The Tale of Sinuhe
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss The Tale of Sinuhe, one of the most celebrated works of ancient Egyptian literature. Written around four thousand years ago, the poem narrates the story of an Egyptian official who is exiled to Syria before returning to his homeland some years later. The number of versions of the poem, which is known from several surviving papyri and inscriptions, suggests that it was seen as an important literary work; although the story is set against a backdrop of real historical events, most scholars believe that the poem is a work of fiction.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss The Tale of Sinuhe, one of the most celebrated works of ancient Egyptian literature. Written around four thousand years ago, the poem narrates the story of an Egyptian official who is exiled to Syria before returning to his homeland some years later. The number of versions of the poem, which is known from several surviving papyri and inscriptions, suggests that it was seen as an important literary work; although the story is set against a backdrop of real historical events, most scholars believe that the poem is a work of fiction.
episodes.summaryAI: Ancient Egyptian literary narrative composed roughly four thousand years ago during the Middle Kingdom. It follows Sinuhe, a court official who flees Egypt after the assassination of Pharaoh Amenemhat I, lives in exile among Syrian chieftains, prospers as a warrior, yet longs for home. Eventually pardoned, he returns to royal favor and a proper Egyptian burial. Multiple surviving copies on papyri and ostraca indicate its wide circulation and literary prestige. Set against documented historical events but almost certainly fictional, it explores themes of loyalty, identity, exile, and the pull of homeland that resonate across cultures.

episodes.title: The Talmud
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the history and contents of the Talmud, one of the most important texts of Judaism. The Talmud was probably written down over a period of several hundred years, beginning in the 2nd century. It contains the authoritative text of the traditional Jewish oral law, and also an account of early Rabbinic discussion of, and commentary on, these laws. In later centuries scholars wrote important commentaries on these texts, which remain central to most strands of modern Judaism.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the history and contents of the Talmud, one of the most important texts of Judaism. The Talmud was probably written down over a period of several hundred years, beginning in the 2nd century. It contains the authoritative text of the traditional Jewish oral law, and also an account of early Rabbinic discussion of, and commentary on, these laws. In later centuries scholars wrote important commentaries on these texts, which remain central to most strands of modern Judaism.
episodes.summaryAI: Central text of Rabbinic Judaism compiled over several centuries beginning in the second century CE. It comprises the Mishnah, a codification of traditional Jewish oral law, and the Gemara, extensive Rabbinic discussion, debate, and commentary expanding upon it. Two versions exist: the Babylonian and the Jerusalem Talmud, with the Babylonian carrying greater authority. Covering law, ethics, philosophy, history, and storytelling, it provides the framework for Jewish legal reasoning and religious practice. The Talmud remains foundational to modern Jewish scholarship, liturgical life, and communal identity across Orthodox, Conservative, and other observant traditions.

episodes.title: The Temperance Movement
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the momentum behind teetotalism in 19th Century Britain, when calls for moderation gave way to complete abstinence in pursuit of a better life. Although arguments for temperance had been made throughout the British Isles beforehand, the story of the organised movement in Britain is often said to have started in 1832 in Preston, when Joseph Livesey and seven others gave a pledge to abstain. The movement grew quickly, with Temperance Halls appearing as new social centres in towns in place of pubs, and political parties being drawn into taking sides either to support abstinence or impose it or reject it.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the momentum behind teetotalism in 19th Century Britain, when calls for moderation gave way to complete abstinence in pursuit of a better life. Although arguments for temperance had been made throughout the British Isles beforehand, the story of the organised movement in Britain is often said to have started in 1832 in Preston, when Joseph Livesey and seven others gave a pledge to abstain. episodes.summaryAI: Nineteenth-century social reform campaign advocating total abstinence from alcohol, rooted in concern over the destructive effects of cheap spirits on working-class families. In 1832 Joseph Livesey and associates in Preston signed a pledge of complete teetotalism, distinguishing their movement from earlier moderation campaigns. Temperance societies spread rapidly, establishing Temperance Halls as alternative social centers to replace public houses with lectures, concerts, and mutual improvement. The movement drew political parties into debates over licensing legislation and local prohibition. It intersected with broader currents of evangelical Christianity, women's rights, and working-class self-improvement.

episodes.title: The Tempest
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Shakespeare's play The Tempest. Written in around 1610, it is thought to be one of the playwright's final works and contains some of the most poetic and memorable passages in all his output. It was influenced by accounts of distant lands written by contemporary explorers, and by the complex international politics of the early Jacobean age.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Shakespeare's play The Tempest. Written in around 1610, it is thought to be one of the playwright's final works and contains some of the most poetic and memorable passages in all his output. It was influenced by accounts of distant lands written by contemporary explorers, and by the complex international politics of the early Jacobean age.
episodes.summaryAI: Late Shakespeare romance composed around 1610, widely considered among his final works. Prospero, exiled Duke of Milan, uses magic on a remote island to conjure a tempest that brings his usurping brother and companions ashore. The play draws on contemporary accounts of New World voyages and the international politics of the Jacobean court. Themes of power, forgiveness, colonialism, and the renunciation of art converge in some of Shakespeare's most poetic and memorable passages. Prospero's farewell to his magic has long been read as the dramatist's own valediction to the stage.

episodes.title: The Tenant of Wildfell Hall
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Anne Bronte's second novel, published in 1848, which is now celebrated alongside those of her sisters but which Charlotte Bronte tried to suppress as a 'mistake'. It examines the life of Helen, who has escaped her abusive husband Arthur Huntingdon with their son to live at Wildfell Hall as a widow under the alias 'Mrs Graham', and it exposes the men in her husband's circle who gave her no choice but to flee. Early critics attacked the novel as coarse, as misrepresenting male behaviour, and as something no woman or girl should ever read; soon after Anne's death, Charlotte suggested the publisher should lose it for good. In recent decades, though, its reputation has climbed and it now sits with Jane Eyre and Wuthering Heights as one of the great novels by the Bronte sisters.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Anne Bronte's second novel, published in 1848, which is now celebrated alongside those of her sisters but which Charlotte Bronte tried to suppress as a 'mistake'. It examines the life of Helen, who has escaped her abusive husband Arthur Huntingdon with their son to live at Wildfell Hall as a widow under the alias 'Mrs Graham', and it exposes the men in her husband's circle who gave her no choice but to flee. Early critics attacked the novel as coarse, as misrepresenting male behaviour, and as something no woman or girl should ever read; soon after Anne's death, Charlotte suggested the publisher should lose it for good. In recent decades, though, its reputation has climbed and it now sits with Jane Eyre and Wuthering Heights as one of the great novels by the Bronte sisters.
episodes.summaryAI: Anne Bronte's 1848 novel follows Helen Graham, who flees her dissolute, abusive husband Arthur Huntingdon and takes refuge at Wildfell Hall with her young son. Narrated partly through Helen's diary, the book offers an unflinching portrayal of alcoholism, marital cruelty, and a woman's legal powerlessness over her own child. Charlotte Bronte attempted to suppress republication after Anne's death, calling it a mistake. Critics attacked its frank depiction of male vice as coarse. Modern reassessment places it alongside Jane Eyre and Wuthering Heights as a major Victorian novel challenging gender conventions.

episodes.title: The Theory of the Leisure Class
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the most influential work of Thorstein Veblen (1857-1929). In 1899, during America’s Gilded Age, Veblen wrote The Theory of the Leisure Class as a reminder that all that glisters is not gold. He picked on traits of the waning landed class of Americans and showed how the new moneyed class was adopting these in ways that led to greater waste throughout society. He called these conspicuous leisure and conspicuous consumption and he developed a critique of a system that favoured profits for owners without regard to social good. The Theory of the Leisure Class was a best seller and funded Veblen for the rest of his life, and his ideas influenced the New Deal of the 1930s. Since then, an item that becomes more desirable as it becomes more expensive is known as a Veblen good.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the most influential work of Thorstein Veblen (1857-1929). In 1899, during America’s Gilded Age, Veblen wrote The Theory of the Leisure Class as a reminder that all that glisters is not gold. He picked on traits of the waning landed class of Americans and showed how the new moneyed class was adopting these in ways that led to greater waste throughout society. He called these conspicuous leisure and conspicuous consumption and he developed a critique of a system that favoured profits for owners without regard to social good. The Theory of the Leisure Class was a best seller and funded Veblen for the rest of his life, and his ideas influenced the New Deal of the 1930s. Since then, an item that becomes more desirable as it becomes more expensive is known as a Veblen good.
episodes.summaryAI: Thorstein Veblen's 1899 treatise introduced conspicuous consumption and conspicuous leisure as defining behaviours of the newly wealthy American class, which imitated the habits of older landed aristocracies. Veblen argued that status-seeking through wasteful display served no productive social function and that profit accumulation divorced from useful work distorted economic life. The book became an unexpected bestseller, blending economic analysis with biting satire. Its critique of unearned privilege and parasitic wealth influenced Progressive Era reform and later New Deal economic thinking about institutional power and distributional justice.

episodes.title: The Thirty Years War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the war in Europe which begain in 1618 and continued on such a scale and with such devastation that its like was not seen for another three hundred years. It pitched Catholics against Protestants, Lutherans against Calvinists and Catholics against Catholics across the Holy Roman Empire, drawing in their neighbours and it lasted for thirty gruelling years, from the Defenestration of Prague to the Peace of Westphalia of 1648. Many more civilians died than soldiers, and famine was so great that even cannibalism was excused. This topic was chosen from several hundred suggested by listeners this autumn.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the war in Europe which begain in 1618 and continued on such a scale and with such devastation that its like was not seen for another three hundred years. It pitched Catholics against Protestants, Lutherans against Calvinists and Catholics against Catholics across the Holy Roman Empire, drawing in their neighbours and it lasted for thirty gruelling years, from the Defenestration of Prague to the Peace of Westphalia of 1648. Many more civilians died than soldiers, and famine was so great that even cannibalism was excused. This topic was chosen from several hundred suggested by listeners this autumn.
episodes.summaryAI: The Thirty Years War engulfed Europe from 1618 to 1648, originating in religious conflict between Catholics, Lutherans, and Calvinists within the Holy Roman Empire. Triggered by the Defenestration of Prague, it drew in major powers including France, Sweden, Spain, and the Habsburg domains. Warfare devastated Central Europe so severely that civilian deaths far exceeded military casualties, with famine driving populations to extremes including documented cannibalism. The Peace of Westphalia established principles of state sovereignty and religious coexistence that reshaped European political order, ending imperial Catholic ambitions and inaugurating the modern state system.

episodes.title: The Time Machine
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas explored in HG Wells' novella, published in 1895, in which the Time Traveller moves forward to 802,701 AD. There he finds humanity has evolved into the Eloi and Morlocks, where the Eloi are small but leisured fruitarians and the Morlocks live below ground, carry out the work and have a different diet. Escaping the Morlocks, he travels millions of years into the future, where the environment no longer supports humanity.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas explored in HG Wells' novella, published in 1895, in which the Time Traveller moves forward to 802,701 AD. There he finds humanity has evolved into the Eloi and Morlocks, where the Eloi are small but leisured fruitarians and the Morlocks live below ground, carry out the work and have a different diet. Escaping the Morlocks, he travels millions of years into the future, where the environment no longer supports humanity.
episodes.summaryAI: H.G. Wells's 1895 novella follows an inventor who travels to the year 802,701 AD, discovering humanity has diverged into two species. The Eloi live above ground as gentle, childlike fruitarians in apparent paradise, while the Morlocks toil underground, maintaining machinery and harboring darker appetites. The division satirises Victorian class separation between leisured elites and industrial labourers taken to evolutionary extremes. The Traveller journeys further into deep time, witnessing Earth's slow death as the environment can no longer sustain complex life, presenting a bleak materialist vision of entropy and extinction.

episodes.title: The Translation Movement
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the greatest intellectual projects in history - the mass translation of Greek ideas into Arabic from the 9th century onwards.One night in Baghdad, the 9th century Caliph Al-Mamun was visited by a dream. The philosopher Aristotle appeared to him, saying that the reason of the Greeks and the revelation of Islam were not opposed. On waking, the Caliph demanded that all of Aristotle’s works be translated into Arabic. And they were. And it wasn’t just Aristotle. Over the next 200 years Greek philosophy, medicine, engineering and maths were all poured and sometimes squeezed into Arabic. Centred on Baghdad, this translation movement introduced the Islamic world to the philosophy of Aristotle, the geometry of Euclid and the Medicine of Galen. It caused an intellectual ferment that demanded the creation of new words to explain new concepts and house new arguments. Over 600 years before the European renaissance the intellectual legacy of Greece was woven into the tapestry of Arabic thought and it was only through the Arabic versions that Europe go its hands on many Greek ideas. With Peter Adamson, Reader in Philosophy at King’s College London; Amira Bennison, Senior Lecturer in Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at the University of Cambridge; and Peter Pormann, Wellcome Trust Assistant Professor in Classics and Ancient History at the University of Warwick

Over the next 200 years Greek philosophy, medicine, engineering and maths were all poured and sometimes squeezed into Arabic. Centred on Baghdad, this translation movement introduced the Islamic world to the philosophy of Aristotle, the geometry of Euclid and the Medicine of Galen. It caused an intellectual ferment that demanded the creation of new words to explain new concepts and house new arguments. Over 600 years before the European renaissance the intellectual legacy of Greece was woven into the tapestry of Arabic thought and it was only through the Arabic versions that Europe go its hands on many Greek ideas. With Peter Adamson, Reader in Philosophy at King’s College London; Amira Bennison, Senior Lecturer in Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at the University of Cambridge; and Peter Pormann, Wellcome Trust Assistant Professor in Classics and Ancient History at the University of Warwick
episodes.summaryAI: Beginning in the ninth century under Abbasid patronage, a sustained campaign translated Greek philosophical, scientific, and medical texts into Arabic. Caliph al-Mamun reportedly dreamed Aristotle assured him that Greek reason and Islamic revelation were compatible, spurring institutional support. Over two centuries, works by Aristotle, Galen, Euclid, Ptolemy, and others were rendered into Arabic by scholars including Hunayn ibn Ishaq. This movement preserved and transmitted classical knowledge that might otherwise have been lost, predating the European Renaissance by six hundred years and shaping Islamic philosophy, medicine, mathematics, and engineering for centuries.

episodes.title: The Treaty of Limerick
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 1691 peace treaty that ended the Williamite War in Ireland, between supporters of the deposed King James II and the forces of William III and his allies. It followed the battles at Aughrim and the Boyne and sieges at Limerick, and led to the disbanding of the Jacobite army in Ireland, with troops free to follow James to France for his Irish Brigade. The Catholic landed gentry were guaranteed rights on condition of swearing loyalty to William and Mary yet, while some Protestants thought the terms too lenient, it was said the victors broke those terms before the ink was dry.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 1691 peace treaty that ended the Williamite War in Ireland, between supporters of the deposed King James II and the forces of William III and his allies. It followed the battles at Aughrim and the Boyne and sieges at Limerick, and led to the disbanding of the Jacobite army in Ireland, with troops free to follow James to France for his Irish Brigade. The Catholic landed gentry were guaranteed rights on condition of swearing loyalty to William and Mary yet, while some Protestants thought the terms too lenient, it was said the victors broke those terms before the ink was dry.
episodes.summaryAI: The 1691 Treaty of Limerick ended the Williamite War in Ireland following decisive Protestant victories at the Boyne and Aughrim. Under its terms, Catholic gentry were guaranteed religious freedom, property rights, and the ability to practise professions, provided they swore loyalty to William and Mary. The treaty was intended as a pragmatic settlement allowing the defeated Jacobite forces to depart for France. However, the Protestant-dominated Irish Parliament almost immediately violated its provisions, enacting penal laws that stripped Catholics of rights, making the broken treaty a lasting symbol of betrayal in Irish historical memory.

episodes.title: The Trial of Charles I
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Justin Champion, Diane Purkiss and David Wootton discuss the trial of Charles I, recounting the high drama in Westminster Hall and the ideas that led to the execution.Begun on 20th January 1649, the trial culminated in the epoch-making execution of an English monarch. But on the way it was a drama of ideas about kingly authority, tax, parliamentary power and religion, all suffused with personal vendettas, political confusion and individual courage. It was also a forum in which the newly-ended Civil War and the events of Charles's reign were picked over by the people who had experienced them. Melvyn and guests recount the events of the trial, explore the central arguments and see whether, 350 years later, we can work out who really won.Justin Champion is Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; Diane Purkiss is a Fellow and tutor at Keble College, Oxford; David Wootton is Professor of History at the University of York.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Justin Champion, Diane Purkiss and David Wootton discuss the trial of Charles I, recounting the high drama in Westminster Hall and the ideas that led to the execution.Begun on 20th January 1649, the trial culminated in the epoch-making execution of an English monarch. But on the way it was a drama of ideas about kingly authority, tax, parliamentary power and religion, all suffused with personal vendettas, political confusion and individual courage. It was also a forum in which the newly-ended Civil War and the events of Charles's reign were picked over by the people who had experienced them. Melvyn and guests recount the events of the trial, explore the central arguments and see whether, 350 years later, we can work out who really won.Justin Champion is Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; Diane Purkiss is a Fellow and tutor at Keble College, Oxford; David Wootton is Professor of History at the University of York.
episodes.summaryAI: The trial of Charles I opened on 20 January 1649, pitting parliamentary sovereignty against divine royal authority. Charles refused to recognise the court's legitimacy, arguing no earthly power could judge an anointed king. The proceedings exposed deep divisions over taxation without consent, religious governance, and the limits of monarchical power. Personal vendettas and political confusion shaped the outcome alongside genuine constitutional principle. The king's execution on 30 January was epoch-making, demonstrating that a sovereign could be held accountable by representatives of the people, establishing precedents for constitutional government whose consequences remain debated.

episodes.title: The Trinity
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Trinity. The idea that God is a single entity, but one known in three distinct forms - Father, Son and Holy Spirit - has been a central belief for most Christians since the earliest years of the religion. The doctrine was often controversial in the early years of the Church, until clarified by the Council of Nicaea in the late 4th century. Later thinkers including St Augustine and Thomas Aquinas recognised that this religious mystery posed profound theological questions, such as whether the three persons of the Trinity always acted together, and whether they were of equal status. The Trinity's influence on Christian thought and practice is considerable, although it is interpreted in different ways by different Christian traditions.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Trinity. The idea that God is a single entity, but one known in three distinct forms - Father, Son and Holy Spirit - has been a central belief for most Christians since the earliest years of the religion. The doctrine was often controversial in the early years of the Church, until clarified by the Council of Nicaea in the late 4th century. Later thinkers including St Augustine and Thomas Aquinas recognised that this religious mystery posed profound theological questions, such as whether the three persons of the Trinity always acted together, and whether they were of equal status. The Trinity's influence on Christian thought and practice is considerable, although it is interpreted in different ways by different Christian traditions.
episodes.summaryAI: The Trinity holds that one God exists eternally in three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, distinct yet sharing a single divine essence. This doctrine emerged from the earliest Christian communities and was formally clarified at the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD against Arian claims that the Son was created. Augustine of Hippo explored the Trinity through analogies of mind, knowledge, and love, while Thomas Aquinas applied Aristotelian categories to articulate divine relations. Eastern Orthodox, Catholic, and Protestant traditions continue to interpret Trinitarian theology with different emphases on procession, personhood, and experience.

episodes.title: The Trojan War
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Trojan War, one of the best known events of Greek mythology. According to the traditional version of the story, the war began when a Trojan prince, Paris, eloped with the Spartan queen Helen. A Greek army besieged Troy for ten years before the city was finally overrun and destroyed. Some of the most familiar names of Greek mythology are associated with the war, including Achilles and Hector, Odysseus and Helen of Troy - and it has also given us the story of the Trojan Horse.The war is the backdrop for Homer's epic poem The Iliad, and features in many other works from classical antiquity. For centuries it was assumed to be a mythical event. But in the nineteenth century a series of archaeological discoveries provided startling evidence that Troy might really have existed, leading some scholars to conclude that there could even be some truth behind the myth. So does the Trojan War story have any basis in fact? And why has it proved such an enduring legend
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Trojan War, one of the best known events of Greek mythology. According to the traditional version of the story, the war began when a Trojan prince, Paris, eloped with the Spartan queen Helen. A Greek army besieged Troy for ten years before the city was finally overrun and destroyed. Some of the most familiar names of Greek mythology are associated with the war, including Achilles and Hector, Odysseus and Helen of Troy - and it has also given us the story of the Trojan Horse.The war is the backdrop for Homer's epic poem The Iliad, and features in many other works from classical antiquity. For centuries it was assumed to be a mythical event. But in the nineteenth century a series of archaeological discoveries provided startling evidence that Troy might really have existed, leading some scholars to conclude that there could even be some truth behind the myth. So does the Trojan War story have any basis in fact? And why has it proved such an enduring legend
episodes.summaryAI: The Trojan War stands as the most celebrated narrative of Greek mythology. Paris of Troy eloped with Helen, wife of Spartan king Menelaus, provoking a Greek coalition that besieged Troy for ten years. The conflict produced iconic figures including Achilles, Hector, Ajax, and Odysseus, culminating in the stratagem of the wooden horse. Homer's Iliad captures a few weeks of the final year, exploring honour, mortality, and wrath. Archaeological excavations at Hisarlik in modern Turkey have revealed a Bronze Age city showing signs of destruction, suggesting possible historical foundations beneath the mythological tradition.

episodes.title: The Tudor State
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discusses the Tudor State. In 1485 Henry Tudor slew Richard III and routed his army at The Battle of Bosworth Field. It was a decisive victory which founded a bold new dynasty; and this date like 1789 and 1066 has been taken by historians to be one of the great ‘year zeros’ of history: Suddenly the muddled Medieval World with its robber barons, feudal barbarism and bloody Wars of the Roses was banished, and the modern age of centralised government and King’s Justice was ushered in. But were the Tudors as instrumental in reshaping the British state as historians have liked to make out, and did their reign throughout the 16th century really lay the political foundations of our own age? With John Guy, Professor of Modern History, University of St Andrews; Christopher Haigh, Tutor of Modern History at Christ Church College, Cambridge; Christine Carpenter, Fellow in History at New Hall, Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discusses the Tudor State. In 1485 Henry Tudor slew Richard III and routed his army at The Battle of Bosworth Field. It was a decisive victory which founded a bold new dynasty; and this date like 1789 and 1066 has been taken by historians to be one of the great ‘year zeros’ of history: Suddenly the muddled Medieval World with its robber barons, feudal barbarism and bloody Wars of the Roses was banished, and the modern age of centralised government and King’s Justice was ushered in. But were the Tudors as instrumental in reshaping the British state as historians have liked to make out, and did their reign throughout the 16th century really lay the political foundations of our own age? With John Guy, Professor of Modern History, University of St Andrews; Christopher Haigh, Tutor of Modern History at Christ Church College, Cambridge; Christine Carpenter, Fellow in History at New Hall, Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Henry Tudor's 1485 victory over Richard III at Bosworth Field founded a dynasty that ruled England until 1603. The Tudors centralised royal administration, subordinated feudal magnates, broke with Rome to establish royal supremacy over the Church, dissolved the monasteries, and expanded parliamentary involvement in governance. Henry VII built financial stability, Henry VIII transformed ecclesiastical authority, and Elizabeth I consolidated Protestant settlement while projecting naval power. Whether the Tudors fundamentally reshaped the British state or merely accelerated existing trends remains debated, but the sixteenth century undeniably laid institutional and political foundations that shaped subsequent centuries.

episodes.title: The Unicorn
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the unicorn. In the 5th century BC a Greek historian, Ctesias, described a strange one-horned beast which he believed to live in a remote area of India. Later classical scholars, including Aristotle and Pliny, added to his account of this animal which they called the monoceros, a vicious ass-like creature with a single horn in the middle of its forehead.For centuries the monoceros or unicorn was widely accepted to be a real - if rarely seen - beast. It appears in the Bible, and in the Middle Ages became a powerful Christian symbol. It continued to be represented in art and literature throughout the Renaissance, when 'unicorn horn' became one of the most valuable commodities on earth, thanks to its supposed properties as an antidote to poison. As late as the seventeenth century, scientists believed they had found conclusive proof of the existence of unicorns. It was some time before the animal was shown to be a myth; four hundred years on, the unicorn retains much of its fascination and symbolic power
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the unicorn. In the 5th century BC a Greek historian, Ctesias, described a strange one-horned beast which he believed to live in a remote area of India. Later classical scholars, including Aristotle and Pliny, added to his account of this animal which they called the monoceros, a vicious ass-like creature with a single horn in the middle of its forehead.For centuries the monoceros or unicorn was widely accepted to be a real - if rarely seen - beast.
