It was some time before the animal was shown to be a myth; four hundred years on, the unicorn retains much of its fascination and symbolic power
episodes.summaryAI: The unicorn first appeared in Western literature through the fifth-century BC Greek historian Ctesias, who described a one-horned beast from India. Biblical translation errors reinforced its existence. Medieval Christianity adopted it as a symbol of purity and Christ, depicted in tapestries being tamed only by a virgin. Unicorn horn, actually narwhal tusk, became the most prized commodity in Europe as a supposed universal antidote to poison, commanding prices exceeding gold. Even into the seventeenth century, scholars debated proof of the creature's existence. The myth retains cultural fascination across art, literature, and popular imagination.

episodes.title: The Universe's Origins
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the history of what we know about the origins of the universe. Some four hundred years ago in Rome, one Giordano Bruno was burnt at the stake for his belief in other inhabited worlds - it’s a possibility which has fascinated scientists, writers, artists and the general public for centuries - and any consideration of the origins of life and matter on other planets, and indeed this one, inevitably raises huge questions. Do other worlds exist? How did our planet come into existence? How can we know anything at all about the origins of life and matter so many billions of years ago, and how has our thinking on these - amongst the deepest of questions - changed over the 20th century? Are we any closer to knowing whether other worlds exist and how our own planet came into being? And does the knowledge we have about these things change our perception of ourselves and our position in the universe?With Professor Sir Martin Rees, Astronomer Royal and Royal Society Research Professor in Astronomy and Physics, Cambridge University; Professor Paul Davies, theoretical physicist and Visiting Professor at Imperial College, London.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg examines the history of what we know about the origins of the universe. Some four hundred years ago in Rome, one Giordano Bruno was burnt at the stake for his belief in other inhabited worlds - it’s a possibility which has fascinated scientists, writers, artists and the general public for centuries - and any consideration of the origins of life and matter on other planets, and indeed this one, inevitably raises huge questions. Do other worlds exist? How did our planet come into existence? How can we know anything at all about the origins of life and matter so many billions of years ago, and how has our thinking on these - amongst the deepest of questions - changed over the 20th century? Are we any closer to knowing whether other worlds exist and how our own planet came into being? And does the knowledge we have about these things change our perception of ourselves and our position in the universe?With Professor Sir Martin Rees, Astronomer Royal and Royal Society Research Professor in Astronomy and Physics, Cambridge University; Professor Paul Davies, theoretical physicist and Visiting Professor at Imperial College, London.
episodes.summaryAI: Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake in 1600 for asserting the existence of innumerable worlds beyond Earth, challenging a cosmos centred on humanity. Questions about planetary origins evolved from theological speculation to scientific inquiry through Kant's nebular hypothesis, Laplace's refinements, and twentieth-century discoveries of stellar nucleosynthesis and planetary accretion. The detection of extrasolar planets from the 1990s onward confirmed Bruno's intuition that other worlds exist. Modern cosmology traces Earth's formation to gravitational collapse within a solar nebula 4.6 billion years ago, fundamentally reshaping understanding of humanity's place in the universe.

episodes.title: The Universe's Shape
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the shape of the universe. In the Beginning, runs one account, was the Big Bang. All matter in existence today originated around 13 billion years ago in a phenomenally hot, extraordinarily condensed primordial atom that exploded with incredible force. Hydrogen and helium were shot across the firmament, gravity caused the gases to condense into clouds and in these clouds the first stars were formed, then galaxies came and more galaxies in clusters, onwards and outwards, ever expanding. It is still expanding, runs the orthodox account, and may even be speeding up. It is still creating new galaxies and it continues to colonise more and more of infinite space, despite the fact that it is supposedly infinite itself.So, if our universe is expanding, what is it expanding into? If it is already infinite how can it be getting any bigger? And is there really only one?With Sir Martin Rees, Royal Society Research Professor in Astronomy and Physics, Cambridge University; Julian Barbour, Independent Theoretical Physicist; Janna Levin, Advanced Fellow in Theoretical Physics at the University of Cambridge.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the shape of the universe. In the Beginning, runs one account, was the Big Bang. All matter in existence today originated around 13 billion years ago in a phenomenally hot, extraordinarily condensed primordial atom that exploded with incredible force. Hydrogen and helium were shot across the firmament, gravity caused the gases to condense into clouds and in these clouds the first stars were formed, then galaxies came and more galaxies in clusters, onwards and outwards, ever expanding. It is still expanding, runs the orthodox account, and may even be speeding up. It is still creating new galaxies and it continues to colonise more and more of infinite space, despite the fact that it is supposedly infinite itself.So, if our universe is expanding, what is it expanding into? If it is already infinite how can it be getting any bigger? And is there really only one?With Sir Martin Rees, Royal Society Research Professor in Astronomy and Physics, Cambridge University; Julian Barbour, Independent Theoretical Physicist; Janna Levin, Advanced Fellow in Theoretical Physics at the University of Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: The Big Bang approximately thirteen billion years ago initiated the expansion of space itself, a process that continues and appears to be accelerating due to dark energy. General relativity permits a universe that is spatially infinite yet still expanding, since expansion describes the stretching of space between objects rather than objects moving into pre-existing emptiness. Observations of cosmic microwave background radiation constrain the universe's geometry to near-flat. Whether the cosmos is finite or infinite, and whether our observable universe is one among many in a multiverse, remain among the deepest open questions in modern cosmology.

episodes.title: The Upanishads
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Upanishads, the ancient sacred texts of Hinduism. Dating from about 700 BC, the Upanishads were passed down through an oral tradition in priestly castes and were not written down until the 6th century AD. They constitute the final part of the Vedas, the collection of texts which form the foundation of the Indian Hindu world, and were originally spoken during sacrificial rituals.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Upanishads, the ancient sacred texts of Hinduism. Dating from about 700 BC, the Upanishads were passed down through an oral tradition in priestly castes and were not written down until the 6th century AD. They constitute the final part of the Vedas, the collection of texts which form the foundation of the Indian Hindu world, and were originally spoken during sacrificial rituals.
episodes.summaryAI: The Upanishads are ancient Sanskrit texts forming the concluding philosophical portions of the Vedas, composed from approximately 700 BC onward. Originally transmitted orally within Brahmin priestly lineages and spoken during sacrificial rituals, they were committed to writing around the sixth century AD. They explore fundamental questions about the nature of reality, the self, and ultimate truth, introducing concepts including Brahman as universal consciousness and Atman as individual soul. Their teaching that Atman and Brahman are identical became foundational to Vedanta philosophy and profoundly shaped Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain religious and philosophical traditions across South Asia.

episodes.title: The Vacuum of Space
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Frank Close, Jocelyn Bell Burnell and Ruth Gregory discuss the Vacuum of Space. The idea that there is a nothingness at the heart of nature has exercised philosophers and scientists for millennia, from Thales's belief that all matter was water to Newton's concept of the Ether and Einstein's idea of Space-Time. Recently, physicists have realised that the vacuum is not as empty as we thought and that the various vacuums of nature vibrate with forces and energies, waves and particles and the mysterious phenomena of the Higgs field and dark energy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Frank Close, Jocelyn Bell Burnell and Ruth Gregory discuss the Vacuum of Space. The idea that there is a nothingness at the heart of nature has exercised philosophers and scientists for millennia, from Thales's belief that all matter was water to Newton's concept of the Ether and Einstein's idea of Space-Time. Recently, physicists have realised that the vacuum is not as empty as we thought and that the various vacuums of nature vibrate with forces and energies, waves and particles and the mysterious phenomena of the Higgs field and dark energy.
episodes.summaryAI: The nature of empty space has preoccupied thinkers since antiquity. Thales proposed water as the fundamental substrate, while Aristotle denied the void's possibility. Newton invoked a luminiferous ether pervading space, which Einstein's relativity eliminated by recasting emptiness as dynamic space-time curved by mass and energy. Quantum field theory revealed that vacuum is far from nothing: it seethes with virtual particle pairs, zero-point energy fluctuations, and field interactions. The Higgs field gives particles mass throughout supposedly empty space, and dark energy driving cosmic acceleration suggests the vacuum itself contains enormous latent energy whose origin remains unexplained.

episodes.title: The Valladolid Debate
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the debate in Valladolid, Spain in 1550, over Spanish rights to enslave the native peoples in the newly conquered lands. Bartolomé de Las Casas (pictured above), the Bishop of Chiapas, Mexico, was trying to end the encomienda system in which those who now owned the land could also take the people in forced labour. Juan Gines Sepulveda, a philosopher, argued for the colonists' property rights over people, asserting that some native Americans were 'natural slaves' as defined by Aristotle. Valladolid became seen as the first open attempt by European colonists to discuss the ethics of slavery, and Las Casas became known as 'Saviour of the Indians' and an advocate for human rights, although for some time he argued that African slaves be imported to do the work in place of the native people, before repenting.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the debate in Valladolid, Spain in 1550, over Spanish rights to enslave the native peoples in the newly conquered lands. Bartolomé de Las Casas (pictured above), the Bishop of Chiapas, Mexico, was trying to end the encomienda system in which those who now owned the land could also take the people in forced labour. Juan Gines Sepulveda, a philosopher, argued for the colonists' property rights over people, asserting that some native Americans were 'natural slaves' as defined by Aristotle. Valladolid became seen as the first open attempt by European colonists to discuss the ethics of slavery, and Las Casas became known as 'Saviour of the Indians' and an advocate for human rights, although for some time he argued that African slaves be imported to do the work in place of the native people, before repenting.
episodes.summaryAI: In 1550, the Spanish crown convened a formal debate at Valladolid over the legitimacy of conquering and enslaving indigenous peoples of the Americas. Dominican friar Bartolome de las Casas argued that Native Americans possessed full rational souls and natural rights, opposing the Aristotelian position of Juan Gines de Sepulveda that indigenous peoples were natural slaves suited to subjugation. This was the first open attempt by a European colonial power to examine the moral foundations of conquest and slavery. Though no formal verdict was issued, Las Casas's arguments advanced humanitarian principles and earned him the title Saviour of the Indians.

episodes.title: The Venerable Bede
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Venerable Bede. In 731 AD, in the most far-flung corner of the known universe, a book was written that represented a height of scholarship and erudition that was not to be equalled for centuries to come. It was called the Ecclesiastical History of the Angle Peoples and its author was Bede. A long way from Rome, in a monastery at Jarrow in the North East of England, his works cast a light across the whole of Western Civilisation and Bede became a bestseller, an internationally renowned scholar and eventually a saint. His Ecclesiastical History has been in copy or in print ever since it was written in the eighth century and his edition of the Bible remains the Catholic Church's most authoritative Latin version to this day.How did Bede achieve such ascendancy from such an obscure part of Christendom? And what was so remarkable about his work?With Richard Gameson, Reader in Medieval History at the University of Kent at Canterbury; Sarah Foot, Professor of Early Medieval History at the University of Sheffield; Michelle Brown, a manuscript specialist from the British Library.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Venerable Bede. In 731 AD, in the most far-flung corner of the known universe, a book was written that represented a height of scholarship and erudition that was not to be equalled for centuries to come. It was called the Ecclesiastical History of the Angle Peoples and its author was Bede. A long way from Rome, in a monastery at Jarrow in the North East of England, his works cast a light across the whole of Western Civilisation and Bede became a bestseller, an internationally renowned scholar and eventually a saint. His Ecclesiastical History has been in copy or in print ever since it was written in the eighth century and his edition of the Bible remains the Catholic Church's most authoritative Latin version to this day.How did Bede achieve such ascendancy from such an obscure part of Christendom? And what was so remarkable about his work?With Richard Gameson, Reader in Medieval History at the University of Kent at Canterbury; Sarah Foot, Professor of Early Medieval History at the University of Sheffield; Michelle Brown, a manuscript specialist from the British Library.
episodes.summaryAI: The Venerable Bede, writing in 731 AD from the monastery at Jarrow in Northumbria, produced the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, a landmark of early medieval scholarship that established him as the father of English history. The work combined rigorous source criticism with theological narrative, becoming an international bestseller copied across European monasteries. Bede also made major contributions to chronology, popularising the Anno Domini dating system. His edition of the Latin Bible remained the Catholic Church's most authoritative version for centuries. Canonised as a saint and declared Doctor of the Church, Bede exemplifies monastic intellectual achievement.

episodes.title: The Venetian Empire
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the remarkable rise of Venice in the eastern Mediterranean. Unlike other Italian cities of the early medieval period, Venice had not been settled during the Roman Empire. Rather, it was a refuge for those fleeing unrest after the fall of Rome who settled on these boggy islands on a lagoon and developed into a power that ran an empire from mainland Italy, down the Adriatic coast, across the Peloponnese to Crete and Cyprus, past Constantinople and into the Black Sea. This was a city without walls, just one of the surprises for visitors who marvelled at the stability and influence of Venice right up to the 17th Century when the Ottomans, Spain, France and the Hapsburgs were to prove too much especially with trade shifting to the Atlantic.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the remarkable rise of Venice in the eastern Mediterranean. Unlike other Italian cities of the early medieval period, Venice had not been settled during the Roman Empire. Rather, it was a refuge for those fleeing unrest after the fall of Rome who settled on these boggy islands on a lagoon and developed into a power that ran an empire from mainland Italy, down the Adriatic coast, across the Peloponnese to Crete and Cyprus, past Constantinople and into the Black Sea. This was a city without walls, just one of the surprises for visitors who marvelled at the stability and influence of Venice right up to the 17th Century when the Ottomans, Spain, France and the Hapsburgs were to prove too much especially with trade shifting to the Atlantic.
episodes.summaryAI: Venice was founded by refugees fleeing barbarian invasions after Rome's fall, settling on marshy islands in a defensible lagoon. From this improbable base, the Republic developed into a major maritime power controlling trade routes across the eastern Mediterranean. Its empire stretched from northeastern Italy through Dalmatia to Crete, Cyprus, and strategic outposts beyond Constantinople. Uniquely among Italian states, Venice had no defensive walls, protected instead by water. Its republican government provided remarkable political stability for over a millennium, sustaining commercial dominance and cultural influence until the seventeenth century when Atlantic trade routes diminished Mediterranean primacy.

episodes.title: The Vienna Secession
episodes.synopsis: In 1897, Gustav Klimt led a group of radical artists to break free from the cultural establishment of Vienna and found a movement that became known as the Vienna Secession.
episodes.description: In 1897, Gustav Klimt led a group of radical artists to break free from the cultural establishment of Vienna and found a movement that became known as the Vienna Secession.
episodes.summaryAI: In 1897, Gustav Klimt and fellow progressive artists broke from Vienna's conservative Kunstlerhaus to form the Vienna Secession, rejecting academic convention and historicist tradition. The movement embraced artistic freedom across painting, architecture, and decorative arts, declaring no distinction between fine and applied art. Their exhibition hall, designed by Joseph Maria Olbrich with its golden dome, became an architectural manifesto. Klimt's decorative, symbolist paintings, alongside work by Koloman Moser, Josef Hoffmann, and Egon Schiele, redefined Viennese visual culture. The Secession catalysed broader Jugendstil and early modernist movements, reshaping European art and design at the turn of the century.

episodes.title: The Volga Vikings
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Volga Vikings. Between the 8th and the 10th centuries AD, fierce Scandinavian warriors raided and then settled large swathes of Europe, particularly Britain, Ireland and parts of northern France. These were the Vikings, and their story is well known today. Far fewer people realise that groups of Norsemen also travelled east.These Volga Vikings, also known as the Rus, crossed the Baltic into present-day Russia and the Ukraine and founded settlements there. They traded commodities including furs and slaves for Islamic silver, and penetrated so far east as to reach Baghdad. Their activities were documented by Arab scholars: one, Ahmad ibn Fadlan, recorded that the Volga Vikings he met were perfect physical specimens but also "the filthiest of God's creatures". Through trade and culture they brought West and East into regular contact; their story sheds light on both Scandinavian and early Islamic history
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the Volga Vikings. Between the 8th and the 10th centuries AD, fierce Scandinavian warriors raided and then settled large swathes of Europe, particularly Britain, Ireland and parts of northern France. These were the Vikings, and their story is well known today. Far fewer people realise that groups of Norsemen also travelled east.These Volga Vikings, also known as the Rus, crossed the Baltic into present-day Russia and the Ukraine and founded settlements there. They traded commodities including furs and slaves for Islamic silver, and penetrated so far east as to reach Baghdad. Their activities were documented by Arab scholars: one, Ahmad ibn Fadlan, recorded that the Volga Vikings he met were perfect physical specimens but also "the filthiest of God's creatures". Through trade and culture they brought West and East into regular contact; their story sheds light on both Scandinavian and early Islamic history
episodes.summaryAI: Between the eighth and tenth centuries, Norse traders and raiders expanded eastward across the Baltic into the river systems of Russia and Ukraine, establishing routes reaching Constantinople and Baghdad. Known as Rus, these Scandinavians traded furs, amber, honey, and slaves for vast quantities of Islamic silver dirhams, thousands of which have been excavated from Scandinavian hoards. The Arab diplomat Ahmad ibn Fadlan recorded detailed observations of their customs, including ship burials along the Volga. These eastern Vikings founded trading settlements that became the nuclei of Kievan Rus, forging commercial and cultural connections between Northern Europe, Byzantium, and the Islamic world.

episodes.title: The Waltz
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the dance which, from when it reached Britain in the early nineteenth century, revolutionised the relationship between music, literature and people here for the next hundred years. While it may seem formal now, it was the informality and daring that drove its popularity, with couples holding each other as they spun round a room to new lighter music popularised by Johann Strauss, father and son, such as The Blue Danube. Soon the Waltz expanded the creative world in poetry, ballet, novellas and music, from the Ballets Russes of Diaghilev to Moon River and Are You Lonesome Tonight.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the dance which, from when it reached Britain in the early nineteenth century, revolutionised the relationship between music, literature and people here for the next hundred years. While it may seem formal now, it was the informality and daring that drove its popularity, with couples holding each other as they spun round a room to new lighter music popularised by Johann Strauss, father and son, such as The Blue Danube. Soon the Waltz expanded the creative world in poetry, ballet, novellas and music, from the Ballets Russes of Diaghilev to Moon River and Are You Lonesome Tonight.
episodes.summaryAI: The waltz emerged in the early nineteenth century as a transformative force linking music, social behavior, and literary culture. Its intimate hold between partners scandalized contemporary society while its spinning three-quarter time captivated audiences across Europe. Johann Strauss became its defining composer. The dance's influence radiated outward into poetry, ballet, and novellas, reshaping artistic expression well beyond the ballroom. From Diaghilev's modernist reinterpretations to Henry Mancini's Moon River, the waltz demonstrated how a single dance form could permeate and reshape Western cultural life across two centuries.

episodes.title: The War of 1812
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the War of 1812, the conflict between America and the British Empire sometimes referred to as the second American War of Independence. In June 1812, President James Madison declared war on Britain, angered by the restrictions Britain had imposed on American trade, the Royal Navy's capture of American sailors and British support for Native Americans. After three years of largely inconclusive fighting, the conflict finally came to an end with the Treaty of Ghent which, among other things, helped to hasten the abolition of the global slave trade.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the War of 1812, the conflict between America and the British Empire sometimes referred to as the second American War of Independence. In June 1812, President James Madison declared war on Britain, angered by the restrictions Britain had imposed on American trade, the Royal Navy's capture of American sailors and British support for Native Americans. After three years of largely inconclusive fighting, the conflict finally came to an end with the Treaty of Ghent which, among other things, helped to hasten the abolition of the global slave trade.
episodes.summaryAI: The War of 1812 pitted the young United States against the British Empire in a conflict sometimes called the second American War of Independence. President Madison declared war over British restrictions on American trade, the impressment of sailors into the Royal Navy, and British support for Native American resistance on the frontier. Three years of fighting proved largely inconclusive, with neither side achieving decisive victory. The Treaty of Ghent ended hostilities and carried broader consequences, contributing to diplomatic efforts that advanced the abolition of the transatlantic slave trade.

episodes.title: The Wars of the Roses
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Wars of the Roses which have been the scene for many a historical skirmish over the ages: The period in the fifteenth century when the House of Lancaster and the House of York were continually at odds is described by Shakespeare, in the three parts of Henry VI and Richard III as a time of enormous moral, military and political turmoil - the quintessential civil war; but twentieth century historians like K.B. Macfarlane argued the political instability is wildly overstated and there were no Wars of the Roses at all. Opposing this position are the many Tudor historians who like to claim that the Wars of the Roses represent the final breakdown of the feudal system and lead directly to the Tudor Era and the birth of the modern age.With Dr Helen Castor, Fellow and Director of Studies in History, Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge; Professor Colin Richmond, Emeritus Professor of History, Keele University; Dr Steven Gunn is a Tudor historian and Fellow and Tutor in Modern History, Merton College, Oxford.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Wars of the Roses which have been the scene for many a historical skirmish over the ages: The period in the fifteenth century when the House of Lancaster and the House of York were continually at odds is described by Shakespeare, in the three parts of Henry VI and Richard III as a time of enormous moral, military and political turmoil - the quintessential civil war; but twentieth century historians like K.B. Macfarlane argued the political instability is wildly overstated and there were no Wars of the Roses at all. Opposing this position are the many Tudor historians who like to claim that the Wars of the Roses represent the final breakdown of the feudal system and lead directly to the Tudor Era and the birth of the modern age.With Dr Helen Castor, Fellow and Director of Studies in History, Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge; Professor Colin Richmond, Emeritus Professor of History, Keele University; Dr Steven Gunn is a Tudor historian and Fellow and Tutor in Modern History, Merton College, Oxford.
episodes.summaryAI: The Wars of the Roses consumed fifteenth-century England as the houses of Lancaster and York contested the throne. Shakespeare dramatized the period as one of enormous turmoil and bloodshed, cementing its reputation in popular imagination. Historians disagree sharply about the conflict's nature and significance. Alan Macfarlane argued there were effectively no Wars of the Roses as traditionally understood. Tudor-era historians interpreted the struggle as the final breakdown of the feudal system, a necessary upheaval clearing the path toward centralized monarchy and the emergence of early modern England.

episodes.title: The Waste Land and Modernity
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss TS Eliot's seminal poem The Waste Land.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss TS Eliot's seminal poem The Waste Land.
episodes.summaryAI: T.S. Eliot's The Waste Land, published in 1922, became one of the most influential poems of the twentieth century and a defining expression of literary modernism. Its fragmented structure, layered allusions spanning multiple languages and traditions, and bleak depiction of postwar spiritual desolation broke fundamentally with Victorian poetic conventions. The poem captured a civilization questioning its own foundations after the catastrophe of the First World War. Its innovative techniques including collage, shifting voices, and dense intertextuality established new possibilities for poetry and profoundly shaped how subsequent writers engaged with modern experience.

episodes.title: The Wealth of Nations
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Adam Smith's celebrated economic treatise The Wealth of Nations. Smith was one of Scotland's greatest thinkers, a moral philosopher and pioneer of economic theory whose 1776 masterpiece has come to define classical economics. Based on his careful consideration of the transformation wrought on the British economy by the Industrial Revolution, and how it contrasted with marketplaces elsewhere in the world, the book outlined a theory of wealth and how it is accumulated that has arguably had more influence on economic theory than any other.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Adam Smith's celebrated economic treatise The Wealth of Nations. Smith was one of Scotland's greatest thinkers, a moral philosopher and pioneer of economic theory whose 1776 masterpiece has come to define classical economics. Based on his careful consideration of the transformation wrought on the British economy by the Industrial Revolution, and how it contrasted with marketplaces elsewhere in the world, the book outlined a theory of wealth and how it is accumulated that has arguably had more influence on economic theory than any other.
episodes.summaryAI: Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations, published in 1776, established the foundations of classical economics and remains arguably the most influential work of economic theory ever written. Smith composed it amid the transformations of the early Industrial Revolution, grounding his analysis in observable economic change. The work advanced a comprehensive theory of wealth accumulation, division of labor, free markets, and the mechanisms by which nations prosper. Its arguments for limited government intervention and the productive power of self-interest shaped economic policy and debate for centuries across the industrializing world.

episodes.title: The Whale - A History
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests Steve Jones, Bill Amos and Eleanor Weston discuss the evolutionary history of the whale. The ancestor of all whales alive today was a small, land-based mammal with cloven hoofs, perhaps like a pig or a big mole. How this creature developed into the celebrated leviathan of the deep is one of the more extraordinary stories in the canon of evolution. The whale has undergone vast changes in size, has moved from land to water, lost its legs and developed specialised features such as filter feeding and echo location. How it achieved this is an exemplar of how evolution works and how natural selection can impose extreme changes on the body shape and abilities of living things. How the story of the whales was pieced together also reveals the various forms of evidence - from fossils to molecules - that we now use to understand the ancestry of life on Earth.Steve Jones is Professor of Genetics at University College London; Eleanor Weston is a mammalian palaeontologist at the Natural History Museum, London; Bill Amos is Professor of Evolutionary Genetics at Cambridge University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests Steve Jones, Bill Amos and Eleanor Weston discuss the evolutionary history of the whale. The ancestor of all whales alive today was a small, land-based mammal with cloven hoofs, perhaps like a pig or a big mole. How this creature developed into the celebrated leviathan of the deep is one of the more extraordinary stories in the canon of evolution. The whale has undergone vast changes in size, has moved from land to water, lost its legs and developed specialised features such as filter feeding and echo location. How it achieved this is an exemplar of how evolution works and how natural selection can impose extreme changes on the body shape and abilities of living things. How the story of the whales was pieced together also reveals the various forms of evidence - from fossils to molecules - that we now use to understand the ancestry of life on Earth.Steve Jones is Professor of Genetics at University College London; Eleanor Weston is a mammalian palaeontologist at the Natural History Museum, London; Bill Amos is Professor of Evolutionary Genetics at Cambridge University.
episodes.summaryAI: Whales descend from a small land mammal with cloven hoofs that transitioned from terrestrial to fully aquatic life over millions of years. This evolutionary journey involved losing hind limbs, developing streamlined bodies, and acquiring remarkable adaptations including filter feeding in baleen whales and echolocation in toothed species. The whale lineage serves as a powerful exemplar of how evolution operates through gradual modification. Evidence drawn from fossils documenting intermediate stages and molecular analysis confirming genetic relationships together illuminate one of the most dramatic transformations in vertebrate evolutionary history.

episodes.title: The Zong Massacre
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the notorious events off Jamaica in 1781 and their background. The British slave ship Zong, having sailed across the Atlantic towards Jamaica, threw 132 enslaved Africans from its human cargo into the sea to drown. Even for a slave ship, the Zong was overcrowded; those murdered were worth more to the ship dead than alive. The crew said there was not enough drinking water to go round and they had no choice, which meant they could claim for the deaths on insurance. The main reason we know of this atrocity now is that the owners took their claim to court in London, and the insurers were at first told to pay up as if the dead slaves were any other lost goods, not people. Abolitionists in Britain were scandalised: if courts treated mass murder in the slave trade as just another business transaction and not a moral wrong, the souls of the nation would be damned. But nobody was ever prosecuted.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the notorious events off Jamaica in 1781 and their background. The British slave ship Zong, having sailed across the Atlantic towards Jamaica, threw 132 enslaved Africans from its human cargo into the sea to drown. Even for a slave ship, the Zong was overcrowded; those murdered were worth more to the ship dead than alive. The crew said there was not enough drinking water to go round and they had no choice, which meant they could claim for the deaths on insurance. The main reason we know of this atrocity now is that the owners took their claim to court in London, and the insurers were at first told to pay up as if the dead slaves were any other lost goods, not people. Abolitionists in Britain were scandalised: if courts treated mass murder in the slave trade as just another business transaction and not a moral wrong, the souls of the nation would be damned. But nobody was ever prosecuted.
episodes.summaryAI: In 1781 the crew of the British slave ship Zong threw 132 enslaved Africans overboard to drown in the Atlantic. The ship's owners calculated that enslaved people were worth more dead than alive through insurance claims for lost cargo. When the case reached court, judges initially ruled the insurers must pay as though the victims were lost goods rather than murdered human beings. No one was ever prosecuted for the killings. The case scandalized abolitionists and became a catalyst in the growing movement to end the British slave trade.

episodes.title: The Zulu Nation's Rise and Fall
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the rise and fall of the Zulu Nation.At the beginning of the 19th century, the Zulus were a small pastoral community of a bare few thousand people in the eastern part of what is now South Africa. Their territory was limited to about ten square miles.But within a decade, led by their warrior king, Shaka, they had managed to carve out an empire with a population of many tens of thousands.Shaka was a skilled politician, successfully co-opting many neighbouring peoples into his kingdom as his conquests advanced its borders.He remains best known as a world-class military strategist, who deployed new weapons, and a devastatingly effective technique of encircling enemy troops.But the ground for the Zulus' breathtaking expansion was shaped in part by the destabilising advance of European settlers.It eventually brought the Zulu into confrontation both with the Afrikaners, as at the Battle of Blood River in 1838, and with the British.In the mid-19th century, the Zulu and the British achieved a sustained period of peaceful co-existence.But, especially after the discovery of diamonds began to transform the southern African economy, British priorities changed, and they began to push for a single confederation of the various provinces and colonies.Zululand's independence became an obstacle, and in 1879 the British invaded.On 22 January 1879, the Zulu were unable to overrun a tiny garrison of invaders at Rorke's Drift.Yet on the same day, at the Battle of Isandhlwana, they inflicted a shocking defeat on the well-armed forces of the British Empire - all the more impressive given that the Zulu soldiers were predominantly armed with spears.Nonetheless, the British invasion of Zululand was ultimately successful, and precipitated first annexation, then the kingdom's absorption into the province of Natal (today, KwaZulu-Natal).During their heyday and in the wake of their decline alike, the Zulu became the subject of much myth-making.To the British, the 'Black Napoleon' figure of Shaka, and the vivid image of a proud warrior race, made the Zulu an object of admiration, fear, and appalled fascination, even as the Army moved to subjugate them.And in the decades since the demise of their independent kingdom, the triumphs of the 19th century long remained an important element of the Zulus' collective self-image

episodes.summaryAI: In the early nineteenth century the Zulus numbered only a few thousand before Shaka transformed them into a formidable empire. A brilliant military strategist, Shaka introduced new weapons and the encircling tactical formation that overwhelmed neighboring peoples. Zulu expansion brought inevitable confrontation with both Afrikaner settlers and the British Empire. The 1879 Battle of Isandhlwana, where Zulu forces annihilated a British column, shocked Victorian Britain and became one of the most famous defeats in imperial military history. The Zulu kingdom was ultimately absorbed into the British colony of Natal.

episodes.title: Thebes
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the myths and history of the ancient Greek city of Thebes and its depiction in Athenian drama. In myths it was said to be home to Heracles, Dionysus, Oedipus and Cadmus among others and, in history, was infamous for supporting Xerxes in the Persian War. Its prominence led to a struggle with the rising force of Macedon in which the Thebans were defeated at Chaironea in 338 BC, one of the most important battles in ancient history. The position of Thebes in Greek culture was enormously powerful. The strength of its myths and its proximity to Athens made it a source of stories for the Athenian theatre, and is the setting for more of the surviving plays than any other location.

episodes.summaryAI: Thebes was an ancient Greek city of extraordinary cultural and mythological significance. It claimed association with Heracles, Dionysus, Oedipus, and the legendary founder Cadmus. The city gained infamy for supporting the Persian king Xerxes during his invasion of Greece. Thebes suffered decisive defeat at Chaironea in 338 BC against Philip of Macedon. Remarkably, Thebes serves as the setting for more surviving Greek plays than any other location, reflecting its enormous power within Greek literary imagination and demonstrating how mythological tradition could sustain a city's cultural importance far beyond its political fortunes.

episodes.title: Theories of Everything
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 30 year search to solve all the biggest questions in physics. At the end of the last century, brave voices were predicting that all the big questions of physics were on the verge of being answered by a Theory of Everything. The disparity between the physics of the very small would finally be reconciled with the very large, and the four forces of nature would finally be united with a single set of equations. It was suggested that with such a theory we might solve the riddle of black holes, unlock the secrets of the Big Bang, probe other universes and even uncover the mystery of travelling through time. But Stephen Hawking, who once said that with a Theory of Everything “we would know the mind of God”, has changed his mind and now says that it may not be possible after all. So what are the prospects for a Theory of Everything? Why do we need one? How do we get one? And what would it mean if we did? With Brian Greene, Professor of Physics and Mathematics at Columbia University and author of The Fabric of the Cosmos; John Barrow, Professor of Mathematical Sciences at the University of Cambridge and author of The Constants of Nature; Dr Val Gibson, particle physicist from the Cavendish Laboratory and Fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge.

episodes.summaryAI: The search for a theory of everything represents physics' most ambitious quest to answer its deepest questions. The central challenge involves reconciling quantum mechanics governing the very small with general relativity describing the very large, and unifying the four fundamental forces of nature into a single coherent framework. This pursuit engages with black holes, the Big Bang, the possibility of other universes, and time travel. Stephen Hawking initially believed such unification was achievable but later changed his position, suggesting a complete theory of everything might not be possible.

episodes.title: Thermopylae
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Battle of Thermopylae. For the historian Herodotus, the Battle of Thermopylae was the defining clash between East and West: “The Persians fell in their scores, for the officers stood behind lashing them forward, forward all the time. Many fell into the sea and were drowned, many more were trampled to death by their comrades ... The Greeks knew they were doomed now the Persians had discovered a way round the hill, and put forth their last ounce of strength, utterly desperate, utterly unsparing of their lives. (King) Leonidas fell in this battle. He had proved himself a great and brave man”.A force of three hundred free Spartans and their King had stood and fallen before an invading army of three million, led by a brutal tyrant. Or so the story goes – such was their courage and its association with freedom that, nearly two and a half thousand years later, William Golding wrote, “A little of Leonidas lies in the fact that I can go where I like and write what I like. He contributed to setting us free”.How important are the Greek/Persian wars to the story of democracy? Was the West and its values really so far removed from life in the Persian Empire?With Tom Holland, historian and author of Persian Fire; Simon Goldhill, Professor in Greek Literature and Culture at King’s College, Cambridge; Edith Hall, Leverhulme Professor of Greek Cultural History at the University of Durham and author of Inventing the Barbarian: Greek Self-Definition through Tragedy.

episodes.summaryAI: In 480 BC a small Greek force including three hundred Spartans under King Leonidas made a legendary stand at the narrow pass of Thermopylae against the vast invading Persian army. Herodotus framed the battle as a defining clash between East and West. The defenders fought to the death, and their sacrifice became a foundational story of Western civilization. William Golding argued that Leonidas contributed to setting humanity free. The battle raises enduring questions about how significant the Greek-Persian wars were to the development and survival of democratic governance.

episodes.title: Third Crusade
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the highs and lows of the Third Crusade. In 1095 Pope Urban II launched the First Crusade and by the end of the 11th century an army of Franks had driven what they called the ‘infidel arab’ out of Jerusalem. The Crusaders held the city for over eighty years until Saladin, Sultan of Egypt and Syria, seized it back in 1187. The Muslim world celebrated as the Christian world shuddered and Pope Gregory VIII issued a Papal Bull for restoring the Holy City to Christian Rule. The Kings of Europe clamoured for the honour to take up the challenge. However, the Third Crusade did not get off to a ripping start. The Holy Roman Emperor, Frederick Barbarossa, set off at the head of the greatest crusader army ever assembled but drowned whilst crossing a small stream in Armenia. This left Phillip of France and ultimately Richard of England to take on Saladin’s supremacy in the Middle East. What happened in that famous encounter? How did the names of Saladin and Richard the Lionheart come to bear such a weight of reputation across the centuries and were the crusades racial, imperial or religious wars? With Jonathan Riley-Smith, Dixie Professor of Ecclesiastical History at Cambridge University and author of many books on the Crusades, Carole Hillenbrand, Professor of Islamic History at the University of Edinburgh, Tariq Ali, novelist, playwright and author of The Book of Saladin.

episodes.summaryAI: When Saladin seized Jerusalem in 1187, Pope Gregory VIII issued a papal bull calling Christendom to reclaim the holy city, launching the Third Crusade. The campaign drew Europe's most powerful monarchs. Emperor Frederick Barbarossa drowned crossing a stream in Armenia before reaching the Holy Land. Philip of France and Richard the Lionheart of England pressed the fight against Saladin with mixed results. The crusade raises persistent questions about whether these expeditions should be understood primarily as racial conflicts, imperial ventures, or religious wars driven by genuine spiritual conviction.

episodes.title: Thomas Becket
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the man who was Henry II's Chancellor and then Archbishop of Canterbury and who was murdered by knights in Canterbury Cathedral (depicted by Matthew Paris, above). Henry believed that Becket owed him loyalty as he had raised him to the highest offices, and that he should agree to Henry's courts having jurisdiction over 'criminous clerics'. They fell out when Becket agreed to this jurisdiction verbally but would not put his seal on the agreement, the Constitutions of Clarendon. The rift deepened when Henry's heir was crowned without Becket, who excommunicated the bishops who took part. Becket's tomb became one of the main destinations for pilgrims for the next 400 years, including those in Chaucer's Canterbury Tales where he was the 'blisful martir'.

episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Becket served as Henry II's Chancellor before becoming Archbishop of Canterbury, where he transformed from royal ally into fierce defender of church independence. His refusal to seal the Constitutions of Clarendon, which sought to limit ecclesiastical authority, placed him in direct conflict with the king. In 1170 four knights murdered Becket inside Canterbury Cathedral. His tomb became England's principal pilgrimage destination for four hundred years, attracting devotees from across Europe and inspiring Geoffrey Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, ensuring Becket's martyrdom shaped English religious and literary culture for centuries.

episodes.title: Thomas Edison
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the innovations and influence of Thomas Edison, one of the architects of the modern age.Edison is popularly remembered as the man who made cheap electric light possible. Born in 1847, he began his career working in the new industry of telegraphy, and while still in his early twenties made major improvements to the technology of the telegraph. Not long afterwards he invented a new type of microphone which was used in telephones for almost a century. In the space of three productive years, Edison developed the phonograph and the first commercially viable light bulb and power distribution system. Many more inventions were to follow: he also played a part in the birth of cinema in the 1890s. When he died in 1931 he had patented no fewer than 1093 devices - the most prolific inventor in history. As the creator of the world's first industrial research laboratory he forever changed the way in which innovation took place

episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Edison, born in 1847, became one of history's most prolific inventors. He made major improvements to the telegraph and invented a new microphone design used for over a century. Edison developed the phonograph, created the first commercially viable incandescent light bulb, and designed electrical power distribution systems that transformed urban life. He played a significant role in the birth of cinema and accumulated 1,093 patents across his career. His establishment of the first industrial research laboratory at Menlo Park pioneered the model of systematic, team-based invention that defined modern technological development.

episodes.title: Thomas Hardy's Poetry
episodes.synopsis: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this second of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss one of his favourite poets.
episodes.description: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this second of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss one of his favourite poets.
episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Hardy is remembered primarily as a Victorian novelist, but his poetry constitutes a body of work of equal or greater literary significance. Writing across several decades into the twentieth century, Hardy brought to verse the same unflinching engagement with fate, loss, landscape, and human vulnerability that characterized his fiction. His poems combine plain diction with formally inventive structures, achieving emotional power through directness and precision rather than ornament. Hardy's poetic voice influenced subsequent generations of English poets who valued authenticity and emotional honesty over rhetorical grandeur in confronting mortality and memory.

episodes.title: Thomas Middleton
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most energetic, varied and innovative playwrights of his time. Thomas Middleton (1580-1627) worked across the London stages both alone and with others from Dekker and Rowley to Shakespeare and more. Middleton’s range included raucous city comedies such as A Chaste Maid in Cheapside and chilling revenge tragedies like The Changeling and The Revenger’s Tragedy, some with the main adult companies and some with child actors playing the scheming adults. Middleton seemed to be everywhere on the Jacobean stage, mixing warmth and cruelty amid laughter and horror, and even Macbeth’s witches may be substantially his work.

episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Middleton was an English playwright active from 1580 to 1627 whose range encompassed sharp city comedies and dark revenge tragedies. Works like A Chaste Maid in Cheapside satirized London's mercantile society with biting wit, while The Changeling and The Revenger's Tragedy explored corruption and moral disintegration. Middleton collaborated with Shakespeare, and scholarly analysis suggests the witches' scenes in Macbeth may be substantially his work. His dramatic output reveals a playwright of considerable versatility whose contributions to Jacobean theater are increasingly recognized as rivaling those of his more celebrated contemporaries.

episodes.title: Thomas Paine's Common Sense
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Thomas Paine and his pamphlet "Common Sense" which was published in Philadelphia in January 1776 and promoted the argument for American independence from Britain. Addressed to The Inhabitants of America, it sold one hundred and fifty thousand copies in the first few months and is said, proportionately, to be the best-selling book in American history. Paine had arrived from England barely a year before. He vigorously attacked monarchy generally and George the Third in particular. He argued the colonies should abandon all hope of resolving their dispute with Britain and declare independence immediately. Many Americans were scandalised. More were inspired and, for Paine's vision of America's independent future, he has been called a Founding Father of the United States.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Thomas Paine and his pamphlet "Common Sense" which was published in Philadelphia in January 1776 and promoted the argument for American independence from Britain. Addressed to The Inhabitants of America, it sold one hundred and fifty thousand copies in the first few months and is said, proportionately, to be the best-selling book in American history. episodes.summaryAI: Thomas Paine's Common Sense, published in Philadelphia in January 1776, became the most influential pamphlet promoting American independence from Britain. It sold 150,000 copies within its first months, making it proportionately the best-selling book in American history at that time. Paine attacked the institution of monarchy directly and denounced George III, translating complex political philosophy into accessible prose that ordinary colonists could embrace. The pamphlet galvanized public opinion decisively toward revolution and earned Paine recognition as a Founding Father of the United States.

episodes.title: Thoreau and the American Idyll
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 19th century American writer and philosopher, Henry David Thoreau. Anti-slavery activist and passionate environmentalist, Thoreau was above all a champion of self-reliance and individualism. He was also a champion of the simple life, a lover of nature and an enemy of the modern who lived alone in a log cabin in the woods away from society. In his seminal work, Walden, published in 1854, he wrote: “I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life and not, when I came to die, discover that I had not lived. I wanted to live deep and suck out all the marrow of life.” Thoreau has become emblematic of one version of American values and his work has been an inspiration to politicians and writers alike, from Martin Luther King to Gandhi, Yeats and Tolstoy. Yet in many ways Thoreau remains a mystery, a man of contradictions who advocated self-sufficiency but was happy to let his mother do his washing and cook his meals.With Kathleen Burk, Professor of American History at University College London; Tim Morris, Lecturer in American Literature at the University of Dundee; Stephen Fender, Honorary Professor in English Literature at University College London.

I wanted to live deep and suck out all the marrow of life.” Thoreau has become emblematic of one version of American values and his work has been an inspiration to politicians and writers alike, from Martin Luther King to Gandhi, Yeats and Tolstoy. Yet in many ways Thoreau remains a mystery, a man of contradictions who advocated self-sufficiency but was happy to let his mother do his washing and cook his meals.With Kathleen Burk, Professor of American History at University College London; Tim Morris, Lecturer in American Literature at the University of Dundee; Stephen Fender, Honorary Professor in English Literature at University College London.
episodes.summaryAI: Henry David Thoreau was a nineteenth-century American writer and philosopher whose ideas encompassed anti-slavery activism, environmentalism, and fierce advocacy for self-reliance and individualism. He famously lived alone in a log cabin at Walden Pond, producing his masterwork Walden in 1854 with its declaration that he went to the woods to live deliberately. His writings on civil disobedience inspired Martin Luther King Jr., Mahatma Gandhi, W.B. Yeats, and Leo Tolstoy. Yet Thoreau's image of radical self-sufficiency was complicated by practical realities, including having his mother do his washing.

episodes.title: Thucydides
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the ancient Greek historian Thucydides. In the fifth century BC Thucydides wrote The History of the Peloponnesian War, an account of a conflict in which he had himself taken part. This work is now seen as one of the first great masterpieces of history writing, a book which influenced writers for centuries afterwards. Thucydides was arguably the first historian to make a conscious attempt to be objective, bringing a rational and impartial approach to his scholarship. Today his work is still widely studied at military colleges and in the field of international relations for the insight it brings to bear on complex political situations.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the ancient Greek historian Thucydides. episodes.summaryAI: Thucydides was a fifth-century BC Greek historian whose History of the Peloponnesian War stands as the first great masterpiece of historical writing. He was the first historian to attempt systematic objectivity, rejecting mythological explanation in favor of rational analysis of political and military events. His account of the conflict between Athens and Sparta established methods and standards that shaped the discipline of history itself. Thucydides remains studied at military colleges and in international relations programs, his insights into power, strategy, and human nature retaining practical relevance across twenty-five centuries.

episodes.title: Tiberius
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman emperor Tiberius. When he was born in 42BC, there was little prospect of him ever becoming Emperor of Rome. Firstly, Rome was still a Republic and there had not yet been any Emperor so that had to change and, secondly, when his stepfather Augustus became Emperor there was no precedent for who should succeed him, if anyone. It somehow fell to Tiberius to develop this Roman imperial project and by some accounts he did this well, while to others his reign was marked by cruelty and paranoia inviting comparison with Nero.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman emperor Tiberius. When he was born in 42BC, there was little prospect of him ever becoming Emperor of Rome. Firstly, Rome was still a Republic and there had not yet been any Emperor so that had to change and, secondly, when his stepfather Augustus became Emperor there was no precedent for who should succeed him, if anyone. It somehow fell to Tiberius to develop this Roman imperial project and by some accounts he did this well, while to others his reign was marked by cruelty and paranoia inviting comparison with Nero.
episodes.summaryAI: Tiberius, born in 42 BC, had little early prospect of ruling Rome, which still maintained republican forms of government. When his stepfather Augustus became the first Emperor, there existed no precedent or mechanism for imperial succession. Tiberius ultimately inherited power and developed the Roman imperial project, consolidating institutions of governance that would endure for centuries. Historical assessments diverge sharply on his character and reign. Some accounts credit him with competent administration and military prudence, while others portray him as cruel and paranoid, comparable to later tyrants like Nero.

episodes.title: Time
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of mankind’s attempt to understand the nature of time. At the end of the 19th century, H.G.Wells imagined travelling through time in The Time Machine; “The palpitation of night and day merged into one continuous greyness; the sky took on a wonderful deepness of blue, a splendid luminous colour like that of early twilight; the jerking sun became a streak of fire, a brilliant arch in space”. When he was writing we thought time was unbending and universal and counted out by Newton’s clock. A hundred years later we have had Einstein and relativity, quantum theory, and atomic clocks, but in the third millennium, is mankind any closer to understanding what time really is? What, in short, do we know about time itself? A Greek philosopher thought that time was a figment of the imagination and there are contemporary physicists who go a long way to agreeing with him. Newton’s views on time were bent by Einstein. The ancient skills of astronomy once ruled the known world and skill in time usage could be said to be enthroned as a master craft in our day. “But at my back I always hear time’s winged chariot hurrying near and yonder all before us lie deserts of vast eternity” - Marvel wrote that of love, but it could be our epigraph for time. With Dr Neil Johnson, theoretical physicist at the Clarendon Laboratory, Oxford University and Royal Institution Christmas Lecturer 1999 on the subject of Time; Lee Smolin, cosmologist and Professor of Physics, Pennsylvania State University.

At the end of the 19th century, H.G.Wells imagined travelling through time in The Time Machine; “The palpitation of night and day merged into one continuous greyness; the sky took on a wonderful deepness of blue, a splendid luminous colour like that of early twilight; the jerking sun became a streak of fire, a brilliant arch in space”. episodes.summaryAI: Time operates as both a measurable physical quantity and a profound philosophical puzzle. Newton treated it as absolute and universal, while Einstein's relativity revealed it as flexible, shaped by gravity and velocity. Atomic clocks now measure time with extraordinary precision, yet fundamental questions persist. Ancient Greek thinkers like Parmenides questioned whether time truly exists or is merely a construct of human perception. The gap between physics' increasingly precise measurements and philosophy's unresolved questions about time's nature remains one of the deepest problems in human understanding.

episodes.title: Titus Oates and his 'Popish Plot'
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Titus Oates (1649-1705) who, with Israel Tonge, spread rumours of a Catholic plot to assassinate Charles II. From 1678, they went to great lengths to support their scheme, forging evidence and identifying the supposed conspirators. Fearing a second Gunpowder Plot, Oates' supposed revelations caused uproar in London and across the British Isles, with many Catholics, particularly Jesuit priests, wrongly implicated by Oates and then executed. Anyone who doubted him had to keep quiet, to avoid being suspected a sympathiser and thrown in prison. Oates was eventually exposed, put on trial under James II and sentenced by Judge Jeffreys to public whipping through the streets of London, but the question remained: why was this rogue, who had faced perjury charges before, ever believed?

episodes.summaryAI: Titus Oates fabricated allegations in 1678 of a Catholic conspiracy to assassinate King Charles II and place his Catholic brother James on the throne. Despite thin evidence and Oates's dubious character, anti-Catholic hysteria gripping Restoration England gave his claims traction. Forged documents and perjured testimony led to the wrongful execution of numerous Catholics, particularly Jesuits. The crisis exposed how political faction, religious prejudice, and public fear could override judicial standards. Oates was eventually discredited and punished, but the episode deepened Protestant-Catholic divisions shaping English constitutional politics for generations.

episodes.title: Tocqueville: Democracy in America
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Alexis de Tocqueville (1805-1859) and his examination of the American democratic system. He wrote De La Démocratie en Amérique in two parts, published in 1835 and 1840, when France was ruled by the July Monarchy of Louis-Philippe. Tocqueville was interested in how aspects of American democracy, in the age of President Andrew Jackson, could be applied to Europe as it moved away from rule by monarchs and aristocrats. His work has been revisited by politicians ever since, particularly in America, with its analysis of the strengths and weaknesses of direct democracy and its warnings of mediocrity and the tyranny of the majority.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Alexis de Tocqueville (1805-1859) and his examination of the American democratic system. He wrote De La Démocratie en Amérique in two parts, published in 1835 and 1840, when France was ruled by the July Monarchy of Louis-Philippe. Tocqueville was interested in how aspects of American democracy, in the age of President Andrew Jackson, could be applied to Europe as it moved away from rule by monarchs and aristocrats. His work has been revisited by politicians ever since, particularly in America, with its analysis of the strengths and weaknesses of direct democracy and its warnings of mediocrity and the tyranny of the majority.
episodes.summaryAI: Alexis de Tocqueville published Democracy in America in two volumes, 1835 and 1840, drawing on his travels through the young republic. He analyzed how equality of conditions shaped American institutions, civic associations, and political culture, identifying strengths such as local self-governance and voluntary association alongside dangers including tyranny of the majority, cultural mediocrity, and democratic despotism through centralized administration. Tocqueville asked whether European societies could adopt democratic principles without sacrificing liberty. His work remains a foundational text in political science, sociology, and comparative government.

episodes.title: Toleration
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas and politics behind the idea of religious toleration. In 1763 Voltaire remarked that "of all religions, the Christian is undoubtedly that which should instil the greatest toleration, although so far the Christians have been the most intolerant of all men". Christian intolerance was brutally enforced across Western Europe in the Middle Ages and the Reformation, with inquisitions, executions, church courts and brandings with hot irons. But during the English Civil War a variety of Christian sects sprang up which challenged the imposition of state religion and opened the floodgates to religious diversity.What were the politics and philosophy behind the idea of toleration in England? Did the rise of toleration go hand in hand with the rise of the secular, or were tolerationists – in fact – deeply religious? And how does toleration differ from tolerance?With Justin Champion, Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; David Wootton, Professor of Intellectual History at Queen Mary, University of London; Sarah Barber, Senior Lecturer in History at Lancaster University.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas and politics behind the idea of religious toleration. In 1763 Voltaire remarked that "of all religions, the Christian is undoubtedly that which should instil the greatest toleration, although so far the Christians have been the most intolerant of all men". Christian intolerance was brutally enforced across Western Europe in the Middle Ages and the Reformation, with inquisitions, executions, church courts and brandings with hot irons. But during the English Civil War a variety of Christian sects sprang up which challenged the imposition of state religion and opened the floodgates to religious diversity.What were the politics and philosophy behind the idea of toleration in England? And how does toleration differ from tolerance?With Justin Champion, Professor of the History of Early Modern Ideas at Royal Holloway, University of London; David Wootton, Professor of Intellectual History at Queen Mary, University of London; Sarah Barber, Senior Lecturer in History at Lancaster University.
episodes.summaryAI: Religious toleration emerged through centuries of persecution, inquisition, and enforced orthodoxy within Christianity. The English Civil War and its aftermath generated unprecedented religious diversity, forcing practical and philosophical reckoning with coexistence among competing faiths. Figures like John Locke argued for toleration on both pragmatic and principled grounds. Contrary to assumptions linking toleration with secularism, many early advocates were deeply devout, believing conscience could not be coerced. The debate illuminates tensions between state authority, individual belief, and the boundaries societies draw around acceptable difference in matters of faith.

episodes.title: Tolstoy
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and work of the 19th century Russian novelist Leo Tolstoy.The Russian novel has been acclaimed as one of the outstanding genres of literature alongside Greek Tragedy, Shakespeare’s Plays and Romantic Poetry. Its heyday was the mid-19th century, and its practitioners gave expression to the compelling moral and social questions of their day - and arguably of the modern era. These men of genius included Dostoevsky, Gogol and Turgenev, but perhaps the greatest of them all was Tolstoy, author of the novels War and Peace and Anna Karenina. Tolstoy took massive subjects and presented them in loving and intricate detail. As Matthew Arnold said, "a work by Tolstoy is not a piece of art but a piece of life". Possessed by an urgent desire to represent real life in his work, and to reject artifice, Tolstoy declared that "The one thing necessary in life as in art is to tell the truth." What did Tolstoy mean by telling the truth? How did he convey these truths to the reader? And why did he, ultimately, give up on literature and concentrate instead on religious and political philosophy? With A N Wilson, Novelist, journalist and biographer of Tolstoy; Catriona Kelly, Reader in Russian, Oxford University; Sarah Hudspith, Lecturer in Russian, University of Leeds.

episodes.summaryAI: Leo Tolstoy produced War and Peace and Anna Karenina, works celebrated for their unmatched verisimilitude and psychological depth. Matthew Arnold praised Tolstoy's writing as life itself rather than art. Tolstoy pursued an almost obsessive fidelity to lived experience, rejecting literary artifice in favor of moral and representational honesty. In later life he renounced fiction entirely, turning to philosophy, Christian anarchism, and social reform, convinced that art served no purpose unless it advanced moral truth. His trajectory raises enduring questions about the relationship between aesthetic achievement and ethical commitment.

episodes.title: Tragedy
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the ancient genre of tragedy and examines whether we have a psychological need for it, either as catharsis or Schadenfreude. You could be forgiven for thinking that in our century, of all centuries, the notion of the death of a tragedy would be comical. But there is a view that in its broad theatrical sense, tragedy, as defined by Aristotle and accepted to the time of Racine, has indeed lost its place and power as a form. Aristotle in his poetics held that tragedy figured men and women, often greater than ourselves, heroic, whose fall excited sensations of pity and fear which purged the emotions in the spectator, provoking a catharsis. And Chaucer defined it as a story “of hym that stood in greet prosperitee/And is yfallen from heigh degree/Into myserie, and endeth wretchedly”. Tragedy has been redefined many times and in many ages, but does it have a place in our own time? Or is the genre “dead for a ducat”. Not in life - the twentieth century is a monument to tragedy - but in art.With Professor George Steiner, critic, Extraordinary Fellow, Churchill College, Cambridge and author of The Death of Tragedy; Professor Catherine Belsey, Chair of the Centre for Critical and Cultural Theory, University of Cardiff and author of The Subject of Tragedy.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the ancient genre of tragedy and examines whether we have a psychological need for it, either as catharsis or Schadenfreude. You could be forgiven for thinking that in our century, of all centuries, the notion of the death of a tragedy would be comical. But there is a view that in its broad theatrical sense, tragedy, as defined by Aristotle and accepted to the time of Racine, has indeed lost its place and power as a form. Aristotle in his poetics held that tragedy figured men and women, often greater than ourselves, heroic, whose fall excited sensations of pity and fear which purged the emotions in the spectator, provoking a catharsis. And Chaucer defined it as a story “of hym that stood in greet prosperitee/And is yfallen from heigh degree/Into myserie, and endeth wretchedly”. episodes.summaryAI: Tragedy as a dramatic form originates with Aristotle's definition of protagonists greater than ordinary people whose downfall provokes pity and fear, achieving catharsis. Chaucer described it as a fall from high degree into misery. From Greek amphitheatres through Shakespeare to modern drama, tragedy explored human limitation against fate, moral law, and social forces. Critics have questioned whether genuine tragedy remains possible in democratic, secular societies lacking shared convictions about heroism and cosmic order. The form may have diminished in art, yet tragic experience persists in human affairs beyond any stage.

episodes.title: Tristan and Iseult
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Tristan and Iseult, one of the most popular stories of the Middle Ages. From roots in Celtic myth, it passed into written form in Britain a century after the Norman Conquest and almost immediately spread throughout northern Europe. It tells of a Cornish knight and an Irish queen, Tristan and Iseult, who accidentally drink a love potion, at the same time, on the same boat, travelling to Cornwall. She is due to marry Tristan's king, Mark. Tristan and Iseult seemed ideally matched and their love was heroic, but could that excuse their adultery, in the minds of medieval listeners, particularly when the Church was so clear they were wrong?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Tristan and Iseult, one of the most popular stories of the Middle Ages. From roots in Celtic myth, it passed into written form in Britain a century after the Norman Conquest and almost immediately spread throughout northern Europe.
