His reputation suffered terribly from his book 'Liber Amoris', a self-revealing memoir of his infatuation with his landlady's daughter. But in the Victorian and Edwardian eras, his importance was acknowledged by writers like Charles Dickens, Robert Louis Stevenson and Ford Madox Ford. In the 180 years since his death, his stature as perhaps the finest essayist in the language has grown and grown
episodes.summaryAI: William Hazlitt began as a philosopher before becoming the foremost English essayist. His early Essay on the Principles of Human Action argued that imagination enables humans to transcend narrow self-interest, a conviction underpinning all his later criticism. A committed republican who revered Napoleon as liberty's champion, he was simultaneously a radical who genuinely admired Edmund Burke's prose and intellect. His essays on literature, art, politics, and everyday life combined passionate conviction with psychological acuity, earning recognition as the finest essayist in the English language.

episodes.title: William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience'
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' by William James. The American novelist Henry James famously made London his home and himself more English than the English. In contrast, his psychologist brother, William, was deeply immersed in his American heritage. But in 1901, William came to Britain too. He had been invited to deliver a series of prestigious public lectures in Edinburgh. In them, he attempted a daringly original intellectual project. For the first time, here was a close-up examination of religion not as a body of beliefs, but as an intimate personal experience. When the lectures were printed, as 'The Varieties of Religious Experience', they were an instant success.They laid the ground for a whole new area of study - the psychology of religion - and influenced figures from the psychiatrist Carl Jung to the novelist Aldous Huxley. To date, James's book has been reprinted thirty-six times and has been hailed as one of the best non-fiction books of the twentieth century
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' by William James. The American novelist Henry James famously made London his home and himself more English than the English. In contrast, his psychologist brother, William, was deeply immersed in his American heritage. But in 1901, William came to Britain too. He had been invited to deliver a series of prestigious public lectures in Edinburgh. In them, he attempted a daringly original intellectual project. For the first time, here was a close-up examination of religion not as a body of beliefs, but as an intimate personal experience. When the lectures were printed, as 'The Varieties of Religious Experience', they were an instant success.They laid the ground for a whole new area of study - the psychology of religion - and influenced figures from the psychiatrist Carl Jung to the novelist Aldous Huxley. To date, James's book has been reprinted thirty-six times and has been hailed as one of the best non-fiction books of the twentieth century
episodes.summaryAI: William James delivered his Gifford Lectures at Edinburgh in 1901, published as The Varieties of Religious Experience. Rather than analyzing theology or institutional doctrine, James examined religion as personal psychological experience, studying conversion, mysticism, saintliness, and prayer through individual testimonies. This empirical approach founded the psychology of religion as a discipline. The work profoundly influenced Carl Jung's explorations of the unconscious and Aldous Huxley's investigations of altered states. Reprinted thirty-six times, it remains a landmark bridging philosophy, psychology, and spiritual inquiry.

episodes.title: William Morris
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas of William Morris, known in his lifetime for his poetry and then his contribution to the Arts and Crafts movement, and increasingly for his political activism. He felt the world had given in to drudgery and ugliness and he found inspiration in the time before industrialisation, in the medieval life which was about fellowship and association and ways of working which resisted the division of labour and allowed the worker to exercise his or her imagination. Seeing a disconnection between art and society, his solution was revolution which in his view was the only way to reset their relationship.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the ideas of William Morris, known in his lifetime for his poetry and then his contribution to the Arts and Crafts movement, and increasingly for his political activism. He felt the world had given in to drudgery and ugliness and he found inspiration in the time before industrialisation, in the medieval life which was about fellowship and association and ways of working which resisted the division of labour and allowed the worker to exercise his or her imagination. Seeing a disconnection between art and society, his solution was revolution which in his view was the only way to reset their relationship.
episodes.summaryAI: William Morris pursued poetry, decorative arts, and political revolution as interconnected responses to industrial capitalism's destruction of meaningful labor. Drawing inspiration from medieval craft traditions where workers exercised imagination and fellowship, he diagnosed a fundamental disconnection between art and society produced by mechanized production. His Arts and Crafts movement sought to reunite beauty with daily life through handmade textiles, wallpapers, furniture, and typography. Concluding that aesthetic reform alone was insufficient, Morris embraced revolutionary socialism as the only path to restoring creative dignity to ordinary work.

episodes.title: Witchcraft
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss witchcraft in Reformation Europe. In 1486 a book was published in Latin, it was called Maleus Mallificarum and it very soon outsold every publication in Europe bar the Bible. It was written by Heinrich Kramer, a Dominican Priest and a witchfinder. "Magicians, who are commonly called witches" he wrote, "are thus termed on account of the magnitude of their evil deeds. These are they who by the permission of God disturb the elements, who drive to distraction the minds of men, such as have lost their trust in God, and by the terrible power of their evil spells, without any actual draught or poison, kill human beings.""Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live" says Exodus, and in the period of the Reformation and after, over a hundred thousand men and women in Europe met their deaths after being convicted of witchcraft.Why did practices that had been tolerated for centuries suddenly become such a threat? What brought the prosecutions of witchcraft to an end, and was there anything ever in Europe that could be truly termed as a witch?With Alison Rowlands, Senior Lecturer in European History at the University of Essex; Lyndal Roper, Fellow and Tutor in History at Balliol College, University of Oxford; Malcolm Gaskill, Fellow and Director of Studies in History at Churchill College, Cambridge.

episodes.summaryAI: The Malleus Maleficarum, published in 1486, became the second most printed book after the Bible, systematizing witch persecution across Europe. Drawing on Exodus's injunction against witchcraft, ecclesiastical and secular authorities executed over 100,000 people, predominantly women. Longstanding folk practices involving healing, cursing, and divination had been tolerated for centuries before suddenly being recast as existential satanic threats. The central historical puzzle concerns why this transformation occurred when it did, and whether those accused ever practiced anything genuinely constituting witchcraft as their persecutors understood it.

episodes.title: Wittgenstein
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life, work and legacy of Ludwig Wittgenstein. There is little doubt that he was a towering figure of the twentieth century; on his return to Cambridge in 1929 Maynard Keynes wrote, “Well, God has arrived. I met him on the 5:15 train”.Wittgenstein is credited with being the greatest philosopher of the modern age, a thinker who left not one but two philosophies for his descendents to argue over: The early Wittgenstein said, “the limits of my mind mean the limits of my world”; the later Wittgenstein replied, “If God looked into our minds he would not have been able to see there whom we were speaking of”. Language was at the heart of both. Wittgenstein stated that his purpose was to finally free humanity from the pointless and neurotic philosophical questing that plagues us all. As he put it, “To show the fly the way out of the fly bottle”.How did he think language could solve all the problems of philosophy? How have his ideas influenced contemporary culture? And could his thought ever achieve the release for us that he hoped it would?With Ray Monk, Professor of Philosophy at the University of Southampton and author of Ludwig Wittgenstein: The Duty of Genius; Barry Smith, Lecturer in Philosophy at Birkbeck, University of London; Marie McGinn, Senior Lecturer in Philosophy at the University of York.

episodes.summaryAI: Ludwig Wittgenstein, whom Keynes announced as God arriving on the 5:15 train, produced two distinct philosophies that dominated twentieth-century thought. His early Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus argued that the limits of language mean the limits of one's world, mapping logic onto reality. His later Philosophical Investigations replaced this with language games, showing meaning arises from use within social practices rather than from correspondence with facts. Both phases shared a therapeutic purpose: freeing humanity from the neurotic philosophical questing generated by misunderstanding how language operates.

episodes.title: Women and Enlightenment Science
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the role played by women in Enlightenment science. During the eighteenth century the opportunities for women to gain a knowledge of science were minimal. Universities and other institutions devoted to research were the preserve of men. Yet many important contributions to the science of the Enlightenment were made by women. These ranged from major breakthroughs like those of the British astronomer Caroline Herschel, the first woman to discover a comet, to important translations of scientific literature such as Emilie du Chatelet's French version of Newton's Principia - and all social classes were involved, from the aristocratic amateur botanists to the women artisans who worked in London's workshops manufacturing scientific instruments. The image above, of Emilie du Chatelet, is attributed to Maurice Quentin de La Tour.

episodes.summaryAI: Eighteenth-century women faced severe barriers to scientific participation yet made remarkable contributions across disciplines. Caroline Herschel became the first woman credited with discovering a comet while assisting her brother William's astronomical observations. Emilie du Chatelet produced the definitive French translation of Newton's Principia Mathematica, adding important mathematical commentary. Beyond these celebrated figures, aristocratic women advanced botanical knowledge through collecting and classification, while women artisans crafted precision scientific instruments. Their diverse roles reveal how Enlightenment science depended on female labor spanning social classes.

episodes.title: Work in the 20th Century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the changing nature of work practices and the work ethic as it pertains at the end of the 20th century. Has our understanding of the nature and function of work really changed so radically since the beginning of the century? How can the past inform the future in the rapidly changing work environment? Has technology usurped craftsmanship, or is this no more than a superficial reading of an increasingly complex scene?With Professor Richard Sennett, visiting professor, London School for Economics and author of The Corrosion of Character - the Personal Consequences of Work in the New Capitalism; Theodore Zeldin, historian and Fellow of St Anthony’s College, Oxford; Melanie Phillips, columnist on The Sunday Times and currently working on a book about The Sex Change State.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the changing nature of work practices and the work ethic as it pertains at the end of the 20th century. Has our understanding of the nature and function of work really changed so radically since the beginning of the century? How can the past inform the future in the rapidly changing work environment? Has technology usurped craftsmanship, or is this no more than a superficial reading of an increasingly complex scene?With Professor Richard Sennett, visiting professor, London School for Economics and author of The Corrosion of Character - the Personal Consequences of Work in the New Capitalism; Theodore Zeldin, historian and Fellow of St Anthony’s College, Oxford; Melanie Phillips, columnist on The Sunday Times and currently working on a book about The Sex Change State.
episodes.summaryAI: The twentieth century transformed both work practices and the ethical frameworks surrounding labor. Mechanization, automation, and digital technology progressively displaced traditional craftsmanship, raising questions about whether technological efficiency destroyed the intrinsic satisfactions of skilled manual production. The Protestant work ethic that once sanctified diligent labor evolved under pressure from mass production, service economies, and changing attitudes toward leisure. Fundamental questions persist about whether contemporary understanding of work's purpose and value has shifted decisively since the century's beginning or whether older tensions between productivity and fulfillment remain unresolved.

episodes.title: Wormholes
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the tantalising idea that there are shortcuts between distant galaxies, somewhere out there in the universe. The idea emerged in the context of Einstein's theories and the challenge has been not so much to prove their unlikely existence as to show why they ought to be impossible. The universe would have to folded back on itself in places, and there would have to be something to make the wormholes and then to keep them open. But is there anywhere in the vast universe like that? Could there be holes that we or more advanced civilisations might travel through, from one galaxy to another and, if not, why not?
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the tantalising idea that there are shortcuts between distant galaxies, somewhere out there in the universe. episodes.summaryAI: Wormholes are hypothetical tunnels through spacetime offering shortcuts between distant galaxies, emerging from solutions to Einstein's general relativity field equations. The universe would need to fold back on itself, connecting remote regions through a bridge requiring exotic matter with negative energy to remain traversable. Physicists have struggled to prove definitively why stable, passable wormholes should be impossible, as no established physical law categorically forbids them. The tantalizing question remains whether advanced civilizations could exploit such structures for interstellar travel or communication across cosmic distances.

episodes.title: Writing and Political Oppression
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines how two writers’ work have been shaped by political oppression and explores whether writers have a political role in modern society. The connection between writers and politics has its roots in classical times, but in the 20th century the writer has been called on as the witness with increasing frequency and intensity. And many times the price of articulation has been severe. In the century in which saw the execution of writers such as Ken Saro Wiwa in Nigeria in 1995, and a fatwa imposed on Salman Rushdie, Melvyn Bragg talks to two writers who between them experienced exile, censorship and the manipulation of authoritarian states - Ariel Dorfman from South America and Nadine Gordimer, the Nobel Prize winner from South Africa, to discuss the writing of fiction and political oppression. What, if any, is the writer’s political role in our world today?With Nadine Gordimer, Nobel Prize-winning South African novelist; Ariel Dorfman, South American journalist, scholar and author of Death and the Maiden.
episodes.description: Melvyn Bragg examines how two writers’ work have been shaped by political oppression and explores whether writers have a political role in modern society. The connection between writers and politics has its roots in classical times, but in the 20th century the writer has been called on as the witness with increasing frequency and intensity. And many times the price of articulation has been severe. In the century in which saw the execution of writers such as Ken Saro Wiwa in Nigeria in 1995, and a fatwa imposed on Salman Rushdie, Melvyn Bragg talks to two writers who between them experienced exile, censorship and the manipulation of authoritarian states - Ariel Dorfman from South America and Nadine Gordimer, the Nobel Prize winner from South Africa, to discuss the writing of fiction and political oppression. What, if any, is the writer’s political role in our world today?With Nadine Gordimer, Nobel Prize-winning South African novelist; Ariel Dorfman, South American journalist, scholar and author of Death and the Maiden.
episodes.summaryAI: The relationship between writers and political power extends from classical antiquity to the present, with literature serving as witness to oppression, resistance, and state violence. Nigerian writer Ken Saro-Wiwa was executed by the military government for his activism, while Salman Rushdie faced a fatwa demanding his death for perceived blasphemy. These cases illustrate persistent patterns of censorship, exile, imprisonment, and murder directed at authors whose work challenges authority. Writers who have endured such persecution illuminate how creative expression becomes both a target of repressive regimes and a powerful instrument of dissent.

episodes.title: Wuthering Heights
episodes.synopsis: In a programme first broadcast in 2017, Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Emily Bronte (1818-1848) and her only novel, published in 1847 under the name 'Ellis Bell' just a year before her death. It is the story of Heathcliff, a foundling from Liverpool brought up in the Earnshaw family at the remote Wuthering Heights, high on the moors, who becomes close to the young Cathy Earnshaw but hears her say she can never marry him. He disappears and she marries his rival, Edgar Linton, of Thrushcross Grange even though she feels inextricably linked with Heathcliff, exclaiming to her maid 'I am Heathcliff!' On his return, Heathcliff steadily works through his revenge on all who he believes wronged him, and their relations. When Cathy dies, Heathcliff longs to be united with her in the grave. The raw passions and cruelty of the story unsettled Emily's sister Charlotte Bronte, whose novel Jane Eyre had been published shortly before, and who took pains to explain its roughness, jealousy and violence when introducing it to early readers. Over time, with its energy, imagination and scope, Wuthering Heights became celebrated as one of the great novels in English.

episodes.summaryAI: Emily Bronte's sole novel, published in 1847 a year before her death at thirty, follows Heathcliff, a foundling brought from Liverpool to the Yorkshire moors, whose intense bond with Catherine Earnshaw is destroyed when she marries his social superior Edgar Linton. Heathcliff's methodical revenge consumes two generations of the Earnshaw and Linton families through calculated cruelty and property acquisition. The novel's raw passions and moral ambiguity unsettled even Charlotte Bronte. Now celebrated as one of the greatest English novels, it defies conventional Victorian narrative with its unflinching exploration of obsession and destruction.

episodes.title: Wyclif and the Lollards
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss John Wyclif and the Lollards.John Wyclif was a medieval philosopher and theologian who in the fourteenth century instigated the first complete English translation of the Bible. One of the most important thinkers of the Middle Ages, he also led a movement of opposition to the Roman Church and its institutions which has come to be seen as a precursor to the Reformation. Wyclif disputed some of the key teachings of the Church, including the doctrine of transubstantiation. His followers, the Lollards, were later seen as dangerous heretics, and in the fifteenth century many of them were burnt at the stake. Today Lollardy is seen as the first significant movement of dissent against the Church in England

episodes.summaryAI: John Wyclif, the fourteenth-century Oxford theologian, instigated the first complete translation of the Bible into English, challenging the Church's monopoly on scriptural interpretation. He led intellectual opposition to Rome's authority, attacking clerical wealth, papal power, and the doctrine of transubstantiation by arguing that bread and wine retained their substance after consecration. His followers, derisively called Lollards, spread vernacular scripture and reformist ideas among laypeople despite fierce ecclesiastical persecution that saw adherents burned at the stake. Wyclif is widely regarded as a precursor to the Protestant Reformation.

episodes.title: Xenophon
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the life and work of Xenophon.Xenophon, an aristocratic Athenian, was one of the most celebrated writers of the ancient world. Born in around 430 BC, he was a friend and pupil of the great philosopher Socrates. In his twenties he took part in an unsuccessful attempt to overthrow the Persian king Artaxerxes II, and played a key role in guiding the surviving Greek troops - known as the Ten Thousand - back to safety. It was a dangerous journey from deep inside hostile territory, and lasted more than a year. Xenophon's gripping account of this military campaign, the Anabasis, is one of the masterpieces of Greek literature.Xenophon went on to write a history of the Peloponnesian War and its aftermath. But he was not just a historian, and his other works include books about household management, hunting and his mentor Socrates. His advice on the education and behaviour of princes had a significant influence in Renaissance Italy, and his treatise on horsemanship is still widely read today

episodes.summaryAI: Xenophon, an aristocratic Athenian and devoted pupil of Socrates, combined military leadership with prolific authorship across diverse subjects. His masterpiece, the Anabasis, recounts how he led ten thousand Greek mercenaries on an extraordinary retreat from deep inside hostile Persian territory back to the Black Sea following their employer's defeat at Cunaxa in 401 BC. Beyond this celebrated military narrative, he wrote histories of the Peloponnesian War continuing Thucydides' account, practical treatises on horsemanship and cavalry command, and works on household management and Socratic philosophy.

episodes.title: Yeats and Irish Politics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the poet W.B. Yeats and Irish politics. Yeats lived through a period of great change in Ireland from the collapse of the home rule bill through to the Easter Rising of 1916 and the partitioning of the country. In May 1916, 15 men were shot by the British government. They were the leaders of the Easter Rising – a doomed attempt to overthrow British rule in Ireland - and they were commemorated by W.B. Yeats in a poem called Easter 1916. It ends with the following lines: MacDonagh and MacBrideAnd Connolly and PearseNow and in time to be,Wherever green is worn,Are changed, changed utterly:A terrible beauty is born.Yeats lived through decades of turbulence in Ireland. He saw the suspension of home rule, civil war and the division of the country, but how did the politics of the age imprint themselves on his poetry, what was the nature of Yeats’ own nationalism, and what did he mean by that most famous of phrases ‘a terrible beauty is born’?With Roy Foster, Carroll Professor of Irish History at Oxford University and Fellow of Hertford College, Oxford; Fran Brearton, Reader in English at Queen’s University, Belfast and Assistant Director of the Seamus Heaney Centre for Poetry; Warwick Gould, Director of the Institute of English Studies in the School of Advanced Study, University of London

episodes.summaryAI: W.B. Yeats lived through Ireland's most turbulent political transformations, from the collapse of constitutional home rule through the 1916 Easter Rising to partition and civil war. His poem Easter 1916 captured the insurrection's paradox with the refrain that Ireland was changed utterly, that a terrible beauty was born. Political violence, nationalist aspiration, and disillusionment with revolutionary outcomes imprinted deeply on his poetry, forcing him to reconcile his romantic vision of Irish cultural identity with the brutal realities of armed struggle and the compromised independence that followed.

episodes.title: Yeats and Mysticism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg explores the strange and mystical world of the poet W B Yeats. Celtic folklore, the Theosophical society, the Golden Dawn group, seances and a wife who communicated with the spirit world all had a huge effect on the work of this great Irish poet. He published his first collection in 1889 and won the Nobel prize for literature in 1923.At the close of the nineteenth century he published one of his best known works. He Wishes for the Cloths of Heaven: “Had I the heavens’ embroidered cloths,Enwrought with golden and silver light,The blue and the dim and the dark clothsOf night and light and the half-light,I would spread the cloths under your feet:But I, being poor, have only my dreams; I have spread my dreams under your feet; Tread softly because you tread on my dreams.” But Yeats the dreamer and the poet was also a mystic, a philosopher and a practitioner of magic. From the occult subcultures of Victorian London to the outlandish folklore of the Irish Peasantry, Yeats’ obsession with the spiritual world infused his poetic mind and even drove him to describe his own religion. Why was the period so alive with spiritualism? And how did the poems reflect the dreams? With Roy Foster, Carroll Professor of Irish History at Oxford University; Warwick Gould, Director of the Institute of English Studies, University of London; Brenda Maddox, author of George’s Ghosts: A New Life of W B Yeats.

episodes.summaryAI: W.B. Yeats immersed himself in Celtic folklore, joined the Theosophical Society and the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, attended seances, and married a wife who practiced automatic writing to communicate with spirits. These mystical pursuits were not incidental but central to his poetic vision, from his first collection in 1889 through the Nobel Prize in 1923 and beyond. Occult symbolism, cyclical theories of history, and visionary experience pervade his mature work. Yeats operated simultaneously as mystic, systematic philosopher, and practicing magician committed to accessing hidden dimensions of reality.

episodes.title: Youth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the idea of youth. In 1898 Joseph Conrad wrote, “I remember my youth and the feeling that will never come back any more – the feeling that I could last for ever, outlast the sea, the earth, and all men; the deceitful feeling that lures us on to perils, to love, to vain effort – to death…”From antiquity to our own time, the concept of youth, with its promise of possibility and adventure, has been greeted with fascination as well as fear. The ancient Greeks saw the period of youth as dangerous and unpredictable, but how did they seek to control it? How did the Renaissance celebrate the ideals and intellect of youth? Why was 19th century British society so preoccupied with the moral well-being of young people? And does a distinct youth culture still exist? With Tim Whitmarsh, Lecturer in Hellenistic Literature at Exeter University; Thomas Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Birkbeck College, London; Deborah Thom, Lecturer in History at Robinson College, Cambridge.

With Tim Whitmarsh, Lecturer in Hellenistic Literature at Exeter University; Thomas Healy, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Birkbeck College, London; Deborah Thom, Lecturer in History at Robinson College, Cambridge.
episodes.summaryAI: Joseph Conrad described youth as a feeling that will never come back. Different civilizations have understood this life stage in contrasting ways. The Greeks regarded youth as inherently dangerous, requiring discipline and restraint. The Renaissance celebrated youthful energy, beauty, and potential. The nineteenth century became preoccupied with youth's moral well-being, developing institutions and literature dedicated to shaping character. The twentieth century saw the emergence of distinct youth cultures with their own music, fashion, and values. Whether such autonomous youth culture persists or has fragmented remains an open question.

episodes.title: Zen
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Zen. It's often thought of as a form of Buddhism that emphasises the practice of meditation over any particular set of beliefs. In fact Zen belongs to a particular intellectual tradition within Buddhism that took root in China in the 6th century AD. It spread to Japan in the early Middle Ages, where Zen practitioners set up religious institutions like temples, monasteries and universities that remain important today.

episodes.summaryAI: Zen is a form of Buddhism that prioritizes direct meditative experience over doctrinal study or scriptural authority. Originating as a distinct intellectual tradition when Buddhism encountered Chinese Daoist thought in the sixth century AD, it developed practices including seated meditation, paradoxical riddles, and austere aesthetic disciplines. Zen spread to Japan during the Middle Ages, where it profoundly shaped martial arts, tea ceremony, garden design, calligraphy, and architecture. Its temples, monasteries, and associated universities remain culturally and spiritually significant institutions in contemporary Japanese society.

episodes.title: Zeno's Paradoxes
episodes.synopsis: After 27 years, Melvyn Bragg has decided to step down from the In Our Time presenter’s chair. With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this third of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Greek philosophy.

With over a thousand episodes to choose from, he has selected just six that capture the huge range and depth of the subjects he and his experts have tackled. In this third of his choices, we hear Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Greek philosophy.
episodes.summaryAI: Zeno of Elea devised philosophical paradoxes in the fifth century BC that challenged fundamental assumptions about motion, space, time, and mathematical continuity. His paradox of Achilles demonstrates that a faster runner can seemingly never overtake a slower one, since the gap must be halved infinitely. The arrow paradox argues that a flying arrow is motionless at every individual instant, making continuous motion illusory. These puzzles exposed deep problems in Greek mathematics concerning infinity and infinitesimals, stimulating developments from Aristotle through calculus to modern set theory and mathematical analysis.

episodes.title: Zero
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the history of the number between 1 and -1, which has strange and uniquely beguiling qualities. Shakespeare’s King Lear warned, “Nothing will come of nothing”. The poet and priest John Donne said from the pulpit, “The less anything is, the less we know it: how invisible, unintelligible a thing is nothing”, and the English monk and historian William of Malmesbury called them “dangerous Saracen magic”. They were all talking about zero, the number or symbol that had been part of the mathematics in the East for centuries but was finally taking hold in Europe.What was it about zero that so repulsed their intellects? How was zero invented? And what role does zero play in mathematics today?With Robert Kaplan, co-founder of the Maths Circle at Harvard University and author of The Nothing That Is: A Natural History of Zero; Ian Stewart, Professor of Mathematics at the University of Warwick; Lisa Jardine, Professor of Renaissance Studies at Queen Mary, University of London.

episodes.summaryAI: Zero, the number between one and negative one, possesses paradoxical mathematical properties that provoked centuries of philosophical and cultural unease. Shakespeare wrote that nothing will come of nothing; John Donne marveled at nothingness's invisibility. William of Malmesbury dismissed it as dangerous Saracen magic. Eastern mathematical traditions, including Indian and Arabic systems, employed zero as placeholder and number for centuries before European adoption. Its eventual acceptance in Western mathematics transformed arithmetic, algebra, and calculus, enabling place-value notation and the concept of the void as a calculable, essential quantity.

episodes.title: Zoroastrianism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discusses Zoroastrianism. "Now have I seen him with my own eyes, knowing him in truth to be the wise Lord of the good mind and of good deeds and words." Thus spake the real Zarathustra, the prophet and founder of the ancient and modern religion of Zoroastrianism. It has claims to be the world's first monotheistic creed and perhaps as long ago as 1200 BC Zarathustra also said, "I point out the way, it is the truth, it is for all living". Truth is a central tenet of the religion which holds that people must above all do good things, hear good things and see good things.How was the religion established in Ancient Persia, what is its body of beliefs and how have they been developed and disseminated?With Vesta Sarkhosh Curtis, Curator of Ancient Iranian Coins in the Department of Coins and Medals at the British Museum; Farrokh Vajifdar, Fellow of the Royal Asiatic Society; Alan Williams, Senior Lecturer in Comparative Religion at the University of Manchester.

episodes.summaryAI: Zoroastrianism, founded by the prophet Zarathustra perhaps around 1200 BC, claims distinction as the world's first monotheistic religion. Established as the dominant faith of ancient Persia, it centers on truth as its supreme tenet, enjoining followers to do good things, hear good things, and see good things. Its cosmology presents existence as a struggle between forces of truth and falsehood, with individual moral choice determining cosmic outcome. Zoroastrian concepts of judgment, heaven, hell, and a final renovation of the world profoundly influenced Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.
